title: Identify the five most harmful anti-patterns in this codebase
prompt: Identify the five most harmful anti-patterns in this codebase. For each, explain the maintenance cost it creates and name the design pattern that would replace it. Order findings by frequency of occurrence across modules.

title: Survey the three most commonly used design patterns in this project
prompt: Survey the three most commonly used design patterns in this project. Verify each is applied consistently. For any inconsistency found, identify the canonical implementation and list which files deviate from it.

title: Analyze the most complex module in the codebase
prompt: Analyze the most complex module in the codebase. Recommend a specific design pattern to reduce its complexity. Explain the migration path, the tradeoffs involved, and what the module looks like after refactoring.

title: Identify every location in the codebase where null or undefined checks a
prompt: Identify every location in the codebase where null or undefined checks are repeated more than three times. For each cluster, assess whether a Null Object implementation would eliminate the defensive checks entirely. Estimate the lines of code removed per Null Object. Rank candidates by frequency of the null check. Recommend the top three Null Object implementations with their proposed interface.

title: Find all deep inheritance hierarchies more than two levels deep in the c
prompt: Find all deep inheritance hierarchies more than two levels deep in the codebase. For each, assess whether the subclasses represent genuine is-a relationships or accidental coupling through a shared base class. Identify any fragile base class risk where a change to the parent breaks subclasses in non-obvious ways. Recommend a composition-based refactor for the highest-risk hierarchy. Describe the migration path in concrete steps.

title: Survey all middleware and decorator chains in the codebase
prompt: Survey all middleware and decorator chains in the codebase. For each chain, identify whether the execution order is documented and whether reordering would silently break behavior. Find any chain missing a security or authorization layer that should be present. Flag chains where a new middleware was added without updating the documented order. Recommend a canonical order with the rationale for each position.

title: Identify every switch or long if/else block that dispatches on a type, r
prompt: Identify every switch or long if/else block that dispatches on a type, role, or status enum. For each, assess the cyclomatic complexity and the number of case arms. Recommend a Strategy or polymorphic dispatch replacement for the three highest-complexity blocks. Describe the data structure changes needed to support the dispatch. Estimate the test surface change from fewer conditional tests to more interface contract tests.

title: Audit all event listeners, pub/sub subscriptions, and hook callbacks in
prompt: Audit all event listeners, pub/sub subscriptions, and hook callbacks in the codebase. For each, trace the full chain of side effects triggered by a single event. Identify any subscription where a side effect in one subscriber silently affects another. Find events with more than five subscribers and assess whether the coupling is intentional or accidental. Recommend a bounded event contract for the highest fan-out events.

title: Survey the entire codebase for design patterns in active use
prompt: Survey the entire codebase for design patterns in active use. Identify which patterns appear most frequently and whether they are applied correctly in every instance. Flag any pattern that is used inconsistently across modules — where one team or layer applies it differently from another. Look specifically for anti-patterns that create long-term maintenance costs: god objects, shotgun surgery, feature envy, and circular dependencies. For each anti-pattern found, explain the blast radius if left unaddressed. Identify areas where no pattern exists but one would significantly reduce complexity. Recommend the top three pattern improvements with migration paths and estimated effort to implement.

title: Evaluate the state management approach used across the application
prompt: Evaluate the state management approach used across the application. Identify all locations where mutable state exists — in memory, in caches, in databases, and in UI stores. Flag any state that crosses service boundaries without a documented contract. Look for state that is duplicated across layers with inconsistent update logic. Assess whether state mutations are predictable and traceable or scattered across multiple callers. Identify race conditions that arise from shared mutable state. Recommend a cleaner state management strategy suited to this application's scale, with specific changes required in the top five most problematic modules.

title: You are a software architect tasked with assessing whether the design pa
prompt: You are a software architect tasked with assessing whether the design patterns in use across this codebase are helping or hindering long-term maintainability. Begin by inventorying every recognizable design pattern in active use and noting which modules apply them. Look for patterns applied inconsistently across the codebase, where one module uses a Repository but another calls the database directly. Identify the five most damaging anti-patterns present and for each explain the maintenance cost it creates in concrete terms such as time to add a new feature or test. Look for missing abstractions where the same concept is implemented differently in multiple places. Assess whether the current pattern usage reduces or increases the cognitive load for a developer new to the codebase. Recommend three targeted pattern improvements that would produce the highest return in terms of reduced complexity, with a migration path and estimated effort for each.

title: Review the codebase for anti-patterns that create testing difficulty, sp
prompt: Review the codebase for anti-patterns that create testing difficulty, specifically focusing on patterns that prevent unit test isolation. Identify every place where a concrete class is instantiated directly inside a function or constructor rather than being injected. Look for global singletons holding mutable state that persists across test cases, causing flaky tests and ordering dependencies. Find long functions with mixed levels of abstraction that make it impossible to test individual behaviors in isolation. Identify any test file that imports real external services, databases, or network clients rather than mocks or stubs. Assess whether the current code structure would allow a new developer to achieve 80 percent unit test coverage without refactoring. Recommend the five highest-leverage refactors that would move the codebase toward testable, pattern-aligned design.

title: Evaluate the codebase for over-engineering and under-engineering simulta
prompt: Evaluate the codebase for over-engineering and under-engineering simultaneously. Over-engineering appears as factory hierarchies with one implementation, abstract base classes with one subclass, strategy patterns where a simple function would do, and event bus indirection for calls that could be direct. Under-engineering appears as long procedural functions with no abstraction, repeated logic in three or more places, and data objects with public setters exposing all fields without invariant protection. For each instance of over-engineering found, explain the cognitive tax it adds for future maintainers. For each instance of under-engineering, estimate the defect probability introduced by the duplication or direct coupling. Produce a balanced assessment recommending which abstractions to collapse, which duplications to consolidate, and which new abstractions to introduce, ordered by the ratio of benefit to implementation effort.

title: Audit codebase compliance with technical standards
prompt: Review the codebase against the defined technical standards (naming conventions, file structure, error handling, logging format). Identify the top violations by frequency and impact.
prompt_short: Find all violations of technical standards across the codebase.
prompt_filtered: Standards compliance only. List violations with file and line references.

title: Identify missing technical standards that should exist
prompt: Review the codebase for areas where conventions are inconsistent, suggesting no standard exists. List the top 10 areas where a written standard would prevent repeated issues.
prompt_short: Find areas lacking standards that are causing inconsistency or bugs.
prompt_filtered: Standards gap analysis only. No implementation recommendations.

title: Document the implicit standards the team follows
prompt: Infer the unstated conventions from the existing codebase. Document them as explicit standards so new contributors follow them without guessing. Focus on naming, structure, error handling, and API contracts.
prompt_short: Infer and document all implicit conventions from the existing codebase.
prompt_filtered: Convention documentation only. No prescriptive changes.

title: Find inconsistent naming conventions across modules
prompt: Survey variable, function, file, and endpoint naming across the entire codebase. Identify the most common inconsistencies. Propose a single canonical naming convention for each category.
prompt_short: Find all naming inconsistencies and propose canonical conventions.
prompt_filtered: Naming conventions only. No logic or behavior analysis.

title: Assess code review standards and enforcement gaps
prompt: Evaluate whether technical standards are enforced via linting, CI, or code review checklists. Identify gaps where violations could slip through. Recommend automated enforcement where manual review is unreliable.
prompt_short: Find gaps in how technical standards are enforced in the review process.
prompt_filtered: Enforcement gaps only. Tooling and process recommendations.

title: Audit logging standards for consistency and completeness
prompt: Review all log statements across the codebase. Identify logs missing required fields — timestamp, correlation ID, log level, service name. Find locations that log sensitive data in plaintext. Verify that error logs include stack traces and enough context to diagnose without a debugger.
prompt_short: Audit log statements for required fields, sensitive data, and diagnostic value.
prompt_filtered: Logging standards only. Field completeness and sensitive data in logs.

title: Check error handling standards across all service boundaries
prompt: Verify that every service boundary — HTTP handler, queue consumer, background job — follows the same error handling contract: catches exceptions, logs with context, returns a structured error response, and never leaks internal stack traces to external callers.
prompt_short: Audit error handling at all service boundaries for consistency.
prompt_filtered: Error handling standards only. Boundary contracts and leak prevention.

title: Find hardcoded configuration values that should be externalized
prompt: Search the codebase for hardcoded URLs, port numbers, timeout values, retry counts, and limits. For each, assess whether it should be in an environment variable or configuration file so it can be changed without a code deployment.
prompt_short: Find hardcoded values that should be externalized to configuration.
prompt_filtered: Configuration externalization only. No secrets — values only.

title: Verify test coverage standards are met for critical paths
prompt: Identify the critical paths in the system — auth, payments, data writes — and verify each has documented coverage requirements. Find critical paths with no unit tests, no integration tests, or no contract tests. Report coverage gaps by risk level.
prompt_short: Find critical paths with insufficient test coverage by risk level.
prompt_filtered: Test coverage standards only. Critical paths with no test safety net.

title: Audit API versioning strategy and backward compatibility rules
prompt: Review the API versioning strategy. Identify APIs with no version, APIs where breaking changes are deployed without a version bump, and APIs where old versions are never deprecated. Recommend a versioning standard with clear deprecation timelines.
prompt_short: Audit API versioning for breaking changes deployed without version bumps.
prompt_filtered: API versioning only. Breaking changes and deprecation policy.

title: Identify missing type safety standards in dynamic language code
prompt: Find areas in dynamically-typed code — or TypeScript with excessive 'any' usage — where runtime type errors are likely. Identify functions accepting untyped input, returning untyped output, or casting through 'any'. Recommend strict typing standards with specific annotations.
prompt_short: Find excessive any usage and untyped functions that hide runtime errors.
prompt_filtered: Type safety only. TypeScript any casts and untyped boundaries.

title: Check dependency injection standards for testability
prompt: Verify that dependencies — services, clients, repositories — are injected rather than instantiated directly inside functions. Find modules that create their own dependencies internally, making them impossible to unit test in isolation. Recommend a DI standard.
prompt_short: Find modules with hardwired dependencies that cannot be mocked in tests.
prompt_filtered: DI standards only. Hardwired internal dependencies.

title: Review documentation standards for public interfaces
prompt: Assess whether public functions, API endpoints, and exported types have adequate documentation — parameter descriptions, return types, error cases, and usage examples. Find public interfaces with no documentation. Recommend a minimum documentation standard.
prompt_short: Find public interfaces with no documentation and set a minimum standard.
prompt_filtered: Documentation standards only. Public API and exported type docs.

title: Audit commit and branch naming standards compliance
prompt: Review recent git history for compliance with commit message and branch naming conventions. Identify commit messages that are too vague to understand without context and branches with no clear relationship to an issue or ticket. Recommend an enforced standard via hooks.
prompt_short: Audit git history for commit message and branch naming standards.
prompt_filtered: Git standards only. Commit messages and branch naming patterns.

title: Find inconsistent async and promise handling patterns
prompt: Identify where the codebase mixes async/await with raw Promise chains and callback patterns for the same type of operation. Flag unhandled promise rejections, missing await on async calls, and fire-and-forget patterns that suppress errors. Recommend a consistent async standard.
prompt_short: Find mixed async patterns and unhandled promises across the codebase.
prompt_filtered: Async standards only. Mixed patterns and unhandled rejections.

title: Assess feature flag standards and dead flag cleanup
prompt: Inventory all active feature flags in the codebase. Identify flags that have been on for more than 90 days with no plan to remove them. Find flags with no documented owner or expiry date. Recommend a feature flag lifecycle standard with mandatory cleanup dates.
prompt_short: Find stale feature flags with no owner or cleanup date.
prompt_filtered: Feature flag standards only. Long-lived flags and cleanup policy.

title: Review secrets management standards for environment variables
prompt: Verify that all secrets are accessed through a standard mechanism — environment variables, a secrets manager, or a vault client — rather than config files, source code, or build-time values. Find any secret accessed outside the standard channel. Recommend a unified secrets standard.
prompt_short: Audit how secrets are accessed and find violations of the standard.
prompt_filtered: Secrets management only. Non-standard secret access patterns.

title: Fill in Missing Data Gaps
prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

title: Gain full understanding before completing
prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

title: Find What's Broken
prompt: Find What's Broken

title: Recommend Next Steps
prompt: Recommend Next Steps

title: Score for Production Readiness
prompt: Score for Production Readiness

title: Audit Standards Compliance
prompt: Audit Standards Compliance

title: Flag Naming Violations
prompt: Flag Naming Violations

title: Detect Hardcoded Configuration Values
prompt: Detect Hardcoded Configuration Values

title: Audit Secrets Management Compliance
prompt: Audit Secrets Management Compliance

title: Check Async Pattern Consistency
prompt: Check Async Pattern Consistency

title: Find Missing Type Annotations
prompt: Find Missing Type Annotations

title: Review Test Coverage Standards
prompt: Review Test Coverage Standards

title: Assess Feature Flag Lifecycle Compliance
prompt: Assess Feature Flag Lifecycle Compliance

title: Validate Commit Message Standards
prompt: Validate Commit Message Standards

title: Find Unforced Standards Violations
prompt: Find Unforced Standards Violations

title: Audit the codebase for violations of the top five documented technical s
prompt: Audit the codebase for violations of the top five documented technical standards. For each violation type, count frequency and identify the modules most affected. Order by violation count and recommend automated enforcement tools for the top three.

title: Find naming inconsistencies across variables, functions, files, and API
prompt: Find naming inconsistencies across variables, functions, files, and API endpoints. For each inconsistency category, identify what convention the majority of the codebase follows. Produce a canonical reference document for each naming category.

title: Evaluate all linting and formatting rules currently enforced
prompt: Evaluate all linting and formatting rules currently enforced. Identify any standard that is documented but not enforced in CI. For each unforced standard, recommend the specific linter rule or tooling change that would close the enforcement gap.

title: Identify every place in the codebase where environment configuration suc
prompt: Identify every place in the codebase where environment configuration such as URLs, ports, timeout values, and feature flags is hardcoded rather than externalized. For each hardcoded value, classify it as a deployment concern, a tuning parameter, or a feature toggle. Recommend the specific externalization mechanism appropriate for each class. Verify that the build pipeline does not bake configuration into artifacts that are promoted across environments.

title: Audit all asynchronous code patterns in the codebase
prompt: Audit all asynchronous code patterns in the codebase. Identify every place where async/await is mixed with raw Promise chains or callback-style APIs for the same category of operation. Find every unhandled promise rejection. Flag fire-and-forget patterns that suppress errors silently. Produce a canonical async standard document that resolves every inconsistency found, with before-and-after code examples.

title: Review all public APIs, exported functions, and module interfaces for ad
prompt: Review all public APIs, exported functions, and module interfaces for adequate documentation. Identify every public function with no parameter documentation, no return type description, and no error case documentation. Assess whether the documentation is accurate where the code behavior has changed but the docstring has not been updated. Recommend a minimum documentation standard with a checklist that can be enforced in code review. Identify the five interfaces whose poor documentation causes the most repeated questions or bugs.

title: Evaluate the secrets management posture of the codebase
prompt: Evaluate the secrets management posture of the codebase. Find every location where a secret is accessed through a mechanism other than the approved secrets channel. Identify secrets that appear in build scripts, Dockerfiles, CI configuration, or environment variable defaults. Assess whether secret rotation is possible without a code deployment. Recommend a unified secrets standard with a migration plan for any non-compliant secret access pattern found.

title: Audit all feature flags in the codebase
prompt: Audit all feature flags in the codebase. Identify flags that have been active for more than 90 days without a documented removal date. Find flags with no documented owner, no rollback procedure, and no coverage in tests for both the enabled and disabled states. Assess the percentage of the codebase protected by flags that have been on so long they effectively represent permanent code paths. Recommend a feature flag lifecycle policy with specific age limits, owner requirements, and automated staleness detection.

title: Review the codebase against every documented technical standard in the p
prompt: Review the codebase against every documented technical standard in the project. Identify the top ten violations by frequency across all modules. For each violation, note the file and function where it most commonly appears and explain the downstream cost — readability loss, debugging difficulty, or onboarding friction. Assess whether the standard is enforced automatically through linting or CI, or whether it relies entirely on human code review. Identify any standard that is so frequently violated it should either be retired or enforced programmatically. Recommend the three highest-leverage enforcement changes that would raise overall compliance with the least implementation effort.

title: Analyze whether the codebase has sufficient technical standards to guide
prompt: Analyze whether the codebase has sufficient technical standards to guide new contributors. Identify the five areas where conventions are most inconsistent, suggesting that no written standard exists. For each area, infer what the implicit standard appears to be from the majority of the codebase. Document these as proposed explicit standards. Then assess the code review process — are standards checked in pull request templates, automated checks, or left to reviewer memory. Identify the most common standard violations that are consistently missed in code review and recommend how to catch them earlier in the development cycle.

title: You are a technical standards auditor reviewing a codebase for a product
prompt: You are a technical standards auditor reviewing a codebase for a production system that has grown across multiple teams and contributors over several years. Your goal is to assess the actual state of standards compliance, not the documented standards but the lived reality of how the codebase is written. Begin by identifying the five most important technical standards the team believes it follows. For each standard, audit a statistically meaningful sample of the codebase to determine the actual compliance rate. Identify the modules or authors most frequently out of compliance. Assess whether the standard is enforced by tooling or relies entirely on human code review. Look for standards that are documented but have no automated enforcement gate, meaning violations regularly reach production. Find the three standards whose non-compliance has caused the most bugs or debugging difficulty. Recommend specific tooling changes such as linter rules, CI checks, and commit hooks that would raise compliance for each standard without increasing the code review burden on humans.

title: Perform a full inventory of the implicit conventions that govern this co
prompt: Perform a full inventory of the implicit conventions that govern this codebase, conventions that exist in the collective developer knowledge but are not written down anywhere. Look for consistent patterns in file naming, module structure, error code formats, response shapes, and identifier casing that reveal an unwritten rule. For each convention found, assess how consistently it is applied across the codebase. Identify conventions that break down at module boundaries where one team's output does not match another's implicit standard. Assess which implicit conventions, if made explicit and enforced, would have the greatest impact on reducing onboarding time for new contributors. Produce a draft standards document covering the ten highest-value implicit conventions, written in the form of actionable rules with a rationale and a bad-pattern versus good-pattern example for each.

title: Audit the logging and observability standards across the entire codebase
prompt: Audit the logging and observability standards across the entire codebase. Identify every log statement in the system and classify it by whether it includes the mandatory fields for your observability platform such as timestamp, correlation ID, service name, log level, and structured context. Find log statements that include sensitive data in plaintext. Identify error logs that do not include enough context to diagnose the issue without access to a debugger or the source code. Look for services with no logging at all on critical code paths. Assess whether log levels are used consistently, whether debug logs are filtered in production, whether info logs are actionable, and whether error logs always represent conditions requiring attention. Recommend a canonical logging standard with a required field checklist and a sensitive data redaction policy, and identify the ten log statements that most urgently need to be updated to comply.

title: Audit all state sources and ownership in the app
prompt: Map every piece of state in the application — local, global, server, URL. For each, identify who owns it, who mutates it, and whether ownership is clear. Flag shared mutable state as a risk.
prompt_short: Map all state sources, owners, and mutation paths across the app.
prompt_filtered: State audit only. No rendering or UI concerns.

title: Find state synchronization bugs and race conditions
prompt: Identify where two or more state sources can fall out of sync. Look for race conditions in async state updates, stale closures, and missing invalidation logic. Prioritize by user-visible impact.
prompt_short: Find all state sync bugs and race conditions in async flows.
prompt_filtered: State synchronization only. Async state only.

title: Identify over-fetching and redundant server state
prompt: Find cases where server data is fetched more than necessary, stored in multiple places, or kept beyond its useful lifetime. Recommend a consistent server-state caching strategy.
prompt_short: Find redundant fetches and over-caching of server state.
prompt_filtered: Server state only. Client-only state is out of scope.

title: Recommend simplifications to the state architecture
prompt: Evaluate whether the current state management approach is appropriate for the app's complexity. Identify where it is over-engineered or under-powered. Recommend specific simplifications with rationale.
prompt_short: Recommend concrete simplifications to how state is managed.
prompt_filtered: Architecture recommendations only. No line-level changes.

title: Check state persistence and rehydration correctness
prompt: Verify that all persisted state (localStorage, cookies, URL params) is correctly rehydrated on load without causing hydration mismatches, stale data, or security leaks.
prompt_short: Verify persisted state rehydrates correctly without mismatches or leaks.
prompt_filtered: Persistence and rehydration only. In-memory state is out of scope.

title: Identify optimistic update patterns with missing rollback logic
prompt: Find all optimistic UI updates — where local state is mutated before server confirmation. For each, verify a rollback path exists if the server request fails. Flag cases where failed requests leave the UI in a permanently incorrect state.
prompt_short: Find optimistic updates with no server-failure rollback path.
prompt_filtered: Optimistic update correctness only. Rollback on server error.

title: Audit derived state for unnecessary recomputation
prompt: Find all derived state — values computed from other state — that is recalculated on every render or update instead of being memoized. Identify the highest-cost derivations and recommend memoization or selector caching strategies.
prompt_short: Find expensive derived state that is recomputed unnecessarily on every update.
prompt_filtered: Derived state performance only. Memoization and selector caching gaps.

title: Find global state that should be co-located with its consumer
prompt: Identify state stored globally — in a store, context, or module singleton — that is only ever consumed by a single component or module. For each, assess whether lifting it down to the consumer would reduce complexity and eliminate unnecessary re-renders.
prompt_short: Find over-elevated global state that should live closer to its consumer.
prompt_filtered: State colocation only. Global state used by a single consumer.

title: Assess loading and error state handling consistency
prompt: Review how loading states and error states are tracked across the application. Identify components that show stale data during a re-fetch, or that fail silently when a request errors. Verify a consistent pattern is applied to all async state — pending, success, error, empty.
prompt_short: Audit async state handling for consistent loading and error state patterns.
prompt_filtered: Async state UX only. Loading, error, and empty state coverage.

title: Check state normalization for relational data
prompt: Identify any state that stores nested relational data — lists of objects that reference each other — without normalization. Nested state creates update consistency bugs when the same entity appears in multiple places. Recommend a normalized store shape for the highest-risk entities.
prompt_short: Find unnormalized relational data in state that causes update inconsistencies.
prompt_filtered: State normalization only. Nested entity duplication risks.

title: Identify state machines that are implemented as ad-hoc flags
prompt: Find components or modules that track state using multiple boolean flags — isLoading, isError, isSuccess, isRetrying — that can be in impossible combinations. For each, assess whether a formal state machine with explicit transitions would eliminate impossible states and simplify the logic.
prompt_short: Find boolean flag combinations that should be a formal state machine.
prompt_filtered: State machine candidates only. Impossible boolean flag states.

title: Review undo/redo state history implementation for correctness
prompt: Find all undo/redo or history features in the application. Verify the history stack is bounded in size, correctly serialized, and handles side effects — network calls triggered during an action — that cannot be undone. Flag unlimited history that grows without a cap.
prompt_short: Audit undo/redo history for size bounds and unundoable side effects.
prompt_filtered: Undo/redo implementation only. Stack bounds and side-effect handling.

title: Identify cross-tab state synchronization requirements
prompt: Assess whether the application needs to synchronize state across browser tabs — session data, cart contents, auth status. Find any state that changes in one tab but leaves other open tabs stale in a way that causes user confusion or data loss. Recommend a BroadcastChannel or storage event strategy.
prompt_short: Find state that should sync across browser tabs but currently does not.
prompt_filtered: Cross-tab sync only. State that diverges across multiple open tabs.

title: Audit selector performance in large state trees
prompt: Identify selectors or mapStateToProps functions that run on every state change rather than only when their input slices change. Find selectors doing expensive computation — filtering large arrays, computing aggregates — without memoization. Recommend reselect or equivalent for the top offenders.
prompt_short: Find expensive unmemoized selectors that run on every state change.
prompt_filtered: Selector performance only. Unmemoized expensive derivations.

title: Find state that leaks across user sessions or authenticated contexts
prompt: Identify state that is not cleared on user logout — cached data, search results, user preferences, in-flight request state. For each, assess whether the retained state could expose previous user data to the next user on a shared device. Recommend a session-scoped state clearing strategy.
prompt_short: Find state not cleared on logout that could leak data between users.
prompt_filtered: Session boundary only. State persisting across logout and login.

title: Assess WebSocket or SSE connection state management
prompt: Review how the application manages real-time connection state — WebSocket or SSE lifecycle, reconnection on disconnect, and state reconciliation after reconnection. Identify connections that do not reconnect automatically, or that miss events during the reconnection gap.
prompt_short: Audit real-time connection state and reconnection logic for gaps.
prompt_filtered: Connection lifecycle only. Reconnect and event-gap recovery.

title: Check that form state is isolated from application state
prompt: Identify forms that write intermediate draft state directly into global application state rather than local form state. This causes partial data to appear in other parts of the app before the user submits. Recommend isolating form drafts in local or form-library-managed state.
prompt_short: Find forms writing draft values into global state before submission.
prompt_filtered: Form state isolation only. Draft state leaking into global context.

prompt: Identify Derived State Recomputation

title: Detect Cross-Tab State Divergence
prompt: Detect Cross-Tab State Divergence

title: Review State Machine Coverage
prompt: Review State Machine Coverage

title: Identify all locations where state crosses a service or component bounda
prompt: Identify all locations where state crosses a service or component boundary. For each crossing, check whether a contract is documented. Flag any boundary where state is passed implicitly or through shared global references.

title: Audit all persisted state storage — localStorage, cookies, URL params, a
prompt: Audit all persisted state storage — localStorage, cookies, URL params, and session storage. For each, verify correct rehydration on page load. Identify any location where stale or corrupt persisted state would cause a broken user experience.

title: Find all places where the same piece of state is stored in two or more l
prompt: Find all places where the same piece of state is stored in two or more locations simultaneously. For each duplication, assess the synchronization logic. Identify any race condition where the two copies can diverge and explain the user-visible symptom.

title: Identify every place in the application where state persists across a us
prompt: Identify every place in the application where state persists across a user logout. For each retained state item, classify it as safe, potentially sensitive, or definitely sensitive. Recommend a session-scoped cleanup strategy for each sensitive item. Verify the cleanup runs synchronously before the redirect to the login page. Identify any state that cannot be cleared without a full page reload.

title: Find all derived state computations in the codebase, values that are com
prompt: Find all derived state computations in the codebase, values that are computed from other state on every render or update. For each, measure the computational cost and assess whether memoization would reduce unnecessary recalculations. Identify derived state that is recomputed even when its input slices have not changed. Recommend specific memoization strategies for the top five most expensive derivations. Calculate the estimated render budget saved per cycle.

title: Audit all global state stores in the application
prompt: Audit all global state stores in the application. For each store, identify whether the state is truly global or whether it could be co-located with a single component without affecting other parts of the app. For any state that is global but consumed by only one consumer, assess the risk of lifting it down. Recommend the specific state items to move, the component to move them to, and the expected reduction in unnecessary re-renders.

title: Identify every asynchronous state transition in the application and veri
prompt: Identify every asynchronous state transition in the application and verify that it handles all four states: loading, success, error, and empty. Find any async operation that transitions directly from loading to success without an error state, making silent failures invisible to the user. Look for loading states that display stale data without a visual indicator. Find operations with no empty state showing nothing when the response is a valid empty result. Recommend a consistent async state pattern with a shared hook or utility that enforces all four states.

title: Review all optimistic UI update patterns in the application
prompt: Review all optimistic UI update patterns in the application. For each optimistic update, verify that a rollback path exists if the server request fails. Identify updates that mutate client state before receiving server confirmation and have no mechanism to revert on failure. Look for cases where a failed request leaves the UI displaying data that the server rejected. Assess whether rollback errors are surfaced to the user or silently dropped. Recommend a consistent optimistic update pattern with mandatory rollback handling.

title: Analyze how state is managed across the full application stack — from cl
prompt: Analyze how state is managed across the full application stack — from client to server to database. Identify every location where mutable state exists and classify it by scope: local component state, shared application state, server-side session state, and persisted state. Flag any state that is accessible from outside its intended scope without an explicit contract. Look for state that is mutated from multiple call sites without a single coordinating mechanism. Identify the most common source of state bugs in the codebase — whether it is initialization order, async timing, or missing invalidation logic. Recommend the minimal changes to state management that would eliminate the top five categories of state-related bugs.

title: Review all state persistence mechanisms in use — localStorage, cookies,
prompt: Review all state persistence mechanisms in use — localStorage, cookies, URL parameters, IndexedDB, and any server-side session stores. For each mechanism, verify that state is correctly serialized on write and correctly deserialized on read. Identify any location where schema changes would silently corrupt existing persisted state. Check whether persisted state is validated on rehydration or blindly trusted. Look for sensitive data stored in client-accessible persistence without encryption. Assess whether state is cleared appropriately on logout or session expiry. Conclude with a ranked list of persistence vulnerabilities sorted by exploitability and user impact.

title: You are a frontend architecture reviewer assessing the state management
prompt: You are a frontend architecture reviewer assessing the state management design of a production application. Begin by mapping every piece of application state, classifying each as local component state, shared application state, server-synchronized state, URL-encoded state, or persisted client-side state. For each category, assess whether the current storage mechanism is appropriate for the data's scope and lifecycle. Identify state that has escaped its intended scope such as local state promoted to global or global state consumed by only one component. Look for state duplicated across the client and the server with inconsistent synchronization logic. Assess the predictability of state mutations, whether every mutation is traceable to a single source of truth with a documented trigger. Identify the five state-related bugs most likely to appear under concurrent user interactions such as rapid clicking, tab switching, or background refetches. Recommend the minimal architectural changes that would make state ownership explicit, mutations traceable, and race conditions structurally impossible in the three highest-risk areas.

title: Audit the application's handling of asynchronous server state, data fetc
prompt: Audit the application's handling of asynchronous server state, data fetched from APIs or databases that is held in client memory. Identify every API call that is made on component mount and assess whether the result is cached, how long it is retained, and what triggers a refetch. Look for data that is fetched multiple times in parallel by different components with no deduplication. Identify stale data that is displayed to users without a loading or freshness indicator. Assess whether optimistic updates correctly revert on server error or leave the client in an inconsistent state. Find any server state that is stored in a global store alongside client-only state, blurring the ownership boundary. Review the cache invalidation strategy, whether writes correctly invalidate the affected read caches and whether the invalidation is immediate or delayed. Conclude with a ranked list of server state management improvements ordered by user impact.

title: Evaluate the state persistence and rehydration story of the application
prompt: Evaluate the state persistence and rehydration story of the application from the perspective of a user returning to the app on a new device or after clearing browser storage. Identify all state that is persisted in localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, cookies, or URL parameters, and assess whether each piece is validated on rehydration. Look for schema assumptions baked into the rehydration logic that would fail silently if the persisted data was written by an older version of the app. Identify sensitive data stored in client-accessible persistence that should be short-lived or encrypted. Assess whether the app gracefully degrades when persisted state is absent, corrupted, or in an unexpected format. Check whether the app correctly handles the state left by a previous user on a shared device after logout. Recommend a persistence standards document covering what should and should not be persisted, the required validation logic on rehydration, and the cleanup contract on session end.

title: Audit event schema consistency across all producers
prompt: Review all event schemas produced by the system. Identify missing fields, inconsistent naming, missing versioning, and events that have drifted from their documented schema.
prompt_short: Audit event schemas for consistency, versioning, and drift.
prompt_filtered: Event schema only. No consumer-side analysis.

title: Find missing events that should be published
prompt: Walk through the key user and system actions. Identify significant events that are not being published but should be — for audit, analytics, or downstream processing needs.
prompt_short: Find significant system actions with no corresponding event published.
prompt_filtered: Missing events only. No schema or consumer analysis.

title: Identify consumer lag and processing bottlenecks
prompt: Assess where consumers fall behind producers. Identify the slowest consumers, the highest-volume topics, and any single-threaded processing that could be parallelized. Estimate acceptable lag thresholds.
prompt_short: Find slow consumers and processing bottlenecks in the event pipeline.
prompt_filtered: Consumer performance only. Producer-side is out of scope.

title: Review event ordering guarantees and failure handling
prompt: Assess which event consumers require ordering guarantees and whether the current setup provides them. Identify consumers that don't handle out-of-order events safely. Review dead-letter queue and retry logic.
prompt_short: Check event ordering guarantees and what happens when processing fails.
prompt_filtered: Ordering and failure handling only. No throughput analysis.

title: Check idempotency of all event consumers
prompt: Verify every event consumer is idempotent — processing the same event twice produces the same result. Identify consumers that would create duplicate data or side effects on re-delivery.
prompt_short: Verify all event consumers are safe to receive duplicate messages.
prompt_filtered: Idempotency only. Flag non-idempotent consumers with risk level.

title: Evaluate exactly-once vs at-least-once delivery trade-offs
prompt: Identify which event streams currently guarantee at-least-once delivery and which consumers require exactly-once semantics. For each mismatch, describe the business impact of a duplicate event and recommend whether to implement idempotency at the consumer or upgrade to a broker supporting exactly-once delivery.
prompt_short: Find consumers needing exactly-once semantics on at-least-once streams.
prompt_filtered: Delivery guarantee mismatch only. At-least-once vs exactly-once gaps.

title: Review partition key design for even distribution
prompt: Audit all Kafka or partitioned stream topics. Identify partition keys that create hot partitions — where one key receives disproportionately more messages than others, causing consumer lag on that partition while others are idle. Recommend rebalanced partition key strategies.
prompt_short: Find partition keys creating hot partitions and uneven consumer load.
prompt_filtered: Partition key design only. Hot partition and consumer imbalance.

title: Assess event schema evolution and backward compatibility
prompt: Review how event schemas change over time. Identify any schema change that would break existing consumers — removed fields, changed types, renamed keys. Verify that schema evolution follows a compatibility mode — backward, forward, or full — and that consumers are tested against new schema versions before deployment.
prompt_short: Audit event schema changes for backward compatibility with existing consumers.
prompt_filtered: Schema evolution only. Breaking changes and compatibility modes.

title: Find events with no downstream consumer registered
prompt: Identify events published to a stream but currently consumed by zero active consumers. For each orphaned event stream, assess whether the producer should be removed, the consumer was accidentally deregistered, or the event should be consolidated with an active stream.
prompt_short: Find published events with no active downstream consumer.
prompt_filtered: Orphaned streams only. Events produced but never consumed.

title: Assess replay capability for incident recovery
prompt: Verify that each event stream retains events long enough to support a full replay for incident recovery. Identify streams with retention shorter than the time needed to detect and respond to a data corruption incident. Recommend retention windows that align with the recovery time objective.
prompt_short: Verify event stream retention supports full replay for incident recovery.
prompt_filtered: Replay capability only. Retention window vs recovery time objective.

title: Identify missing correlation IDs for distributed tracing
prompt: Verify that every event payload includes a correlation ID that links it to the originating request. Find events with no correlation ID, making it impossible to trace the end-to-end flow from a user action through all downstream processing steps.
prompt_short: Find events missing correlation IDs that break distributed tracing.
prompt_filtered: Correlation ID coverage only. Events that break trace continuity.

title: Assess consumer group isolation and offset management
prompt: Review Kafka or stream consumer group configurations. Identify consumer groups sharing an offset with another group — causing one group's progress to affect another's. Verify each consumer group manages its own offset and that offset commits occur after successful processing, not before.
prompt_short: Audit consumer group isolation and offset commit order correctness.
prompt_filtered: Consumer group config only. Offset sharing and commit ordering.

title: Find events that should trigger notifications but do not
prompt: Review all significant business events in the system. Identify events where a downstream user, system, or team should be notified but the current implementation produces no notification. Map each missing notification to the consumer that should receive it.
prompt_short: Find business events that should trigger notifications but currently do not.
prompt_filtered: Missing notification triggers only. Business event to notification mapping.

title: Audit event bus for security and authorization gaps
prompt: Verify that event producers are authenticated before they can publish to a topic. Identify topics where any service can publish — allowing a compromised service to inject fake events. Assess whether sensitive event payloads are encrypted at rest on the broker.
prompt_short: Audit event stream authorization and encryption for security gaps.
prompt_filtered: Event bus security only. Producer auth and payload encryption.

title: Evaluate dead-letter queue strategy for all event streams
prompt: Verify every event stream has a dead-letter topic or queue configured. Identify streams where failed events are discarded with no DLQ routing. Assess whether DLQ events are monitored and whether a replay procedure is documented and tested.
prompt_short: Check that every event stream routes failed events to a dead-letter queue.
prompt_filtered: DLQ configuration only. Missing dead-letter queues and replay procedures.

title: Check event timestamp handling for clock skew correctness
prompt: Identify all event timestamps and assess whether they represent the event-time (when the event occurred) or processing-time (when it was written to the broker). Find windowed aggregations or time-ordered queries that fail under clock skew between producers. Recommend event-time watermark strategies.
prompt_short: Find timestamp handling bugs under clock skew in event-driven pipelines.
prompt_filtered: Event-time correctness only. Clock skew and watermark handling.

title: Identify compaction and retention policies for stateful topics
prompt: Find topics used as event-sourced state — where the latest value per key represents the current system state. Verify log compaction is enabled on these topics so old values are safely removed. Identify infinite-retention topics that will grow unbounded and should be compacted or archived.
prompt_short: Find stateful topics missing log compaction that will grow unbounded.
prompt_filtered: Topic retention policy only. Compaction candidates and unbounded growth.

title: Fill in Missing Data Gaps
prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

prompt: Verify Idempotency

prompt: Audit Event Ordering

title: Audit Dead Letter Queue Coverage
prompt: Audit Dead Letter Queue Coverage

title: Check Partition Key Balance
prompt: Check Partition Key Balance

title: Find Orphaned Event Streams
prompt: Find Orphaned Event Streams

title: Verify Correlation ID Coverage
prompt: Verify Correlation ID Coverage

title: Assess Schema Evolution Safety
prompt: Assess Schema Evolution Safety

title: Detect Missing Event Timestamps
prompt: Detect Missing Event Timestamps

title: Review Consumer Group Isolation
prompt: Review Consumer Group Isolation

title: Find Events With No Consumers
prompt: Find Events With No Consumers

title: List every event consumer in the system
prompt: List every event consumer in the system. For each, determine whether it is idempotent. Flag any consumer that would produce duplicate writes, duplicate notifications, or duplicate charges if the same event were delivered twice.

title: Audit event ordering guarantees across all producers and consumers
prompt: Audit event ordering guarantees across all producers and consumers. Identify any consumer that assumes strict ordering but receives events from a source that only guarantees at-least-once delivery. Describe the failure mode and recommend a fix.

title: Find all event schemas that have changed without a versioning strategy
prompt: Find all event schemas that have changed without a versioning strategy. For each schema change, assess whether old consumers would break if they received a new event format. Recommend a backward-compatible schema evolution approach.

title: Audit all event schemas in the system for backward compatibility
prompt: Audit all event schemas in the system for backward compatibility. Identify any schema change deployed in the last 90 days that was not versioned. For each change, assess whether existing consumers would have broken on receiving a new-format event. Recommend a schema registry and compatibility mode for the event bus. Produce a compatibility matrix showing which consumer versions are compatible with which producer schema versions.

title: Review the dead-letter queue strategy across every event stream
prompt: Review the dead-letter queue strategy across every event stream. Identify streams with no DLQ configured. For each missing DLQ, describe what happens to a message that fails processing. Recommend a DLQ configuration for each stream with appropriate retention, monitoring, and a documented replay procedure. Verify that DLQ depth triggers an on-call alert before it exceeds the replay capacity of the team.

title: Identify every event consumer that modifies state outside the event brok
prompt: Identify every event consumer that modifies state outside the event broker such as writing to a database, calling an external API, or sending a notification. For each, verify the operation is idempotent. Find consumers where re-processing an event would create a duplicate charge, duplicate email, or duplicate database record. Recommend an idempotency token strategy for the five highest-risk non-idempotent consumers, with the specific field to use as the idempotency key.

title: Evaluate event replay capability for incident recovery across all stream
prompt: Evaluate event replay capability for incident recovery across all streams. Identify the retention window for each stream and compare it to the team's mean time to detect a data corruption incident. Find streams whose retention is shorter than the detection time, meaning a full replay for recovery is not possible. Recommend retention windows aligned to each stream's recovery time objective. Assess whether consumers are designed to handle replay without producing duplicate side effects.

title: Assess the observability of the event streaming layer
prompt: Assess the observability of the event streaming layer. Identify which metrics are currently collected such as consumer lag, processing latency, error rate, DLQ depth, and throughput per topic. Find streams with no consumer lag alert. Identify consumers with no processing time histogram. Look for missing correlation IDs that break distributed tracing across event boundaries. Recommend a standard observability contract for event consumers with the minimum set of metrics, alerts, and trace context fields required.

title: Audit every event producer and consumer in the system for correctness un
prompt: Audit every event producer and consumer in the system for correctness under failure conditions. Identify all event streams and classify their delivery guarantees — at-most-once, at-least-once, or exactly-once. For each consumer, verify it handles duplicate delivery without corrupting data or triggering duplicate side effects like charges or notifications. Look for consumers that assume ordering but receive from brokers that do not guarantee it. Identify any consumer that has no dead-letter queue for messages it cannot process. Assess whether event replay is possible for incident recovery and whether the schema supports it without breaking existing consumers. Conclude with a ranked list of event-handling bugs by potential user impact.

title: Evaluate the event schema design across all streams
prompt: Evaluate the event schema design across all streams. Identify schemas that have changed over time and assess whether version compatibility is maintained. Look for schemas with no versioning field that would break consumers on any change. Check whether event payloads include all data needed by consumers or force consumers to make additional API calls to resolve context. Identify any event that carries sensitive data without appropriate access controls on the stream. Assess the observability of the event pipeline — whether failed events, processing latency, and consumer lag are monitored and alerted on. Summarize the top five operational risks in the event streaming layer.

title: You are a streaming systems engineer auditing an event-driven architectu
prompt: You are a streaming systems engineer auditing an event-driven architecture for production readiness. Begin by enumerating every event producer and consumer in the system, the topics or streams they publish to and consume from, and the delivery guarantee each stream provides. For each producer-consumer pair, verify that the delivery guarantee matches the consumer's idempotency guarantees, because an at-least-once stream feeding a non-idempotent consumer is a production defect. Assess whether partition keys are chosen to distribute load evenly or whether any key creates hot partitions that cause lag on specific partitions while others are idle. Review the schema design for each event type, whether each event is self-contained with all the context a consumer needs, or whether consumers must make additional API calls to resolve the event payload. Identify events that carry sensitive user data without appropriate access controls on the stream. Evaluate the dead-letter queue configuration, replay capability, and consumer lag alerting for each stream. Conclude with a streaming readiness scorecard for each stream, rating it on delivery reliability, idempotency, schema stability, observability, and operational recovery capability.

title: Perform a security and compliance audit of the event streaming layer
prompt: Perform a security and compliance audit of the event streaming layer. Identify every topic or stream that carries personally identifiable information, payment data, or authentication credentials. For each sensitive stream, verify that access control is enforced at the broker level, that only authorized producers can publish and only authorized consumers can subscribe. Assess whether sensitive event payloads are encrypted in transit and at rest. Look for events that carry more data than consumers need, violating the principle of minimum data exposure. Identify any stream accessible from outside the trusted network without authentication. Review whether sensitive events are retained longer than required by data retention policies. Assess whether consuming a sensitive stream requires authorization review. Recommend the specific access control, encryption, and retention changes required to bring the streaming layer into compliance with a minimum security standard.

title: Evaluate the operational maturity of the event streaming infrastructure
prompt: Evaluate the operational maturity of the event streaming infrastructure. Assess whether engineers on-call can diagnose a consumer lag incident without direct broker access, whether dashboards show lag per consumer group, per topic, and per partition. Identify the runbook coverage for the most common streaming incidents: consumer crash, DLQ overflow, poison message blocking a partition, and broker node failure. Assess whether message replay is self-service or requires a code deployment. Find consumer groups that share an offset with another group, meaning one group's progress affects another's. Identify topics with compaction enabled where it should not be, or without compaction where it is needed for stateful consumers. Evaluate the broker's replication configuration including replication factor and minimum in-sync replicas, and identify any configuration that would allow data loss on a single broker failure. Recommend the specific operational improvements that would reduce mean time to resolution for streaming incidents by the largest margin.

title: Audit queue depth, throughput, and consumer health
prompt: Review all message queues: current depth, throughput rate, consumer count, and health. Identify queues approaching capacity or with consumers that are consistently slow.
prompt_short: Audit all queues for depth, throughput, and consumer health status.
prompt_filtered: Queue health only. No application logic analysis.

title: Find tasks that should be queued but are synchronous
prompt: Identify synchronous operations in the request path that should be offloaded to a queue — sending emails, file processing, external API calls, heavy computation. Estimate latency saved per operation.
prompt_short: Find synchronous operations that should be moved to a background queue.
prompt_filtered: Synchronous-to-async candidates only. No infrastructure changes.

title: Review dead-letter queue handling and retry strategy
prompt: Assess how failed messages are handled across all queues. Verify DLQ alerting is set up, retry backoff is configured, and there is a process to inspect and replay failed messages.
prompt_short: Review how failed messages are handled, retried, and alerted on.
prompt_filtered: Dead-letter and retry only. No throughput or depth analysis.

title: Check message TTL and poison message protection
prompt: Verify that message TTLs are set appropriately. Identify any messages that could loop indefinitely (poison messages) and verify the max-receive-count is configured to catch them before they stall consumers.
prompt_short: Check TTLs and poison message protection across all queues.
prompt_filtered: TTL and poison message configuration only.

title: Assess queue visibility and operational tooling gaps
prompt: Evaluate how easy it is for an engineer on-call to inspect queue state, replay messages, purge a queue safely, and understand what's in the DLQ. Identify operational gaps that increase MTTR during incidents.
prompt_short: Find gaps in queue observability and operator tooling for incidents.
prompt_filtered: Operational tooling only. No throughput or schema analysis.

title: Audit message deduplication strategy across all queues
prompt: Identify which queues use at-least-once delivery and verify that consumers have a deduplication strategy — idempotency keys, dedup IDs, or an idempotency table. Find consumers that process duplicate messages as unique, causing duplicate charges, emails, or state updates.
prompt_short: Find queues with no deduplication strategy for at-least-once delivery.
prompt_filtered: Message deduplication only. At-least-once consumers with no dedup.

title: Identify queues with no backpressure signaling to producers
prompt: Find queues where producers publish at full speed regardless of consumer lag. A queue with no backpressure signal allows producers to overwhelm consumers during bursts. Assess each queue for a backpressure mechanism — queue depth limits, producer throttling, or flow control.
prompt_short: Find queues where producers get no signal to slow down under consumer lag.
prompt_filtered: Backpressure only. Queues with uncapped producer rates.

title: Review message ordering guarantees per queue type
prompt: Identify queues that must process messages in order for correctness. Verify the queue type supports FIFO ordering and that consumers do not process messages in parallel in a way that breaks order. Find consumers that assume order but receive from queues with no ordering guarantee.
prompt_short: Find consumers assuming ordering on queues that do not guarantee it.
prompt_filtered: Message ordering only. FIFO requirements vs queue capabilities.

title: Assess large message handling and payload size limits
prompt: Identify queues that could receive large payloads — file content, bulk data exports, webhook payloads. Verify each queue's maximum message size is not exceeded. Find producers that embed large binary data in message bodies rather than storing in object storage and passing a reference key.
prompt_short: Find queues receiving oversized payloads that should reference object storage.
prompt_filtered: Payload size only. Large message handling and size limit violations.

title: Find queues crossing trust boundaries without message validation
prompt: Identify queues that receive messages from external or less-trusted sources — webhooks, partner integrations, public APIs. Verify consumers validate message structure and authenticity before processing. Find queues that blindly process untrusted input with no schema validation or signature verification.
prompt_short: Find queues receiving external messages without validation or signature checks.
prompt_filtered: Queue trust boundary only. External-source message validation.

title: Evaluate message broker high availability configuration
prompt: Assess whether the message broker is configured for high availability — clustered brokers, replication factor, minimum in-sync replicas. Identify single-node broker deployments or replication factors below the minimum for durability guarantees. Describe the data loss scenario if the primary broker node fails.
prompt_short: Audit broker HA configuration for replication and durability guarantees.
prompt_filtered: Broker HA only. Replication factor and single-node risk.

title: Identify queues that should use priority lanes for urgent messages
prompt: Find queues mixing high-priority and low-priority workloads — where a batch of low-priority jobs could delay urgent messages. For each mixed queue, assess whether a dedicated priority queue or separate queue per priority class would improve latency for critical tasks.
prompt_short: Find mixed-priority queues where urgent messages get delayed behind bulk jobs.
prompt_filtered: Queue prioritization only. High-priority message delay in shared queues.

title: Audit consumer acknowledgment timing for at-risk messages
prompt: Review when consumers send acknowledgments — before processing, after processing, or after a side effect commits. Identify consumers that acknowledge before the side effect completes, meaning a crash after ack but before commit causes silent data loss. Recommend ack-after-commit patterns.
prompt_short: Find consumers acknowledging messages before their side effects are committed.
prompt_filtered: Ack timing only. Pre-processing acknowledgment causing data loss risk.

title: Assess queue-based throttling for downstream rate limit protection
prompt: Identify external APIs called from queue consumers that have rate limits. Find consumers that process messages at full speed without throttling, risking rate limit exhaustion on the external API. Recommend a rate-aware consumer with token bucket throttling or scheduled batch processing.
prompt_short: Find queue consumers calling rate-limited APIs without throttling.
prompt_filtered: Consumer rate control only. External API rate limit protection.

title: Review schema registry integration for typed message contracts
prompt: Assess whether a schema registry — Confluent Schema Registry, AWS Glue — is used to enforce message schemas at publish time. Find topics where schemas are not registered, allowing malformed messages to reach consumers silently. Recommend schema enforcement at the producer.
prompt_short: Find topics with no schema registry enforcement allowing malformed messages.
prompt_filtered: Schema registry only. Unregistered schemas and producer enforcement.

title: Identify poison message isolation gaps in consumer error handling
prompt: Find queues where a single malformed or unprocessable message can block all other messages indefinitely if the consumer retries it without a maximum retry count. Verify each queue has a max-receive-count after which messages are routed to the DLQ rather than blocking the queue.
prompt_short: Find queues where one bad message can block all consumer progress.
prompt_filtered: Poison message isolation only. Retry cap and DLQ routing.

title: Evaluate fan-out queue topology for write amplification risk
prompt: Identify queue fan-out patterns — one producer publishing to multiple downstream queues or topics. Estimate the write amplification factor at peak publish rate. Find fan-out paths where a single high-volume event produces more downstream messages than the downstream consumers can process, causing sustained lag.
prompt_short: Find fan-out queue topologies risking write amplification under load.
prompt_filtered: Fan-out topology only. Write amplification and consumer capacity mismatch.
