title: Evaluate Cost Per Invocation
prompt: Evaluate Cost Per Invocation

title: List all serverless functions with cold start latency above 500ms
prompt: List all serverless functions with cold start latency above 500ms. For each, identify the root cause — large bundle size, slow initialization, or heavy imports. Recommend the specific change that would bring cold start below 200ms.

title: Find all serverless functions that could exceed provider concurrency or
prompt: Find all serverless functions that could exceed provider concurrency or execution time limits under peak load. For each, describe the failure mode and recommend a throttling strategy or architectural change to handle bursts safely.

title: Audit serverless function costs at current and 10x traffic
prompt: Audit serverless function costs at current and 10x traffic. Identify the most expensive functions per invocation. Find functions whose cost scales super-linearly and recommend refactoring to reduce per-invocation compute time.

title: Identify the five functions with the highest cold start latency in the s
prompt: Identify the five functions with the highest cold start latency in the serverless deployment. For each, analyze the root cause such as large dependency bundle, heavy initialization code, VPC cold start penalty, or missing provisioned concurrency. Recommend a specific cold start reduction strategy for each function. Estimate the p99 latency improvement from applying each recommendation. Identify which functions serve user-facing synchronous requests and therefore have the strictest cold start tolerance.

title: Audit all serverless functions for stateful anti-patterns
prompt: Audit all serverless functions for stateful anti-patterns. Find functions that write to local disk and assume the data persists across invocations. Identify functions that open database connections in the function body rather than the module scope, paying connection establishment cost on every invocation. Find functions that cache data in module-level variables and assess whether the cache invalidation is correct given that the module may be reused across many invocations or discarded without warning. Recommend stateless implementations for each stateful pattern found.

title: Review the concurrency and throttling configuration for all serverless f
prompt: Review the concurrency and throttling configuration for all serverless functions. Identify functions with no reserved concurrency that could consume the entire account concurrency limit under a traffic spike, starving other functions. Find functions with a reserved concurrency below their expected peak invocation rate, causing throttling and user-visible errors. Assess whether each function has a dead-letter queue for failed asynchronous invocations. Recommend reserved concurrency values calibrated to each function's traffic pattern and the account-wide concurrency budget.

title: Evaluate the timeout configuration for every serverless function
prompt: Evaluate the timeout configuration for every serverless function. Find functions whose timeout is set to the platform maximum by default rather than calibrated to the expected execution time. Identify functions that process messages from a queue where the timeout is shorter than the message visibility timeout, creating a window where the message is re-enqueued while the function is still processing it. Recommend timeout values set to the p99 execution time plus a 20 percent buffer, with a specific rationale for each function.

title: Assess the cost efficiency of the serverless deployment
prompt: Assess the cost efficiency of the serverless deployment. Identify the five functions with the highest monthly cost by invocation count multiplied by average duration multiplied by memory allocation. For each, assess whether the memory allocation is higher than needed. Find functions invoked more frequently than necessary due to missing caching or batching. Recommend memory right-sizing and batching strategies for the highest-cost functions with an estimated monthly cost reduction for each.

title: Review all serverless functions for production readiness
prompt: Review all serverless functions for production readiness. Inventory each function, its runtime, its bundle size, its memory allocation, and its timeout configuration. Identify functions with cold start times above an acceptable threshold for their use case — latency-sensitive APIs versus background jobs have different requirements. Look for functions that import unnecessary dependencies that inflate bundle size without adding needed functionality. Assess whether function timeouts are set conservatively enough to fail fast rather than accumulating cost on stuck invocations. Find functions that assume warm instance state between invocations without documenting that assumption. Summarize the top five cold start and cost optimization opportunities.

title: Evaluate serverless function scalability and failure handling
prompt: Evaluate serverless function scalability and failure handling. Identify functions that can be invoked concurrently and assess whether they handle concurrent access to shared state correctly. Find functions that hit provider concurrency limits under current peak load. Assess what happens when a function times out — whether callers retry, whether idempotency is maintained, and whether partial side effects leave data in an inconsistent state. Review error handling: do functions return structured errors that callers can act on, or do they throw unstructured exceptions that upstream services cannot distinguish. Recommend the changes that would most improve serverless reliability at scale.

title: You are a cloud architect reviewing a serverless deployment for correctn
prompt: You are a cloud architect reviewing a serverless deployment for correctness, performance, and cost efficiency. Begin by enumerating every function in the deployment including its trigger type, expected invocation rate, average duration, memory allocation, and timeout setting. Classify each function as synchronous user-facing, asynchronous background processing, or scheduled. For synchronous user-facing functions, measure cold start frequency and p99 latency and identify any function where cold starts measurably affect user experience. For asynchronous functions, assess whether failed invocations are routed to a dead-letter queue or silently dropped. Review the concurrency configuration, whether reserved concurrency prevents a traffic spike on one function from exhausting the account limit for all others. Identify functions with stateful anti-patterns such as local disk writes, in-memory caches with incorrect invalidation, or connection pools opened inside the handler body. Evaluate the cost profile, whether memory allocations are right-sized, whether invocations can be reduced through caching or batching, and whether the pricing model is optimized for the usage pattern. Conclude with a function-by-function readiness assessment covering latency, reliability, cost, and statelessness.

title: Evaluate the operational observability of the serverless deployment
prompt: Evaluate the operational observability of the serverless deployment. Assess whether every function emits structured logs with a correlation ID, function name, invocation ID, and execution duration. Identify functions with no error rate alarm where failures go undetected until a user reports them. Find functions with no latency alarm that could silently degrade past acceptable thresholds. Assess whether distributed tracing is instrumented across function boundaries so a request that traverses multiple functions can be traced end to end. Identify functions where the only signal of a problem is a spike in the provider's throttle metric, a reactive signal rather than a proactive one. Review whether cold start frequency is tracked and alerted on for latency-sensitive functions. Assess whether the deployment pipeline includes canary releases or gradual traffic shifting for high-risk function updates. Recommend the specific logging, alerting, and tracing additions that would bring the serverless observability posture to a level where any user-visible degradation is detected by monitoring before a user files a bug report.

title: Audit the security posture of the serverless deployment
prompt: Audit the security posture of the serverless deployment. Assess whether each function follows the principle of least privilege, whether its execution role grants only the specific permissions required for its operations with no wildcard resource or action grants. Identify functions with overly broad roles that would allow a compromised function to escalate privileges or access data beyond its scope. Find functions that access secrets through environment variables set at deploy time rather than fetching from a secrets manager at runtime, meaning secret rotation requires a redeployment. Assess whether functions in a VPC have outbound internet access that is not required for their operation. Look for function URLs or HTTP triggers with no authentication requirement. Identify functions that process user-supplied input without input validation, creating injection vulnerabilities. Assess whether the function runtime versions are current and whether the platform automatically applies security patches. Recommend the IAM, networking, and input validation changes that close the highest-severity security gaps.

title: Identify logic that should run at the edge
prompt: Analyze the request path and identify logic that executes on origin servers but could be moved to the edge — auth token validation, A/B routing, geolocation-based redirects, static response serving. Estimate latency reduction per case.
prompt_short: Find origin-server logic that could move to the edge to reduce latency.
prompt_filtered: Edge migration candidates only. No implementation.

title: Audit edge functions for Node.js compatibility issues
prompt: Review all edge functions for imports or API usage that won't work in a V8-isolate runtime. Flag Node.js built-ins, native modules, and large npm packages with Node dependencies. Provide Web API equivalents.
prompt_short: Find compatibility issues in edge functions with V8 isolate runtimes.
prompt_filtered: Compatibility only. Flag incompatible imports with replacements.

title: Review edge caching strategy and cache-control headers
prompt: Audit all routes for correct cache-control headers. Identify responses that should be cached at the edge but aren't, and responses that are cached but shouldn't be (user-specific, auth-gated, time-sensitive).
prompt_short: Audit cache-control headers for correct edge caching behavior.
prompt_filtered: Cache headers only. No application logic analysis.

title: Find edge function timeouts and CPU limit risks
prompt: Identify edge functions that risk hitting CPU time limits (typically 50ms on Cloudflare, lower on Vercel). Flag synchronous loops, large JSON parsing, or expensive crypto operations that could exceed the budget.
prompt_short: Find edge functions risking CPU time limit violations.
prompt_filtered: CPU budget only. Synchronous heavy operations in edge context.

title: Assess data access patterns from the edge layer
prompt: Review how edge functions access data. Identify cases where they make synchronous calls back to origin or distant databases, negating the latency benefit of the edge. Recommend edge-native data sources (KV, Durable Objects, D1).
prompt_short: Find edge functions making slow round-trips that defeat edge placement.
prompt_filtered: Data access patterns only. Latency-negating calls from edge.

title: Evaluate Durable Object usage for coordinated edge state
prompt: Identify use cases requiring consistent state at the edge — rate limiting, presence counters, session locks, leaderboards. Find implementations using KV for strongly-consistent operations — noting KV is eventually consistent and unsuitable for counters or locks. Recommend Durable Objects for the cases requiring strong consistency at the edge.
prompt_short: Find edge KV used for strongly-consistent operations that need Durable Objects.
prompt_filtered: Durable Object candidates only. Strong consistency requirements at the edge.

title: Audit edge function secrets and binding security
prompt: Verify that secrets used by edge functions are stored as platform-managed bindings — Cloudflare Secrets, Vercel Environment Variables — rather than hardcoded in source code. Find any secret embedded in function code or committed to version control. Assess whether binding access is scoped to the specific function that needs it.
prompt_short: Find hardcoded secrets in edge functions that should use platform bindings.
prompt_filtered: Edge secret bindings only. Hardcoded values vs managed secret bindings.

title: Identify edge functions exceeding CPU time budgets
prompt: Find edge functions doing CPU-intensive work — JSON parsing of large payloads, encryption, complex routing logic — that risks exceeding the platform CPU time limit. On Cloudflare Workers, the limit is 10ms wall time on free and 50ms on paid plans. Identify functions approaching these limits and recommend offloading heavy computation to origin or using streaming.
prompt_short: Find edge functions at risk of hitting CPU time wall limits.
prompt_filtered: CPU time budget only. Edge function computation exceeding platform limits.

title: Review edge request coalescing for high-traffic origins
prompt: Identify whether the edge layer collapses concurrent requests for the same cacheable resource into a single origin request while the first response is in flight. Without request coalescing, a sudden burst of cache misses on a popular resource sends hundreds of simultaneous requests to the origin. Find high-traffic routes missing coalescing and recommend enabling it.
prompt_short: Find routes missing request coalescing that create cache miss thundering herd.
prompt_filtered: Request coalescing only. Cache miss origin request storms.

title: Assess edge-to-origin mTLS and request authentication
prompt: Verify that requests from the edge layer to the origin server are authenticated — not just unauthenticated HTTP forwarded from the edge. Find origins that accept any request from the edge IP range without verifying the request came from the authorized edge function. Recommend mTLS, a shared secret header, or an origin-bound JWT.
prompt_short: Find origins accepting unauthenticated requests from the edge without verification.
prompt_filtered: Edge-to-origin auth only. Origin request authentication and mTLS.

title: Identify missed personalization opportunities at the edge
prompt: Find pages or responses currently served as fully static from origin that could be partially personalized at the edge using cookie values, geolocation headers, or KV user data — without a full origin round-trip. Identify the highest-traffic routes where edge personalization would eliminate origin calls while still serving user-specific content.
prompt_short: Find static responses that could be edge-personalized without an origin call.
prompt_filtered: Edge personalization only. Cookie and geo-based edge rendering.

title: Review edge function error handling and fallback to origin
prompt: Verify that edge functions handle runtime errors gracefully — returning the origin response rather than a raw error when the edge function fails. Find edge functions that return 500 errors on exception without attempting an origin fallback. Assess whether edge function errors are logged and alerted on separately from origin errors.
prompt_short: Find edge functions that throw 500 instead of falling back to origin on error.
prompt_filtered: Edge error fallback only. Exception handling and origin passthrough.

title: Assess CORS configuration correctness at the edge layer
prompt: Audit CORS headers set by edge functions. Find responses returning Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on APIs that return authenticated or sensitive data — wildcard CORS on credentialed requests is a security vulnerability. Verify preflight OPTIONS responses return the correct headers and that CORS policies match the actual client origins.
prompt_short: Find wildcard CORS on authenticated APIs and incorrect preflight responses.
prompt_filtered: CORS configuration only. Wildcard origins and preflight correctness.

title: Find edge functions with no retry on transient upstream failures
prompt: Identify edge functions that make a single upstream fetch call with no retry on transient failures — connection resets, 503s, timeouts. A single transient failure returns an error to the user unnecessarily. Recommend a retry with exponential backoff for idempotent upstream calls, with a maximum of two retries within the edge CPU budget.
prompt_short: Find edge functions with no retry logic on transient upstream failures.
prompt_filtered: Edge retry logic only. Idempotent upstream calls with no transient retry.

title: Evaluate geographic routing rules and latency targeting
prompt: Review geographic routing rules that direct requests to regional origins. Identify regions where the nearest origin is unexpectedly far — causing latency worse than a non-edge deployment. Find routing rules that send Asia-Pacific traffic to a US-only origin due to misconfigured routing policies. Recommend anycast routing adjustments and origin colocation for the highest-latency regions.
prompt_short: Find geographic routing rules sending traffic to distant origins unnecessarily.
prompt_filtered: Geographic routing only. Regional latency and anycast routing gaps.

title: Audit KV namespace key structure for namespace collision risks
prompt: Review all edge KV namespaces and their key naming conventions. Identify keys that could collide between environments — production and staging sharing a KV namespace — or between tenants. Find keys with no environment or tenant prefix that risk cross-environment data leakage during testing.
prompt_short: Find KV key naming patterns that could collide across environments or tenants.
prompt_filtered: KV key design only. Namespace collision and cross-environment leakage.

title: Identify edge worker lifecycle hooks affecting startup latency
prompt: Review edge worker initialization code that runs at module scope — top-level await, large data fetches, SDK initialization. This code runs on cold start and extends the first request's latency for all users assigned to that isolate. Find heavy initialization that could be deferred to request time or moved to a warmup event handler.
prompt_short: Find module-scope initialization code that inflates edge worker cold start.
prompt_filtered: Worker startup only. Top-level initialization deferred to request time.

title: Identify Stateful Edge Anti-Patterns
prompt: Identify Stateful Edge Anti-Patterns

title: Verify Edge Cache Purge Propagation
prompt: Verify Edge Cache Purge Propagation

title: Find Secrets Hardcoded in Edge Code
prompt: Find Secrets Hardcoded in Edge Code

title: Assess Durable Object Usage Patterns
prompt: Assess Durable Object Usage Patterns

title: Check Response Size Limits
prompt: Check Response Size Limits

title: Identify all data that edge functions read from origin on every request
prompt: Identify all data that edge functions read from origin on every request. For each data type, assess whether it can be cached at the edge and for how long. Recommend specific cache headers or edge KV strategies to reduce origin round-trips.

title: Audit geographic data residency requirements for the application
prompt: Audit geographic data residency requirements for the application. Identify any edge function that stores or processes data in a region that violates compliance requirements. Recommend routing rules or data partitioning strategies to maintain residency.

title: Find all edge functions that make synchronous calls to origin services
prompt: Find all edge functions that make synchronous calls to origin services. For each, measure the added latency of the origin round-trip. Identify which calls can be eliminated through edge caching, precomputation, or edge-side data stores.

title: Identify every place in the edge codebase that imports Node
prompt: Identify every place in the edge codebase that imports Node.js-specific APIs such as fs, crypto, path, buffer, http, or net. For each import, find the Web API equivalent and assess the migration complexity. Prioritize by the risk of a silent failure at the edge where the Node API import causes a runtime error not caught in local development. Recommend a migration order from highest to lowest risk.

title: Audit all edge functions for response size compliance
prompt: Audit all edge functions for response size compliance. Identify the maximum response size limit for the edge platform in use. Find responses that could exceed this limit such as large JSON payloads, embedded binary data, or uncompressed assets. For each, recommend a streaming response, redirect to object storage, or payload reduction strategy. Verify that responses include Content-Encoding headers when compression is applied so clients decompress correctly.

title: Assess the caching strategy for all edge-deployed routes
prompt: Assess the caching strategy for all edge-deployed routes. Identify routes that are cacheable but have no Cache-Control header or a no-cache directive preventing edge caching. Find routes that are cached but should not be, such as authenticated routes, personalized responses, or routes with side effects. Recommend Cache-Control header corrections for each misconfigured route. Calculate the expected cache hit rate improvement from correcting the misconfigured routes.

title: Evaluate the secrets management strategy for all edge deployments
prompt: Evaluate the secrets management strategy for all edge deployments. Identify secrets accessed as hardcoded values, build-time constants, or plaintext environment variables visible in deployment configuration. Find edge functions that log secret values in structured logs accessible from the provider's dashboard. Assess whether secrets are rotated without requiring a redeployment. Recommend a secrets injection strategy using the edge platform's native secret store with runtime fetch rather than deploy-time binding.

title: Review the error handling and observability of edge functions
prompt: Review the error handling and observability of edge functions. Identify functions that return 500 errors without a structured error body, making client-side error handling impossible. Find functions with no error tracking integration where unhandled exceptions are logged only to the provider's console and not routed to the team's alerting system. Assess whether distributed traces include edge function invocations in addition to application-level spans. Recommend error handling and observability additions that meet the team's minimum alerting and tracing standards.

title: Evaluate all edge computing functions for correctness and efficiency
prompt: Evaluate all edge computing functions for correctness and efficiency. Identify every function deployed to the edge, its purpose, and the data it needs to execute. Assess which data is available locally at the edge versus which requires an origin round-trip. Find any function that is deployed to the edge but performs work that requires centralized state, defeating the purpose of edge deployment. Review whether edge functions handle geographic routing correctly and whether they respect data residency requirements. Assess the cache hit rate for edge-cached responses and identify the content types with the lowest hit rates. Summarize the top five opportunities to shift more work to the edge layer.

title: Review the security model for edge functions
prompt: Review the security model for edge functions. Identify any edge function that processes or stores sensitive user data without appropriate encryption or access controls. Check whether edge functions validate request authenticity or blindly trust incoming headers that could be spoofed. Assess whether edge function secrets are stored securely in edge KV or environment bindings rather than hardcoded in function code. Find edge functions that make outbound calls to third-party APIs from the edge without rate limiting or circuit breakers. Recommend the security hardening changes that would most reduce the attack surface of the edge layer.

title: You are an edge computing specialist reviewing all edge-deployed functio
prompt: You are an edge computing specialist reviewing all edge-deployed functions for correctness, performance, and security. Begin by enumerating every function deployed to the edge including the platform, the route pattern it handles, the expected traffic volume, and the latency target. Identify functions that import Node.js-specific APIs that will fail silently or throw at runtime in the edge environment. Review the response size for each function and flag any that could exceed the platform's response size limit, particularly for endpoints that return dynamic content that grows with data volume. Assess whether all functions correctly handle the edge runtime's constraints including no persistent local disk, no in-memory state between invocations on different edge nodes, and no blocking I/O. Identify any function performing a blocking database query in the edge handler body rather than caching the result at the edge. Evaluate the secrets management approach, whether secrets are injected through the platform's native mechanism or hardcoded in the function code. Assess the cache configuration for each route, whether cacheable routes have appropriate Cache-Control headers and whether authenticated routes are correctly excluded from shared caches. Conclude with a function-by-function readiness scorecard covering runtime compatibility, response compliance, caching correctness, and secrets management.

title: Evaluate the performance characteristics of the edge deployment with a f
prompt: Evaluate the performance characteristics of the edge deployment with a focus on latency optimization. Identify the functions with the highest time-to-first-byte by endpoint over the last 30 days. For each slow function, diagnose the root cause such as synchronous origin fetch, large response payload, missing edge cache, or computation that could be pre-computed. Assess whether the edge cache hit rate for cacheable routes is above 80 percent and if not, diagnose the cause such as key variability from dynamic query parameters or headers. Identify functions that make multiple sequential origin requests and assess whether they could be parallelized or consolidated. Review whether the edge platform's streaming response capability is used for large payloads to reduce time-to-first-byte. Assess the geographic coverage of edge nodes relative to the user population and identify regions where a significant user base is served from a geographically distant edge node. Recommend specific optimizations for the five highest-latency endpoints with an estimated improvement for each.

title: Audit the security posture of all edge-deployed functions
prompt: Audit the security posture of all edge-deployed functions. Assess whether each function validates and sanitizes user-supplied input including URL parameters, query strings, request headers, and request bodies before using them in downstream queries or responses. Identify functions that construct database queries, cache keys, or downstream API calls using unsanitized user input, creating injection vulnerabilities. Find functions that include sensitive data in response headers or bodies without verifying the requesting user is authorized to receive it. Assess whether CORS configuration is correctly restricted to allow only known origins, or whether it uses a wildcard that allows any origin to make credentialed cross-origin requests. Identify functions that forward authentication headers to downstream services without stripping headers not intended for the downstream service. Evaluate the rate limiting configuration, whether edge functions enforce request rate limits per IP, user, or API key to prevent abuse. Recommend the specific input validation, CORS, and rate limiting changes that close the highest-severity edge security gaps.

title: Audit all exposed ports and network surface area
prompt: List every port exposed by the system — containers, VMs, cloud services. For each, verify it is necessary, bound to the correct interface (not 0.0.0.0 unless intentional), and protected by appropriate auth or firewall rules.
prompt_short: Audit all exposed ports and verify each one is necessary and protected.
prompt_filtered: Port and network exposure only. No application logic.

title: Find services communicating over unencrypted channels
prompt: Identify any service-to-service communication that uses HTTP instead of HTTPS, unencrypted database connections, or plaintext protocols. Flag by severity based on sensitivity of data in transit.
prompt_short: Find all unencrypted service-to-service communication paths.
prompt_filtered: Encryption-in-transit only. Flag plaintext connections with severity.

title: Review DNS configuration for TTL and failover
prompt: Audit DNS records for all services. Verify TTLs are appropriate for the failover requirements. Identify records with very high TTLs that would slow failover, and assess whether health-based DNS failover is configured.
prompt_short: Audit DNS TTLs and failover configuration for all services.
prompt_filtered: DNS configuration only. TTL and health-based routing.

title: Identify missing network segmentation between services
prompt: Assess whether services that don't need to communicate directly are isolated from each other. Identify cases where a compromised service could reach sensitive internal services it has no business connecting to.
prompt_short: Find services that can reach each other when they shouldn't.
prompt_filtered: Network segmentation only. East-west lateral movement risks.

title: Check egress filtering and outbound connection controls
prompt: Review what external endpoints each service is allowed to connect to. Identify services with unrestricted egress (can call any external URL), which creates data exfiltration and SSRF risks. Recommend allowlist-based egress controls.
prompt_short: Find services with unrestricted outbound network access.
prompt_filtered: Egress filtering only. Data exfiltration and SSRF risks.

title: Review BGP and ASN routing configuration for multi-homed networks
prompt: For networks with multiple upstream ISPs or cloud providers, review BGP peering and AS-PATH prepend configuration. Identify routes that have a single AS path with no backup, causing full outage if the upstream ISP withdraws the route. Assess whether anycast prefixes are announced from all required PoPs.
prompt_short: Audit BGP routing for single-path dependencies and AS-PATH gaps.
prompt_filtered: BGP routing only. Single-path routes and anycast announcement coverage.

title: Assess CIDR allocation and IP space exhaustion risk
prompt: Review the CIDR allocation for all VPCs and subnets. Identify subnets where the available IP space is within 20% of exhaustion — which would prevent new instances from starting. Find CIDR ranges that were allocated too small for the service's current scale. Recommend CIDR expansion or VPC peering changes.
prompt_short: Find subnets approaching IP exhaustion that would prevent new instance launches.
prompt_filtered: CIDR allocation only. IP space exhaustion and subnet sizing.

title: Identify missing DDoS mitigation at the network perimeter
prompt: Assess the DDoS mitigation controls at the network perimeter. Identify services exposed directly to the internet without upstream scrubbing or rate limiting. Find any origin IP that is reachable directly — bypassing CDN or DDoS protection — enabling an attacker to bypass scrubbing by targeting the origin IP.
prompt_short: Find exposed origin IPs that bypass CDN and DDoS scrubbing.
prompt_filtered: DDoS mitigation only. Origin IP exposure and scrubbing gaps.

title: Review sticky session configuration and session affinity risks
prompt: Identify load balancers configured with sticky sessions — routing the same client to the same backend instance. Sticky sessions reduce horizontal scalability and cause uneven load distribution when one client generates disproportionate traffic. Find cases where sticky sessions are used for stateful reasons that could be eliminated by moving state to a shared store.
prompt_short: Find sticky session dependencies that could be eliminated with shared state.
prompt_filtered: Session affinity only. Sticky session removal candidates.

title: Audit TLS certificate management for expiry and renewal automation
prompt: Identify all TLS certificates in use — load balancers, service-to-service, client certificates. Find certificates expiring in the next 60 days with no automated renewal. Identify certificates managed manually rather than through an ACME client or certificate manager. Find wildcard certificates used in cases where per-service certificates are more appropriate.
prompt_short: Find TLS certificates expiring without automated renewal in the next 60 days.
prompt_filtered: TLS certificates only. Expiry, renewal automation, and manual management.

title: Find services without network connection pool limits
prompt: Identify services that open new network connections on every request without a connection pool. Find pools configured without a maximum connection limit, allowing a traffic spike to exhaust database or upstream connection limits. Verify each connection pool has a maximum size, idle timeout, and connection health check.
prompt_short: Find services with no connection pool or unbounded pool limits.
prompt_filtered: Connection pool limits only. Max connections and idle timeout configuration.

title: Assess network timeout configuration across all service calls
prompt: Review connection timeout, read timeout, and write timeout settings on every service-to-service network call. Find calls with no timeout configured — they will hang indefinitely if the upstream becomes unresponsive, consuming a thread or connection. Find timeouts shorter than the operation's worst-case valid duration, causing false timeout errors.
prompt_short: Find network calls with missing or incorrect timeout configurations.
prompt_filtered: Network timeout only. Missing timeouts and false-timeout misconfiguration.

title: Identify HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 upgrade opportunities for multiplexing
prompt: Find service-to-service or client-to-server connections using HTTP/1.1 where HTTP/2 would reduce head-of-line blocking and improve throughput via multiplexing. Identify high-frequency API callers that would benefit from a persistent multiplexed connection rather than a new TCP connection per request. Assess whether the backend supports HTTP/2 before recommending upgrades.
prompt_short: Find HTTP/1.1 connections that could use HTTP/2 multiplexing for efficiency.
prompt_filtered: Protocol upgrade only. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 adoption opportunities.

title: Review DNS resolution caching and negative TTL handling
prompt: Identify services that re-resolve DNS on every connection rather than caching the result. Find services with hardcoded TTL overrides that ignore short TTLs published by the upstream DNS — preventing fast failover. Assess whether DNS negative caching is configured, causing NXDOMAIN responses to be cached longer than needed after a new record is published.
prompt_short: Find DNS resolution misconfigurations blocking fast failover or caching NXDOMAIN.
prompt_filtered: DNS caching only. Negative TTL and per-connection re-resolution.

title: Audit load balancer health check sensitivity and thresholds
prompt: Review the health check configuration for all load balancers — path, interval, timeout, healthy threshold, unhealthy threshold. Find health checks with too-low thresholds that remove instances on a single transient failure, and too-high thresholds that keep a degraded instance in rotation too long. Recommend thresholds that balance stability against responsiveness.
prompt_short: Audit load balancer health check thresholds for over-aggressive and too-lenient settings.
prompt_filtered: Health check thresholds only. Interval, timeout, and threshold calibration.

title: Identify missing network flow logging for incident investigation
prompt: Verify that VPC flow logs or equivalent network traffic logs are enabled for all production environments. Identify networks with no flow logging, making post-incident investigation of network-based attacks impossible. Assess flow log retention period against the typical time between attack and discovery.
prompt_short: Find production networks with no VPC flow logs for incident investigation.
prompt_filtered: Flow logging only. Traffic log enablement and retention period.

title: Assess private link and endpoint usage for sensitive service access
prompt: Find services accessing cloud provider APIs — S3, RDS, DynamoDB, Secrets Manager — over the public internet rather than through VPC endpoints or PrivateLink. Public internet paths expose service-to-cloud traffic to network inspection and increase data transfer costs. Recommend VPC endpoints for the highest-traffic or most sensitive service connections.
prompt_short: Find cloud service access over public internet that should use VPC endpoints.
prompt_filtered: VPC endpoints only. Public internet access to cloud services.

title: Find Unencrypted Internal Traffic
prompt: Find Unencrypted Internal Traffic

title: Assess Retry Budget Across Services
prompt: Assess Retry Budget Across Services

title: Check Network Segmentation Isolation
prompt: Check Network Segmentation Isolation

title: Audit all firewall and security group rules
prompt: Audit all firewall and security group rules. Identify any rule allowing unrestricted inbound access (0.0.0.0/0) to non-public services. For each overly permissive rule, recommend the minimum required source restriction.

title: Find all services that have no egress filtering — able to make outbound
prompt: Find all services that have no egress filtering — able to make outbound connections to any destination. For each, assess the data exfiltration risk. Recommend egress allow-lists scoped to the specific external endpoints each service needs.

title: Identify all internal service-to-service connections
prompt: Identify all internal service-to-service connections. For each, verify that mutual TLS or an equivalent authentication mechanism is in use. Flag any internal connection that uses plain HTTP or shared API keys without rotation.

title: Audit all internal service-to-service traffic for encryption
prompt: Audit all internal service-to-service traffic for encryption. Identify any service pair communicating over plaintext HTTP inside the cluster or VPC. For each unencrypted internal connection, assess the data sensitivity of the payloads transmitted and the risk if an attacker achieved network access to the internal network. Recommend mTLS or TLS upgrade paths for each unencrypted connection, prioritizing by data sensitivity. Assess whether the chosen networking approach is consistent across all services.

title: Review the load balancer configuration for all services
prompt: Review the load balancer configuration for all services. Identify load balancers with health check intervals longer than 30 seconds that would route traffic to failed backends for an extended period before detection. Find load balancers using TCP health checks rather than HTTP checks that verify application-layer health. Identify services with no connection draining configured, causing in-flight requests to be dropped during deployments. Recommend health check and connection draining configurations that minimize user-visible errors during backend failures and rolling deployments.

title: Assess the DNS configuration for all services
prompt: Assess the DNS configuration for all services. Identify services with DNS TTLs longer than 60 seconds that would slow client failover during a backend IP change. Find clients that resolve DNS once at startup and cache the result indefinitely, ignoring TTL. Identify services with no DNS-based health routing where a failed service continues to receive traffic because its DNS record still resolves. Recommend DNS TTL values and client-side DNS refresh strategies aligned to each service's failover requirement.

title: Evaluate the egress filtering configuration for all services
prompt: Evaluate the egress filtering configuration for all services. Identify services with unrestricted outbound internet access that only need to reach a known set of external endpoints. Find container workloads on a default network with no egress policy preventing lateral movement or data exfiltration. Assess whether outbound DNS queries are restricted to a known resolver or open to any resolver. Recommend egress network policies that allow only the specific external endpoints each service requires, with a default-deny baseline.

title: Review the retry and timeout configuration across all service-to-service
prompt: Review the retry and timeout configuration across all service-to-service calls. Identify service calls with no timeout set where a hung downstream service would block the caller indefinitely. Find calls with retry logic but no exponential backoff where simultaneous retries from multiple callers amplify load on an already-struggling downstream service. Identify the total retry budget and assess whether the downstream service can absorb it. Recommend timeout, retry count, and backoff configurations for each service pair that prevent retry storms while maintaining acceptable success rates.

title: Review the network architecture for security and segmentation correctnes
prompt: Review the network architecture for security and segmentation correctness. Identify all network zones in use — public, DMZ, private, and management — and verify that traffic flows between zones match the intended security model. Find any service in the private zone that is reachable from the public zone without passing through an explicit ingress control. Audit all security group and firewall rules for overly permissive ingress — particularly any rules allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on non-public ports. Assess whether internal service-to-service communication is encrypted and authenticated. Identify services with no egress filtering that could exfiltrate data to arbitrary external destinations. Summarize the five network security gaps with the highest risk.

title: Evaluate the operational network design for reliability and performance
prompt: Evaluate the operational network design for reliability and performance. Identify services that have no redundant network path — where a single network failure would cause complete service loss. Check whether DNS TTLs are set appropriately for the failover time objectives. Find any hardcoded IP addresses in application configuration that would break on infrastructure changes. Assess load balancing configuration for all public-facing services — whether health checks are correctly configured and whether session affinity requirements are documented. Review network timeout and retry settings across all service-to-service calls. Recommend the network changes that would most improve reliability during partial infrastructure failures.

title: You are a network engineer reviewing the networking architecture of a mu
prompt: You are a network engineer reviewing the networking architecture of a multi-service production system. Begin by mapping all service-to-service communication paths, identifying which services call which, whether the calls are synchronous or asynchronous, and whether the connections traverse the internet, a VPC, or a service mesh. Assess whether all inter-service traffic is encrypted in transit and identify any service pair communicating over plaintext HTTP inside the network boundary. Review the load balancing configuration for each service, whether health checks are correctly configured to detect application-layer failures and not just TCP connectivity. Assess the connection pool configuration, whether each service's connection pool size is matched to the expected concurrency and the downstream service's connection limit. Identify any service with no connection draining configuration that drops in-flight requests during deployments. Review DNS TTL settings and client-side DNS caching behavior relative to each service's failover requirement. Evaluate the egress filtering posture, whether services have unrestricted outbound internet access or whether egress is restricted to known external endpoints. Conclude with a network architecture assessment covering encryption, load balancing correctness, DNS reliability, and egress security.

title: Audit the network security configuration across all services
prompt: Audit the network security configuration across all services. Identify network security groups, firewall rules, or Kubernetes network policies that grant broader access than required, particularly rules that allow any source to reach any destination on any port. Find services whose management interfaces such as debug endpoints, metrics endpoints, and admin APIs are accessible from the same network as user-facing traffic rather than a separate management network. Assess whether the internal network is segmented such that a compromised frontend service cannot reach the database directly. Identify any service binding to all network interfaces including the external interface where only loopback or internal interface binding is required. Look for missing rate limiting at the network layer where a single source IP can send unlimited requests to an internal service. Assess whether network traffic is logged for security incident investigation. Recommend the specific network policy, security group, and interface binding changes that reduce the attack surface to the minimum required for each service.

title: Evaluate the resilience of the networking layer under failure conditions
prompt: Evaluate the resilience of the networking layer under failure conditions. Identify every network path that, if interrupted, would cause complete service unavailability rather than degraded availability. For each critical path, verify that redundant routes exist across multiple availability zones, multiple upstream providers, or multiple peering points. Assess whether the system can survive a DNS resolver failure, whether services fall back to cached records or hard-coded IPs for critical dependencies. Review the behavior of each service when a downstream TCP connection is dropped unexpectedly, whether the client correctly detects the dropped connection and re-establishes it, or whether it blocks indefinitely. Identify services with connection pools that do not periodically validate idle connections, keeping stale connections that will fail on first use. Assess whether anycast or geo-DNS is used to route users to the nearest available region and whether the failover behavior is correct when a region becomes unavailable. Recommend the specific resilience changes that address the highest-priority single points of failure.

title: Audit service-to-service authentication and mTLS coverage
prompt: Review whether all service-to-service calls are authenticated. Identify services communicating without mTLS or equivalent, allowing any service in the cluster to impersonate another.
prompt_short: Audit mTLS and auth coverage for all service-to-service communication.
prompt_filtered: Service auth only. mTLS and identity verification.

title: Find missing retry and timeout policies in the mesh
prompt: Identify services with no retry policy (single failures cause user-visible errors) or no timeout policy (slow dependencies cascade into full outages). Recommend appropriate values based on each service's SLA.
prompt_short: Find missing retry and timeout policies across all mesh-managed routes.
prompt_filtered: Retry and timeout policy only. No business logic analysis.

title: Assess circuit breaker configuration for critical paths
prompt: Review circuit breaker configuration on all critical service-to-service dependencies. Identify paths with no circuit breaker, thresholds set too high to be effective, or no fallback defined for the open state.
prompt_short: Review circuit breaker configuration on all critical service dependencies.
prompt_filtered: Circuit breaker config only. Thresholds and fallback behavior.

title: Review traffic shaping and canary routing configuration
prompt: Evaluate the traffic shaping rules in the service mesh. Identify any active canary deployments, weighted routing, or header-based routing. Verify rules are documented and have a defined rollback trigger.
prompt_short: Audit traffic shaping rules, canary weights, and rollback triggers.
prompt_filtered: Traffic routing only. Weights and rollback criteria.

title: Identify observability gaps in mesh telemetry
prompt: Assess the telemetry emitted by the service mesh. Verify that all service-to-service calls produce traces, latency histograms, and error rate metrics. Identify services missing from mesh observability coverage.
prompt_short: Find services not covered by mesh telemetry and distributed tracing.
prompt_filtered: Mesh observability only. Tracing and metric coverage gaps.

title: Audit sidecar resource overhead and injection completeness
prompt: Review sidecar proxy resource consumption — CPU and memory — across all enrolled services. Identify services where sidecar overhead is disproportionate to the workload it proxies. Find services that have escaped automatic injection and are running without a sidecar, receiving no mesh enforcement or telemetry.
prompt_short: Find sidecar resource overhead hotspots and services missing sidecar injection.
prompt_filtered: Sidecar injection and overhead only. CPU/memory cost per enrolled service.

title: Review mesh traffic policy consistency across namespaces
prompt: Identify whether traffic policies — retry, timeout, circuit breaker — are defined per-service or inherited from namespace defaults. Find services with no explicit policy that fall through to a permissive or missing namespace default. Assess whether policy gaps exist at namespace boundaries where traffic crosses from one policy zone to another.
prompt_short: Find services with no explicit mesh traffic policy relying on permissive defaults.
prompt_filtered: Traffic policy coverage only. Namespace-boundary policy gaps.

title: Assess mesh control plane availability and failover
prompt: Evaluate whether the service mesh control plane — Istio Pilot, Linkerd control plane, Consul Connect — is highly available. Identify control plane components with no replica or no pod disruption budget. Assess what happens to data plane traffic if the control plane becomes unavailable — whether existing policies continue to be enforced or whether traffic stops flowing.
prompt_short: Find control plane SPOFs and assess data plane behavior during control plane outage.
prompt_filtered: Control plane HA only. Single-instance control plane risk.

title: Find authorization policies that are too permissive at the mesh level
prompt: Review all service mesh authorization policies. Identify policies that allow any source within the cluster to call a given service — mesh-level allow-all — rather than restricting by source workload identity. Find services with no authorization policy, relying on mTLS identity only without restricting which identities may call which APIs.
prompt_short: Find mesh authorization policies that allow any source to call sensitive services.
prompt_filtered: Mesh authorization policy only. Allow-all and missing source restrictions.

title: Evaluate mesh egress gateway configuration and enforcement
prompt: Assess whether outbound traffic from services to external destinations is routed through a mesh egress gateway. Find services that bypass the egress gateway and make direct external calls — evading traffic policies and audit logging. Verify that the egress gateway enforces an allowlist of permitted external destinations.
prompt_short: Find services bypassing the mesh egress gateway for external calls.
prompt_filtered: Egress gateway only. External call bypass and destination allowlisting.

title: Review header propagation for distributed trace context
prompt: Verify that all services propagate trace context headers — W3C Trace-Context, B3, or X-Trace-ID — on every outbound call. Find services that consume an inbound trace context but do not forward it on their downstream calls, breaking the trace chain. Assess whether the mesh automatically propagates headers or whether application code must do it explicitly.
prompt_short: Find services that break distributed trace continuity by not propagating headers.
prompt_filtered: Trace header propagation only. Services breaking trace context forwarding.

title: Identify mesh policy drift from intended security posture
prompt: Compare the current deployed mesh policies against the documented intended security posture. Identify policies that were added as temporary exceptions and never removed. Find any service that was exempted from mTLS or authorization requirements and has no documented justification or review date. Recommend a policy audit cycle with mandatory expiry on exceptions.
prompt_short: Find mesh policy exceptions with no expiry date or documented justification.
prompt_filtered: Policy drift only. Temporary exceptions without expiry in mesh config.

title: Assess service mesh upgrade path and backward compatibility
prompt: Identify the current version of the service mesh control plane and data plane. Assess the gap between the installed version and the latest stable release. Find services using deprecated API versions that will break on the next mesh upgrade. Recommend an upgrade plan with canary data plane upgrades before control plane upgrade.
prompt_short: Find deprecated mesh API usage and assess upgrade complexity.
prompt_filtered: Mesh versioning only. API deprecations and control/data plane upgrade order.

title: Find mesh-managed services with missing PodDisruptionBudgets
prompt: Identify services enrolled in the mesh that have no PodDisruptionBudget. A rolling control plane upgrade or node drain will simultaneously terminate multiple pods from unprotected services. Find the services most likely to lose all replicas during a planned maintenance event and recommend PDB minimums for each.
prompt_short: Find mesh-enrolled services with no PodDisruptionBudget during upgrades.
prompt_filtered: PodDisruptionBudget only. Mesh services vulnerable during node drains.

title: Review mesh locality load balancing and failover configuration
prompt: Assess whether the mesh uses locality-aware load balancing — preferring replicas in the same zone or region before spilling to other zones. Find services with no locality configuration that route equally across all zones, increasing cross-zone data transfer costs. Verify locality failover thresholds are configured so traffic shifts to the next zone when the local zone is degraded.
prompt_short: Audit locality-aware load balancing config and cross-zone failover thresholds.
prompt_filtered: Locality load balancing only. Zone preference and failover configuration.

title: Identify missing mesh health endpoints for control plane reconciliation
prompt: Verify that all mesh-enrolled services expose a health endpoint that the mesh can use to detect unhealthy instances and remove them from load balancing. Find services with no readiness probe that the mesh uses, causing traffic to be sent to pods that are not ready to serve. Assess the reconciliation loop that syncs health status between the mesh and the orchestrator.
prompt_short: Find services missing readiness probes used by the mesh for load balancing.
prompt_filtered: Mesh health endpoint only. Readiness probe absence and traffic routing.

title: Audit mesh-level rate limiting configuration and bypass paths
prompt: Identify whether the service mesh enforces rate limits at the network layer — using EnvoyFilter or equivalent — in addition to application-level rate limiting. Find services that rely solely on application rate limiting, which can be bypassed by sending requests directly to a pod IP. Assess whether mesh rate limiting provides a defense-in-depth layer that enforces limits even if the application is compromised.
prompt_short: Find services relying only on application rate limiting with no mesh enforcement.
prompt_filtered: Mesh rate limiting only. Network-layer rate limit bypass paths.

prompt: Find Services Bypassing the Mesh

title: Review Traffic Policy Consistency
prompt: Review Traffic Policy Consistency

title: Assess Retry Budget Configuration
prompt: Assess Retry Budget Configuration

title: Check Circuit Breaker Mesh Rules
prompt: Check Circuit Breaker Mesh Rules

title: Identify Observability Blind Spots
prompt: Identify Observability Blind Spots

title: Evaluate Canary Traffic Split Rules
prompt: Evaluate Canary Traffic Split Rules

title: Audit mTLS coverage across all service-to-service connections in the mes
prompt: Audit mTLS coverage across all service-to-service connections in the mesh. Identify any connection that does not enforce mutual TLS. For each gap, assess the data interception risk and recommend the mesh configuration change to close it.

title: Find the three services with the highest inter-service call latency as m
prompt: Find the three services with the highest inter-service call latency as measured by the mesh telemetry. For each, identify whether the latency is in the network, in the upstream service, or in the mesh sidecar overhead. Recommend the optimization that addresses the root cause.

title: Assess service mesh observability completeness
prompt: Assess service mesh observability completeness. Identify which services are not enrolled in the mesh and receiving no telemetry. For each unenrolled service, describe what failure modes would be invisible to the operations team and recommend enrollment priority.

title: Audit the mTLS enforcement posture across the service mesh
prompt: Audit the mTLS enforcement posture across the service mesh. Identify services in PERMISSIVE mode that accept both plaintext and mTLS traffic. For each permissive service, assess the risk of a man-in-the-middle attack on the plaintext path. Recommend a migration plan to STRICT mode for all services, ordered by data sensitivity. Verify that the migration plan accounts for services that have not yet injected sidecars and would be excluded from mTLS.

title: Review all circuit breaker and outlier detection policies in the service
prompt: Review all circuit breaker and outlier detection policies in the service mesh. Identify service-to-service connections with no circuit breaker configured. Find connections where the circuit breaker thresholds are set to default values without calibration to the actual traffic pattern. Recommend calibrated circuit breaker settings for each connection based on observed error rates and acceptable degradation thresholds.

title: Evaluate the traffic management policies for all services in the mesh
prompt: Evaluate the traffic management policies for all services in the mesh. Identify services with no retry policy on idempotent endpoints. Find services with retry policies on non-idempotent endpoints where retries could cause duplicate side effects. Identify timeout settings that are inconsistent across services calling the same downstream. Recommend a consistent timeout and retry policy matrix for each downstream service that all callers should apply.

title: Assess the service mesh observability configuration
prompt: Assess the service mesh observability configuration. Identify services with telemetry collection disabled. Find service-to-service connections with no latency histogram or error rate metric in the mesh control plane. Assess whether distributed traces include mesh-level spans in addition to application-level spans. Identify services with no golden signal dashboard in the observability platform. Recommend telemetry additions that bring all services to the minimum observability standard.

title: Review the canary and progressive delivery configuration in the service
prompt: Review the canary and progressive delivery configuration in the service mesh. Identify services deployed with blue-green or canary strategies and assess whether traffic weights are managed by the mesh or by the deployment controller. Find canary deployments with no automated rollback trigger. Assess whether the mesh is configured to split traffic based on header matching for internal testing of new versions before any production traffic is shifted. Recommend a progressive delivery standard using mesh traffic management that enforces automated rollback based on error rate or latency thresholds.

title: Evaluate the service mesh configuration for security and observability c
prompt: Evaluate the service mesh configuration for security and observability completeness. Identify every service in the system and whether it is enrolled in the mesh. For enrolled services, verify that mTLS is enforced for all inbound connections rather than allowed in permissive mode. Find any service-to-service connection that bypasses the mesh sidecar entirely and receives no telemetry. Assess whether traffic policies — retries, timeouts, and circuit breakers — are configured consistently across all services or only in isolated spots. Review whether the mesh telemetry includes distributed traces with enough context to diagnose cross-service latency issues. Summarize the top five mesh configuration gaps that create security or observability blind spots.

title: Review the operational readiness of the service mesh for incident respon
prompt: Review the operational readiness of the service mesh for incident response. Assess whether mesh telemetry feeds into alerting that fires before user-visible degradation occurs. Identify services with no SLO defined in the mesh, leaving the operations team with no threshold for when to escalate. Find any mesh configuration that has never been validated in a chaos engineering exercise. Assess whether the mesh control plane itself is a single point of failure — what happens to service-to-service communication if the control plane becomes unavailable. Recommend the mesh configuration changes that would most improve incident detection and response time.

title: You are a service mesh engineer reviewing the mesh deployment for securi
prompt: You are a service mesh engineer reviewing the mesh deployment for security, reliability, and operational correctness. Begin by enumerating every service in the mesh and verifying that sidecar injection is enabled and healthy for each. Identify services that have opted out of sidecar injection or are not reachable through the mesh, these are blind spots where mesh-level security and observability do not apply. Assess the mTLS configuration, whether all service-to-service communication is enforced as STRICT mTLS or whether PERMISSIVE mode is in use, allowing plaintext traffic on the same port. Review the traffic policies for each service pair, whether retry counts, timeouts, and circuit breaker settings are explicitly configured or left at defaults. Identify service connections where default retry settings cause retry storms on upstream failures. Assess the outlier detection configuration, whether unhealthy backend instances are ejected from load balancer rotation based on consecutive errors or error percentage thresholds. Evaluate the observability data generated by the mesh, whether distributed traces include mesh spans, whether latency histograms are available per service pair, and whether the golden signal dashboard is populated for all services. Conclude with a mesh readiness assessment covering mTLS posture, traffic policy coverage, circuit breaker configuration, and observability completeness.

title: Evaluate the security posture of the service mesh deployment
prompt: Evaluate the security posture of the service mesh deployment. Assess whether the mesh control plane is exposed only to cluster-internal traffic or whether its management API is accessible from outside the cluster. Identify service accounts with overly broad mesh policy permissions where a single service account can modify traffic policies for other services. Review the authorization policy configuration, whether each service has an explicit allow list of permitted callers or whether it accepts traffic from any identity in the mesh. Find services with no authorization policy that accept traffic from any mesh identity without restriction. Assess whether the mesh certificate authority is the platform default or a custom CA with a documented rotation procedure. Identify any service exempt from mTLS that handles sensitive data. Assess whether the mesh's access logs are enabled and shipped to the centralized logging system for security incident investigation. Recommend the authorization policy additions and mTLS enforcement changes that eliminate the highest-risk lateral movement paths within the cluster.

title: Assess the operational maturity of the service mesh from the perspective
prompt: Assess the operational maturity of the service mesh from the perspective of an on-call engineer managing an incident. Evaluate whether a service-to-service latency spike is immediately visible in the mesh's observability dashboard or requires custom queries against raw telemetry. Identify services with no golden signal alert where degradation is detected only by user reports. Review whether circuit breaker state changes generate alerts or are only visible through dashboard inspection. Assess whether traffic weight changes for canary deployments can be made without a code deployment or cluster configuration change. Identify the runbook coverage for common mesh incidents including sidecar crash, certificate rotation failure, control plane outage, and traffic policy misconfiguration. Assess whether the mesh has been tested with chaos experiments such as sidecar kill, control plane partition, and certificate expiration, and whether the results informed the current configuration. Recommend the specific alerting, runbook, and chaos engineering additions that would reduce mean time to detect and resolve mesh-related incidents.

title: Audit all background workers for health and monitoring
prompt: List every background worker process. For each, verify there is a health check, a restart policy, alerting on failure, and a way to monitor job completion rate and lag. Identify workers running silently without visibility.
prompt_short: Audit all background workers for monitoring, health checks, and alerting.
prompt_filtered: Worker health only. Monitoring and restart policy.

title: Find workers with no concurrency or rate limiting
prompt: Identify workers that can spawn unlimited concurrent jobs. For each, assess the risk of overwhelming downstream dependencies and recommend appropriate concurrency limits and backpressure mechanisms.
prompt_short: Find workers with no concurrency limits that risk overwhelming dependencies.
prompt_filtered: Concurrency control only. Rate and concurrency limits.

title: Identify workers with missing idempotency guarantees
prompt: Assess whether all background jobs are idempotent — safe to run more than once with the same input. Identify jobs that would create duplicates, double-charge, or corrupt state if retried after a partial failure.
prompt_short: Find background jobs that are not safe to retry or run twice.
prompt_filtered: Idempotency only. Double-run safety for background jobs.

title: Review worker failure handling and retry configuration
prompt: Review error handling in all background workers. Identify jobs that swallow errors silently, have no retry logic, or retry indefinitely without a cap. Verify there is a dead-letter destination for permanent failures.
prompt_short: Audit background worker error handling, retries, and dead-letter config.
prompt_filtered: Failure handling only. Retry logic and dead-letter queues.

title: Find long-running workers blocking job queues
prompt: Identify background jobs that take significantly longer than expected and are blocking queue progress. Assess whether they can be parallelized, chunked into smaller units, or moved to a dedicated queue to avoid starving faster jobs.
prompt_short: Find long-running jobs blocking queue throughput and faster tasks.
prompt_filtered: Queue starvation only. Long-running job impact on queue throughput.

title: Assess worker heartbeat and liveness detection mechanisms
prompt: Verify that all long-running background workers emit heartbeats at regular intervals. Find workers that can hang in a processing loop indefinitely without any external signal of being alive. Assess how quickly a hung worker is detected and whether the job scheduler reclaims the job for retry automatically.
prompt_short: Find workers without heartbeat that can hang silently with no detection.
prompt_filtered: Worker liveness only. Heartbeat and hang detection mechanisms.

title: Identify workers with no priority queue separation
prompt: Find workers consuming from a single flat queue that mixes high-priority and low-priority jobs. Assess whether a slow batch of low-priority jobs can delay user-visible high-priority work. Recommend a multi-queue architecture with dedicated workers for high-priority lanes.
prompt_short: Find workers mixing high and low-priority jobs with no priority separation.
prompt_filtered: Priority queue separation only. High-priority job delay by bulk jobs.

title: Review worker concurrency model for thread safety
prompt: Identify workers running multiple jobs concurrently in the same process. Find shared state — module-level caches, connection pools, global variables — that could cause race conditions when accessed from concurrent job goroutines or threads. Recommend thread-safe alternatives or concurrency limits that prevent simultaneous access.
prompt_short: Find shared mutable state accessed by concurrent background workers.
prompt_filtered: Thread safety only. Concurrent job access to shared module state.

title: Find workers with hardcoded schedule times ignoring timezone
prompt: Identify cron-scheduled jobs that use UTC times with the assumption that UTC equals the business timezone. Find jobs that should run at a specific local business time — like midnight or 9 AM local — but run at different local times during daylight saving transitions. Recommend explicit timezone-aware scheduling libraries.
prompt_short: Find cron jobs assuming UTC that break during daylight saving transitions.
prompt_filtered: Timezone handling only. Cron schedule correctness across DST changes.

title: Assess checkpoint and resumption for long-running batch jobs
prompt: Identify batch jobs that process large datasets in a single transaction with no checkpointing. A failure midway requires reprocessing from the start. Find jobs where adding periodic checkpoints would reduce recovery time from hours to minutes. Assess whether the batch job is designed to resume from a checkpoint or always restarts from zero.
prompt_short: Find batch jobs with no checkpointing that restart from scratch on failure.
prompt_filtered: Checkpoint and resumption only. Long batch recovery time reduction.

title: Evaluate worker scaling strategy under backlog pressure
prompt: Assess how background workers scale when queue depth increases. Find workers with fixed replica counts that cannot scale to drain a growing backlog. Identify queue metrics — depth, age of oldest message — that are not used as scaling signals. Recommend autoscaling triggers based on queue depth with a maximum worker count to protect downstream dependencies.
prompt_short: Find fixed-scale workers that cannot drain backlogs and need autoscaling.
prompt_filtered: Worker autoscaling only. Queue depth as scaling signal configuration.

title: Identify workers making unbounded external API calls without rate limiting
prompt: Find background workers that call external APIs — payment processors, email providers, webhooks — without rate limiting or throttling. A large batch job can exhaust external API rate limits, causing failures for all other system components that use the same API. Recommend a rate-aware worker with token bucket throttling.
prompt_short: Find batch workers calling external APIs without throttling the call rate.
prompt_filtered: External API rate limiting only. Worker call rate protection.

title: Review worker alerting on job failure rate thresholds
prompt: Verify that alerts are configured for job failure rates, not just total job counts. Find queues with no failure rate alarm that could silently accumulate DLQ messages for hours. Assess whether failure alerts fire quickly enough to meet the error budget for the downstream feature that depends on successful job completion.
prompt_short: Find worker queues with no failure rate alarm or too-slow alert threshold.
prompt_filtered: Worker failure alerting only. Failure rate threshold and alert timing.

title: Audit worker deployment for zero-downtime job hand-off
prompt: Assess whether deploying a new version of a worker gracefully hands off in-progress jobs. Find workers that are terminated mid-job on deployment, leaving jobs in a partially-processed state. Verify that SIGTERM is handled, in-progress jobs are allowed to complete or are released back to the queue, and the new version starts only after clean shutdown.
prompt_short: Find workers with no graceful shutdown that leave jobs mid-process on deploy.
prompt_filtered: Graceful shutdown only. In-progress job handling during rolling deployment.

title: Identify workers processing sensitive data without audit logging
prompt: Find background workers that access, transform, or delete sensitive data — PII, payment data, health records — without emitting an audit log entry. Audit logs for background jobs are often overlooked despite the same compliance requirements as interactive user actions. Verify each sensitive data operation has a corresponding audit entry with the job ID and data scope.
prompt_short: Find workers processing sensitive data with no audit log for compliance.
prompt_filtered: Worker audit logging only. Sensitive data access without audit trail.

title: Find workers with excessive database connection consumption
prompt: Identify background workers that acquire a database connection for the lifetime of the job rather than per-operation. Find workers that scale to many concurrent instances, each holding a persistent connection, exhausting the database connection pool and starving foreground user requests. Recommend connection acquisition scoped to the minimum required window.
prompt_short: Find workers holding database connections for entire job duration under concurrency.
prompt_filtered: Connection management only. Worker connection pool exhaustion under scale.

title: Evaluate worker job payload size and serialization overhead
prompt: Identify workers that serialize large payloads — full document bodies, large arrays — into the job message. Large payloads increase queue storage costs, serialization overhead, and the risk of exceeding message size limits. Find workers embedding more than 10KB in a job message when a reference key to a stored object would suffice.
prompt_short: Find workers embedding large payloads in job messages that should use references.
prompt_filtered: Job payload size only. Large payload in message vs object storage reference.
