prompt: Review Graceful Shutdown Handling

title: Check Distributed Lock Correctness
prompt: Check Distributed Lock Correctness

title: Find all background workers that run without a timeout
prompt: Find all background workers that run without a timeout. For each, describe what happens if the job hangs indefinitely — whether it blocks the queue, consumes resources, or prevents retry. Recommend timeout values based on expected worst-case job duration.

title: Identify the background worker queue with the longest average processing
prompt: Identify the background worker queue with the longest average processing lag. Trace the root cause — too few consumers, slow processing, or large job payloads. Recommend the scaling or optimization change that would bring queue lag within acceptable bounds.

title: Audit all background jobs for idempotency
prompt: Audit all background jobs for idempotency. Identify any job that would produce duplicate side effects — duplicate charges, duplicate emails, duplicate writes — if retried after a partial failure. For each, recommend the idempotency key strategy that prevents double execution.

title: Audit all background workers for graceful shutdown handling
prompt: Audit all background workers for graceful shutdown handling. Identify workers that do not listen for SIGTERM and SIGINT signals. Find workers that, when terminated, abandon in-progress jobs without re-enqueuing them. Assess whether job visibility timeouts are long enough to survive a planned worker restart without re-enqueuing still-processing jobs. Recommend graceful shutdown implementations for each worker that complete the current job before exiting or re-enqueue it if the job is too long to complete within the shutdown grace period.

title: Review all distributed locks used by background workers to prevent concu
prompt: Review all distributed locks used by background workers to prevent concurrent job execution. Identify lock implementations using Redis SETNX without an expiry, which create permanent locks if the acquiring process crashes. Find locks with an expiry shorter than the expected job execution time, which release the lock while the job is still running allowing a second worker to start the same job. Recommend lock implementations using SET NX EX with an expiry conservatively longer than the p99 job execution time, plus a lock refresh mechanism for long-running jobs.

title: Assess the scaling configuration for all background worker pools
prompt: Assess the scaling configuration for all background worker pools. Identify workers with a fixed worker count that does not scale with queue depth. Find workers that scale based on CPU or memory rather than queue depth, the wrong scaling signal for I/O-bound queue consumers. Assess whether the worker pool can scale to zero during idle periods to reduce cost. Recommend auto-scaling configurations triggered by queue depth with minimum, maximum, and target concurrency values for each worker pool.

title: Evaluate the job prioritization strategy for all background queues
prompt: Evaluate the job prioritization strategy for all background queues. Identify queues that mix high-priority jobs such as password reset emails and payment confirmations with low-priority jobs such as weekly digest emails and analytics aggregations. Find cases where a burst of low-priority jobs delays high-priority jobs. Recommend a queue separation strategy with dedicated high-priority queues, dedicated worker pools, and a prioritization scheme that guarantees processing of high-priority jobs within a maximum delay.

title: Review all background jobs for idempotency correctness
prompt: Review all background jobs for idempotency correctness. Identify jobs that write to a database, send notifications, or charge payment methods, which are operations with user-visible side effects. For each, verify that running the job twice with the same input produces the same outcome. Find jobs in at-least-once queues with no idempotency check that would create duplicate charges, duplicate notifications, or duplicate records on re-delivery. Recommend idempotency key designs and deduplication table patterns for the five highest-risk non-idempotent jobs.

title: Review all background worker implementations for reliability and correct
prompt: Review all background worker implementations for reliability and correctness. Inventory every worker, its job types, its retry strategy, and its timeout configuration. Identify workers with no timeout that can hang indefinitely without being forcibly terminated. Find workers that block on I/O without an async pattern, consuming a worker thread while waiting. Assess whether failed jobs are retried with exponential backoff or with fixed intervals that could cause thundering herd. Look for jobs that are not idempotent — where retry after partial failure produces duplicate side effects. Assess whether workers have a dead-letter queue for jobs that exceed retry limits. Summarize the top five worker reliability gaps.

title: Evaluate background worker observability and operational readiness
prompt: Evaluate background worker observability and operational readiness. Identify whether job processing latency, queue depth, and failure rates are monitored with alerts. Find any worker whose failures are silently swallowed without logging or alerting. Assess whether the team has visibility into which specific jobs are in the queue and their current state. Look for jobs that run for unexpectedly long durations and are not flagged as anomalies. Check whether worker scaling is automatic based on queue depth or requires manual intervention. Recommend the monitoring and operational tooling additions that would give the team sufficient visibility to diagnose worker issues within five minutes of an incident starting.

title: You are a backend engineer reviewing all background worker infrastructur
prompt: You are a backend engineer reviewing all background worker infrastructure for production readiness. Begin by enumerating every background job type, its trigger such as queue message, cron schedule, or event, its expected execution time, its resource requirements, and its failure handling behavior. For each job, verify that a maximum retry count is configured and that exhausted jobs are routed to a dead-letter queue rather than silently discarded. Assess whether each job is idempotent, safe to execute twice without producing duplicate side effects. Identify jobs that acquire distributed locks and review whether the lock implementation is correct, with expiry longer than execution time and released on crash. Review the worker shutdown behavior, whether workers finish their current job before exiting or abandon it mid-execution. Assess whether cron-scheduled jobs have overlap protection to prevent two instances running simultaneously. Evaluate whether job progress is tracked and visible to operators without direct database access. Conclude with a worker readiness assessment covering idempotency, failure handling, scaling, and operational visibility for each job type.

title: Evaluate the observability and operational tooling available for backgro
prompt: Evaluate the observability and operational tooling available for background workers. Assess whether queue depth, job execution time, job success rate, and retry rate are tracked per job type with dashboards visible to on-call engineers. Identify job types with no execution time histogram where slow jobs are invisible until they cause queue backup. Find job types with no error rate alert where failures accumulate silently. Assess whether engineers can inspect the contents of the job queue and DLQ without direct database or broker access. Review whether failed jobs can be replayed from the DLQ without a code deployment. Find job types where the only way to diagnose a failure is to search raw application logs rather than a structured job execution record. Assess whether the job processing rate is monitored relative to the enqueue rate. Recommend the specific monitoring, alerting, and tooling additions that would reduce mean time to detect and resolve worker-related incidents.

title: Audit the security and resource isolation of background worker processes
prompt: Audit the security and resource isolation of background worker processes. Assess whether workers run with the minimum credentials required for their specific job and not a shared credential set with access to all queues, databases, and external APIs. Identify jobs that process user-supplied input without validation where a malformed job payload could cause an unhandled exception or trigger an injection vulnerability in a downstream system. Find workers that log job payloads containing PII or sensitive data in plaintext. Assess whether resource limits such as CPU, memory, and execution time are enforced per job to prevent a single long-running or memory-intensive job from starving other workers. Review whether external API calls made by workers are authenticated with per-job or per-worker credentials rather than a shared application-level credential. Identify workers that write temporary files without cleanup, potentially filling disk and causing failures for all processes on the host. Recommend the specific credential scoping, input validation, logging redaction, and resource limit changes that eliminate the highest-risk worker security gaps.

title: Audit rate limiting coverage across all API endpoints
prompt: Review every public-facing API endpoint. Identify those with no rate limiting, those sharing a rate limiter key in a way that allows one user to affect others, and those with limits too high to provide meaningful protection.
prompt_short: Audit rate limiting coverage and correctness on all API endpoints.
prompt_filtered: Rate limit coverage only. Flag unprotected and misconfigured endpoints.

title: Find rate limiter key design flaws and bypass paths
prompt: Audit the key used for each rate limiter. Identify keys that are too coarse (shared across users), missing tenant isolation, or easily forged. Verify IP-based limits account for proxy and load balancer headers correctly.
prompt_short: Find rate limiter key design flaws that allow bypass or cross-tenant impact.
prompt_filtered: Key design only. Bypass and cross-tenant risks.

title: Verify rate limiters are durable across restarts
prompt: Check whether rate limiters are backed by a durable store (Redis, Cloudflare Workers Rate Limiting) or in-memory only. In-memory limits reset on restart and don't work across multiple instances. Flag in-memory implementations as a risk.
prompt_short: Verify rate limiters survive process restarts and work across instances.
prompt_filtered: Durability only. In-memory vs distributed rate limiter storage.

title: Assess rate limit responses for client-friendliness
prompt: Review what clients receive when rate limited. Verify the response includes the correct HTTP 429 status, a Retry-After header, and a consistent error body. Identify endpoints that return 400 or 500 instead of 429 when rate-limited.
prompt_short: Check that rate-limited responses include 429, Retry-After, and proper body.
prompt_filtered: Response format only. HTTP status, headers, and error body.

title: Find endpoints missing brute-force protection
prompt: Identify all authentication-adjacent endpoints — login, password reset, OTP verification, API key validation. Verify each has strict rate limiting specifically configured for brute-force protection, not just general API rate limits.
prompt_short: Find auth endpoints missing brute-force rate limiting protection.
prompt_filtered: Auth endpoints only. Brute-force protection gaps.

title: Assess sliding window vs fixed window rate limiter correctness
prompt: Identify rate limiters using fixed window counting. Fixed window limiters allow burst traffic at window boundaries — a client can send double the limit by sending at the end of one window and the start of the next. Find high-value endpoints using fixed window limiting and recommend sliding window or token bucket algorithms.
prompt_short: Find fixed-window rate limiters vulnerable to boundary burst exploitation.
prompt_filtered: Algorithm correctness only. Fixed window boundary burst vulnerability.

title: Evaluate rate limiter behavior under Redis or backend failure
prompt: Assess what happens when the rate limiter backend — Redis, Cloudflare Workers Rate Limiting — becomes unavailable. Identify implementations that fail open — allowing all traffic — versus fail closed — blocking all traffic. Recommend fail-open for user-facing endpoints with logging, fail-closed for security-critical endpoints.
prompt_short: Find rate limiters that fail open or closed on backend unavailability.
prompt_filtered: Rate limiter resilience only. Behavior when rate limit store is unavailable.

title: Find rate limits that do not account for distributed client identity
prompt: Identify rate limiters keyed by IP address only. Legitimate users behind NAT or corporate proxies share an IP — aggressive IP rate limiting blocks them all when one user triggers the limit. Find high-traffic endpoints where IP-based limits should be replaced with or supplemented by user-ID-based limits for authenticated requests.
prompt_short: Find IP-only rate limits that block legitimate users behind shared IPs.
prompt_filtered: Rate limit key design only. IP-only vs user-ID limit granularity.

title: Audit rate limit headers for RFC 6585 compliance
prompt: Verify that all rate-limited endpoints return the correct response headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, and Retry-After on 429 responses. Find endpoints returning 429 with no headers, leaving clients unable to implement correct back-off. Assess compliance with the IETF RateLimit header draft standard.
prompt_short: Find rate-limited responses missing RFC-compliant headers for client back-off.
prompt_filtered: Rate limit headers only. RFC 6585 response header compliance.

title: Identify rate limiting gaps on internal service-to-service APIs
prompt: Find internal APIs — between microservices, between background workers and databases — with no rate limiting. Internal APIs are often unprotected because they are not client-facing, but a misbehaving internal service can overwhelm a shared internal API. Recommend caller-scoped rate limits on all high-impact internal endpoints.
prompt_short: Find internal service APIs with no rate limiting on inter-service calls.
prompt_filtered: Internal API rate limits only. Service-to-service call rate protection.

title: Assess GraphQL query depth and complexity limiting
prompt: For GraphQL APIs, verify that query depth limiting and query complexity scoring are configured. A deeply nested GraphQL query can trigger exponential database load. Find GraphQL endpoints with no depth limit or complexity limit that are vulnerable to denial of service via introspection or nested relationship queries.
prompt_short: Find GraphQL APIs with no query depth or complexity limits.
prompt_filtered: GraphQL rate limiting only. Depth and complexity denial-of-service protection.

title: Find missing rate limits on webhook delivery endpoints
prompt: Identify webhook endpoints that receive inbound calls from external systems — payment processors, CI systems, third-party SaaS. Verify each has rate limiting to prevent a misbehaving sender from flooding the endpoint. Find webhook endpoints with no rate limiting or signature verification that could be exploited for denial of service.
prompt_short: Find webhook endpoints with no rate limiting or signature verification.
prompt_filtered: Webhook rate limiting only. Inbound webhook flood protection.

title: Evaluate cost-aware rate limiting for expensive operations
prompt: Identify operations with disproportionate backend cost — AI inference calls, large file processing, complex database aggregations. Find these operations rate-limited by count only rather than by cost or compute units. Recommend cost-weighted rate limits where one heavy operation counts as more than one unit toward the budget.
prompt_short: Find expensive operations limited by count not by compute cost.
prompt_filtered: Cost-aware rate limits only. Compute-unit weighting for expensive operations.

title: Assess rate limit quota sharing across an organization or tenant
prompt: Identify tenants or organizations sharing a rate limit quota across all their members. Find configurations where one power user within a tenant can exhaust the quota for all other users in the same organization. Recommend per-user sub-quotas within a tenant pool to prevent intra-tenant abuse.
prompt_short: Find shared tenant rate limit quotas where one user can exhaust all others.
prompt_filtered: Tenant quota sharing only. Intra-tenant rate limit fairness.

title: Review rate limit exempt paths and allowlist management
prompt: Find all paths explicitly exempted from rate limiting — health checks, internal callbacks, monitoring endpoints. Verify that exempt paths do not expose sensitive operations and that the exemption list is documented with justification for each entry. Find any path that was exempted temporarily and never re-protected.
prompt_short: Audit rate limit exemption list for unjustified or stale exemptions.
prompt_filtered: Exemption list only. Undocumented rate limit bypass paths.

title: Identify retry storm risk from upstream client retry behavior
prompt: Assess whether the combination of client retry policies and rate limiting can cause a retry storm. If all clients retry immediately on 429, the combined retry traffic can maintain or exceed the rate limit permanently. Find client retry configurations without exponential backoff and jitter that would amplify rate limit responses into a self-sustaining traffic spike.
prompt_short: Find client retry configs that amplify 429 responses into retry storms.
prompt_filtered: Retry storm risk only. Client retry behavior under rate limiting.

title: Evaluate rate limiting coverage on file upload and download endpoints
prompt: Find file upload and download endpoints with rate limiting on request count only — not on bytes transferred. An attacker can upload or download large files with few requests, consuming bandwidth and storage I/O far beyond what the request count limit implies. Recommend byte-transfer rate limits or file size limits on upload and download paths.
prompt_short: Find upload and download endpoints rate limited by request count not bytes.
prompt_filtered: Bandwidth rate limiting only. File transfer size limits vs request count limits.

title: Find Endpoints With No Rate Limiting
prompt: Find Endpoints With No Rate Limiting

title: Check Distributed Counter Consistency
prompt: Check Distributed Counter Consistency

title: Identify Rate Limit Header Compliance
prompt: Identify Rate Limit Header Compliance

title: Assess Burst vs Sustained Limit Separation
prompt: Assess Burst vs Sustained Limit Separation

title: Find Admin Endpoints Without Limits

title: Evaluate Rate Limit Error Response Format

title: Check Retry-After Header Correctness
prompt: Check Retry-After Header Correctness

title: List all public API endpoints
prompt: List all public API endpoints. For each, identify whether a rate limit is applied. Find endpoints with no rate limit that could be exploited for denial of service or credential stuffing. Recommend limit values based on expected legitimate usage patterns.

title: Audit rate limiting implementation for bypass paths
prompt: Audit rate limiting implementation for bypass paths. Identify any endpoint where the rate limit key — IP, user ID, or API key — can be rotated cheaply by an attacker to evade the limit. Recommend a multi-signal rate limiting strategy that is harder to bypass.

title: Find all auth-related endpoints — login, password reset, OTP verificatio
prompt: Find all auth-related endpoints — login, password reset, OTP verification — and verify brute-force protection is in place. For each, check the lockout threshold, lockout duration, and whether lockout state is durable across server restarts.

title: Identify every API endpoint with no rate limiting applied
prompt: Identify every API endpoint with no rate limiting applied. For each unprotected endpoint, assess the worst-case impact of an unconstrained request burst such as database connection exhaustion, downstream API rate limit violation, or service memory exhaustion. Prioritize unprotected endpoints by potential blast radius. Recommend specific rate limits including window size, request count, and key for the five highest-risk unprotected endpoints.

title: Audit all rate limiter key designs
prompt: Audit all rate limiter key designs. Identify rate limiters keyed only on IP address, which are bypassed by rotating IPs or shared NAT exit nodes. Find rate limiters keyed only on user ID that do not prevent pre-authentication abuse on login, signup, and password reset endpoints. Recommend layered key designs that apply IP-based limits at the unauthenticated layer and user-based limits at the authenticated layer.

title: Evaluate the distributed consistency of all rate limiters
prompt: Evaluate the distributed consistency of all rate limiters. Identify rate limiters implemented with in-memory counters that do not synchronize across multiple server instances or edge nodes, allowing a client to exceed the limit by spreading requests across instances. Find rate limiters using Redis with a fixed-window algorithm that allows a double-limit burst at window boundaries. Recommend sliding window or token bucket implementations backed by a distributed counter for all rate limiters that must enforce consistent limits across instances.

title: Review the rate limit response behavior for all protected endpoints
prompt: Review the rate limit response behavior for all protected endpoints. Identify endpoints that return a 429 without a Retry-After header, forcing clients to guess when to retry or retry immediately in a tight loop. Find endpoints that return a 403 or 400 on rate limit exhaustion rather than a 429, preventing clients from implementing correct backoff. Assess whether rate limit headers are included in responses so clients can self-throttle before hitting the limit. Recommend a standard rate limit response format with mandatory Retry-After and rate limit headers.

title: Assess whether rate limits are calibrated to protect downstream dependen
prompt: Assess whether rate limits are calibrated to protect downstream dependencies rather than just the API itself. Identify external APIs called from rate-limited endpoints and compare the rate limit on the endpoint to the rate limit of the external API. Find cases where the internal rate limit allows more requests per minute than the external API supports, meaning a burst of internal requests that each stay within the internal limit can exhaust the external limit. Recommend internal rate limits set to a fraction of the external API limit, accounting for concurrent callers and retry amplification.

title: Audit rate limiting coverage across all API endpoints
prompt: Audit rate limiting coverage across all API endpoints. Identify every endpoint and whether it has a rate limit applied, what the limit is, and how the limit key is constructed. Look for authentication endpoints — login, signup, password reset, and token refresh — that have no brute-force protection. Find endpoints that are rate limited by IP address only, which is ineffective when attackers use rotating residential proxies. Assess whether rate limits are stored in a durable, distributed store or in-memory — noting that in-memory limits reset on deployment and do not work across multiple instances. Identify the endpoints that would be most damaging if abused without rate limiting. Summarize the five highest-risk rate limiting gaps.

title: Evaluate the rate limiting implementation for correctness and resilience
prompt: Evaluate the rate limiting implementation for correctness and resilience. Check whether rate limiting runs before authentication checks — processing rate limits after auth creates a denial-of-service vector by exhausting auth compute for free. Assess whether the rate limit store can become a single point of failure — what happens to the API if Redis or the rate limit backend becomes unavailable. Find any rate limit configuration that is too aggressive and would throttle legitimate users under normal peak load. Review whether rate limit violations are logged with enough context to distinguish targeted attacks from legitimate traffic spikes. Recommend the implementation changes that would most improve the security and resilience of the rate limiting layer.

title: You are a platform engineer reviewing the rate limiting architecture of
prompt: You are a platform engineer reviewing the rate limiting architecture of a multi-tenant API. Begin by enumerating every API endpoint and classifying each as currently rate limited or unprotected. For each protected endpoint, review the rate limit configuration including the window size, the request limit, the key used to identify the client, and the enforcement mechanism. Assess whether the enforcement mechanism is consistent across all server instances, since in-memory counters provide no protection in a horizontally scaled deployment. Identify endpoints that are rate limited per IP but not per user, allowing authenticated users to bypass the limit through a proxy or VPN. Find endpoints that are rate limited per user but not per IP, leaving unauthenticated endpoints vulnerable to pre-authentication abuse. Assess whether burst limits are separated from sustained rate limits. Evaluate the rate limit response format, whether 429 responses include a Retry-After header and rate limit headers that enable client-side self-throttling. Conclude with a rate limiting coverage matrix showing each endpoint, its current protection status, the recommended configuration, and the priority for remediation.

title: Evaluate the rate limiting design for its effectiveness against common a
prompt: Evaluate the rate limiting design for its effectiveness against common abuse patterns. Assess whether credential stuffing is blocked by the login endpoint's rate limit. Identify the exact number of login attempts an attacker could make per minute per IP and per username and verify this is insufficient to compromise accounts with strong passwords. Assess whether account enumeration is possible through differential rate limiting where valid usernames are rate limited differently from invalid ones. Review the signup rate limit and verify it prevents bulk account creation. Find any endpoint that takes longer to respond for valid inputs than invalid inputs, creating a timing oracle. Assess whether rate limits are applied before or after authentication, since limits applied after authentication can be bypassed by creating disposable accounts. Recommend the specific rate limit adjustments that would close the most impactful abuse vectors while minimizing friction for legitimate users.

title: Audit the operational monitoring and bypass-resistance of the rate limit
prompt: Audit the operational monitoring and bypass-resistance of the rate limiting implementation. Assess whether rate limit hit rates are tracked per endpoint with dashboards and alerts for anomalous spikes, since a spike in 429s may indicate an attack or a misconfigured client. Identify rate limiters with no monitoring where an attacker could probe without generating a visible signal. Review whether rate limits can be adjusted without a code deployment, whether limits are configurable via environment variables or a control plane rather than hardcoded constants. Find rate limits so tight that legitimate power users regularly hit them, generating support tickets and degraded experience without a security benefit. Assess whether rate limit bypass attempts such as unusually uniform request spacing, IP rotation patterns, or distributed user agent strings are detected and flagged. Evaluate whether the rate limit configuration is tested in the CI pipeline to prevent accidentally removing limits from critical endpoints. Recommend the specific monitoring additions and configuration management changes that make the rate limiting layer more operationally visible and harder to bypass silently.

title: Identify critical dependencies missing circuit breakers
prompt: Map all external and internal dependencies in the critical request path. For each, verify a circuit breaker is configured. Flag dependencies with no circuit breaker whose failure would cascade to user-facing errors.
prompt_short: Find critical dependencies with no circuit breaker protection.
prompt_filtered: Missing circuit breakers only. Critical-path dependencies.

title: Review circuit breaker thresholds and trip conditions
prompt: Audit the error rate and latency thresholds configured for each circuit breaker. Identify thresholds too high (won't trip until damage is done) or too low (trips on normal transient errors). Recommend evidence-based values.
prompt_short: Review circuit breaker thresholds for appropriate trip sensitivity.
prompt_filtered: Threshold configuration only. Error rate and latency settings.

title: Assess fallback behavior when circuit is open
prompt: For each circuit breaker, verify that a meaningful fallback is defined for the open state — cached data, degraded response, or graceful error. Identify breakers that return a hard error with no fallback, causing user-visible failures.
prompt_short: Verify all open-circuit states have a defined fallback or degraded response.
prompt_filtered: Fallback behavior only. Open-state handling.

title: Check half-open state and recovery probe configuration
prompt: Review how circuit breakers recover from the open state. Verify the half-open probe rate is low enough to test recovery without overwhelming a struggling dependency, and that full recovery requires multiple successful probes.
prompt_short: Review half-open recovery probes to avoid overwhelming a recovering dependency.
prompt_filtered: Half-open configuration only. Recovery probe rate and success criteria.

title: Ensure circuit breaker state is observable and alertable
prompt: Verify that circuit breaker state transitions (closed → open, open → half-open, half-open → closed) emit metrics and trigger alerts. Identify breakers that trip silently with no on-call notification.
prompt_short: Verify circuit breaker state changes are monitored and trigger alerts.
prompt_filtered: Observability only. State transition metrics and alerting.

title: Audit circuit breaker retry budget and interaction with retry logic
prompt: Identify services with both a retry policy and a circuit breaker. Verify that the retry budget — maximum retries per request — is counted against the circuit breaker failure rate, so retries trip the breaker when the upstream is degraded. Find configurations where retries are transparent to the circuit breaker, causing the breaker to undercount failures.
prompt_short: Find circuit breakers that do not count retries against their failure threshold.
prompt_filtered: Retry budget only. Retry-to-breaker failure counting integration.

title: Find circuit breakers with no timeout configuration on probes
prompt: Verify that circuit breaker probe requests — made in the half-open state — have a short timeout configured. A probe with no timeout will wait indefinitely for the recovering dependency, keeping the breaker in half-open state and blocking recovery detection. Find breakers whose probe timeout equals or exceeds the request timeout, which provides no recovery advantage.
prompt_short: Find circuit breaker probes with no timeout preventing recovery detection.
prompt_filtered: Probe timeout only. Half-open probe timeout configuration.

title: Assess circuit breaker granularity for endpoint-level isolation
prompt: Identify circuit breakers applied at the service level rather than the endpoint level. A service-level breaker opens for all endpoints when any one endpoint fails, blocking healthy endpoints unnecessarily. Find high-traffic services where endpoint-level circuit breakers would prevent a failure in one API path from blocking the entire service.
prompt_short: Find service-level breakers that should be per-endpoint for finer isolation.
prompt_filtered: Breaker granularity only. Service-level vs endpoint-level circuit isolation.

title: Review bulkhead isolation between circuit breaker pools
prompt: Identify services using a single shared thread pool or connection pool for all upstream calls, with circuit breakers wrapping individual calls. A slow upstream can exhaust the shared pool, causing circuit breakers on other upstreams to be starved of resources even before they trip. Recommend per-upstream bulkhead pools to isolate resource exhaustion.
prompt_short: Find shared execution pools where one slow upstream starves all circuit breakers.
prompt_filtered: Bulkhead isolation only. Shared pool exhaustion across circuit breakers.

title: Find services missing circuit breakers on database connections
prompt: Verify that circuit breakers protect database connections in addition to external service calls. A degraded database that responds slowly rather than refusing connections can hold all available connection pool slots, starving the application. Find services relying on connection timeouts alone with no circuit breaker to fast-fail when the database is degraded.
prompt_short: Find database connection paths with no circuit breaker for slow-response scenarios.
prompt_filtered: Database circuit breakers only. Slow DB response vs connection timeout protection.

title: Evaluate circuit breaker error classification for correct trip signals
prompt: Review which error types are counted toward the circuit breaker failure rate. Find breakers that count all exceptions — including business logic errors like 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found — against the failure threshold. Business errors should not trip the breaker. Only timeout, connection refused, and 5xx errors should count as circuit breaker failures.
prompt_short: Find circuit breakers counting business errors as failure signals.
prompt_filtered: Error classification only. Which error types should and should not trip the breaker.

title: Assess circuit breaker recovery time and SLO alignment
prompt: Review the wait duration in the open state — how long the breaker remains open before attempting a probe. Find breakers with open durations too short — they will probe a still-recovering dependency prematurely — and too long — they slow recovery unnecessarily. Recommend open durations aligned with the typical recovery time of each dependency, informed by incident history.
prompt_short: Calibrate circuit breaker open duration to match dependency recovery times.
prompt_filtered: Recovery timing only. Open state duration vs dependency recovery time.

title: Identify cascading circuit breaker failures across service layers
prompt: Trace the dependency chain for critical service paths. Identify scenarios where a failure in a leaf dependency trips a circuit breaker, which causes failures in the intermediate layer, which trips another circuit breaker, cascading up to user-visible errors. Find chains where a single leaf failure could trip multiple layers of breakers simultaneously.
prompt_short: Find multi-layer breaker cascade paths where one failure causes total outage.
prompt_filtered: Cascade topology only. Multi-hop circuit breaker failure chains.

title: Review circuit breaker configuration management and drift
prompt: Assess whether circuit breaker configurations are managed as code in version control or set manually at runtime. Find breakers configured via runtime admin APIs with no source-of-truth in code — these drift silently after incidents when values are tweaked manually. Recommend infrastructure-as-code management for all circuit breaker thresholds.
prompt_short: Find circuit breakers configured manually that can drift from documented policy.
prompt_filtered: Configuration management only. Runtime vs code-managed breaker settings.

title: Find manual circuit breaker controls for incident response
prompt: Assess whether engineers can manually force a circuit breaker open or closed during an incident without deploying code. Find breakers with no manual override capability — requiring a deployment to shed load from a failing dependency. Recommend a runtime control plane with role-gated override capability for on-call engineers.
prompt_short: Find circuit breakers with no manual override for incident load shedding.
prompt_filtered: Manual override only. Operator-controlled breaker force-open for incident response.

title: Assess success rate threshold calibration against baseline error rates
prompt: Identify circuit breakers using a failure rate threshold without accounting for the baseline error rate of the dependency under normal conditions. A dependency with a 2% natural error rate should not have a 1% trip threshold — it would always be tripped. Find miscalibrated thresholds by comparing them against observed error rates from the last 30 days of production data.
prompt_short: Find circuit breaker thresholds below the dependency's natural baseline error rate.
prompt_filtered: Threshold calibration only. Trip threshold vs observed baseline error rate.

title: Evaluate circuit breaker coverage for async and background callers
prompt: Find background workers and async event consumers that call external dependencies without circuit breakers. Circuit breakers are often applied to synchronous request paths but omitted from background processing. A broken dependency will cause background jobs to fail and retry indefinitely, exhausting DLQ capacity, without a circuit breaker to pause processing automatically.
prompt_short: Find background workers calling dependencies with no circuit breaker.
prompt_filtered: Async caller coverage only. Background job circuit breaker gaps.

title: List all external service dependencies and identify which have a circuit
prompt: List all external service dependencies and identify which have a circuit breaker protecting inbound calls. Find dependencies with no circuit breaker whose failures would cascade. For each, recommend a failure threshold, timeout, and half-open probe strategy.

title: Audit the fallback behavior of every circuit breaker in the system
prompt: Audit the fallback behavior of every circuit breaker in the system. Identify breakers that open correctly but provide no fallback — returning errors instead of degraded responses. For each, design a fallback that maintains partial functionality while the upstream is unavailable.

title: Assess circuit breaker observability
prompt: Assess circuit breaker observability. Identify breakers that have no monitoring on their state transitions. Find any breaker that has tripped in the past 30 days and was not detected by alerting. Recommend the metrics and alert thresholds that would give the team immediate visibility into breaker state changes.

title: Identify every external service call with no circuit breaker
prompt: Identify every external service call with no circuit breaker. For each unprotected call, assess the failure mode when the external service becomes unavailable, whether it causes a thread or connection exhaustion cascade, a timeout cascade, or a graceful degradation. Prioritize unprotected calls by upstream call rate and downstream service reliability history. Recommend circuit breaker configurations including error threshold, window size, and sleep time for the five highest-risk unprotected calls.

title: Audit all circuit breaker threshold configurations
prompt: Audit all circuit breaker threshold configurations. Find breakers with error thresholds so high they trip only after a complete downstream failure, providing no protection against partial degradation. Find breakers with thresholds so low they trip on normal transient errors, causing unnecessary service degradation. Recommend threshold values calibrated to each downstream service's normal error baseline plus a margin that distinguishes degradation from noise.

title: Evaluate the fallback behavior for each circuit breaker in the system
prompt: Evaluate the fallback behavior for each circuit breaker in the system. Identify breakers that return a generic 503 without a graceful fallback where the fallback could serve cached data, a default response, or a subset of functionality. Assess whether fallback responses are clearly distinguished from live responses so clients know they are receiving degraded results. Find breakers whose fallback is itself dependent on a slow or failing service. Recommend fallback implementations that are independent of the failing service and that degrade gracefully with clear client communication.

title: Review the circuit breaker state transitions for correctness in all impl
prompt: Review the circuit breaker state transitions for correctness in all implementations. Identify breakers that move directly from OPEN to CLOSED without a HALF-OPEN probe phase, allowing a still-unhealthy service to receive full traffic immediately after the sleep window. Find breakers that send too many probe requests in the HALF-OPEN state, overwhelming a recovering service. Assess whether the probe request count and success threshold for HALF-OPEN to CLOSED transition are correctly calibrated. Recommend state machine corrections for each misconfigured breaker.

title: Assess the observability and operational control of all circuit breakers
prompt: Assess the observability and operational control of all circuit breakers. Identify breakers with no state change alert where a breaker trips and the on-call engineer is not notified. Find breakers whose current state is not visible in the monitoring dashboard without a code query or log search. Assess whether circuit breakers can be manually forced OPEN or CLOSED by an operator without a code deployment, a critical capability during incidents. Evaluate whether breaker state changes are correlated with downstream service health in the observability platform. Recommend the specific alerting, dashboard, and manual override additions that make the circuit breaker layer fully operational.

title: Audit every circuit breaker in the system for correct configuration and
prompt: Audit every circuit breaker in the system for correct configuration and effectiveness. Inventory all external dependencies and identify which are protected by a circuit breaker. For unprotected dependencies, describe the cascade failure that would occur if the dependency became slow or unavailable. For each existing circuit breaker, review the failure threshold, timeout, and half-open probe configuration. Assess whether the threshold is tuned to the expected error rate under normal conditions — a threshold too low causes false trips while a threshold too high allows cascading failures to build before the breaker opens. Identify breakers that open correctly but return raw errors rather than degraded fallback responses. Summarize the top five circuit breaker gaps ordered by blast radius.

title: Evaluate the observability of all circuit breakers in the system
prompt: Evaluate the observability of all circuit breakers in the system. Identify which breakers emit metrics on state transitions — closed, open, and half-open — and which are silent. Find any breaker that has tripped in production without generating an alert. Assess whether engineers on-call have a dashboard showing the current state of all circuit breakers in real time. Look for circuit breakers implemented in shared libraries versus those ad hoc in individual services — noting that ad-hoc implementations are often misconfigured. Recommend the monitoring additions and implementation standardization changes that would give the team complete visibility into the circuit breaker layer during incidents.

title: You are a resilience engineering specialist reviewing the circuit breake
prompt: You are a resilience engineering specialist reviewing the circuit breaker implementation across a distributed system. Begin by enumerating every service-to-service and service-to-external-API call in the system and identifying which calls have circuit breakers and which do not. For each unprotected call, assess the worst-case cascade if the downstream service becomes unavailable, including how many threads or connections would be blocked waiting for a response before the caller times out. Assess the error threshold and window size configuration for each active circuit breaker, whether the threshold is calibrated to the downstream service's normal error baseline or whether it uses a default value that is either too sensitive or too permissive. Review the HALF-OPEN state behavior, whether it sends a controlled number of probe requests to validate recovery or transitions immediately to CLOSED on the first successful probe. Evaluate the fallback response for each breaker, whether it serves cached data, a degraded response, or a hard failure. Conclude with a circuit breaker coverage matrix showing each call, its current protection status, its threshold configuration, and the recommended changes.

title: Evaluate the operational maturity of the circuit breaker layer from the
prompt: Evaluate the operational maturity of the circuit breaker layer from the perspective of an on-call engineer during an incident. Assess whether circuit breaker state changes generate real-time alerts that reach the on-call engineer before user impact is reported. Identify breakers whose state is only visible through log queries or application-level metrics rather than a dedicated circuit breaker dashboard. Review whether an operator can manually force a breaker OPEN to shed load during an incident without requiring a code deployment or infrastructure change. Assess whether the circuit breaker implementation correctly distinguishes between client errors such as 4xx and server errors such as 5xx, counting only server errors toward the trip threshold. Identify breakers that count timeout errors separately from error-rate thresholds, providing an additional signal for a degraded-but-not-erroring downstream service. Recommend the specific monitoring, alerting, and manual control additions that would make circuit breaker incidents detectable and manageable in under five minutes.

title: Audit the interaction between circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts ac
prompt: Audit the interaction between circuit breakers, retries, and timeouts across the full call chain. Identify call chains where retry logic wraps a circuit breaker, meaning a tripped breaker causes retries that multiply the failed requests before returning an error to the caller. Find call chains where the timeout on the caller is shorter than the circuit breaker sleep time, meaning the caller will time out and retry before the breaker has a chance to recover, preventing recovery. Assess whether the retry budget is bounded to prevent a retry storm from overwhelming a recovering downstream service. Identify nested circuit breakers where service A calls service B which calls service C, each with its own breaker, and assess whether a C failure trips B's breaker before A's breaker in a predictable cascade order. Recommend a coordinated timeout, retry, and circuit breaker configuration across the full call chain that prevents retry amplification, allows orderly recovery, and limits cascade depth.

title: Audit VM sizing for over and under-provisioning
prompt: Review all virtual machine sizes against actual CPU and memory utilization. Identify VMs that are consistently over-provisioned (wasting cost) and under-provisioned (causing performance issues under load).
prompt_short: Audit VM sizing against actual utilization to find waste and gaps.
prompt_filtered: VM sizing only. CPU and memory utilization analysis.

title: Find VMs with outdated or unpatched base images
prompt: Identify virtual machines running base images older than 90 days without security patches applied. Assess the CVE exposure of unpatched images and prioritize by internet exposure level.
prompt_short: Find VMs running unpatched or outdated base images with CVE risk.
prompt_filtered: Patch status only. Base image age and known CVE exposure.

title: Review VM backup and snapshot configuration
prompt: Audit backup and snapshot schedules for all production VMs. Identify VMs with no backup, snapshots older than the recovery point objective, and missing backup verification processes.
prompt_short: Audit VM backup schedules and verify they meet recovery objectives.
prompt_filtered: Backup configuration only. Schedule, retention, and verification.

title: Assess VM network isolation and security groups
prompt: Review security group and network ACL rules for all VMs. Identify overly permissive rules (0.0.0.0/0 ingress), unused rules, and VMs that can reach sensitive resources they have no business accessing.
prompt_short: Audit VM security groups for overly permissive inbound rules.
prompt_filtered: Security group rules only. Ingress exposure analysis.

title: Identify VMs that should be migrated to containers
prompt: Review all VMs running stateless workloads. Identify candidates for migration to containers or serverless based on deployment frequency, resource utilization patterns, and operational overhead. Estimate cost and complexity for each migration.
prompt_short: Find VMs running stateless workloads that should move to containers.
prompt_filtered: Migration candidates only. Stateless VM identification.

title: Assess vCPU oversubscription ratio and CPU ready time
prompt: Review the vCPU to physical CPU ratio across all hypervisor hosts. Identify hosts with oversubscription ratios above 4:1 for CPU-sensitive workloads. Find VMs experiencing high CPU ready time which manifests as application latency spikes.
prompt_short: Find high vCPU oversubscription ratios causing CPU ready time and latency spikes.
prompt_filtered: vCPU oversubscription only. CPU ready time and ratio analysis.

title: Review VM live migration impact on latency-sensitive workloads
prompt: Identify VMs running latency-sensitive workloads subject to live migration events. Live migration causes brief network and CPU stalls. Find VMs that experienced latency spikes coinciding with migration events. Recommend pinning latency-sensitive VMs to dedicated hosts.
prompt_short: Find latency-sensitive VMs impacted by live migration events.
prompt_filtered: Live migration impact only. Latency spikes during VM migration.

title: Audit VM anti-affinity rules for availability zone distribution
prompt: Identify VM groups that should be distributed across availability zones. Find VM groups with no anti-affinity rules that may all be placed in the same physical rack or zone, making them vulnerable to a single zone outage.
prompt_short: Find VM groups with no anti-affinity rules vulnerable to zone-level failures.
prompt_filtered: VM placement rules only. Anti-affinity and AZ distribution.

title: Evaluate VM disk I/O performance and storage over-provisioning
prompt: Assess disk I/O throughput and IOPS for all production VMs. Find VMs on shared storage hosts with too many I/O-intensive VMs per host causing contention. Identify VMs where thin-provisioned disks have grown close to the physical datastore limit. Recommend IOPS reservations and datastore capacity alerts.
prompt_short: Find VM storage contention and thin-provisioned disks nearing capacity.
prompt_filtered: VM storage only. IOPS contention and thin provision capacity risk.

title: Identify VM sprawl and zombie VMs with no active workload
prompt: Audit all VMs for activity. Find VMs with no network I/O, disk activity, or CPU usage for more than 30 days. Assess the cost of zombie VMs and recommend a VM lifecycle policy with automated decommissioning for inactive VMs.
prompt_short: Find zombie VMs with no activity in 30+ days that should be decommissioned.
prompt_filtered: VM sprawl only. Inactive VMs with no workload and decommissioning cost.

title: Review hypervisor host patching and reboot cadence
prompt: Assess the hypervisor host patching schedule. Identify hosts not patched in more than 90 days. Find environments where host patching requires unplanned VM migration not yet tested. Recommend a rolling patch schedule with tested live migration procedures.
prompt_short: Find hypervisor hosts not patched in 90+ days and assess migration readiness.
prompt_filtered: Host patching only. Hypervisor patch age and migration pre-testing.

title: Assess VM template and golden image management process
prompt: Review the process for maintaining and updating VM templates and golden images. Identify images not patched since creation. Find environments where VMs are provisioned from outdated templates. Recommend a monthly golden image refresh cycle with automated scanning before promotion.
prompt_short: Find stale VM templates used for provisioning that have no update cycle.
prompt_filtered: Golden image management only. Template age and patch currency.

title: Evaluate VM snapshot sprawl and retention policy
prompt: Identify VMs with many accumulated snapshots. Each snapshot degrades VM disk I/O as the delta chain grows. Find VMs with snapshot chains longer than 3 levels or snapshots older than 7 days with no documented reason. Recommend automated snapshot expiry and a cleanup policy.
prompt_short: Find VMs with long or old snapshot chains degrading performance and wasting storage.
prompt_filtered: Snapshot management only. Chain depth, age, and performance impact.

title: Identify VM memory ballooning and swap activity under pressure
prompt: Find VMs experiencing memory balloon driver activity or hypervisor-level swapping. Memory ballooning reclaims VM memory under host pressure, causing the guest OS to swap to disk. Identify hosts with sustained balloon activity and recommend adding host memory or reducing allocations.
prompt_short: Find VMs experiencing memory balloon reclamation causing guest OS swap.
prompt_filtered: Memory pressure only. Balloon driver activity and host memory overcommit.

title: Review NUMA topology alignment for high-performance VMs
prompt: For VMs running latency-sensitive workloads, verify that NUMA topology is respected so vCPUs and memory are allocated from the same NUMA node. Find VMs with vCPUs spanning NUMA nodes, causing remote memory access latency. Recommend NUMA-aligned VM sizing.
prompt_short: Find VMs with vCPUs spanning NUMA nodes causing remote memory access latency.
prompt_filtered: NUMA alignment only. Cross-NUMA memory access and latency impact.

title: Assess VM disaster recovery RPO and RTO configuration
prompt: Verify that disaster recovery configurations exist for all production VMs. Find VMs with no DR configuration or with DR replication lag exceeding the documented RPO. Assess whether the RTO has been measured and is within the service SLA.
prompt_short: Find VMs with no DR replication or with replication lag exceeding the RPO.
prompt_filtered: VM DR configuration only. RPO compliance and RTO measurement.

title: Identify VMs with direct device passthrough security implications
prompt: Find VMs using PCIe or GPU passthrough that bypass hypervisor isolation. Passthrough grants the guest OS direct DMA access exploitable for DMA attacks. Identify passthrough devices not strictly required and assess whether the security trade-off is documented.
prompt_short: Find VM device passthrough configurations that bypass hypervisor isolation.
prompt_filtered: Device passthrough only. DMA access and hypervisor isolation bypass risk.

title: Fill in Missing Data Gaps
prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

title: Gain full understanding before completing
prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

title: Find What's Broken
prompt: Find What's Broken

title: Recommend Next Steps

prompt: Score for Production Readiness

title: Audit VM Sizing
prompt: Audit VM Sizing

title: Check Migration Candidates
prompt: Check Migration Candidates

title: Audit VM Resource Overcommit Ratios
prompt: Audit VM Resource Overcommit Ratios

title: Find VMs Without Snapshot Policies
prompt: Find VMs Without Snapshot Policies

title: Check Hypervisor Patch Compliance
prompt: Check Hypervisor Patch Compliance

title: Identify Underutilized VM Instances
prompt: Identify Underutilized VM Instances

title: Assess VM Backup and Recovery RTOs
prompt: Assess VM Backup and Recovery RTOs

title: Review Network Segment Isolation
prompt: Review Network Segment Isolation

title: Find VM Sprawl and Orphaned Instances
prompt: Find VM Sprawl and Orphaned Instances

title: Check Storage I/O Bottlenecks
prompt: Check Storage I/O Bottlenecks

title: Audit all virtual machines for over and under-provisioning
prompt: Audit all virtual machines for over and under-provisioning. Find VMs consistently using less than 20% of allocated CPU and memory. For each, calculate monthly cost savings from right-sizing. Order findings by annual savings potential.

title: Identify VMs that are candidates for containerization
prompt: Identify VMs that are candidates for containerization. For each, assess the statelessness of the workload, the presence of multiple services in one VM, and the deployment frequency. Score each candidate on migration effort versus operational benefit.

title: Find all VMs running base images older than 90 days without documented p
prompt: Find all VMs running base images older than 90 days without documented patch exceptions. For each, identify the highest-severity CVE in the current image. Prioritize by internet exposure level and recommend a patching schedule.

title: Audit all virtual machine resource allocations for overcommit risk
prompt: Audit all virtual machine resource allocations for overcommit risk. Identify hosts where total allocated vCPU or RAM across all VMs exceeds the physical capacity by more than the safe overcommit ratio for the workload type. Find VMs with no memory ballooning or swap configured that would crash under memory pressure. Assess the worst-case scenario if the busiest VM on each host runs at its peak simultaneously. Recommend resource reservations and overcommit ratio limits for each host.

title: Identify all virtual machines with no snapshot policy or backup configur
prompt: Identify all virtual machines with no snapshot policy or backup configuration. Find VMs running production workloads with no backup in the last 7 days. Assess the recovery point objective for each unprotected VM and the data loss consequence of a host failure. Recommend a snapshot schedule and offsite backup policy for each production VM based on its RPO requirement. Verify that the backup procedure has been tested with a restore drill in the last 90 days.

title: Evaluate VM network segmentation
prompt: Evaluate VM network segmentation. Identify VMs with network interfaces on multiple segments including internal, DMZ, and management, that create a network bridge path between isolated zones. Find VMs reachable from the internet without a firewall or NAT boundary. Assess whether the management network is segregated from the production network, preventing a compromised production VM from reaching hypervisor management interfaces. Recommend VLAN and firewall rule changes that eliminate the highest-risk cross-segment paths.

title: Review hypervisor patch compliance across all hosts
prompt: Review hypervisor patch compliance across all hosts. Identify hosts running a hypervisor version with known critical CVEs that have not been patched in the last 30 days. Find hosts where patch application requires a maintenance window that has not been scheduled. Assess the risk of a VM escape vulnerability in an unpatched hypervisor. Recommend a patch cadence with specific deadlines for each severity level and a live migration strategy that avoids maintenance windows for critical patches.

title: Assess VM sprawl and orphaned instance management
prompt: Assess VM sprawl and orphaned instance management. Identify VMs that have been powered off for more than 30 days with no documented reason. Find VMs with no owner tag or cost allocation metadata. Assess the total resource waste consumed by powered-off or untagged VMs. Recommend a VM lifecycle policy with required owner tags, automatic power-off notifications for idle VMs, and a decommission workflow for VMs past their documented end-of-life date.

title: Review the entire virtual machine inventory for cost efficiency and oper
prompt: Review the entire virtual machine inventory for cost efficiency and operational overhead. Identify every VM, its allocated resources, its actual average utilization, and its monthly cost. Find VMs that are consistently over-provisioned — allocated far more CPU or memory than they use — and calculate the annual cost of that excess. Identify VMs that are under-provisioned and experiencing performance throttling during peak load. Assess whether any VM is running multiple unrelated services that would be better separated. Find VMs with no automated patching or image update process. Summarize the top five cost and operational efficiency improvements available through right-sizing and consolidation.

title: Evaluate which VMs are candidates for migration to containers
prompt: Evaluate which VMs are candidates for migration to containers. For each VM, assess whether the workload is stateless or stateful, how frequently it is deployed, and whether its dependencies make containerization straightforward. Identify VMs running a single long-lived process that is a natural container candidate. Find VMs where containerization would improve deployment speed, resource density, or operational consistency. Assess the migration complexity for the top three candidates — what would need to change in the application, the infrastructure, and the deployment pipeline. Recommend a migration order based on effort-to-benefit ratio, starting with the highest-value, lowest-effort candidates.

title: You are a virtualization engineer reviewing the hypervisor environment f
prompt: You are a virtualization engineer reviewing the hypervisor environment for performance, availability, and security. Begin by enumerating all physical hosts and their virtual machine workloads including the vCPU count, memory allocation, storage IOPS, and network bandwidth reserved for each VM versus the physical capacity of the host. Identify hosts approaching memory or CPU saturation under normal load and assess the headroom remaining before VMs begin to compete for resources. Review the memory overcommit configuration, whether ballooning, swapping, or transparent page sharing is enabled and whether these mechanisms would cause performance degradation under the current allocation. Assess the storage I/O configuration, whether VMs with high IOPS requirements are on shared storage that is also serving VMs with lower priority workloads, creating contention. Evaluate the network configuration, whether high-throughput VMs have dedicated vNICs or must share bandwidth with other VMs on the same physical interface. Identify VMs with no resource reservation that could be starved by other VMs during peak load. Conclude with a capacity and performance assessment per host, identifying the most constrained resource per host and the specific VMs whose workloads are at risk of performance degradation first.

title: Evaluate the high availability and disaster recovery configuration of th
prompt: Evaluate the high availability and disaster recovery configuration of the virtualization environment. Assess whether VM high availability is enabled, whether the hypervisor automatically restarts VMs on a healthy host if their current host fails. Identify VMs not covered by HA, whether because they are on a standalone host, excluded from the HA cluster, or on a host with insufficient memory to restart all VMs from a failed host. Review the snapshot and backup policy for each production VM including the frequency of snapshots, the retention period, and whether backups are stored offsite or only on the same storage array as the VM. Assess the restore procedure, whether a full VM restore has been tested in the last 90 days and whether the recovery time objective is documented and achievable. Identify VMs with no live migration capability that are pinned to a specific host by licensing, hardware dependencies, or configuration, and assess the maintenance and failure risk. Recommend the HA configuration, backup policy, and restore procedure changes that would bring all production VMs to a documented and tested recovery standard.

title: Perform a security audit of the virtualization environment
prompt: Perform a security audit of the virtualization environment. Assess whether the hypervisor management interface is accessible only from a dedicated management network with MFA enforced, or whether it is reachable from the production network or the internet. Identify VMs with network interfaces bridging between the management VLAN and the production VLAN, creating a path that bypasses network segmentation. Review whether guest VM console access requires authentication at the hypervisor layer and whether console sessions are logged. Assess whether the hypervisor host operating system is hardened with unnecessary services disabled, SSH access restricted to known keys, and audit logging enabled. Find VMs running outdated guest operating systems with known unpatched CVEs. Identify VMs with shared credentials across multiple administrators rather than individual accounts with role-based access. Assess whether VM images are scanned for vulnerabilities before deployment. Recommend the management network isolation, authentication, and patching changes that eliminate the highest-risk paths from a compromised VM or guest account to the hypervisor host.

title: Audit WebSocket connection lifecycle and reconnection logic
prompt: Review every WebSocket connection in the application. Verify that automatic reconnection is implemented with exponential backoff and jitter, the reconnection loop has a stop condition, and state is reconciled after reconnection to recover missed events.
prompt_short: Audit WebSocket reconnection logic, backoff, and post-reconnect state sync.
prompt_filtered: WebSocket lifecycle only. Reconnection and state reconciliation.

title: Identify real-time features that should use SSE instead of WebSocket
prompt: Find WebSocket connections used for one-directional server-to-client streaming only. WebSockets have higher overhead than SSE and are harder to operate behind proxies. Identify server-push patterns such as notifications, live counters, and feed updates where SSE would be simpler and equally effective.
prompt_short: Find one-way WebSocket streams that should use SSE for simplicity.
prompt_filtered: Protocol selection only. WebSocket vs SSE for server-push use cases.

title: Assess long-polling implementation for correctness and efficiency
prompt: Find long-polling implementations. Verify the server holds the request until data is available or a timeout occurs rather than polling a database in a tight loop. Identify long-poll implementations with no timeout that hold connections indefinitely and those with timeouts too short to reduce polling frequency.
prompt_short: Audit long-polling for server-side correctness and timeout configuration.
prompt_filtered: Long-polling correctness only. Server hold vs database polling and timeout.

title: Review pub/sub channel naming conventions and topic scoping
prompt: Audit all pub/sub channels or topics used for real-time messaging. Identify channels too broad that send all events to all subscribers when tenant or user scoping is required. Find channels with predictable names an unauthorized client could guess and subscribe to, receiving events meant for another user.
prompt_short: Find pub/sub channels too broad or predictable that leak events across users.
prompt_filtered: Channel scoping only. Broad channels and guessable topic names.

title: Find presence tracking implementations with memory leak risk
prompt: Identify presence tracking systems that record connected users. Find implementations that add users to the presence set on connect but fail to remove them on disconnect, particularly on unclean disconnect. Assess whether presence data has a TTL or heartbeat expiry to prevent stale entries from accumulating.
prompt_short: Find presence registries that leak stale entries on unclean disconnect.
prompt_filtered: Presence tracking only. Stale entry accumulation on unclean disconnect.

title: Assess event ordering guarantees in the real-time layer
prompt: Identify real-time event streams where order matters such as chat messages, collaborative edits, and ordered notifications. Verify the real-time transport guarantees ordering or that the client has a sequence number to detect and reorder out-of-order messages. Find streams where ordering is assumed but not guaranteed by the transport.
prompt_short: Find real-time streams assuming ordering the transport does not guarantee.
prompt_filtered: Ordering guarantees only. Client sequence handling on out-of-order delivery.

title: Identify WebSocket message size limits and fragmentation handling
prompt: Review the maximum message size configured for WebSocket servers. Find connections where large messages are sent without size limits. Identify clients that buffer entire messages before processing rather than streaming, creating memory pressure on large payloads. Recommend message chunking and progressive processing for large payloads.
prompt_short: Find WebSocket connections sending large messages without size limits or chunking.
prompt_filtered: Message size limits only. Large payload WebSocket memory and fragmentation.

title: Review Durable Object or stateful WebSocket session isolation
prompt: For real-time systems using Cloudflare Durable Objects or equivalent stateful edge sessions, verify each session belongs to exactly one isolate with no shared mutable state across sessions. Find patterns that read shared KV state in the critical message path without locking, creating race conditions under concurrent delivery.
prompt_short: Audit Durable Object sessions for shared state race conditions.
prompt_filtered: Stateful session isolation only. Cross-session shared state race conditions.

title: Evaluate real-time connection authentication and token refresh
prompt: Verify that WebSocket or SSE connections are authenticated before establishment. Find implementations that pass tokens via query parameters which appear in server logs rather than upgrade headers. Assess whether long-lived connections handle token expiry by refreshing the token or closing the connection when it expires.
prompt_short: Audit real-time connection auth and token refresh for long-lived sessions.
prompt_filtered: Connection auth only. Token placement and expiry handling on persistent connections.

title: Identify missing back-pressure on real-time message producers
prompt: Find real-time event producers that publish at full speed regardless of subscriber consumption rate. A slow subscriber that cannot keep up will eventually have its buffer exhausted or connection dropped. Find producers with no rate limit or back-pressure mechanism that would slow publishing when subscriber lag grows.
prompt_short: Find real-time producers with no back-pressure when subscribers fall behind.
prompt_filtered: Back-pressure only. Producer rate vs slow subscriber consumption.

title: Assess real-time feature degradation path when transport fails
prompt: Identify real-time features with no fallback when the WebSocket server or SSE endpoint is unavailable. Find features that show a blank or broken state instead of falling back to polling or showing cached data. Assess the user experience during a real-time service outage and recommend a graceful degradation mode with polling fallback.
prompt_short: Find real-time features with no polling fallback when the transport is down.
prompt_filtered: Degradation path only. Real-time outage UX and fallback to polling.

title: Review fan-out scalability for high-subscriber broadcast events
prompt: Identify events broadcast to a large number of concurrent subscribers such as system announcements, live leaderboard updates, and shared state changes. Assess whether the fan-out implementation scales linearly or super-linearly with subscriber count. Find implementations serializing delivery to each subscriber sequentially rather than using a pub/sub broker for parallel fan-out.
prompt_short: Find broadcast events with sequential fan-out that bottleneck at scale.
prompt_filtered: Fan-out scalability only. Sequential vs parallel broadcast delivery.

title: Find real-time features missing offline event queuing
prompt: Identify real-time features where events sent while a user is disconnected are permanently lost. Find message systems that do not queue undelivered events for delivery on reconnect. Assess whether the missed event window is bounded or unbounded, risking infinite queues for offline users.
prompt_short: Find real-time systems that drop events sent while the user is disconnected.
prompt_filtered: Offline event queuing only. Missed event delivery on reconnect.

title: Audit WebSocket server scalability across multiple nodes
prompt: Verify that WebSocket connections can be handled across multiple server instances without requiring sticky sessions. Find systems where a message sent to one server node is not delivered to clients connected on a different node. Assess whether a pub/sub broker such as Redis, Ably, or Pusher fans out messages across all nodes.
prompt_short: Find WebSocket servers requiring sticky sessions to route messages correctly.
prompt_filtered: Multi-node WebSocket scalability only. Cross-node message delivery.

title: Evaluate real-time observability for connection counts and message rates
prompt: Assess whether the real-time layer exposes operational metrics including active connection count, messages per second, connection error rate, and reconnection rate. Find real-time servers with no monitoring where a mass disconnect or delivery failure would be invisible until users report it. Recommend metric export and alerting thresholds for connection health.
prompt_short: Find real-time servers with no connection count or message rate monitoring.
prompt_filtered: Real-time observability only. Connection and message delivery metrics.

title: Identify real-time message deduplication needs on reconnect
prompt: Find real-time systems that deliver the same event more than once from reconnect replays or at-least-once pub/sub brokers. Identify clients that process duplicate events without deduplication, causing duplicate UI updates, duplicate counts, or duplicate notifications. Recommend event ID deduplication at the client with a bounded dedup window.
prompt_short: Find real-time clients that process duplicate events without deduplication.
prompt_filtered: Real-time deduplication only. Duplicate event processing on client.

title: Assess real-time feature impact on battery for mobile clients
prompt: Identify real-time connections that remain open while the app is backgrounded on mobile devices. Persistent WebSocket connections and frequent polling prevent the CPU from entering low-power states, draining battery. Find connections that do not pause when the app is backgrounded. Recommend Page Visibility API and lifecycle hooks to pause real-time activity when invisible.
prompt_short: Find real-time connections that drain mobile battery by staying active in background.
prompt_filtered: Mobile power impact only. Background connection pause and visibility-aware polling.

title: Review CRDT or operational transform strategy for collaborative editing
prompt: For collaborative real-time editing features, assess whether the conflict resolution strategy is correct under concurrent edits. Find implementations using last-write-wins that silently discard concurrent edits from other users. Assess whether CRDTs or operational transforms are used and whether the implementation handles network partition and merge correctly.
prompt_short: Audit collaborative editing conflict resolution for concurrent edit correctness.
prompt_filtered: Conflict resolution only. CRDT and OT correctness under concurrent edits.

title: Find real-time subscription authorization bypass paths
prompt: Verify that real-time channel subscriptions enforce authorization so a user can only subscribe to channels they have permission to read. Find pub/sub implementations where any authenticated user can subscribe to any channel by guessing the resource ID in the channel name. Recommend server-side subscription authorization with explicit permission checks.
prompt_short: Find real-time channels where any user can subscribe by guessing the channel ID.
prompt_filtered: Subscription authorization only. Guessable channel names and permission bypass.
