prompt: Audit Runbook Coverage

title: Check Escalation Paths
prompt: Check Escalation Paths

title: Draft War Room Protocol
prompt: Draft War Room Protocol

title: Audit Postmortem Quality
prompt: Audit Postmortem Quality

title: Find MTTR Regressions
prompt: Find MTTR Regressions

title: Map Communication Gaps
prompt: Map Communication Gaps

title: Review On-Call Load
prompt: Review On-Call Load

title: Identify all incident response runbooks and match them to the most likel
prompt: Identify all incident response runbooks and match them to the most likely failure scenarios for each production service. Find failure modes with no runbook. For each gap, outline the minimum runbook content — symptoms, diagnosis steps, and remediation — needed to reduce MTTR.

title: Audit the on-call rotation for coverage gaps
prompt: Audit the on-call rotation for coverage gaps. Identify services or time windows with no designated responder. Find runbooks that require knowledge held by a single engineer who is not always on-call. Recommend knowledge distribution changes that eliminate single-engineer dependencies.

title: Review the last three production incidents
prompt: Review the last three production incidents. For each, identify how long it took to detect, diagnose, and remediate. Find the specific tooling or process gaps that added the most time to each phase. Recommend targeted improvements to reduce total incident duration by 30%.

title: Design a game day exercise that tests the team's incident response capab
prompt: Design a game day exercise that tests the team's incident response capability under realistic conditions. Define the scenario as a silent data corruption affecting 5 percent of users, the injection method, and the success criteria. Specify observer roles distinct from responder roles. Define the metrics to capture including time to detection, time to classification, time to mitigation, and communication quality. Recommend the debrief structure to maximize learning.

title: Review all postmortem action items from the past six months
prompt: Review all postmortem action items from the past six months. Calculate the completion rate. Identify action items older than 45 days with no progress. Find items assigned to engineers who have since changed teams. Design a tracking process that assigns a due date to every action item, escalates overdue items to team leads, and reports completion rate as a reliability metric on a monthly basis.

title: Audit the incident classification process for consistency
prompt: Audit the incident classification process for consistency. Review the last 20 incidents and assess whether the severity classification was correct in hindsight, whether P1 incidents were truly business-critical or over-escalated, and whether any P3 incidents caused user impact warranting a higher classification. Identify the classification criteria that caused the most ambiguity. Recommend revised criteria with concrete, objective thresholds for each severity level.

title: Assess the effectiveness of external incident communication over the pas
prompt: Assess the effectiveness of external incident communication over the past 90 days. Review all status page updates for timeliness, whether the first external communication appeared within the defined SLA for each severity level. Identify incidents where user-facing impact was visible for more than 15 minutes before a status page update appeared. Review the language quality of past communications and whether technical jargon was avoided. Recommend communication process improvements.

title: Design a metrics-driven incident response improvement program
prompt: Design a metrics-driven incident response improvement program. Define the baseline for three core metrics: MTTD, MTTR, and repeat incident rate. Set 90-day improvement targets for each. Identify the top three process or tooling changes most likely to move each metric. Assign an owner to each improvement initiative. Define the measurement methodology to track progress and the review cadence to assess whether targets are on track.

title: Evaluate incident response readiness across all production services
prompt: Evaluate incident response readiness across all production services. Identify whether every service has a designated on-call owner and a documented escalation path. Find services where an incident would require contacting a single engineer with no backup. Assess whether runbooks exist for the top five most likely failure scenarios for each service, and whether those runbooks have been tested in a drill. Review the incident communication process — whether stakeholders are notified appropriately and whether status updates follow a consistent cadence. Assess post-incident review practices — whether every significant incident produces a blameless post-mortem with action items tracked to completion. Summarize the five incident response gaps with the highest MTTR impact.

title: Assess the tooling available to engineers during an incident
prompt: Assess the tooling available to engineers during an incident. Determine whether engineers have read-only access to production data for debugging without requiring a deployment or an escalation. Find any diagnostic action that requires production access beyond what on-call engineers normally have. Assess whether runbooks can be executed autonomously or require approvals that add delay during incidents. Check whether incident response tooling remains available during partial infrastructure failures — that dashboards, runbooks, and communication tools are not hosted on the same infrastructure that is failing. Recommend the tooling and access improvements that would most reduce the friction engineers face during the first 15 minutes of a major incident.

title: Conduct a comprehensive review of the incident response process from the
prompt: Conduct a comprehensive review of the incident response process from the moment an alert fires to the moment the postmortem action items are closed. Assess the end-to-end workflow for a high-severity incident: how the on-call engineer is notified, how the severity is classified, how additional responders are engaged, how the incident commander role is established and transferred during long incidents, how external customers are notified, how the incident is declared resolved, and how learnings are captured and acted upon. Identify the longest delays in this workflow, whether they occur during the detection phase due to slow alerting, during the triage phase due to unclear runbooks, during the resolution phase due to insufficient tooling access, or during the postmortem phase due to a lack of structured review process. Evaluate whether the on-call engineer has everything they need to respond effectively without relying on institutional knowledge held by a small group of senior engineers. Recommend the incident response workflow improvements that would most reduce mean time to resolution.

title: Evaluate the organizational health of the on-call function and its long-
prompt: Evaluate the organizational health of the on-call function and its long-term sustainability. Assess the current on-call load for each rotation, the average number of pages per shift, the fraction of pages occurring outside business hours, and the number of engineers sharing each rotation. Identify rotations where the per-engineer page rate is high enough to cause fatigue and erode the quality of incident response over time. Review whether on-call compensation and recognition practices reflect the burden appropriately. Assess whether there is a structured on-call onboarding process that prepares engineers for their first shift before they are the sole responder for a production incident. Evaluate whether the team reviews on-call load metrics and takes corrective action when load exceeds sustainable levels. Recommend the on-call sustainability improvements that would reduce burnout risk and maintain high-quality incident response over the long term.

title: Review the knowledge management practices that support effective inciden
prompt: Review the knowledge management practices that support effective incident response. Assess whether runbooks are stored in a location that is accessible during a production incident, not only on the internal wiki that may be unavailable if the infrastructure it runs on is affected by the incident. Identify runbooks that are out of date, referencing services that no longer exist, commands that have changed, or contacts who have left the team. Evaluate whether runbooks are written at the appropriate level of detail for an on-call engineer who is not an expert in the specific service, providing enough context to follow the steps without assuming specialized knowledge. Review whether incident response knowledge is concentrated in a small group of experienced engineers who are not always on-call, creating a bus factor risk. Assess whether there is a structured process for capturing and sharing incident response knowledge through game days, shadowing programs, and postmortem reviews. Recommend the knowledge management improvements that would make incident response capability resilient to personnel changes.

title: Define RTO and RPO targets for each service
prompt: For every production service, define the Recovery Time Objective (how long until back online) and Recovery Point Objective (how much data loss is acceptable). Verify the current architecture can actually meet these targets.
prompt_short: Define and verify RTO/RPO targets for every production service.
prompt_filtered: RTO/RPO definition only. No recovery procedure analysis.

title: Document the full service restoration order after outage
prompt: Create a service dependency graph for disaster recovery. Define the order services must be restored in — from foundational (auth, database) to dependent (application, frontend). Identify any service that cannot start until N others are healthy.
prompt_short: Document the correct order to restore all services after a full outage.
prompt_filtered: Restoration order only. Dependency-based startup sequence.

title: Audit DR plan for untested assumptions
prompt: Review the current DR plan for steps that have never been tested in a real or simulated disaster. Identify assumptions about recovery speed, data integrity, and service behavior that might not hold under real failure conditions.
prompt_short: Find untested assumptions in the disaster recovery plan.
prompt_filtered: Untested assumptions only. DR plan validation gaps.

title: Assess geographic redundancy and failover capability
prompt: Evaluate whether the system can survive a full data center or regional failure. Identify single-region dependencies, data stores with no cross-region replication, and traffic routing that cannot failover automatically.
prompt_short: Assess whether the system survives a full regional outage.
prompt_filtered: Geographic redundancy only. Single-region SPOFs and replication.

title: Schedule and run a disaster recovery drill
prompt: Design a DR drill scenario appropriate for this system. Define the failure scenario, success criteria, which teams need to participate, and how to measure RTO and RPO actually achieved vs target. Schedule it for a low-traffic period.
prompt_short: Design a DR drill scenario with success criteria and measurement plan.
prompt_filtered: Drill design only. Scenario, success criteria, and measurement approach.

title: Design active-active multi-region architecture for zero-RPO
prompt: Assess the feasibility of active-active multi-region deployment for this system. Identify components that require consensus across regions (distributed transactions, unique constraint enforcement), estimate latency penalties, and recommend which services to run active-active vs active-passive.
prompt_short: Assess feasibility and design for active-active multi-region deployment.
prompt_filtered: Active-active design only. Cross-region consensus and latency trade-offs.

title: Define and test regional failover automation
prompt: Design automated regional failover: DNS failover health check configuration, database promotion from replica to primary, traffic rerouting, and the validation steps run before declaring the failover complete. Measure automated failover time vs the RTO target.
prompt_short: Design automated regional failover and measure failover time vs RTO.
prompt_filtered: Failover automation only. DNS, DB promotion, traffic rerouting, and validation.

title: Audit replication lag monitoring for cross-region replicas
prompt: Verify replication lag is monitored and alerted for all cross-region database replicas. Identify the maximum observed lag in the past 30 days and assess whether this violates the RPO target. Design an alert that fires when lag exceeds 50% of the RPO window.
prompt_short: Verify replication lag monitoring and alert when lag approaches RPO limit.
prompt_filtered: Replication lag only. Monitoring, alert thresholds, and RPO compliance.

title: Assess data consistency guarantees after a regional failover
prompt: Identify the consistency risks introduced by a regional failover: uncommitted transactions at the moment of failure, replication lag at failover time, and application behavior when serving from a stale replica. Design compensating controls for each risk.
prompt_short: Identify data consistency risks introduced by regional failover and mitigate them.
prompt_filtered: Failover consistency only. Transaction loss, lag, and stale read risks.

title: Review DR documentation freshness and accuracy
prompt: Audit DR documentation for freshness. Identify runbooks that describe services or infrastructure that no longer exists, missing steps added since the last update, and incorrect contact information. Assign a documentation owner and a quarterly review cadence.
prompt_short: Find stale DR documentation describing outdated services or procedures.
prompt_filtered: DR documentation only. Accuracy, freshness, and review cadence.

title: Design a tabletop exercise for the executive DR decision tree
prompt: Design a tabletop exercise that walks executives through the decision points in a DR scenario: when to declare a disaster, who approves regional failover, how to communicate with regulators, and when to invoke business continuity plans. Focus on decision clarity, not technical steps.
prompt_short: Design a tabletop exercise for executive decision points during a disaster.
prompt_filtered: Executive DR exercise only. Declaration, approval, and communication decisions.

title: Assess third-party service continuity during a disaster
prompt: For each critical third-party service, review their SLA for DR scenarios: uptime guarantee during a regional AWS outage, data export capabilities, and whether a competitor's service could be substituted within the RTO window. Identify third-party SPOFs with no DR contingency.
prompt_short: Find third-party services with no DR contingency if they go down during a disaster.
prompt_filtered: Third-party DR only. Substitutability and SLA coverage during regional failures.

title: Design cold start recovery procedure from zero infrastructure
prompt: Document the recovery procedure to rebuild all infrastructure from zero using infrastructure-as-code — assuming all cloud resources in the primary region are destroyed. Estimate the time to reach minimum viable production, and identify manual steps not yet automated.
prompt_short: Document and time the recovery from zero using IaC and identify manual gaps.
prompt_filtered: Cold start recovery only. IaC-driven rebuild time and manual step gaps.

title: Validate backup-to-restore chain for each data store
prompt: For each production data store, validate the full backup-to-restore chain: confirm the backup is readable, the restore procedure is documented step-by-step, and a recent restore drill confirmed data integrity. Flag any data store where the chain has not been validated end-to-end.
prompt_short: Validate the full backup-to-restore chain for every production data store.
prompt_filtered: Restore chain only. Readability, procedure completeness, and integrity verification.

title: Map service dependencies for sequenced DR recovery
prompt: Build a full dependency graph of all production services. Use it to generate a sequenced recovery order for DR: Layer 0 (auth, database), Layer 1 (core APIs), Layer 2 (application services), Layer 3 (user-facing). Identify cycles or circular dependencies that complicate sequencing.
prompt_short: Generate a layered DR recovery sequence from the full service dependency graph.
prompt_filtered: Recovery sequencing only. Dependency layers and circular dependency conflicts.

title: Assess DNS TTL configuration for fast failover
prompt: Review DNS TTL values for all public-facing endpoints. High TTLs (>300s) slow regional failover because clients cache stale DNS for minutes after a cutover. Identify endpoints with TTLs too high to meet the RTO target and recommend a pre-failure TTL reduction strategy.
prompt_short: Find DNS TTLs too high to support RTO targets during regional failover.
prompt_filtered: DNS TTL only. Failover impact and pre-failure reduction strategy.

title: Define maximum tolerable downtime per service
prompt: For each service, define the Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD) — beyond which the business impact is unacceptable regardless of recovery effort. Compare MTD to the current RTO estimate. Services where MTD < RTO represent critical DR gaps requiring immediate investment.
prompt_short: Find services where Maximum Tolerable Downtime is less than the current RTO.
prompt_filtered: MTD vs RTO only. Gap identification per service.

title: Review infrastructure-as-code coverage for DR reproducibility
prompt: Inventory all production infrastructure. Identify components provisioned manually or via clickops with no IaC representation — these cannot be reliably recreated during a DR event. Estimate the IaC coverage percentage and prioritize manual infrastructure for Terraform or CDK adoption.
prompt_short: Find manually provisioned infrastructure that cannot be recreated during DR.
prompt_filtered: IaC coverage only. Manual resources blocking reproducible DR.

title: Assess compliance requirements for DR testing frequency
prompt: Identify regulatory frameworks applicable to this system (SOC2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001). For each, determine the required DR test frequency and documentation requirements. Compare against the actual test schedule and identify compliance gaps in DR drill frequency.
prompt_short: Find DR test frequency gaps against regulatory compliance requirements.
prompt_filtered: Compliance DR frequency only. Regulatory requirements vs actual test schedule.

title: Design DR communication plan for extended outages
prompt: Design the communication plan for an extended DR scenario lasting 4+ hours: customer-facing status page cadence, internal stakeholder updates, regulatory notification requirements, and media response strategy. Define who owns each communication stream and their escalation path.
prompt_short: Design a communication plan covering all stakeholders for a 4-hour+ DR scenario.
prompt_filtered: DR communication only. Customer, internal, regulatory, and media streams.

title: Identify DR Knowledge Silos
prompt: Identify DR Knowledge Silos

title: Identify all services with no documented RTO and RPO
prompt: Identify all services with no documented RTO and RPO. For each, estimate the business cost of one hour of downtime and one hour of data loss. Use that estimate to recommend appropriate RTO and RPO targets that balance recovery investment against business risk.

title: Find all disaster scenarios — region failure, data corruption, ransomwar
prompt: Find all disaster scenarios — region failure, data corruption, ransomware — with no tested recovery procedure. For each scenario, outline the minimum recovery steps and estimate the time to recover with current tooling. Identify the preparation gaps that would most reduce recovery time.

title: Audit geographic redundancy across all production data stores
prompt: Audit geographic redundancy across all production data stores. Identify databases and file stores with no off-site replica. For each, calculate the maximum data loss that would occur in a primary region failure and compare against the defined RPO.

title: Identify all stateful services that have no automated failover capabilit
prompt: Identify all stateful services that have no automated failover capability. For each, document the manual steps required to promote a standby and redirect traffic. Estimate the time required to execute the manual procedure by an on-call engineer unfamiliar with the specific service. Identify the steps where a mistake could cause data loss. Recommend automation that eliminates manual intervention from the critical path.

title: Assess recovery time estimation accuracy
prompt: Assess recovery time estimation accuracy. Identify the three most critical recovery scenarios: primary database failure, region outage, and ransomware affecting all encrypted data. For each, compare the documented RTO against the actual recovery time measured in the most recent drill or real event. Identify the gap between documented and actual recovery time. Recommend the specific procedure improvements that would close the gap.

title: Review the disaster recovery communication plan
prompt: Review the disaster recovery communication plan. Define who is notified when a DR event is declared, in what order, and through which channels. Identify stakeholders with no notification path including executives, key customers, and regulatory bodies. Design a communication timeline template specifying what information is communicated at 0 minutes, 30 minutes, 1 hour, and every subsequent hour until service is restored.

title: Audit the dependency chain for disaster recovery procedures
prompt: Audit the dependency chain for disaster recovery procedures. Identify tools and systems required to execute recovery that are themselves hosted on the infrastructure being recovered, such as a recovery runbook stored only in the internal wiki running on the affected infrastructure. Design the independence requirements for all recovery tooling to ensure they remain available during the scenarios they are designed to address.

title: Design a tabletop exercise simulating a complete primary region failure
prompt: Design a tabletop exercise simulating a complete primary region failure affecting all production services. Define the scenario timeline, the questions posed to participants at each stage, and the evaluation criteria. Identify gaps exposed by the exercise including missing runbooks, untested procedures, and undocumented dependencies. Assign owners to address each gap before the next exercise. Schedule the next tabletop within 90 days.

title: Review the disaster recovery strategy for all production systems
prompt: Review the disaster recovery strategy for all production systems. Identify every data store and stateful service, and assess whether a recovery procedure exists for each. Find services with no defined RTO or RPO — where recovery would proceed without a clear target. Assess whether recovery procedures have been tested end to end within the past 12 months, distinguishing between a tested restore and a procedure that exists only in documentation. Look for single-region deployments with no failover path. Review data backup coverage — whether all production data stores are backed up, at what frequency, and to what geographic location. Summarize the five disaster recovery gaps ordered by potential data loss and downtime impact.

title: Evaluate the recovery testing program
prompt: Evaluate the recovery testing program. Identify how frequently restore procedures are validated — whether actual restores are performed or only backup completion is verified. Find any production restore that has never been performed outside of an actual emergency. Assess whether recovery procedures can be executed by any on-call engineer or only by a small group with specialized knowledge. Review whether recovery time estimates are based on actual timed drills or optimistic assumptions. Identify tooling or access dependencies that would add delay during an actual disaster scenario. Recommend the specific changes to the recovery testing program that would give the team confidence in executing recovery within the defined RTO.

title: Conduct a thorough assessment of the disaster recovery program's complet
prompt: Conduct a thorough assessment of the disaster recovery program's completeness and confidence level. Identify every category of disaster that could cause a production outage, including infrastructure provider failure, data center power loss, network partition, data corruption, ransomware, accidental data deletion, and supply chain compromise of a critical dependency. For each category, assess whether a documented recovery procedure exists, whether the procedure has been executed end-to-end in a drill within the past 12 months, and whether any engineer on the current on-call rotation could execute the procedure without escalating to a specialist. Find categories with no tested recovery path, which represent the organization's blind spots where a real disaster would produce the highest uncertainty and delay. Assess whether recovery time objectives are based on actual measured drill results or optimistic estimates that have never been validated under realistic conditions. Evaluate whether recovery procedures are stored in a location accessible during the disaster they are designed to address, such as offline copies or a backup documentation system in a separate region. Recommend the disaster recovery program improvements that would most reduce recovery time uncertainty.

title: Evaluate the geographic distribution of backup data and its adequacy for
prompt: Evaluate the geographic distribution of backup data and its adequacy for recovery from a regional infrastructure failure. Identify every data store in production, including relational databases, document stores, object storage, message queues, and secrets managers, and assess whether data is replicated to a geographically separate location with sufficient frequency to meet the defined RPO. Calculate the maximum data loss window for each data store: if the primary region failed at the worst possible moment in the replication cycle, how much data would be unrecoverable. Identify data stores relying on snapshot-based backups with infrequent capture windows, where an RPO of one hour requires point-in-time recovery capability rather than daily snapshots. Assess whether replica data is tested for integrity independently of the primary, whether corrupted primary data would be replicated faithfully to the backup making both useless. Review the cost of geographic replication and whether the cost is justified by the RPO it achieves. Recommend the backup strategy changes that would meet defined RPOs at the lowest cost.

title: Assess the organizational and procedural aspects of disaster recovery re
prompt: Assess the organizational and procedural aspects of disaster recovery readiness that determine whether a technically sound DR plan translates into effective execution during an actual disaster. Identify whether DR responsibilities are clearly owned, whether specific individuals are assigned to each recovery procedure and understand their role before an event occurs. Evaluate whether the recovery team has the access, credentials, and tooling pre-provisioned to execute recovery without requesting access during the incident, recognizing that identity and access management systems may themselves be affected by the disaster. Review whether decision-making authority during a DR event is clearly defined, who can declare a disaster, who can approve a regional failover that may affect data consistency, and who communicates to customers and regulators. Assess whether the team has rehearsed working under the psychological pressure of an actual disaster, whether game days create sufficiently realistic stress to expose decision-making weaknesses. Evaluate whether lessons from past DR events and drills have been incorporated into updated procedures, or whether the DR documentation is a static artifact that diverges from current architecture over time.

title: Audit backup coverage for all production data stores
prompt: List every production data store — databases, object storage, configuration, secrets. For each, verify a backup is configured, the schedule meets the RPO, and backups are retained for the required duration.
prompt_short: Audit backup configuration and schedule for all production data stores.
prompt_filtered: Backup coverage only. Schedule and retention per data store.

title: Verify backup restoration has been tested recently
prompt: For each backup, check when the restoration process was last tested. Identify backups that exist but have never been restored, or were last restored more than 90 days ago. A backup that has never been tested is not a backup.
prompt_short: Find backups that exist but have never been tested with a restore drill.
prompt_filtered: Restore testing only. Last restoration date and untested backups.

title: Find data that is not backed up but should be
prompt: Survey all production data — not just primary databases but also: uploaded files in object storage, configuration in secret managers, email templates, feature flag state, and user-generated content. Identify what's missing from backup coverage.
prompt_short: Find production data sources with no backup configured.
prompt_filtered: Coverage gaps only. Unprotected data stores and artifacts.

title: Review backup encryption and access control
prompt: Verify that all backups are encrypted at rest and in transit. Audit who has access to backup storage — ensure that backup access is as restricted as production access. Identify backups accessible to more people than production data itself.
prompt_short: Verify backup encryption and audit who can access backup storage.
prompt_filtered: Backup security only. Encryption and access control.

title: Assess backup storage cost and retention optimization
prompt: Review backup retention policies against actual compliance and recovery requirements. Identify backups retained far longer than needed (wasting cost) and backups with no retention policy (growing indefinitely). Recommend tiered retention with cost estimates.
prompt_short: Audit backup retention for cost waste and compliance alignment.
prompt_filtered: Retention and cost only. Retention period vs requirement analysis.

title: Verify backup encryption key management and rotation
prompt: Verify that backup encryption keys are managed separately from backup data — stored in a dedicated key management service (KMS, Vault). Identify backups encrypted with keys that have never been rotated, and assess what happens to backup decryptability when a key is rotated.
prompt_short: Verify backup encryption keys are in a KMS and have rotation policies.
prompt_filtered: Backup key management only. KMS usage and rotation impact on decryptability.

title: Design immutable backup storage to prevent ransomware deletion
prompt: Assess whether backups are stored in a write-once medium that cannot be deleted by any compromised application or operator credential — S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode, Wasabi immutable buckets, or air-gapped tape. Identify backups deletable by a compromised account.
prompt_short: Find backups that can be deleted by a compromised account and design immutability.
prompt_filtered: Immutable storage only. Write-once controls and ransomware deletion prevention.

title: Audit restore time against RTO for the largest data stores
prompt: For each large data store, measure or estimate the restore time from backup: download time from storage, decompression time, restore time into the database engine. Compare the total to the service's RTO target. Identify data stores where restore time alone exceeds RTO.
prompt_short: Measure restore time for each data store and compare against RTO targets.
prompt_filtered: Restore time only. Duration estimate vs RTO compliance per data store.

title: Verify point-in-time recovery coverage for transactional databases
prompt: Confirm that transactional databases have point-in-time recovery (PITR) enabled — continuous WAL archiving or equivalent — allowing restore to any second within the retention window. Identify databases with only daily snapshots where a mid-day failure would lose hours of transactions.
prompt_short: Verify PITR is enabled for all transactional databases.
prompt_filtered: PITR coverage only. Continuous archiving vs snapshot-only backup gaps.

title: Test backup restoration integrity with automated checksums
prompt: Design an automated restore verification job that runs weekly: restores the latest backup to a scratch environment, runs a row count and checksum against expected values, and alerts if integrity verification fails. Identify data stores with no automated integrity check.
prompt_short: Design automated backup integrity verification with checksums and alerts.
prompt_filtered: Restore integrity only. Automated checksum verification and failure alerting.

title: Assess cross-account and cross-region backup replication
prompt: Verify that critical backups are replicated to a different AWS account and a different region from the primary. A backup in the same account can be deleted by a compromised root credential; a backup in the same region is lost in a regional failure. Identify backups failing either condition.
prompt_short: Find backups not replicated to a separate account and region.
prompt_filtered: Backup replication only. Cross-account and cross-region coverage.

title: Audit application-level backup for user-generated content
prompt: Identify all user-generated content stored outside the primary database — files in object storage, media in CDN origins, emails in third-party services. Verify each has an application-level backup beyond the provider's default redundancy, and that the backup is in your control.
prompt_short: Find user-generated content in third-party storage with no controlled backup.
prompt_filtered: UGC backup only. Application-level backups for user files and media.

title: Design backup monitoring dashboards for operations teams
prompt: Design a backup health dashboard showing: last successful backup time per store, backup size trend over time, restore test last run and outcome, and storage cost per day. Include alerts for: backup older than RPO window, backup size drop > 10% (data loss indicator), restore test failure.
prompt_short: Design a backup health dashboard with size trends and restore test status.
prompt_filtered: Backup monitoring only. Dashboard design and alerting thresholds.

title: Review backup access logging for compliance and breach detection
prompt: Verify that all access to backup storage is logged — who accessed what backup, when, from what IP. Identify backup stores with no access logging. Access logs serve both compliance purposes and as an early indicator of a ransomware actor staging a data exfiltration.
prompt_short: Verify all backup access is logged for compliance and exfiltration detection.
prompt_filtered: Backup access logs only. Who accessed, when, and from where.

title: Assess backup coverage for infrastructure configuration state
prompt: Verify that infrastructure configuration is backed up alongside data: Terraform state files, Kubernetes secrets, application configuration in parameter stores, TLS certificates. Identify configuration that must be manually recreated after a disaster because it has no backup.
prompt_short: Find infrastructure configuration with no backup that must be recreated manually.
prompt_filtered: Config backup only. Terraform state, K8s secrets, and parameter store coverage.

title: Define backup ownership and on-call responsibility
prompt: Verify every backup job has a named owner responsible for: monitoring success, responding to failures, executing restore drills, and updating the restore runbook. Identify backup jobs owned by a team alias with no individual accountability or backup jobs with no owner at all.
prompt_short: Find backup jobs with no named individual owner and assign accountability.
prompt_filtered: Backup ownership only. Named responsibility and on-call assignment.

title: Estimate backup storage growth and cost projection
prompt: Analyze backup storage size trends over the past 6 months. Project storage size and cost at 12 and 24 months assuming current growth rate. Identify backups growing super-linearly and recommend deduplication, compression, or tiered storage to control future cost.
prompt_short: Project backup storage cost at 12 and 24 months and find super-linear growers.
prompt_filtered: Backup cost projection only. Growth trends and cost control recommendations.

title: Verify backup schedule alignment with RPO requirements
prompt: For each data store, compare the backup schedule frequency against the service's RPO target. A store with a 1-hour RPO and daily backups has an implicit 23-hour maximum data loss window. Identify all stores where backup frequency alone violates the RPO without relying on PITR.
prompt_short: Find data stores where backup frequency violates the RPO without PITR.
prompt_filtered: Backup frequency vs RPO only. Schedule gap and data loss window analysis.

title: Assess secret and credential backup with secure access controls
prompt: Verify that secrets — API keys, database passwords, TLS private keys — are backed up in a dedicated secret manager with versioning (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Identify secrets only stored in environment variables or application config with no versioned backup.
prompt_short: Find secrets not backed up in a versioned secret manager.
prompt_filtered: Secret backup only. Versioned storage and recovery path for credentials.

title: Design a backup runbook for first-responder engineers
prompt: Write a backup restoration runbook for on-call engineers who are not backup experts: step-by-step restore procedure for each critical data store, where to find backup files, how to verify integrity after restore, and who to escalate to if a step fails. Keep each procedure under 10 steps.
prompt_short: Write a first-responder backup restoration runbook for on-call engineers.
prompt_filtered: Restore runbook only. Step-by-step procedures for non-specialist engineers.

prompt: Test Point-in-Time Recovery

title: Audit Retention Policy Gaps
prompt: Audit Retention Policy Gaps

title: Find Unprotected Data Stores
prompt: Find Unprotected Data Stores

title: Review Backup Encryption
prompt: Review Backup Encryption

title: Map Recovery Time by Service
prompt: Map Recovery Time by Service

title: Identify all production data stores and verify each has a tested backup
prompt: Identify all production data stores and verify each has a tested backup and restore procedure. For any store with an untested restore, outline the minimum validation test — what data to restore and how to verify correctness — that would confirm the backup is viable.

title: Audit backup retention against compliance and business requirements
prompt: Audit backup retention against compliance and business requirements. Find stores where backups are deleted before the compliance minimum retention period. Identify stores where backups are retained longer than needed, creating unnecessary cost and data liability.

title: Assess backup storage security
prompt: Assess backup storage security. Identify backups stored in the same location as the primary data — where a single compromise would destroy both. Find backups without encryption at rest. Recommend the minimum storage security changes needed to meet the 3-2-1 backup rule.

title: Audit backup completeness across all data stores
prompt: Audit backup completeness across all data stores. List every database, object store, and secret store in production. For each, verify a backup policy exists, the backup frequency meets the RPO, and the backup storage location is geographically separate from the primary. Identify data stores with no backup policy. For each gap, estimate the data loss in the worst-case failure scenario and recommend the minimum backup configuration.

title: Design a backup restore drill program
prompt: Design a backup restore drill program. Identify the three most critical data stores by recovery impact. For each, define the drill procedure: restore to an isolated environment, validate row count and data integrity checksums, and measure elapsed time. Schedule quarterly drills and define the success criteria. Identify who is responsible for executing each drill and what constitutes a drill failure requiring immediate remediation.

title: Assess backup encryption at rest and in transit
prompt: Assess backup encryption at rest and in transit. Identify backup storage locations where data is not encrypted at rest. Find backup transfer processes where data moves unencrypted between the production system and backup storage. Review key management for backup encryption, whether encryption keys are stored independently of the backup data they protect. Recommend the encryption changes needed to meet current security standards.

title: Evaluate backup monitoring and alerting
prompt: Evaluate backup monitoring and alerting. Identify backup jobs that run without verification of completion. Find backup jobs with no alert configured for failure or missed schedule. Assess whether backup size anomalies are detected, a backup significantly smaller than the previous one may indicate a partial backup or data loss event. Design a backup health dashboard showing completion rate, size trend, and last successful restore date per data store.

title: Review the backup strategy for application secrets and configuration
prompt: Review the backup strategy for application secrets and configuration. Identify secrets manager instances, certificate stores, and configuration databases that are not backed up. Assess the recovery path if the primary secrets manager is unavailable, whether the application can be started from a backup secrets store. Evaluate whether infrastructure-as-code configuration is version-controlled and can reconstruct the environment from scratch. Recommend the secrets and configuration backup strategy.

title: Audit backup coverage across all production data stores
prompt: Audit backup coverage across all production data stores. Inventory every database, file store, and configuration store, and verify that each has an automated backup configured. Assess backup frequency against the defined RPO — whether point-in-time recovery is possible within the RPO window. Check whether backups are stored in a different geographic region from the primary data, providing protection against a region-level failure. Find any backup process that has no alerting on failure, allowing silent backup gaps to accumulate undetected. Assess whether backups are encrypted at rest and whether encryption keys are stored separately from the backup data. Summarize the five backup coverage gaps ordered by data loss risk.

title: Evaluate the restore process for all backup types
prompt: Evaluate the restore process for all backup types. Determine whether restore procedures are documented with step-by-step instructions that any engineer can follow. Identify the last time each backup type was actually restored and validated for data integrity. Find restore procedures that require specialized tools or credentials held by a small number of people. Assess the restore time for the largest data store — whether it can be recovered within the defined RTO. Look for partial restore scenarios — restoring a single table or a specific time range — and whether the tooling supports granular restores. Recommend the improvements to the restore process that would most reduce recovery time and eliminate single-engineer dependencies.

title: Perform a comprehensive assessment of backup reliability and restore cap
prompt: Perform a comprehensive assessment of backup reliability and restore capability across all production data stores. Identify every system that stores state, including relational databases, document stores, object storage buckets, search indexes, message queue offsets, secrets managers, and infrastructure configuration, and verify that each has a backup policy with a defined frequency, retention period, and geographic destination. Assess the backup frequency against the defined RPO for each system: if the RPO is one hour but backups run daily, there is a 23-hour data loss window on any given failure. Evaluate whether continuous data protection or point-in-time recovery capability exists for the most critical databases, or whether the organization relies on snapshot-based backups that capture state at fixed intervals. Review the restore procedure for each data store, whether the restore process has been documented, whether it has been tested in an isolated environment distinct from production, and whether the documented restore time matches actual measured restore durations. Identify backup jobs that are configured but not monitored, where a backup failure goes undetected until a restore is attempted during a crisis. Recommend the backup reliability improvements that would give the team confidence in restoring from any backup within the defined RTO.

title: Evaluate the compliance and regulatory alignment of the backup strategy
prompt: Evaluate the compliance and regulatory alignment of the backup strategy. Identify which regulatory frameworks apply to the organization's data, including GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and SOC 2, and the specific backup retention requirements each imposes. Assess whether backup retention periods are calibrated to meet the minimum retention required by each framework without retaining data longer than necessary, which increases storage cost and creates privacy risk for personal data subject to deletion requirements. Review whether backups containing personal data are subject to the same data subject access and erasure requests as primary data, and whether the organization has a technical capability to delete a specific user's data from backup archives within the required time window. Evaluate whether the backup encryption key management practices meet the requirements of applicable security frameworks. Assess whether backup access is logged and audited, whether the organization can demonstrate to an auditor that backup data was accessed only by authorized personnel for authorized purposes. Recommend the backup compliance improvements that would address the most significant regulatory gaps.

title: Assess the operational maturity of the backup and restore program and it
prompt: Assess the operational maturity of the backup and restore program and its integration into routine engineering practices. Evaluate whether backup configuration is managed as infrastructure-as-code alongside application configuration, or whether backup policies are configured manually through management consoles and therefore at risk of configuration drift. Review whether new services and data stores are enrolled in the backup program automatically as part of the provisioning process, or whether backup configuration is an afterthought requiring a separate request. Assess whether the engineering team has sufficient tooling to perform self-service restores for common scenarios, including restoring a single accidentally deleted record, restoring a table to a point-in-time before a bad migration, or restoring a test environment from a production backup, without requiring a specialized operator. Evaluate whether the cost of backup storage is reviewed periodically and whether retention periods are adjusted as data volumes grow. Identify backup procedures that have not been updated to reflect recent infrastructure changes including new database versions, new storage services, or new encryption requirements. Recommend the process improvements that would make backup management a low-friction, continuously maintained capability.

title: Audit error handling for swallowed exceptions
prompt: Find catch blocks that swallow exceptions without logging or re-throwing. Identify error paths that silently return empty results, default values, or success responses when an error actually occurred. These hide bugs from developers and monitoring.
prompt_short: Find all catch blocks that swallow errors without logging.
prompt_filtered: Swallowed errors only. Silent catch blocks and empty returns on failure.

title: Review user-facing error messages for clarity
prompt: Audit all error messages that reach end users. Identify messages that are too technical (stack traces, internal error codes), too vague ("Something went wrong"), or reveal internal implementation details. Recommend user-friendly messages for each.
prompt_short: Audit user-facing error messages for clarity and appropriate detail level.
prompt_filtered: User-facing errors only. Message quality and information disclosure.

title: Find inconsistent error response formats across the API
prompt: Compare error response shapes across all API endpoints. Identify inconsistencies in status codes, error body structure, error code conventions, and field names. Recommend a single canonical error response format.
prompt_short: Find API endpoints returning error responses in inconsistent formats.
prompt_filtered: Error format consistency only. Response shape and field naming.

title: Identify errors that should trigger alerts but don't
prompt: Review the error types generated by the system. Identify categories of errors that indicate real problems but are not connected to any alert — payment failures, auth errors above a threshold, repeated 500s, data integrity violations.
prompt_short: Find error types that should trigger on-call alerts but currently don't.
prompt_filtered: Alert coverage only. Error types without monitoring connection.

title: Check error handling in background and async code
prompt: Assess error handling in background jobs, event handlers, and async operations. Identify code paths where errors are thrown but no caller is listening — unhandled promise rejections, uncaught async exceptions, and fire-and-forget calls with no error handling.
prompt_short: Find unhandled errors in async code, background jobs, and event handlers.
prompt_filtered: Async error handling only. Unhandled rejections and background job failures.

title: Design an error taxonomy for structured error classification
prompt: Design a hierarchical error taxonomy for this codebase: error domain (auth, payment, data, network), error kind (validation, not_found, permission, timeout, internal), and severity (fatal, recoverable, informational). Define how each combination maps to HTTP status code, log level, and alert threshold.
prompt_short: Design a hierarchical error taxonomy mapping domain, kind, and severity to responses.
prompt_filtered: Error taxonomy only. Classification hierarchy and response mapping.

title: Audit fail-fast vs fail-safe decisions per operation type
prompt: Review error handling decisions for each operation type. Data mutations that fail should fail-fast to prevent partial writes. Read operations may fail-safe with default values. Identify mismatches: read operations that throw hard errors, and mutations that silently return defaults on failure.
prompt_short: Find mismatches between fail-fast and fail-safe behavior per operation type.
prompt_filtered: Fail-fast vs fail-safe only. Decision appropriateness per operation.

title: Find error messages leaking internal implementation details
prompt: Scan all error messages returned to external callers — API responses, web page errors, email notifications. Identify messages that expose: stack traces, internal service names, database schema details, file paths, or error codes that reveal implementation. Each is an IDOR and fingerprinting risk.
prompt_short: Find API error messages that expose internal stack traces or service details.
prompt_filtered: Error message leakage only. Internal detail exposure in external responses.

title: Assess error propagation in multi-step transaction flows
prompt: Trace error propagation through multi-step flows — order creation, payment, fulfillment. Identify steps where an error in a downstream step leaves upstream state committed but inconsistent. Recommend compensating transactions or saga pattern for each affected flow.
prompt_short: Find multi-step flows where downstream errors leave upstream state inconsistent.
prompt_filtered: Transaction error propagation only. Inconsistent state from partial failure.

title: Standardize retry behavior for idempotent vs non-idempotent calls
prompt: Audit retry configuration across all outbound calls. Verify retries are only applied to idempotent operations (GET, PUT with idempotency key, DELETE) and explicitly disabled for non-idempotent operations (POST without idempotency key). Find non-idempotent calls with automatic retry enabled.
prompt_short: Find non-idempotent calls with automatic retry that could cause duplicate actions.
prompt_filtered: Retry idempotency only. Non-idempotent operations with retry enabled.

title: Review error boundary coverage in the frontend application
prompt: Audit React error boundary placement in the component tree. Verify error boundaries wrap each major page section so one component crash does not blank the entire page. Identify routes with no error boundary — where a rendering error shows the user a white screen.
prompt_short: Find routes missing React error boundaries that show white screens on crash.
prompt_filtered: Frontend error boundaries only. Component tree crash containment.

title: Assess error deduplication to prevent alert flood from repeated errors
prompt: Identify error types that recur thousands of times per minute — a single bad deployment can generate millions of identical error events. Verify error tracking (Sentry, Datadog) is configured to deduplicate and fingerprint errors, and that alert thresholds fire once per error type, not per occurrence.
prompt_short: Verify error tracking deduplicates repeated errors to prevent alert floods.
prompt_filtered: Error deduplication only. Fingerprinting and per-type alert configuration.

title: Design structured error codes for machine-readable API errors
prompt: Design a structured error code scheme for the API: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE codes in a single canonical registry (AUTH_TOKEN_EXPIRED, PAYMENT_DECLINED, RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED). Clients can switch on codes without parsing message strings. Identify responses currently returning only human-readable messages.
prompt_short: Design a machine-readable error code registry and find responses missing them.
prompt_filtered: Error codes only. Code scheme design and API response gaps.

title: Audit exception handling in ORM and database layer
prompt: Review database access code for exception handling patterns. Identify code that catches generic database exceptions and returns 500 when the actual error is a unique constraint violation (409), a not-found (404), or a connection timeout (503). Map database exceptions to correct HTTP status codes.
prompt_short: Find database exceptions incorrectly mapped to 500 instead of correct status codes.
prompt_filtered: Database exception mapping only. ORM error to HTTP status code correctness.

title: Implement structured error context for faster debugging
prompt: Identify the 10 most common error types in the error log. For each, assess whether the logged error includes enough context to reproduce and fix the issue without additional investigation — request ID, user ID, input values, system state. Add context to each error that lacks it.
prompt_short: Add structured context to the 10 most common errors to speed up debugging.
prompt_filtered: Error context only. Structured fields needed for reproduction and diagnosis.

title: Review SDK and library error handling for unexpected exceptions
prompt: Identify third-party SDKs used in the codebase. For each, review the exception types documented by the library and verify the calling code handles all documented failure modes — not just the happy path and a generic catch block. Identify unhandled SDK exception types.
prompt_short: Find third-party SDK calls missing handlers for documented exception types.
prompt_filtered: SDK error handling only. Documented exception types vs actual catch coverage.

title: Assess error recovery in distributed message processing
prompt: Review message consumer error handling. Verify failed messages are sent to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) rather than dropped or infinitely retried. Assess DLQ monitoring — how long before a DLQ message is investigated — and the process for replaying or discarding DLQ messages.
prompt_short: Verify failed messages route to a DLQ with monitoring and a replay process.
prompt_filtered: DLQ strategy only. Consumer error routing, monitoring, and replay.

title: Define error handling conventions for the engineering team
prompt: Write engineering-wide error handling conventions: when to throw vs return error type, when to retry vs fail immediately, mandatory context fields on all errors, prohibition on empty catch blocks, and the process for adding a new error code to the registry. Include annotated codebase examples.
prompt_short: Write error handling conventions with prohibited patterns and codebase examples.
prompt_filtered: Error conventions only. Team-wide rules and anti-patterns.

title: Audit third-party API error handling for partial failure modes
prompt: Review integrations with external APIs that can return partial success — bulk operations returning mixed success/failure arrays, batch inserts acknowledging some records but rejecting others. Identify integrations that treat a partial success response as a complete success.
prompt_short: Find integrations treating partial API success responses as complete success.
prompt_filtered: Partial failure handling only. Bulk operation mixed-result processing.

title: Validate error handling under network partition scenarios
prompt: Simulate network partitions between services using traffic shaping or chaos engineering tools. Verify services handle: complete connection refusal, slow response timeout, intermittent packet loss, and DNS failure. Identify services that hang indefinitely instead of failing within the configured timeout.
prompt_short: Test error handling under network partition and timeout scenarios per service.
prompt_filtered: Network partition errors only. Connection failure, timeout, and DNS error handling.

prompt: Find Swallowed Exceptions

title: Map User-Facing Error Messages
prompt: Map User-Facing Error Messages

title: Identify Missing Error Boundaries
prompt: Identify Missing Error Boundaries

title: Find all error handling paths that catch exceptions but return a generic
prompt: Find all error handling paths that catch exceptions but return a generic 500 response with no distinguishing information. For each, identify what the caller loses by not receiving a specific error code. Recommend the structured error response format that enables callers to handle each failure type correctly.

title: Audit error propagation across service boundaries
prompt: Audit error propagation across service boundaries. Find cases where a downstream service error is wrapped and re-thrown in a way that loses the original error type and message. For each, recommend the propagation strategy that preserves enough context for end-to-end debugging.

title: Identify all unhandled promise rejections and uncaught exceptions in the
prompt: Identify all unhandled promise rejections and uncaught exceptions in the codebase. For each, describe the runtime behavior — crash, silent failure, or hung request — and recommend the error boundary that would catch it gracefully.

title: Identify all catch blocks in the codebase that swallow exceptions withou
prompt: Identify all catch blocks in the codebase that swallow exceptions without logging. For each, describe the failure scenario that would become completely invisible to the team. Recommend the minimum handling: a structured log entry with context, a metric increment, or re-throw as a domain-specific error. Prioritize by the potential user impact of the silenced failure.

title: Design a consistent API error response format for all endpoints
prompt: Design a consistent API error response format for all endpoints. Define the required fields including error code in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE, human-readable message, optional details object, and timestamp. Audit the current API responses for format inconsistencies, endpoints returning plain strings, HTML error pages, or inconsistent field names. Recommend the migration plan to enforce the canonical format.

title: Review error handling for all third-party API integrations
prompt: Review error handling for all third-party API integrations. Identify integrations that fail without retry on transient errors. Find integrations that propagate raw third-party error messages to users, exposing internal system information. Assess whether circuit breakers exist for integrations that fail frequently. Recommend the error handling improvements for the three most user-impacting integrations.

title: Audit error rate trending across all services
prompt: Audit error rate trending across all services. Identify services where the error rate has increased over the past 30 days without a corresponding incident. Find error types that are increasing in frequency, suggesting an underlying issue not yet manifesting as user complaints. Recommend the investigation priority order for trending error types based on user impact potential.

title: Design error boundary placement for all critical user flows
prompt: Design error boundary placement for all critical user flows. Identify flows where an error in a non-critical subsystem such as a recommendation engine, analytics, or notification service crashes the entire flow rather than degrading gracefully. For each, design the error boundary that catches the subsystem failure, logs it, and returns a partial success response. Estimate the reduction in user-facing errors from implementing each boundary.

title: Review error handling design across the entire system
prompt: Review error handling design across the entire system. Identify every layer — API, service, data access — and assess whether errors are caught, classified, and returned in a consistent structured format. Find locations where bare try-catch blocks swallow exceptions without logging or re-throwing, creating invisible failure paths. Assess whether error responses distinguish between client errors — 4xx — and server errors — 5xx — with actionable codes that callers can handle programmatically. Look for error messages that leak internal implementation details — stack traces, SQL query text, internal paths — that could assist an attacker. Check whether errors from downstream dependencies are wrapped with context before propagation. Summarize the five error handling gaps that most impede debugging and caller error recovery.

title: Evaluate error handling consistency at API boundaries
prompt: Evaluate error handling consistency at API boundaries. Verify that every API endpoint returns errors in the same structure — with an error code, a human-readable message, and optional details. Find endpoints that return HTTP 200 with an error flag in the body, breaking standard HTTP error detection. Identify error codes that are ambiguous — used for multiple distinct failure conditions — making automated error handling by callers unreliable. Assess whether validation errors return specific field-level details or only a generic message that forces the user to guess which field failed. Review error logging at API boundaries — whether every 5xx response logs the full error context server-side. Recommend the error handling standardization changes that would most improve debuggability and API consumer reliability.

title: Conduct a comprehensive audit of error handling practices across the ent
prompt: Conduct a comprehensive audit of error handling practices across the entire codebase with a focus on the correctness, consistency, and user impact of error responses. Begin by categorizing error handling into four tiers: errors that are caught and handled correctly with appropriate user feedback and internal logging, errors that are caught but handled silently without logging or user notification, errors that propagate unhandled to a generic top-level handler that loses context, and errors that crash the process entirely. For each tier, identify the services and code paths that fall into it. Assess whether the error taxonomy, the set of error types used across the system, is well-defined and consistently applied, or whether error types are created ad-hoc leading to inconsistent handling behavior. Evaluate whether error messages surfaced to users are helpful without being dangerously informative, avoiding the exposure of stack traces, internal service names, database schemas, or system paths that could assist an attacker. Review whether errors that require human attention are routed to monitoring and alerting with sufficient context including user ID, request parameters, environment, and stack trace to diagnose the root cause without reproducing the issue. Identify the error handling anti-patterns, swallowed exceptions, generic catch-all handlers, and missing error boundaries, that most frequently contribute to user-visible failures that could have been handled gracefully.

title: Evaluate the design of error recovery mechanisms across all production s
prompt: Evaluate the design of error recovery mechanisms across all production services. Assess whether the system implements the principle of failing fast for unrecoverable errors, returning an error response immediately rather than waiting for a timeout when a precondition is violated. Identify operations that retry indefinitely without a maximum retry count, potentially blocking resources and delaying error propagation to the caller. Review whether retry logic distinguishes between transient errors such as network timeout and temporary unavailability, and permanent errors such as invalid input and authorization failure, retrying only the former. Assess whether the system implements deadline propagation: when an incoming request has a deadline, whether all downstream calls inherit a proportionally shorter deadline so that a slow dependency does not cause the caller to wait past the point where a useful response can be returned. Evaluate whether error responses include appropriate HTTP status codes that allow clients to implement correct retry behavior, distinguishing 429 Too Many Requests from 503 Service Unavailable, and 400 Bad Request from 422 Unprocessable Entity. Recommend the error recovery improvements that would most reduce unnecessary retries, reduce resource consumption under error conditions, and improve the client's ability to respond intelligently to errors.

title: Review the observability of error conditions and the effectiveness of er
prompt: Review the observability of error conditions and the effectiveness of error information in accelerating incident diagnosis. Assess whether every error log entry includes sufficient context for an engineer to diagnose the root cause without access to the affected user, capturing the request parameters, the user's session state, the component that failed, the error message, and the full stack trace. Evaluate whether errors are classified and tagged consistently, whether the error type, the affected service, and the user impact level are structured fields that enable aggregation and trend analysis. Review whether error rates are tracked as SLI metrics and whether SLO error budget consumption is calculated from production error rates. Identify errors that occur in production but produce no log entry, gaps in error observability that make certain failure modes completely invisible to the team. Assess whether error dashboards enable the team to distinguish between a spike in a known low-severity error class and the emergence of a new high-severity error type. Recommend the error observability improvements that would most reduce the time from error occurrence to team awareness and root cause identification.

title: Find the root cause of this production bug
prompt: Analyze the provided error, stack trace, and symptoms. Trace the issue from the user-visible symptom back to the root cause. List every hypothesis, eliminate them systematically, and provide a concrete fix with a test to verify it.
prompt_short: Trace this bug from symptom to root cause and provide a verified fix.
prompt_filtered: Root cause only. Systematic elimination of hypotheses.

title: Create a debugging guide for common failure patterns
prompt: Based on this codebase's stack and architecture, write a debugging guide covering the most common failure patterns — auth failures, DB connection issues, queue stalls, memory leaks. For each, provide the first 3 commands to run during an incident.
prompt_short: Write a debugging guide for the 5 most common failures in this stack.
prompt_filtered: Debugging guide only. Common patterns and first-response commands.

title: Add structured context to improve debugging speed
prompt: Identify the 10 places in the code where adding structured context (request ID, user ID, operation type, timing) to logs would most significantly reduce debugging time during incidents. Provide the specific log statement changes for each.
prompt_short: Find where adding structured log context would most speed up debugging.
prompt_filtered: Log context only. High-value structured data additions.

title: Reproduce this intermittent bug reliably
prompt: Analyze the conditions under which this bug occurs. Identify timing dependencies, environment-specific conditions, concurrency requirements, or data state preconditions. Design a minimal reproduction case that triggers the bug reliably.
prompt_short: Design a minimal, reliable reproduction case for this intermittent bug.
prompt_filtered: Reproduction only. Conditions and minimal test case design.

title: Trace data flow to find where corruption occurs
prompt: Given corrupted or unexpected data in the system, trace its full lifecycle — where it enters, where it's transformed, where it's stored, and where it's read. Identify the exact point where the value diverges from expectation.
prompt_short: Trace data through the system to find where corruption is introduced.
prompt_filtered: Data flow only. Entry point to corruption point tracing.

title: Apply hypothesis-driven debugging to eliminate root cause candidates
prompt: Given the observed symptoms, generate an ordered list of root cause hypotheses from most to least likely. For each, define a falsifying test that can be run in under 5 minutes. Work through the list systematically, eliminating hypotheses until only one remains.
prompt_short: Generate falsifiable hypotheses ordered by likelihood and test each systematically.
prompt_filtered: Hypothesis testing only. Ordered candidates and falsifying tests.

title: Use distributed tracing for time-travel debugging of past failures
prompt: Use the trace data from the time of the incident for time-travel debugging. Identify the span where latency first diverges from baseline, the error that first appeared in the trace tree, and the service boundary where the failure crossed. Reconstruct the exact request that triggered the issue.
prompt_short: Use trace data from the incident to reconstruct the failing request timeline.
prompt_filtered: Time-travel tracing only. Span anomaly and failure origin from historical traces.

title: Build a minimal reproduction package for a distributed bug
prompt: Design a minimal reproduction package for a bug that only occurs in a distributed environment: Docker Compose setup, minimal service configurations, seed data, and a single script that triggers the bug reliably. Document the package so any engineer can reproduce the bug within 10 minutes.
prompt_short: Build a Docker Compose reproduction package for a bug requiring multiple services.
prompt_filtered: Minimal reproduction only. Multi-service package for distributed bugs.

title: Identify race conditions through concurrent request log analysis
prompt: Analyze logs for interleaved request traces that share the same resource (user ID, order ID, session ID). Look for timing overlaps where two requests read the same state, make decisions, and write conflicting results — the signature of a read-modify-write race condition.
prompt_short: Find read-modify-write race conditions through interleaved request log analysis.
prompt_filtered: Race condition detection only. Concurrent request interleaving patterns.

title: Debug a memory leak using heap snapshot comparison
prompt: Describe the process to capture heap snapshots before and after a load test cycle for this runtime. Compare snapshots to find object types growing in count — these are the retained objects. Trace retained objects back to their allocation site and the retention path that prevents GC.
prompt_short: Capture and compare heap snapshots to find the leak source and retention path.
prompt_filtered: Memory leak only. Heap snapshot comparison and retention path tracing.

title: Correlate frontend errors with backend traces for full-stack debugging
prompt: Verify that frontend error tracking (Sentry, Datadog Browser SDK) captures the trace ID from the API response and links it to the backend distributed trace. Identify frontend error types that have no backend trace link — making the server-side cause uninvestigable from the error report.
prompt_short: Verify frontend errors link to backend traces via trace ID correlation.
prompt_filtered: Full-stack correlation only. Frontend error to backend trace linkage.

title: Design a feature flag for safe production debugging
prompt: Design a production debug mode feature flag: when enabled for a specific user or tenant, the service emits verbose DEBUG logs, adds timing headers to responses, and returns extended error details. All debug output is flagged with a marker so it can be filtered from normal dashboards.
prompt_short: Design a per-user debug mode flag enabling verbose logs in production safely.
prompt_filtered: Production debug flag only. Verbose logging without polluting normal telemetry.

title: Audit logging gaps in the authentication and session flow
prompt: Trace the authentication flow end-to-end: token issuance, validation, refresh, and revocation. Identify steps with no log entry, steps where the log entry lacks the token hash or session ID needed to correlate events, and steps where a failure produces only a generic error log.
prompt_short: Find logging gaps in the auth flow that make session-related bugs undebuggable.
prompt_filtered: Auth flow logging only. Session event coverage and correlation fields.

title: Find the debugging blind spot revealed by the last three incidents
prompt: Review the last three incident postmortems. For each, identify the specific telemetry that was missing and had to be added after the incident. Use this pattern to proactively identify other areas of the system with the same telemetry gap before the next incident surfaces it.
prompt_short: Find the recurring telemetry gap revealed by the last three incidents.
prompt_filtered: Incident-derived gaps only. Telemetry added post-incident as a gap pattern.

title: Use canary comparison to isolate environment-specific bugs
prompt: For bugs that only occur in production and not staging, design a systematic comparison of the two environments: infrastructure config, dependency versions, traffic patterns, data volume, feature flags, and secret values (masked). Produce a diff of all differences as the bug isolation strategy.
prompt_short: Systematically diff production vs staging to isolate environment-specific bugs.
prompt_filtered: Environment comparison only. Config, version, and flag diffs for bug isolation.

title: Instrument a function for performance profiling without code change
prompt: Describe the dynamic instrumentation options available for this runtime — eBPF probes, Node.js async_hooks, Python sys.settrace, Java byte-code instrumentation — that allow adding timing measurements to a function in production without deploying a code change.
prompt_short: Identify dynamic instrumentation options for production profiling without deploys.
prompt_filtered: Dynamic instrumentation only. Runtime-specific no-redeploy profiling options.

title: Analyze thread dump or goroutine dump for deadlock detection
prompt: Describe how to capture a thread dump (JVM), goroutine dump (Go), or async task dump (Node.js) for this runtime. Analyze the dump to find threads/goroutines blocked waiting for locks held by other blocked threads — the signature of a deadlock or livelock.
prompt_short: Capture and analyze a runtime dump to find deadlocks or goroutine leaks.
prompt_filtered: Thread/goroutine dump only. Deadlock and livelock detection.

title: Create a debugging checklist for new engineers joining the team
prompt: Write a debugging onboarding checklist for this codebase: where to find logs, how to query traces, which dashboards to check first, the local debugging setup, how to run the service in debug mode, and the five commands most useful in a production incident.
prompt_short: Write a debugging onboarding checklist covering logs, traces, and incident commands.
prompt_filtered: Debugging onboarding only. New engineer setup and incident readiness.

title: Debug slow startup causing cascading restarts under load
prompt: Diagnose slow service startup causing cascading restarts: the service starts slowly, fails its readiness check, Kubernetes kills and restarts it, load goes to other instances, they get overloaded, their latency rises, and more restarts follow. Identify startup bottlenecks to break the cascade.
prompt_short: Diagnose slow service startup causing Kubernetes cascading restart loops.
prompt_filtered: Startup performance only. Readiness check delay and cascade prevention.

title: Establish a known-good baseline for regression comparison
prompt: Define a known-good performance baseline for the 10 most critical API endpoints: p50, p95, p99 latency, error rate, and throughput. Record the baseline in a persistent document and configure an automated regression check that compares each deployment against it.
prompt_short: Define a performance baseline for critical endpoints and automate regression checks.
prompt_filtered: Baseline definition only. p50/p95/p99 values and regression detection.

prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

prompt: Gain full understanding before completing
