prompt: Check Insight Documentation

title: Synthesize Interview Findings
prompt: Synthesize Interview Findings

title: Identify Unmet User Needs
prompt: Identify Unmet User Needs

title: Map Emotional Journey Highs Lows
prompt: Map Emotional Journey Highs Lows

title: Prioritize Research Questions
prompt: Prioritize Research Questions

title: Spot Confirmation Bias Risk
prompt: Spot Confirmation Bias Risk

title: Identify the five most important user decisions the product team has mad
prompt: Identify the five most important user decisions the product team has made in the past six months. For each, determine what user research informed the decision. Find decisions made with no user research backing. Assess the risk that each unsupported decision introduced.

title: Audit user research documentation
prompt: Audit user research documentation. Find research conducted more than 12 months ago that is still referenced as the basis for product decisions. For each piece of stale research, identify whether the user context has changed enough to invalidate its conclusions.

title: Find the top three user pain points identified in recent research that h
prompt: Find the top three user pain points identified in recent research that have not been addressed in the product roadmap. For each, estimate the user impact and the engineering investment to resolve it. Recommend a prioritization decision based on effort-to-impact ratio.

title: Analyze the last ten user interview transcripts
prompt: Analyze the last ten user interview transcripts. Extract the top five recurring pain points ranked by frequency of mention. For each pain point, note the user segment most affected and the current workaround users employ. Identify any pain points that have appeared in three or more consecutive research rounds. Output a synthesis report with verbatim quotes supporting each finding.

title: Design a research plan for validating a proposed product feature
prompt: Design a research plan for validating a proposed product feature. Define the primary research question the study must answer. Select the most appropriate research method from usability testing, contextual inquiry, diary study, or survey, and justify the choice. Write a screener questionnaire to recruit five participants who match the target user profile. Outline a sixty-minute interview guide with a warm-up, task scenarios, and debrief questions.

title: Evaluate the research repository for coverage gaps
prompt: Evaluate the research repository for coverage gaps. List the user segments for which you have conducted at least three studies in the past year. Identify segments with no research data. For each gap, estimate the business risk of making product decisions without research coverage. Prioritize the top three gaps and propose a lightweight research approach for each that can be completed within two weeks.

title: Synthesize findings from a recent diary study into actionable insights
prompt: Synthesize findings from a recent diary study into actionable insights. Group diary entries by the phase of the user journey in which they occurred. For each phase, identify the dominant emotion expressed and the most common task failure. Map each failure to a product decision that caused it. Draft three design recommendations directly traceable to diary evidence. Include a confidence rating for each recommendation.

title: Conduct a competitive experience audit
prompt: Conduct a competitive experience audit. Select three direct competitors and evaluate their onboarding flow against your own. For each product, measure the number of steps to first value, the clarity of value proposition on the landing page, and the presence of in-app guidance during the first session. Score each on a rubric from one to five. Identify the two competitor strengths most worth addressing in your roadmap.

title: Evaluate the user research program for completeness and influence
prompt: Evaluate the user research program for completeness and influence. Identify the research methods in use — interviews, usability tests, surveys, behavioral analytics — and assess whether they are applied across the full user journey or concentrated at specific stages. Find product areas where decisions are made based on assumptions rather than research. Assess whether research findings are documented in a retrievable format that future team members can reference, or whether insights live only in presentation decks that are never revisited. Look for user segments that are systematically under-researched. Summarize the five research gaps that represent the greatest risk to product-market fit.

title: Assess how effectively user research influences product decisions
prompt: Assess how effectively user research influences product decisions. Identify the most recent product change that was directly driven by user research findings and trace the path from insight to implementation. Find product decisions in the past quarter where research was available but not consulted. Assess whether research findings are shared with the full team or siloed within a research function. Look for cases where quantitative behavioral data contradicts qualitative user research — and how the team resolves the conflict. Recommend the research program improvements that would most increase the proportion of product decisions informed by user evidence.

title: You are a principal UX researcher leading a research program for a finte
prompt: You are a principal UX researcher leading a research program for a fintech company preparing to launch a new budgeting feature. The product team has a hypothesis that users want automated savings recommendations, but you suspect the real need is better visibility into spending patterns rather than automation. Your task is to design a mixed-methods research program that either validates or challenges the product hypothesis before development begins. Start by defining the research questions in order of priority, separating attitudinal questions about what users say they want from behavioral questions about what they actually do. Design a two-week diary study recruiting fifteen participants in the target income bracket who currently use any budgeting tool. Specify the daily prompts participants will respond to, covering their financial decisions, emotional state around money, and workarounds they use when their current tool falls short. Follow the diary study with sixty-minute contextual interviews with eight of the diary participants, focusing on moments of friction identified in their entries. Design an unmoderated usability test of three lo-fi prototypes representing different feature directions to measure which approach generates the highest task completion rate. Define the synthesis method you will use to triangulate findings across the three data sources. Specify how you will present findings to stakeholders in a way that separates observation from interpretation and grounds every design recommendation in direct evidence. Include a plan for sharing the research repository with the engineering team so findings remain accessible after the project closes.

title: You are a user research operations lead building a research practice fro
prompt: You are a user research operations lead building a research practice from the ground up at a company of two hundred employees. Currently there is no dedicated researcher, and product decisions are made based on support tickets and sales call notes. Your mandate is to establish a lightweight but rigorous research infrastructure within three months using a team of one full-time researcher and two product managers who will conduct research part-time. Begin by designing the research request intake process that product managers use to submit research needs, including a template that forces requesters to articulate the decision the research will inform. Define the minimum viable research toolkit covering the three methods the team will use most often: moderated usability testing, unmoderated concept testing, and in-depth interviews. Build a participant recruitment pipeline by partnering with customer success to identify and tag willing research participants in the CRM. Design the research repository structure in Notion or a similar tool that allows findings to be tagged by user segment, product area, and research question, making them searchable for future studies. Create a two-page research brief template that communicates methodology, timeline, and findings in a format that busy executives will read in under five minutes. Define the standard for when a finding is strong enough to act on versus when it requires additional validation. Develop a quarterly research calendar that allocates fifty percent of capacity to proactive generative research and fifty percent to reactive evaluative research. Deliver a research practice launch plan including a sixty-day readiness milestone and a six-month maturity benchmark.

title: You are a senior researcher preparing a longitudinal study on how profes
prompt: You are a senior researcher preparing a longitudinal study on how professional musicians discover and adopt new digital audio tools. The study will run for six months and track twenty participants across three experience levels: hobbyist, semi-professional, and professional. Your goal is to understand the full adoption arc from first awareness through habituated daily use or abandonment, and to identify the specific moments where tools are rejected. Design the study structure including the recruitment criteria, the mix of experience levels, and the compensation model. Define the data collection cadence including monthly two-hour interviews, bi-weekly diary prompts, and a tool-usage log participants maintain independently. Specify the interview guide for each monthly touchpoint, explaining how questions will evolve as participants move through the adoption arc. Design the analysis framework that will allow you to compare adoption trajectories across experience levels and identify the leading indicators of abandonment that appear six to eight weeks before a participant stops using a tool. Define how you will handle participant dropout and ensure the study retains enough statistical power if attrition reaches twenty percent. Describe the ethical considerations specific to diary studies where participants share creative work and potentially sensitive professional information. Outline the deliverable format, including a longitudinal findings report, a persona update, and a set of design principles derived from the study that the product team can apply immediately.

title: Audit knowledge base coverage against team needs
prompt: Survey which questions team members regularly ask each other that should be answerable from documentation. Identify the 10 most common knowledge gaps. For each, note what type of doc is missing and where it should live.
prompt_short: Find the top questions team members ask that should be self-serve docs.
prompt_filtered: Coverage gaps only. Questions without documentation answers.

title: Find documentation that is stale or contradicts code
prompt: Compare key documentation against the current codebase. Identify docs describing APIs, workflows, or configurations that no longer match the actual implementation. Prioritize stale docs on critical paths that mislead developers.
prompt_short: Find documentation that contradicts or no longer matches the current code.
prompt_filtered: Staleness only. Documentation vs code divergence.

title: Identify tribal knowledge that must be documented now
prompt: Identify knowledge that lives only in specific individuals' heads — systems only they understand, decisions only they remember, processes only they can perform. Prioritize by bus factor risk and operational impact.
prompt_short: Find tribal knowledge held by one person that must be documented.
prompt_filtered: Tribal knowledge only. Single-person knowledge with bus factor risk.

title: Design a documentation structure for this project
prompt: Propose a documentation structure for this project that covers: architecture, API reference, runbooks, onboarding, decision logs, and playbooks. Define where each type lives, who maintains it, and how freshness is ensured.
prompt_short: Design a documentation structure covering all doc types for this project.
prompt_filtered: Structure design only. Doc types, locations, and ownership.

title: Create documentation standards to prevent future gaps
prompt: Write a documentation standard for this project: when to document (new service, new API, new runbook trigger), what to include in each doc type, how long docs should be, and how to mark docs as deprecated. Make it short enough to be followed.
prompt_short: Write concise documentation standards to prevent future coverage gaps.
prompt_filtered: Standards only. When to document, what to include, how to maintain.

title: Implement a doc review trigger on code changes
prompt: Design a process to flag documentation for review when code it describes changes. Identify the highest-risk paths — API reference docs, runbooks, configuration guides — and recommend whether review triggers should be automated (linting doc links against current API) or manual (PR checklist item).
prompt_short: Design a process to trigger doc review when code the doc describes changes.
prompt_filtered: Staleness trigger only. Code-change-to-doc-review signal design.

title: Find documentation with no clear audience or purpose
prompt: Audit existing documentation for documents that do not specify their intended audience or the decision they enable. Identify docs that were written to record that work was done rather than to enable a future reader to act. Recommend rewrites that state audience, purpose, and the key action each doc enables.
prompt_short: Find docs with no stated audience or actionable purpose.
prompt_filtered: Purpose clarity only. Docs written to record vs docs written to enable.

title: Assess knowledge base search relevance and zero-result queries
prompt: Review knowledge base search analytics — zero-result queries and low-relevance result queries. Identify the top 10 zero-result searches that represent real user needs. For each, either create the missing document or add synonyms and tags to surface the correct existing document.
prompt_short: Find the top zero-result knowledge base searches and fill the gaps.
prompt_filtered: Search gaps only. Zero-result and low-relevance queries.

title: Design a knowledge base contribution workflow for the team
prompt: Create a contribution workflow that makes adding to the knowledge base low-friction. Include: a quick-capture template for writing insights during or after work, a lightweight review step to ensure quality, a tagging taxonomy, and a process for converting Slack answers into permanent documentation.
prompt_short: Design a low-friction knowledge base contribution workflow from daily work.
prompt_filtered: Contribution workflow only. Capture, review, tagging, and Slack-to-doc flow.

title: Evaluate onboarding documentation for new hire completeness
prompt: Test the onboarding documentation by walking through it as a new hire. Identify steps with missing context, assumed knowledge, broken links, or sequences that must be done in order but are not marked as such. Track the time to reach productive contribution using only the documentation.
prompt_short: Walk through onboarding docs as a new hire and find every gap.
prompt_filtered: Onboarding docs only. Missing context, broken links, and missing sequencing.

title: Create a decision log for architectural choices
prompt: Design and populate an Architecture Decision Record log. For each major architectural decision made in the past 12 months, capture: the context, the alternatives considered, the decision made, and the consequences. Identify decisions where the rationale exists only in a meeting recording or individual memory.
prompt_short: Create ADRs for the top architectural decisions made in the past 12 months.
prompt_filtered: ADR log only. Decision context, alternatives, and consequences.

title: Map documentation to the onboarding checklist for each role
prompt: For each role — engineers, designers, and PMs — create a documentation checklist of which documents must be read in the first week and which in the first month. Identify roles where no such checklist exists and important docs that are never surfaced in onboarding.
prompt_short: Map required reading to onboarding checklists for each role.
prompt_filtered: Role-specific onboarding only. Required reading per role and week.

title: Find documentation referenced in code comments that does not exist
prompt: Search the codebase for code comments referencing external documentation — "see the wiki for details," "refer to the runbook at..." — where the linked document has been moved, deleted, or never existed. Replace with inline documentation or restore the missing link.
prompt_short: Find code comments referencing docs that no longer exist or are unreachable.
prompt_filtered: Dead doc links only. Code comments pointing to missing documentation.

title: Establish a quarterly documentation health review process
prompt: Design a quarterly documentation health review. Include: automated age-based staleness flagging, an owner-initiated review checklist, a sample of five random docs reviewed by a non-author for clarity, and a metric — percentage of docs reviewed in the last 12 months — tracked in the team health dashboard.
prompt_short: Design a quarterly doc health review with automated staleness flagging.
prompt_filtered: Review cadence only. Staleness detection and quarterly audit process.

title: Assess documentation format standards for scanability
prompt: Review the most-used documents for scanability. Identify docs written as long prose where structured formats — numbered steps, tables, code blocks, callout boxes — would reduce reading time and error rate. Recommend a documentation style guide with format recommendations per doc type.
prompt_short: Find prose-heavy docs that would be more useful in structured formats.
prompt_filtered: Format standards only. Prose vs structure assessment per doc type.

title: Identify knowledge gaps causing repeated Slack questions
prompt: Audit the last 30 days of team Slack messages for repeated questions — questions asked and answered at least three times in the same channel. Each repeated question is an undocumented knowledge gap. Convert the top 10 repeated Q&A threads into permanent documentation.
prompt_short: Find the top Slack questions asked three or more times and document them.
prompt_filtered: Slack-to-doc only. Repeated questions indicating missing documentation.

title: Review API documentation for developer experience quality
prompt: Evaluate API documentation from a first-time developer's perspective. Identify: missing authentication examples, endpoints with no request/response examples, error codes without description or remediation, and any endpoint where the documentation does not match the actual response shape. Prioritize fixes by endpoint usage frequency.
prompt_short: Audit API documentation for missing examples, errors, and schema mismatches.
prompt_filtered: API docs only. Authentication, examples, error docs, and schema accuracy.

prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

prompt: Surface Stale Documentation

prompt: Map Knowledge Gaps by Role

title: Design Article Review Cadence
prompt: Design Article Review Cadence

title: Consolidate Duplicate Articles
prompt: Consolidate Duplicate Articles

title: Improve Search Discoverability
prompt: Improve Search Discoverability

title: Audit the knowledge base for staleness
prompt: Audit the knowledge base for staleness. Identify all documents last updated more than six months ago. For each, determine whether the subject matter has changed enough to make the document misleading. Recommend a staleness review cadence and an ownership assignment for each category of documentation.

title: Find the five most frequently searched topics in the knowledge base that
prompt: Find the five most frequently searched topics in the knowledge base that return no results or low-quality results. For each, identify what a new team member would need to know and recommend the documentation that should be created to fill the gap.

title: Assess knowledge base discoverability
prompt: Assess knowledge base discoverability. Identify important documentation that exists but is not linked from any index or related document. For each orphaned document, recommend the navigation addition that would make it discoverable without a keyword search.

title: Audit the knowledge base for content freshness
prompt: Audit the knowledge base for content freshness. Pull a list of all articles with their last-edited date. Flag any article not updated in over twelve months that covers a product feature, process, or policy. For flagged articles, assign ownership based on the team responsible for the related product area. Create a batch review task list with deadlines sorted by criticality of the topic. Output the list sorted by staleness and estimated review effort.

title: Analyze search query logs from the knowledge base to identify the top tw
prompt: Analyze search query logs from the knowledge base to identify the top twenty searches returning zero results or results with a click-through rate below ten percent. For each failed search, determine whether the content gap represents a missing article, a discoverability problem with existing content, or a terminology mismatch between how users search and how articles are tagged. Prioritize the gaps by query volume and produce a content creation backlog.

title: Evaluate knowledge base article quality for the top fifty most-viewed ar
prompt: Evaluate knowledge base article quality for the top fifty most-viewed articles. Score each on a rubric covering accuracy, completeness, clarity, and presence of screenshots or examples. Flag articles scoring below three out of five on any dimension. Identify the three most common quality deficiencies across the corpus. Draft a style guide section addressing each deficiency. Estimate the editor hours needed to remediate the bottom quartile.

title: Map the knowledge base coverage against the top twenty support ticket ca
prompt: Map the knowledge base coverage against the top twenty support ticket categories from the past quarter. For each ticket category, check whether a self-service article exists and whether the article is linked in the auto-response for that ticket type. Identify the five ticket categories generating the most volume where self-service content either does not exist or is not surfaced. Estimate ticket deflection potential if content gaps are closed.

title: Design an article feedback loop for the knowledge base
prompt: Design an article feedback loop for the knowledge base. Define the feedback mechanisms users can use on each article including a rating widget, a comment field, and a report-an-error button. Specify how feedback is routed to the article owner, what the SLA is for responding to error reports, and how aggregate ratings trigger a mandatory review. Create a monthly feedback digest template that surfaces the lowest-rated articles and common user complaints.

title: Review the knowledge base for completeness and usefulness
prompt: Review the knowledge base for completeness and usefulness. Identify the primary audiences — engineers, operators, support, and new hires — and assess whether documentation exists for the top five questions each audience has. Find areas where documentation is fragmented across multiple locations, forcing users to synthesize information from multiple sources. Assess whether the knowledge base has a clear structure that allows a user to navigate to what they need without knowing the exact document name. Look for critical system knowledge — architecture decisions, runbook procedures, deployment processes — that exists only in people's heads. Summarize the five knowledge base gaps with the highest operational and onboarding risk.

title: Evaluate the documentation maintenance process
prompt: Evaluate the documentation maintenance process. Identify who is responsible for keeping each category of documentation current. Find documentation with no owner — created by a team member who has since left — that is now orphaned and potentially outdated. Assess whether documentation is updated as part of the development process or treated as a separate task that is frequently skipped under time pressure. Look for documentation that has been invalidated by system changes but not updated, creating misleading information. Recommend the documentation process changes — ownership assignment, update checklists, automated staleness detection — that would most improve documentation reliability.

title: You are a knowledge management lead at a software company with a support
prompt: You are a knowledge management lead at a software company with a support knowledge base of over three thousand articles accumulated over seven years. The knowledge base is suffering from severe content decay: articles reference discontinued features, use inconsistent terminology, and duplicate each other across product lines. Support ticket volume has increased twenty percent year over year despite no corresponding increase in product complexity, suggesting the knowledge base is failing to deflect tickets effectively. Your mandate is to design and execute a knowledge base transformation program within six months. Begin with a content audit that scores every article on freshness, accuracy, and search visibility, and produces a tiered triage list distinguishing articles to archive, consolidate, update, and leave as-is. Design a tagging taxonomy that maps every article to the product feature it covers, the user role it serves, and the stage of the customer journey it addresses. Implement a federated ownership model where each product team owns the articles in their domain and a central knowledge ops team maintains standards and runs the review cadence. Define the editorial calendar that schedules quarterly reviews for evergreen content and triggers an immediate review any time a product feature ships or is deprecated. Specify the metrics you will use to measure knowledge base health including deflection rate, search success rate, article rating distribution, and time-to-first-result. Design the author enablement program including a style guide, an article template library, and a peer review workflow. Deliver a six-month transformation roadmap with milestones and success criteria.

title: You are a technical writer leading a documentation overhaul for a develo
prompt: You are a technical writer leading a documentation overhaul for a developer-focused API product. The current documentation was written by engineers without editorial standards and lacks consistent structure, code examples, and error reference tables. Developer satisfaction scores for the documentation are at thirty-two percent, and the support team reports that forty percent of inbound tickets are questions already answered in the docs, indicating a severe discoverability problem. Your task is to redesign the documentation architecture and produce a content strategy that will raise developer satisfaction to at least sixty-five percent within one year. Start by conducting a developer documentation audit using a structured rubric that evaluates getting-started experience, conceptual overview depth, reference completeness, code example coverage, and error message documentation. Survey twenty developers who have used the API in the past ninety days to identify the five most common points of confusion. Design a new information architecture that separates quickstart guides, conceptual explanations, task-based how-tos, and API reference into clearly labeled sections with cross-links. Define a code example standard that requires every endpoint to have a working example in at least three languages matching your top developer segments. Specify the error documentation standard requiring a plain-language explanation, common causes, and a resolution path for every error code. Design a docs-as-code workflow where documentation lives in the same repository as the API code, pull requests require a docs checklist, and documentation is reviewed alongside code changes. Produce a content roadmap prioritizing the highest-impact documentation gaps identified in the audit.

title: You are a learning and development manager designing a company-wide know
prompt: You are a learning and development manager designing a company-wide knowledge sharing system for a remote-first company of four hundred employees across twelve time zones. The company has no central knowledge repository, and institutional knowledge lives in Slack messages, personal Google Docs, and the memories of long-tenured employees. You have been given a budget to implement a tool and build the practices that will make knowledge sharing a daily habit. Begin by defining the three categories of knowledge the system must support: reference knowledge such as policies and procedures, how-to knowledge covering repeatable processes, and discovery knowledge capturing lessons learned and decision rationale. Evaluate three to five knowledge management platforms against criteria including search quality, integration with existing tools such as Slack and Google Workspace, access control granularity, and total cost of ownership at your headcount. Design the contribution incentive model that makes adding to the knowledge base feel like a natural part of completing work rather than an additional administrative burden. Specify the governance model including who can create and retire content, how content is reviewed for accuracy, and how the system handles conflicting information from different sources. Design the onboarding integration that routes new employees through the knowledge base as part of their first-week experience and teaches them to contribute by the end of week two. Define the adoption metrics you will track in the first six months, the thresholds that would trigger an intervention, and the quarterly review process for evaluating system health. Deliver an implementation plan with a tool recommendation, a governance charter, and a twelve-month adoption roadmap.

title: Write a runbook for the most critical service failure
prompt: Create a runbook for the most likely and most impactful service failure. Include: symptoms, initial checks, common root causes with diagnostic commands, remediation steps in order, escalation contacts, and rollback commands.
prompt_short: Write a complete runbook for the highest-impact service failure.
prompt_filtered: Single runbook only. Symptoms, commands, and remediation steps.

title: Audit all runbooks for completeness and accuracy
prompt: Review every runbook against the current state of the system. Identify runbooks with outdated commands, missing steps, broken links, or steps that require knowledge not in the runbook. Flag by how often the runbook is likely needed.
prompt_short: Audit all runbooks for outdated commands and missing steps.
prompt_filtered: Runbook accuracy only. Outdated content and missing steps.

title: Find failure scenarios with no runbook coverage
prompt: Enumerate the top 20 failure scenarios for this system. For each, check whether a runbook exists. For the most critical scenarios with no runbook, create a stub with at minimum: symptoms, first diagnostic command, and escalation path.
prompt_short: Find critical failure scenarios with no runbook and create stubs.
prompt_filtered: Missing runbooks only. Coverage gaps for likely failure scenarios.

title: Create a war room communication playbook
prompt: Write a war room playbook for major incidents. Include: who to page, the communication cadence (internal and external), status page update templates, stakeholder update frequency, and the format for the all-clear message.
prompt_short: Write a war room playbook covering communication, cadence, and templates.
prompt_filtered: Communication playbook only. War room roles and update templates.

title: Design runbook testing and rotation procedures
prompt: Propose a process for keeping runbooks accurate and useful. Include: how often to test each runbook in a simulated environment, who is responsible for updates after each incident, and how to rotate on-call engineers through runbook walkthroughs.
prompt_short: Design a process for testing and maintaining runbook accuracy.
prompt_filtered: Runbook maintenance only. Testing cadence and update ownership.

title: Write a database failover runbook for the on-call engineer
prompt: Create a step-by-step database failover runbook. Include: how to confirm the primary is unreachable, how to promote a replica, how to update application connection strings, how to verify the failover was successful, and how to restore the failed primary after resolution.
prompt_short: Write a step-by-step database failover runbook for on-call.
prompt_filtered: Database failover only. Promotion steps, connection updates, and verification.

title: Create a credential rotation playbook for all secret types
prompt: Write a credential rotation playbook covering all secret types in the system — API keys, database passwords, JWT signing keys, OAuth client secrets. For each type, document: how to generate the new credential, how to deploy it without downtime, how to verify the old credential is no longer active, and who must be notified.
prompt_short: Write a zero-downtime credential rotation playbook for each secret type.
prompt_filtered: Credential rotation only. Per-secret-type rotation without downtime.

title: Design an on-call escalation decision tree
prompt: Create a decision tree for on-call engineers that maps symptom to action: if users report login failures, check X; if X is healthy, check Y; if both are healthy, escalate to Z. Cover the top 10 most common user-facing symptoms with a clear decision path to either resolution or escalation.
prompt_short: Design a symptom-to-action decision tree for the top 10 on-call scenarios.
prompt_filtered: Escalation decision tree only. Symptom-to-action mapping for on-call.

title: Write a capacity scaling runbook for traffic spikes
prompt: Create a runbook for responding to unexpected traffic spikes. Include: leading indicators that signal an incoming spike before it causes degradation, commands to scale compute horizontally, database connection pool adjustments, CDN cache configuration to reduce origin load, and rollback steps if scaling worsens the situation.
prompt_short: Write a runbook for scaling horizontally in response to unexpected traffic spikes.
prompt_filtered: Traffic spike runbook only. Scaling commands and rollback steps.

title: Create a data breach response playbook
prompt: Write a data breach response playbook. Include: initial triage steps, how to scope the breach (which data, which users, what timeframe), legal and regulatory notification obligations, customer communication templates, credential invalidation steps, and the post-breach audit procedure.
prompt_short: Write a data breach response playbook with triage, notification, and remediation.
prompt_filtered: Breach response only. Triage, scope assessment, and notification obligations.

title: Write a deployment rollback runbook for failed releases
prompt: Create a rollback runbook for failed production deployments. Specify: the rollback decision criteria (error rate threshold, latency threshold, user-reported symptoms), the exact rollback command per service, the database migration reversion procedure, the feature flag disable process, and the post-rollback verification checklist.
prompt_short: Write a deployment rollback runbook with decision criteria and exact commands.
prompt_filtered: Rollback runbook only. Decision criteria, commands, and verification.

title: Assess runbook accessibility during an incident
prompt: Evaluate whether runbooks are accessible when most needed. Identify runbooks hosted in systems that may be unavailable during the incident they address — documentation in a service that depends on the failing database. Recommend hosting critical runbooks on a separate resilient system.
prompt_short: Find runbooks that would be inaccessible if the service they describe fails.
prompt_filtered: Runbook availability only. Hosting resilience during the incidents they address.

title: Write a third-party API outage response playbook
prompt: For each critical external API dependency, write a degraded-mode response playbook. Define: the symptoms of the third-party failure in your system, the feature degradation behavior to activate (cache stale data, show maintenance message, disable the feature), and the recovery steps when the API becomes available.
prompt_short: Write degraded-mode playbooks for each critical third-party API dependency.
prompt_filtered: Third-party outage only. Degraded mode activation and recovery steps.

title: Define SLOs and SLAs with error budgets for each service
prompt: For each production service, define an SLO — the target availability and latency percentage — and calculate the monthly error budget in minutes. Document how error budget burn is tracked, the threshold that triggers a reliability sprint, and the stakeholder communication process when an SLO is breached.
prompt_short: Define SLOs and error budgets for each service with breach procedures.
prompt_filtered: SLO definition only. Availability targets, error budgets, and breach communication.

title: Create a change freeze playbook for high-risk periods
prompt: Design a change freeze playbook for high-risk periods — peak traffic events, financial close periods, major product launches. Specify: what constitutes a change, the freeze window duration, the exception approval process, and the communication protocol for informing all engineering stakeholders.
prompt_short: Design a change freeze playbook for peak-risk deployment periods.
prompt_filtered: Change freeze only. Scope, duration, exception process, and communication.

title: Audit alerting rules for missing coverage on critical paths
prompt: Review all alerting rules against the critical user paths. Identify paths where no alert would fire for a complete failure — no synthetic monitor, no error rate alert, no latency alert. Find the paths where detection would rely on a user filing a support ticket. Prioritize by user impact.
prompt_short: Find critical user paths with no alert that would fire on complete failure.
prompt_filtered: Alert coverage only. Missing alerts on critical user-facing paths.

title: Write a post-incident on-call handoff procedure
prompt: Create a standardized handoff procedure for on-call transitions during an active incident. Include: the handoff document template (current status, actions taken, open questions, next steps, monitoring links), the synchronous handoff checklist, and the minimum context required before the incoming engineer takes over command.
prompt_short: Write an on-call handoff procedure for transitions during active incidents.
prompt_filtered: Handoff procedure only. Handoff template and minimum context requirements.

title: Define Runbook Ownership

title: Standardize Decision Criteria
prompt: Standardize Decision Criteria

title: Identify all production services and match each to its operational runbo
prompt: Identify all production services and match each to its operational runbooks. Find services with no runbook for their most likely failure mode. For each gap, outline the minimum runbook content — symptoms, diagnostic commands, and remediation steps — needed to enable an on-call engineer with no prior service knowledge to resolve the issue.

title: Audit all existing runbooks for currency
prompt: Audit all existing runbooks for currency. Find runbooks that reference commands, endpoints, or infrastructure components that no longer exist. For each outdated step, identify the current equivalent and recommend the runbook update.

title: Assess playbook coverage for security incidents
prompt: Assess playbook coverage for security incidents. Identify whether playbooks exist for credential compromise, data exfiltration, and ransomware scenarios. For any missing scenario, outline the first five response steps that should be documented for an on-call engineer encountering the scenario for the first time.

title: Audit the runbook library for coverage completeness
prompt: Audit the runbook library for coverage completeness. List every production service and check whether a corresponding operational runbook exists. For services with runbooks, verify that the runbook was used in at least one incident in the past six months and was updated afterward. Flag services with no runbook or with runbooks last updated over a year ago. Output a gap report ranked by service criticality.

title: Review the incident response playbook for the most critical service tier
prompt: Review the incident response playbook for the most critical service tier. Walk through the playbook step by step and identify any step that requires undocumented tribal knowledge to execute. For each such step, write the missing context that a new on-call engineer would need. Identify the longest sequence of steps that must be executed manually before automation kicks in. Recommend two automation opportunities that would reduce manual intervention time by at least thirty percent.

title: Design a runbook testing protocol
prompt: Design a runbook testing protocol. Define a quarterly drill schedule where on-call engineers walk through each Tier 1 runbook using a staging environment. Specify the pass/fail criteria for each drill including step completion time, number of escalations needed, and whether the runbook contained any errors. Create a drill report template. Recommend a process for updating the runbook immediately after each drill based on findings.

title: Evaluate the escalation matrix in the current incident response playbook
prompt: Evaluate the escalation matrix in the current incident response playbook. For each severity level, verify that the escalation chain includes a primary and backup contact, that contact information is current, and that the expected response time is defined. Identify any gaps where an escalation path leads to a team or individual who is no longer responsible for that service. Produce an updated escalation matrix with recommended changes.

title: Write a playbook for handling a data pipeline outage that affects downst
prompt: Write a playbook for handling a data pipeline outage that affects downstream reporting. Cover the initial detection phase including alert sources and triage criteria. Document the investigation phase including the five most common root causes and their diagnostic queries. Write the communication template for notifying stakeholders during an outage lasting more than thirty minutes. Define the resolution criteria and the post-incident review requirements. Include a rollback procedure for each common root cause.

title: Evaluate runbook completeness across all operational scenarios
prompt: Evaluate runbook completeness across all operational scenarios. Inventory every runbook and match it to the failure mode it addresses. Find the most likely failure scenarios — service crash, database unavailability, network partition, dependency timeout — for each production service, and verify a runbook exists for each. Assess whether runbooks are written at a level of detail that an on-call engineer unfamiliar with the service could execute them under pressure. Find runbooks that assume knowledge of specific tooling or access credentials that may not be immediately available at 3am. Summarize the five runbook gaps that would most increase mean time to recovery during an incident.

title: Review the runbook maintenance process
prompt: Review the runbook maintenance process. Identify when each runbook was last validated through a drill or an actual incident execution. Find runbooks that have never been executed in practice and assess whether they contain assumptions that would prove incorrect under real conditions. Assess whether runbooks are updated after every incident that reveals a gap in the documented procedure. Look for runbooks that are too long to navigate under pressure — where the key remediation step is buried in narrative context. Recommend the runbook format and maintenance process improvements that would most increase their reliability and usability during incidents.

title: You are a site reliability engineering lead building a runbook program f
prompt: You are a site reliability engineering lead building a runbook program for a platform engineering team that currently has no standardized operational documentation. The team supports twenty-two production services ranging from high-traffic APIs to batch pipelines, and on-call incidents frequently extend beyond their expected resolution time because engineers lack consistent reference material. Your mandate is to design the runbook standard, build the template, and drive adoption within one quarter. Begin by defining the required sections for every runbook: service overview, dependencies, common failure modes, diagnostic commands, step-by-step triage procedures for each failure mode, escalation contacts, rollback procedures, and change log. Design the template in a format that engineers can edit during an incident without context switching, such as a wiki page structured for fast scanning rather than linear reading. Define the severity classification system the runbook should reference, including the criteria that distinguish P1 from P2 and P2 from P3. Specify how the runbook should handle branching logic where the diagnostic result determines which triage path to follow, and whether decision trees or prose-based conditional steps are more appropriate for your team culture. Design the runbook review cadence, including a mandatory review after every incident involving the service and a scheduled annual review for services that have been stable. Create an ownership model where each runbook has a named primary and backup owner, and ownership transfers automatically when team membership changes. Develop the adoption plan including a runbook writing workshop, a peer review process, and a completeness score that appears on the service catalog page for each service. Deliver the template, style guide, and a twelve-week rollout timeline.

title: You are an incident management consultant helping a fintech company redu
prompt: You are an incident management consultant helping a fintech company reduce its mean time to resolve from four hours to under ninety minutes. The company has experienced three major incidents in the past quarter, each of which exceeded four hours of resolution time due to communication delays, unclear ownership, and inconsistent triage practices. Your analysis of the incident logs reveals three recurring patterns: the on-call engineer spends the first thirty minutes identifying the right team to engage, the engineering lead spends twenty minutes reconstructing the system topology before beginning diagnosis, and stakeholder communications are sent ad hoc with inconsistent content and timing. Your task is to redesign the incident response playbook to address all three patterns. For the ownership problem, design a service catalog enrichment process that links every alert to its responsible team and primary on-call contact without requiring the on-call engineer to look it up. For the topology problem, design a system context card that is automatically attached to every Tier 1 incident ticket, containing the service dependency graph, recent deployment history, and the three most common failure modes. For the communication problem, design a structured status update template with a fifteen-minute initial acknowledgment, a thirty-minute status update, and an hourly update thereafter, each requiring only a fill-in-the-blank completion to send. Define the war room protocol including who joins by default for a P1, what role each person plays, and how decisions are made when experts disagree. Design the incident commander role and specify the handoff procedure when an incident spans multiple shifts. Produce the revised playbook as a ready-to-publish document with all templates embedded.

title: You are a DevOps engineer writing a comprehensive deployment playbook fo
prompt: You are a DevOps engineer writing a comprehensive deployment playbook for a microservices platform that deploys to Kubernetes across three production regions. The platform currently has no standardized deployment procedure, and recent incidents have been caused by inconsistent deployment practices across teams, missing pre-deployment validation steps, and no documented rollback procedure. Your task is to write the deployment playbook that all teams will follow for any change affecting a production service. Begin by defining the deployment readiness criteria that must be verified before any deployment begins, covering test coverage thresholds, security scan results, dependency version compatibility, and change advisory board approval for major changes. Design the pre-deployment checklist that the deploying engineer runs through, including confirming the deployment window, notifying stakeholders, verifying the rollback artifact is available, and setting up enhanced monitoring for the deployment period. Write the deployment execution procedure covering traffic shifting strategy, health check intervals, and the specific metrics that must remain within bounds throughout the deployment before the next traffic increment is applied. Define the deployment pause and rollback criteria, specifying the exact metric thresholds and error rates that trigger an immediate rollback without waiting for human judgment. Write the rollback procedure step by step, including how to revert Kubernetes deployments, how to roll back database migrations, and how to communicate the rollback to stakeholders. Design the post-deployment verification checklist that confirms the deployment is stable before the engineer hands back the service. Specify the post-deployment monitoring window duration and the escalation path if an anomaly surfaces within that window. Deliver the complete playbook in a format ready for your internal wiki.

title: Write a blameless postmortem for this incident
prompt: Write a blameless postmortem covering: incident timeline, root cause, contributing factors, impact (duration, affected users, revenue), immediate mitigation, short-term fixes, and long-term systemic improvements. Avoid naming individuals as root causes.
prompt_short: Write a blameless postmortem with timeline, root cause, and action items.
prompt_filtered: Postmortem document only. Timeline, root cause, and systemic improvements.

title: Find the systemic root cause behind recurring incidents
prompt: Analyze the last 5 incidents in a given category. Use the 5 Whys technique to find the systemic root cause behind recurring failures. Identify whether they stem from process, tooling, ownership, or architectural gaps.
prompt_short: Apply 5 Whys to find the systemic root cause of recurring incidents.
prompt_filtered: Root cause analysis only. Systemic vs surface cause identification.

title: Audit action item completion from past postmortems
prompt: Review all postmortems from the past 6 months. For each action item, check whether it was completed, owned, and had a deadline. Identify the pattern in which action items get deprioritized and recommend a follow-through process.
prompt_short: Audit postmortem action item completion and find systemic follow-through gaps.
prompt_filtered: Action item tracking only. Completion rate and prioritization patterns.

title: Create a postmortem template for this organization
prompt: Design a postmortem template tailored to this system and team. Include sections for: timeline, detection, impact, root cause analysis, contributing factors, action items with owners and deadlines, and lessons shared with the broader team.
prompt_short: Design a postmortem template tailored to this team and system.
prompt_filtered: Template design only. Structure and required sections.

title: Find detection gaps that extended this incident duration
prompt: For the given incident, identify the gap between when the problem started and when it was detected. Find what monitoring, alert, or synthetic test would have caught it earlier. Estimate MTTR reduction if detection had been immediate.
prompt_short: Find monitoring gaps that delayed detection of this incident.
prompt_filtered: Detection gap only. Time from failure start to alert fire.

title: Conduct a fishbone diagram analysis for a complex incident
prompt: Apply the fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram method to identify contributing causes of the incident. Group causes under: People, Process, Technology, Environment, and Management. Identify the category with the most contributing factors and recommend systemic changes in that category.
prompt_short: Apply fishbone analysis to categorize and group all incident contributing factors.
prompt_filtered: Fishbone analysis only. Cause grouping by category for this incident.

title: Identify follow-through failure patterns in postmortem action items
prompt: Analyze postmortem action items from the past year. Find the patterns that predict non-completion: assigned to a team rather than an individual, no deadline, low priority designation, assigned to the most stretched team member. Recommend the assignment and tracking process that maximizes completion rate.
prompt_short: Find what predicts postmortem action item non-completion and fix the pattern.
prompt_filtered: Follow-through only. Predictors of action item abandonment.

title: Design a lightweight postmortem format for low-severity incidents
prompt: Create a lightweight postmortem format for P3/P4 incidents — low severity but still worth learning from. It should be completable in under 20 minutes: 5-10 lines covering what happened, why it happened, and one action item. Prevent postmortem debt from incidents that get skipped because the full template is too burdensome.
prompt_short: Design a lightweight postmortem format completable in under 20 minutes.
prompt_filtered: Lightweight format only. Low-severity postmortem that gets done vs skipped.

title: Create a pre-mortem for the next major product launch
prompt: Run a pre-mortem for the next major launch. Ask the team to imagine it is 6 months from now and the launch failed. Collect every plausible failure scenario. Rank by likelihood and impact. For each high-risk scenario, assign a mitigation owner and a go/no-go decision criterion to check before launch.
prompt_short: Run a pre-mortem for the next launch and rank failure scenarios by risk.
prompt_filtered: Pre-mortem only. Failure scenarios and mitigation owners before launch.

title: Track mean time to detect and mean time to recover trends
prompt: Calculate MTTD and MTTR for the last 12 months of incidents. Plot trends by quarter. Identify whether detection time and recovery time are improving, degrading, or flat. Find the incident category with the worst MTTR and correlate it to runbook completeness or on-call training gaps.
prompt_short: Track MTTD and MTTR trends over the past 12 months by incident category.
prompt_filtered: MTTD and MTTR only. Trend analysis and worst-performing incident category.

title: Identify which teams have never completed a postmortem
prompt: Audit whether all engineering teams that have had production incidents in the past 6 months have completed at least one postmortem. Identify teams with zero postmortems despite incidents. Assess whether this reflects a process gap, cultural resistance, or definitional disagreement about what triggers a postmortem.
prompt_short: Find teams that had incidents but completed no postmortems in 6 months.
prompt_filtered: Postmortem coverage only. Teams with incidents and no postmortem record.

title: Share postmortem learnings across teams systematically
prompt: Design a postmortem learning-sharing process. Include: a monthly digest of the three most significant learnings from recent postmortems, a searchable tag system so teams can find postmortems relevant to their stack, and a quarterly review of recurring themes. Prevent teams from independently discovering the same failure patterns.
prompt_short: Design a postmortem learning-sharing process to prevent repeated discoveries.
prompt_filtered: Sharing process only. Digest format, tagging, and quarterly theme review.

title: Find the longest recurring incident pattern without resolution
prompt: Identify the incident pattern that has recurred most frequently in the past 12 months despite appearing in postmortem action items. This is the systemic gap with the longest unresolved lifespan. Escalate its resolution as a P0 reliability initiative with an executive-level owner and a deadline.
prompt_short: Find the most frequently recurring incident pattern still unresolved despite postmortems.
prompt_filtered: Recurrence analysis only. Longest-standing unresolved incident pattern.

title: Assess whether postmortem culture is genuinely blameless
prompt: Evaluate whether postmortems in this organization are genuinely blameless. Review language in past postmortems for attribution of fault to individuals rather than systems. Check whether engineers feel psychologically safe to report incidents promptly. Identify whether delayed incident reporting correlates with fear of blame.
prompt_short: Assess whether postmortems are genuinely blameless by reviewing language and behavior.
prompt_filtered: Blame culture only. Language patterns and reporting delay correlation.

title: Design a severity classification matrix with clear criteria
prompt: Create a severity classification matrix (P0-P4) with explicit criteria — percentage of users affected, revenue impact per minute, data loss risk, regulatory exposure — that enables any on-call engineer to correctly classify an incident independently without escalating for guidance.
prompt_short: Create a severity matrix with explicit criteria an on-call engineer can apply alone.
prompt_filtered: Severity classification only. Criteria per level without judgment calls.

title: Audit incident detection sources for blind spots
prompt: Inventory how incidents are typically detected — automated alert, synthetic monitor, user complaint, internal user report, partner notification. Find the failure modes where detection relies entirely on user complaints, meaning the team is the last to know. Recommend automated detection for the highest-impact user-reported failure modes.
prompt_short: Find failure modes detected only via user complaints and add automated detection.
prompt_filtered: Detection sources only. User-reported vs automated incident discovery.

title: Create a postmortem review board for systemic improvements
prompt: Design a monthly postmortem review board meeting. Include: attendees (cross-functional representation), agenda (top recurring themes, action item completion review, pattern nomination for systemic projects), escalation path for action items stalled for 60+ days, and a quarterly report to engineering leadership on reliability trends.
prompt_short: Design a monthly postmortem review board for systemic reliability improvements.
prompt_filtered: Review board only. Structure, agenda, escalation, and leadership reporting.

prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

title: Gain full understanding before completing
prompt: Gain full understanding before completing
