prompt: Find What's Broken

title: Recommend Next Steps
prompt: Recommend Next Steps

title: Score for Production Readiness
prompt: Score for Production Readiness

title: Audit Postmortem Quality
prompt: Audit Postmortem Quality

title: Track Action Item Completion
prompt: Track Action Item Completion

title: Identify Contributing Causes
prompt: Identify Contributing Causes

title: Extract Systemic Patterns
prompt: Extract Systemic Patterns

title: Write Blameless Timeline
prompt: Write Blameless Timeline

title: Prioritize Action Items by Impact
prompt: Prioritize Action Items by Impact

title: Track Follow-Through Rate
prompt: Track Follow-Through Rate

title: Review the last five postmortems
prompt: Review the last five postmortems. For each, identify whether a true root cause was identified — a systemic factor — or whether the analysis stopped at a proximate cause — the immediate trigger. Find postmortems that blamed human error without analyzing the system conditions that made the error likely.

title: Audit action items from postmortems in the past 12 months
prompt: Audit action items from postmortems in the past 12 months. Find action items that were never completed. For each, assess whether the corresponding incident has recurred. Identify the completion rate pattern — whether action items assigned to teams versus individuals have different completion rates.

title: Identify the single most impactful reliability improvement available bas
prompt: Identify the single most impactful reliability improvement available based on postmortem history. Trace the recurring failure pattern across the last 12 months of incidents. Find the root cause that appears most frequently and recommend the architectural or process change that would eliminate it.

title: Conduct a five-whys analysis on the most recent P1 incident
prompt: Conduct a five-whys analysis on the most recent P1 incident. Start with the user-visible symptom and ask why it occurred five times, documenting the answer at each level. Identify whether the root cause is technical, process, or organizational in nature. Distinguish between the proximate cause and the underlying systemic cause. Output the complete five-whys chain and a root cause classification.

title: Audit the postmortem archive for the past twelve months
prompt: Audit the postmortem archive for the past twelve months. Count the total number of postmortems completed and calculate the percentage that included at least three action items. Track what percentage of action items were closed within their target date. Identify the three root cause categories that appear most frequently across all postmortems. Recommend a systemic change for each recurring category that would prevent reoccurrence.

title: Write a blameless postmortem template for the engineering team
prompt: Write a blameless postmortem template for the engineering team. The template must include a timeline of events with timestamps, a summary of user impact quantified by affected users and duration, a root cause section using the five-whys method, a section distinguishing contributing factors from the root cause, an action items table with owner and due date, and a lessons learned section. Include guidance notes for the facilitator on how to run a blameless postmortem discussion.

title: Analyze the gap between postmortem action items and actual follow-throug
prompt: Analyze the gap between postmortem action items and actual follow-through. Pull all action items created in postmortems over the past six months. Calculate the on-time completion rate per team and per severity level. Identify action items that were deprioritized because they required cross-team coordination. Recommend a tracking mechanism that surfaces overdue postmortem action items in the weekly engineering all-hands agenda.

title: Design a postmortem review process for a team that currently completes p
prompt: Design a postmortem review process for a team that currently completes postmortems inconsistently. Define the trigger criteria that make a postmortem mandatory versus optional. Specify the maximum time between incident resolution and postmortem publication. Create a postmortem quality rubric that reviewers use to score draft postmortems before publication. Define who is responsible for ensuring postmortem quality and tracking action item completion.

title: Evaluate the postmortem and root cause analysis process
prompt: Evaluate the postmortem and root cause analysis process. Assess whether every significant production incident — affecting users or breaching SLOs — produces a written postmortem. Find incidents that were resolved without a postmortem and assess what learning was lost. Review the quality of existing postmortems — whether they reach true systemic root causes or stop at immediate triggers. Assess whether postmortems are blameless — focused on system improvements rather than individual fault. Look for recurring incident patterns that postmortems have identified but that action items have not resolved. Summarize the five postmortem process improvements that would most increase learning and incident prevention.

title: Analyze the action item completion rate from postmortems
prompt: Analyze the action item completion rate from postmortems. Identify the total number of postmortem action items generated in the past 12 months and the percentage completed. Find action items that were deprioritized until the corresponding incident recurred. Assess whether action items are assigned to individuals with deadlines or to teams without accountability. Look for patterns in which types of action items — tooling, process, architectural — have the lowest completion rate. Recommend the postmortem follow-up process changes that would most increase action item completion rates and reduce incident recurrence.

title: You are an engineering leader facilitating a postmortem for a four-hour
prompt: You are an engineering leader facilitating a postmortem for a four-hour database outage that caused complete service unavailability for sixty thousand users during business hours. The incident resulted in significant customer complaints, an SLA breach, and a ten percent spike in churn risk for enterprise accounts. Your goal is to produce a postmortem document that is thorough enough to prevent recurrence, blameless enough to encourage honest participation, and specific enough that action items cannot be deprioritized. Begin by constructing a precise timeline that covers the first alert, the initial hypothesis, each diagnostic step attempted, the moment of root cause identification, the resolution action, and the return to normal operation. Annotate the timeline with the names of the systems and tools involved rather than the individuals who performed each step. Apply the five-whys method to trace the immediate cause, which was a connection pool exhaustion, back to the underlying systemic cause, which may be a capacity planning gap, a deployment process failure, or a missing circuit breaker. Distinguish between the root cause and the contributing factors such as insufficient alerting lead time, the absence of a playbook for this failure mode, and an undetected configuration drift introduced two weeks prior. For each contributing factor, write an action item that is specific, measurable, and owned by a named team rather than an individual. Estimate the blast radius reduction that would result if each action item were completed. Design the action item tracking cadence, including a two-week check-in and a thirty-day completion review. Conclude with a lessons learned section that captures the top three insights the team gained and how those insights apply beyond this specific incident.

title: You are a reliability program manager building a postmortem culture at a
prompt: You are a reliability program manager building a postmortem culture at a company where postmortems are currently written only for the most severe outages, completed inconsistently, and rarely result in follow-through on action items. Engineering leaders have expressed that postmortems feel like a blame exercise rather than a learning opportunity, and several engineers have been reluctant to document their own mistakes honestly. Your mandate is to design a postmortem program that changes this culture within two quarters. Begin by conducting a series of listening sessions with engineers at all levels to understand the specific fears and friction points that discourage honest postmortems. Use those findings to design a blameless postmortem framework that explicitly names the principle of separating systemic causes from individual actions and trains facilitators to redirect blame-seeking language during review meetings. Define the postmortem trigger criteria using a severity-based matrix that makes postmortems mandatory for P1 and P2 incidents and optional but encouraged for P3 and significant near-misses. Design the postmortem template with guidance notes that help authors write without attributing failure to specific people. Create a facilitator certification program where engineers complete a two-hour training on blameless facilitation before leading a postmortem review. Specify the publication standard including who reviews a postmortem before it is published, what the maximum time-to-publish SLA is, and where postmortems are stored and made accessible. Design the action item governance process including tooling, escalation for overdue items, and a quarterly postmortem themes review that surfaces systemic patterns across all incidents. Measure culture change using a bi-annual survey on psychological safety in incident reviews. Deliver a program launch plan with a ninety-day milestone and a six-month maturity target.

title: You are a principal engineer writing a detailed RCA for a security incid
prompt: You are a principal engineer writing a detailed RCA for a security incident where a misconfigured API gateway allowed unauthenticated read access to a staging database containing production-mirrored customer PII for a window of seventy-two hours. The incident was discovered by an external security researcher rather than internal monitoring. Your RCA must meet the standards required for regulatory disclosure to your relevant compliance authority and must satisfy your legal team's requirements for a defensible incident narrative. Begin with an executive summary that states the incident clearly, quantifies the exposure window and the number of records potentially accessed, and confirms the remediation steps taken. Construct the technical timeline covering the configuration change that introduced the vulnerability, the deployment event that activated it, the expiry of the detection window in which automated scanning should have caught it, and the external report that triggered discovery. Apply the five-whys method to trace from the misconfiguration to the process gaps that allowed it to reach production, including the absence of automated security scanning in the deployment pipeline and the staging environment's access to production data. Distinguish the root cause from the contributing factors, which include the lack of environment isolation, the absence of network segmentation between staging and production databases, and an incomplete security review checklist for gateway configuration changes. Write action items that address each contributing factor with thirty-day, ninety-day, and six-month timelines that demonstrate a credible remediation roadmap. Include a section on what the company did well, noting that the external researcher was engaged professionally and that the containment response once discovered was completed within four hours. Deliver the RCA in a format appropriate for both internal engineering review and external regulatory submission.

title: Clarify the product vision and near-term strategic focus
prompt: Synthesize the stated product vision, current feature set, and competitive position into a clear strategic narrative. Identify where the vision is unclear, contradicted by the current product, or lacking a credible path to execution.
prompt_short: Clarify the product vision and identify gaps in the strategic narrative.
prompt_filtered: Vision clarity only. Mission, differentiation, and execution path.

title: Map current product to stated strategic goals
prompt: For each stated strategic goal, assess how much of the current product investment actually advances it. Identify work in progress that doesn't map to any strategic goal (distraction) and goals with no corresponding investment (drift).
prompt_short: Find investment not mapped to strategic goals and goals with no investment.
prompt_filtered: Goal alignment only. Work vs stated strategy mapping.

title: Identify the biggest strategic risks in the next 6 months
prompt: Assess the top strategic risks for the next 6 months — competitor moves, regulatory changes, key dependency failures, team capacity constraints, market shifts. For each, estimate likelihood, impact, and the earliest warning signal.
prompt_short: Identify the top strategic risks in the next 6 months with warning signals.
prompt_filtered: Strategic risk only. Likelihood, impact, and early warning indicators.

title: Define what winning looks like for this product in 1 year
prompt: Define the concrete, measurable outcome that would constitute success for this product in 12 months. Translate the vision into 3-5 specific metrics with target values. Identify which metrics are leading indicators of success vs lagging confirmations.
prompt_short: Define success for this product in 12 months with measurable targets.
prompt_filtered: Success definition only. Measurable 12-month outcomes.

title: Identify assumptions in the product strategy to validate
prompt: List the top 10 assumptions embedded in the current product strategy — about user needs, market size, competitive response, technical feasibility, and team capacity. For each, assess confidence level and recommend the cheapest way to validate or invalidate it.
prompt_short: List the top 10 unvalidated assumptions in the product strategy.
prompt_filtered: Assumptions only. Identify and classify by confidence level.

title: Write a strategic narrative for the product to align the team
prompt: Draft a strategic narrative — a 500-word document explaining why this product exists, what problem it uniquely solves, who it is for, and what winning looks like. This narrative should resolve ambiguity for engineers making daily prioritization decisions without PM guidance.
prompt_short: Write a 500-word strategic narrative that guides daily engineering decisions.
prompt_filtered: Narrative only. Why, who, what, and winning for this product.

title: Identify gaps between stated values and product decisions
prompt: Compare the organization's stated values — privacy-first, user-centric, quality over speed — against recent product decisions. Identify decisions that contradict stated values. These are credibility risks with users and alignment risks within the team. Recommend either a values update or a decision reversal.
prompt_short: Find product decisions that contradict the organization's stated values.
prompt_filtered: Values alignment only. Decisions that contradict stated principles.

title: Assess competitor positioning against the product strategy
prompt: Map the three closest competitors on two dimensions: the user segment they serve and the primary value proposition. Identify the positioning white space — user segments underserved by all competitors. Assess whether the current product strategy claims a distinct position or is competing in a crowded quadrant.
prompt_short: Map competitor positioning and find the white space the product should claim.
prompt_filtered: Competitor positioning only. Positioning map and white space identification.

title: Define the product's moat and what strengthens it over time
prompt: Identify the product's defensible competitive advantage — network effects, proprietary data, switching costs, brand, regulatory advantages. Assess how strong each moat is today and whether it is growing or eroding. Recommend the product investments that most directly strengthen the strongest moat.
prompt_short: Identify the product's moat and the investments that strengthen it over time.
prompt_filtered: Moat analysis only. Defensibility sources and strengthening investments.

title: Identify the biggest strategic bets implied by the current roadmap
prompt: Review the product roadmap and identify the 3-5 biggest implicit strategic bets — assumptions about market direction, user behavior change, technology readiness, or competitive response. Make each bet explicit: state what must be true for the bet to pay off and what the fallback is if it does not.
prompt_short: Make the implicit strategic bets in the roadmap explicit with payoff conditions.
prompt_filtered: Strategic bets only. Implicit assumptions that must be true for the roadmap to succeed.

title: Define the product's BHAG for a 3-year horizon
prompt: Define a Big Hairy Audacious Goal for the product at a 3-year horizon — ambitious enough to require fundamental change, specific enough to evaluate clearly. Distinguish it from the annual OKRs. Make it inspiring to the team while being grounded in a realistic path from today's position.
prompt_short: Define a BHAG for the 3-year horizon that inspires while remaining grounded.
prompt_filtered: BHAG only. 3-year ambitious goal distinct from annual OKRs.

title: Assess the product's position in the market adoption curve
prompt: Evaluate where the product sits on the technology adoption lifecycle — innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, chasm. Identify whether the current strategy is appropriate for the current adoption stage and what strategic shift is required to cross into the next stage.
prompt_short: Identify adoption curve stage and what strategy shift is needed to advance.
prompt_filtered: Adoption curve only. Current stage and next-stage strategy transition.

title: Design a strategic planning process for the next annual cycle
prompt: Design the annual strategic planning process. Include: the inputs (market analysis, competitive review, user research, team capacity), the facilitation structure, the output artifacts (strategic narrative, annual priorities, 3-year vision), the review cadence, and how the output cascades into team OKRs and roadmaps.
prompt_short: Design an annual strategic planning process from inputs to team OKRs.
prompt_filtered: Planning process only. Inputs, facilitation, outputs, and cascade to OKRs.

title: Find the biggest strategic drift between this year and last year
prompt: Compare this year's strategy to last year's plan. Identify the biggest deviations — goals dropped, priorities reversed, new bets added mid-year without removing old ones. Assess whether each deviation represents healthy adaptation or reactive drift. Recommend a process for explicitly documenting strategy pivots.
prompt_short: Compare current strategy to last year's plan and find the biggest deviations.
prompt_filtered: Strategic drift only. Year-over-year strategy comparison and deviation analysis.

title: Communicate the strategy in a format every team member can repeat
prompt: Translate the product strategy into a one-page format that every team member can understand and repeat. Test whether a randomly selected engineer can accurately state the strategy without prompting. If not, rewrite the strategy document in plain language with specific examples.
prompt_short: Rewrite the strategy so every team member can accurately repeat it.
prompt_filtered: Communication format only. Strategy legibility for IC-level team members.

title: Identify strategic dependencies on external partners or platforms
prompt: Map all strategic dependencies on external partners, platforms, or regulatory bodies. Identify dependencies that could unilaterally block the strategy — a platform API change, a partner exit, a regulatory ruling. For each high-risk dependency, assess mitigation options: alternative providers, ownership, or strategy pivots.
prompt_short: Map external dependencies that could unilaterally block the product strategy.
prompt_filtered: External dependencies only. Platform, partner, and regulatory blocking risks.

title: Facilitate a strategy stress-test for the pessimistic case
prompt: Design a strategy stress-test session. Assume the most pessimistic realistic scenario: key competitor ships a free version, a regulatory restriction hits the core feature, a major distribution partner exits. For each scenario, assess whether the strategy survives and what the contingency plan is.
prompt_short: Stress-test the strategy against three pessimistic realistic scenarios.
prompt_filtered: Stress test only. Strategy survival under worst-case realistic conditions.

prompt: Stress Test Strategic Assumptions

title: Map Vision to Market Trends
prompt: Map Vision to Market Trends

title: Identify Strategic Dead Zones
prompt: Identify Strategic Dead Zones

title: Define Winning Conditions
prompt: Define Winning Conditions

title: Challenge Competitive Moat Claims
prompt: Challenge Competitive Moat Claims

title: Evaluate the current product strategy for internal consistency
prompt: Evaluate the current product strategy for internal consistency. Identify whether the stated vision, the target customer segment, the differentiation claim, and the near-term priorities all reinforce each other or work against each other. Flag any strategic contradiction and recommend how to resolve it.

title: Assess the strategy against the competitive landscape
prompt: Assess the strategy against the competitive landscape. Identify the three strongest competitors and what makes them compelling to users who choose them over this product. Find the specific strategic gaps — features, pricing, distribution, or brand — that most account for competitive losses.

title: Identify strategic bets in the current roadmap that have the highest exe
prompt: Identify strategic bets in the current roadmap that have the highest execution risk. For each, assess the assumption that must prove true for the bet to pay off. Recommend validation approaches — experiments, prototypes, or customer conversations — that would reduce uncertainty before full investment.

title: Evaluate the current product vision statement against five criteria: it
prompt: Evaluate the current product vision statement against five criteria: it describes a meaningful change in the world, it is specific enough to guide trade-offs, it is ambitious enough to motivate over a three to five year horizon, it is plausible given the company's current capabilities, and it is differentiated from competitor vision statements. Score each criterion from one to five. Identify the two lowest-scoring criteria and rewrite the vision statement to address them.

title: Identify the three biggest strategic assumptions embedded in the current
prompt: Identify the three biggest strategic assumptions embedded in the current three-year plan. For each assumption, describe what would have to be true for the assumption to hold. Define a leading indicator that would signal whether the assumption is tracking toward being validated or falsified. Recommend a quarterly assumption review process that uses those leading indicators to trigger plan adjustments before the annual planning cycle.

title: Conduct a scenario planning exercise for the next three years
prompt: Conduct a scenario planning exercise for the next three years. Define three plausible scenarios based on different combinations of market growth rate and competitive intensity. For each scenario, describe the world in two sentences, then list the three strategic moves the company would prioritize in that world. Identify the strategic moves that appear in all three scenarios as the must-do investments regardless of how the market evolves.

title: Map the company's current strategic moat against four dimensions: switch
prompt: Map the company's current strategic moat against four dimensions: switching costs, network effects, proprietary data, and operational scale advantages. Rate each dimension from one to five based on evidence from the market. Identify the single strongest moat dimension and the single weakest. Recommend two investments that would materially strengthen the weakest dimension within twelve months.

title: Design the strategic narrative for an upcoming board presentation
prompt: Design the strategic narrative for an upcoming board presentation. The narrative should cover where the market is going, why the company is uniquely positioned to win, what the company is betting on in the next eighteen months, and what success looks like in three years. Draft each section in three to five sentences using language appropriate for a sophisticated investor audience. Flag any section where the supporting evidence is thin or where the logic depends on an unvalidated assumption.

title: Evaluate the clarity and coherence of the product vision and strategy
prompt: Evaluate the clarity and coherence of the product vision and strategy. Assess whether the vision is specific enough to guide day-to-day prioritization decisions or whether it is aspirational enough to be compatible with almost any roadmap. Identify whether the target customer is defined precisely enough that the team could determine whether a proposed feature serves that customer or a different one. Review the differentiation strategy — what makes this product distinctly better for the target customer than available alternatives. Find areas where the strategy is ambiguous, leading different team members to make conflicting assumptions about priorities. Summarize the five strategic clarity improvements that would most align team decision-making.

title: Assess the strategic planning process
prompt: Assess the strategic planning process. Identify how strategy is developed — whether it emerges from customer research, competitive analysis, and market insight, or whether it is driven primarily by internal preferences. Review whether strategy is revisited at appropriate intervals — neither so rarely that it becomes stale nor so frequently that teams cannot execute with stability. Find decisions in the past quarter that conflicted with the stated strategy and assess whether they represent intentional pivots or strategic drift. Evaluate whether the strategy is communicated clearly enough that an engineer or designer making a daily tradeoff decision can use it as a guide. Recommend the strategic process improvements that would most improve decision alignment across the organization.

title: You are a strategy director preparing a three-year strategic plan for a
prompt: You are a strategy director preparing a three-year strategic plan for a SaaS company at a critical growth inflection point. The company has achieved product-market fit in its core segment but faces increasing competitive pressure from both a well-funded startup entering from below and a large incumbent expanding from above. The founding team's instinct is to defend the core market, but several board members are pushing for expansion into an adjacent vertical. Your task is to facilitate a strategy process that produces a defensible, differentiated three-year plan with clear resource allocation priorities. Begin by conducting a thorough situation analysis covering market size and growth rate in both the core and adjacent markets, the company's relative competitive position on the key dimensions customers use to make purchasing decisions, the capabilities required to win in each market, and the financial implications of each strategic choice. Facilitate a leadership team workshop using the Jobs-to-Be-Done framework to articulate what customers are truly hiring the product to do and whether that job is evolving in ways that favor the startup challenger or the incumbent. Apply a capability gap analysis to assess what new capabilities the adjacent market would require versus what the company already has. Design three strategic options ranging from core defense to aggressive expansion, with each option including a resource allocation model, a three-year revenue projection under base and downside assumptions, and the key risks that could cause the option to underperform. Recommend one option with a clear strategic rationale, a set of strategic bets with measurable milestones, and a set of watch-out conditions that would trigger a strategy revision. Deliver the plan as a board-ready presentation with a supporting financial model.

title: You are a chief product officer facilitating a product strategy refresh
prompt: You are a chief product officer facilitating a product strategy refresh after your company's annual planning cycle revealed that the current product roadmap is fragmented across thirteen different strategic priorities with no clear hierarchy. Multiple product teams are building features that serve different customer segments with different success metrics, making it impossible to know whether the portfolio is winning. Your goal is to design a product strategy framework that gives the CEO, the board, and every product team a shared mental model for where to invest and where to stop investing. Begin by conducting a portfolio audit that maps every initiative on the roadmap to the customer segment it serves, the strategic job it advances, and the expected contribution to the company's north star metric. Identify initiatives that cannot be linked to the north star and classify them as either legacy obligations to be wound down or exploratory bets to be time-boxed. Design a three-horizon investment model where horizon one covers the core product improvements that protect and grow the existing customer base, horizon two covers the emerging opportunities that will generate growth in two to three years, and horizon three covers the exploratory bets on opportunities that may become significant in four to five years. Specify the investment ratio across the three horizons appropriate for your company's growth stage and competitive position. Define the governance model for how initiatives move between horizons, including the success criteria that graduate an exploration to a committed investment and the failure criteria that retire an initiative. Create the one-page strategy document that every product team can reference to make local trade-off decisions without escalating to leadership. Deliver the framework, the portfolio audit results, and a proposed roadmap that reflects the new investment priorities.

title: You are a management consultant advising a founder preparing to pitch a
prompt: You are a management consultant advising a founder preparing to pitch a Series B to institutional investors. The founder has built a defensible business in the creator economy with strong product metrics but a narrative that is too feature-focused and fails to convey the strategic insight that makes the company a long-term winner. Your task is to help the founder articulate a compelling strategic vision that stands up to sophisticated investor scrutiny. Begin by identifying the core strategic insight: the non-obvious observation about how the market is changing that the founding team understood before others did and built the product around. Test whether the strategic insight is genuinely non-obvious by asking whether a well-informed generalist investor would agree with it without prompting. Articulate the market timing argument that explains why this insight is actionable now rather than too early or too late. Design the competitive positioning map that shows the two dimensions on which the company is building its strongest differentiation and places three to five competitors on the map to make the positioning concrete. Develop the business model story that explains how the company's strategic position translates into durable pricing power and expanding gross margins over time. Design the growth model narrative that covers the current growth engine, the next growth engine that will activate when the current one saturates, and the long-term structural advantage that keeps the company ahead of well-funded imitators. Anticipate the top five investor objections and write a concise, evidence-based response to each. Deliver the complete strategic narrative as a ten-slide story framework with guidance on the data and evidence required for each slide.

title: Review OKRs for measurability and stretch ambition
prompt: Audit the current OKRs. Identify key results that are not measurable (no number), too easy (already achievable), or too vague to track. For each weak key result, suggest a specific, measurable, time-bound alternative.
prompt_short: Audit OKRs for measurability, vagueness, and appropriate stretch.
prompt_filtered: OKR quality only. Measurability and ambition level assessment.

title: Find OKRs misaligned with the product strategy
prompt: Compare team-level OKRs to the stated product strategy. Identify OKRs that don't contribute to any strategic goal, OKRs that conflict with each other, and strategic goals with no OKR coverage at any team level.
prompt_short: Find OKRs that don't map to strategy or conflict with each other.
prompt_filtered: OKR alignment only. Strategy gap and cross-team conflicts.

title: Assess OKR check-in cadence and confidence tracking
prompt: Review how OKR progress is tracked. Verify there is a regular check-in cadence, confidence scores are updated, at-risk OKRs surface early enough to course-correct, and OKRs are not scored based on completion but on impact achieved.
prompt_short: Assess OKR check-in process and find gaps in confidence tracking.
prompt_filtered: OKR tracking only. Check-in cadence and confidence score practices.

title: Create OKRs for this team's next quarter
prompt: Based on the product strategy and current state, draft 2-3 Objectives and 3 Key Results each for next quarter. Make each KR specific, measurable, and ambitious but achievable. Identify which requires the most assumption validation.
prompt_short: Draft next quarter's OKRs with specific, measurable key results.
prompt_filtered: OKR drafting only. 2-3 objectives with 3 KRs each.

title: Identify top contributors to OKR misses this quarter
prompt: For OKRs that were missed or are at risk this quarter, identify the root causes — estimation errors, external blockers, scope changes, team capacity issues, or invalid underlying assumptions. Recommend process changes to prevent the same issues next quarter.
prompt_short: Find root causes of OKR misses and recommend process improvements.
prompt_filtered: Miss root causes only. Estimation, blockers, and capacity analysis.

title: Design an OKR ladder from company to individual level
prompt: Design an OKR cascade from company-level objectives down to team and individual OKRs. Verify that each level's key results are either a contributing input to the level above or a direct proxy for it. Find objectives that exist at team level with no connection to any company-level goal.
prompt_short: Design an OKR cascade from company to individual with explicit alignment.
prompt_filtered: OKR cascade only. Alignment chain from company to individual level.

title: Find sandbagging patterns in OKR setting
prompt: Analyze historical OKR completion rates. Identify teams or individuals with consistently 90-100% completion — which in the OKR model suggests insufficient stretch ambition. Compare against teams with 60-70% completion, which is the healthy target zone. Recommend recalibration for both extremes.
prompt_short: Find teams always hitting 100% OKRs — a sign of sandbagging, not excellence.
prompt_filtered: Sandbagging detection only. 100% completion as a red flag for insufficient ambition.

title: Separate aspirational OKRs from committed OKRs
prompt: Review the current OKRs and classify each key result as committed (must achieve, plan around) or aspirational (stretch, 70% is success). Identify key results that should be committed but are treated as aspirational, creating false optionality on critical deliverables. Recommend explicit labeling for each.
prompt_short: Classify OKRs as committed vs aspirational and find mislabeled critical KRs.
prompt_filtered: KR type only. Committed vs aspirational distinction and mislabeling.

title: Assess the OKR review meeting for quality and cadence
prompt: Evaluate the OKR check-in meetings. Assess whether they surface at-risk OKRs early enough to course-correct, whether confidence scores are updated honestly, and whether the meeting results in concrete actions or merely status reporting. Find the cadence change that would most improve OKR outcomes.
prompt_short: Assess OKR check-in meeting quality for early warning and action-taking.
prompt_filtered: Review meeting only. At-risk detection, confidence honesty, and action outputs.

title: Find OKRs that incentivize the wrong team behavior
prompt: Identify OKRs that could be hit by behaviors that do not create genuine value — increasing support ticket closure rate by lowering resolution quality, improving signup conversion by removing plan details users need to decide. For each misaligned incentive, recommend a paired counter-metric that makes gaming detectable.
prompt_short: Find OKRs that could be gamed and add counter-metrics to detect it.
prompt_filtered: Incentive alignment only. OKR gaming risk and counter-metric design.

title: Write an OKR retrospective for the completed quarter
prompt: Write a structured OKR retrospective for the completed quarter. For each missed KR, identify root cause: estimation error, external blocker, deprioritization, or invalid assumption. For each hit KR, assess whether success was due to effort or easy baseline. Document learnings that should change next quarter's OKR setting process.
prompt_short: Write an OKR retrospective identifying root causes of hits and misses.
prompt_filtered: OKR retro only. Root cause for each hit and miss, and process learnings.

title: Identify the OKR that best signals company health to the board
prompt: Identify the one OKR — or combination of two — that best signals company health to a board member who has limited context. It should be an outcome metric, not an output metric, and should move only when genuine value is created. Recommend it as the headline metric for board reporting.
prompt_short: Find the OKR that best signals genuine company health for board reporting.
prompt_filtered: Board signal only. Outcome-based metric for executive-level health reporting.

title: Assess cross-team OKR dependencies and coordination gaps
prompt: Identify OKRs from different teams that depend on each other — where Team A's KR requires Team B to ship something. Find these interdependencies and assess whether they are acknowledged by both teams, have a coordination plan, and have an agreed delivery commitment. Missing agreements are roadmap risks.
prompt_short: Find cross-team OKR dependencies with no explicit coordination agreement.
prompt_filtered: Cross-team dependencies only. Unacknowledged OKR interdependencies.

title: Design OKRs for a newly formed team with no baseline data
prompt: Design a first-quarter OKR set for a team that has no historical metric baseline. Include: a measurement establishment objective, a learning objective, and one aspirational outcome objective with a range estimate rather than a point target.
prompt_short: Design first OKRs for a new team with no historical metric baseline.
prompt_filtered: New team OKRs only. Measurement, learning, and aspirational objectives for Q1.

title: Evaluate the OKR tool for adoption and data quality
prompt: Assess whether the OKR tool is adopted consistently across teams. Identify teams with outdated confidence scores, missing check-in notes, or OKRs that were never updated after the first week. Recommend the minimum viable OKR tooling process that gets consistent adoption without creating process overhead.
prompt_short: Assess OKR tool adoption consistency and find teams with stale data.
prompt_filtered: OKR tooling only. Adoption gaps and stale update patterns.

title: Find objectives that are outputs not outcomes
prompt: Review every objective in the current OKR set. Identify objectives stated as outputs (ship the feature, complete the migration) rather than outcomes (improve activation rate, reduce churn). Rewrite output-stated objectives as outcomes while preserving the underlying intent. This makes the OKRs measure value delivered, not work done.
prompt_short: Rewrite output-stated OKR objectives as outcomes that measure value delivered.
prompt_filtered: Output vs outcome only. Rewrite objectives to measure impact not activity.

title: Assess OKR grading honesty and calibration across teams
prompt: Review end-of-quarter OKR grades across all teams. Find teams that consistently grade key results as fully achieved without accompanying evidence of impact. Assess whether grading criteria are documented and applied consistently. Recommend a grading rubric and peer-review step that increases honesty and comparability.
prompt_short: Assess OKR grading consistency and find teams grading without impact evidence.
prompt_filtered: Grading calibration only. Honesty and consistency of KR scoring across teams.

title: Fill in Missing Data Gaps
prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

title: Gain full understanding before completing
prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

title: Find What's Broken
prompt: Find What's Broken

title: Recommend Next Steps
prompt: Recommend Next Steps

title: Score for Production Readiness
prompt: Score for Production Readiness

title: Audit OKR Alignment
prompt: Audit OKR Alignment

title: Check Key Result Measurability
prompt: Check Key Result Measurability

title: Rewrite Vague Key Results
prompt: Rewrite Vague Key Results

title: Align Team OKRs to Company
prompt: Align Team OKRs to Company

title: Identify Sandbagged Targets
prompt: Identify Sandbagged Targets

title: Check OKR Measurement Feasibility
prompt: Check OKR Measurement Feasibility

title: Remove Conflicting Key Results
prompt: Remove Conflicting Key Results

title: Audit all current OKRs for measurability
prompt: Audit all current OKRs for measurability. Identify key results that cannot be objectively scored at the end of the period — those with no defined data source or measurement methodology. For each unmeasurable KR, recommend a specific, queryable metric that would serve as an objective proxy.

title: Find OKRs where all key results measure output — features shipped, tasks
prompt: Find OKRs where all key results measure output — features shipped, tasks completed — rather than outcome — user behavior changed, business metric improved. For each output-only KR, recommend an outcome-based replacement that would make it harder to hit by doing low-value work.

title: Assess OKR cascade completeness
prompt: Assess OKR cascade completeness. Identify team-level OKRs with no visible connection to a company-level objective. For each disconnected OKR, either recommend the company objective it should link to, or question whether the team's work is strategically aligned.

title: Audit the current quarter's OKRs for measurability
prompt: Audit the current quarter's OKRs for measurability. For each key result, verify that a metric exists, that the baseline value is documented, and that the data source and measurement method are specified. Flag key results defined in qualitative terms such as "improve" or "enhance" without a quantified target. Rewrite each flagged key result with a specific numeric target, a measurement method, and a baseline value.

title: Evaluate the alignment between team-level OKRs and company-level OKRs
prompt: Evaluate the alignment between team-level OKRs and company-level OKRs. Map each team key result to the company objective it supports. Identify team key results that cannot be traced to a company objective and classify them as operational tasks that should move to the team's project tracker rather than OKRs. Calculate the percentage of company key result value that is covered by team-level ownership. Flag any company key result with no team-level owner.

title: Assess the ambition level of the current OKRs using a stretch target fra
prompt: Assess the ambition level of the current OKRs using a stretch target framework. For each key result, compare the target to historical performance and ask whether achieving the target would require a meaningfully different approach or merely better execution of the current approach. Flag key results where the target is achievable without any new initiatives or capabilities. Rewrite those key results with targets that require at least one significant behavior change to achieve.

title: Design the OKR grading process for the end of the current quarter
prompt: Design the OKR grading process for the end of the current quarter. Define the grading scale for key results, specifying what scores of zero, point three, point seven, and one point zero represent in terms of target achievement. Write guidance on how to handle key results that were invalidated mid-quarter due to changes in business conditions. Design a grade calibration meeting agenda that ensures grades reflect actual impact rather than activity level. Specify how OKR grades feed into the next quarter's planning cycle.

title: Write OKRs for a newly formed platform engineering team responsible for
prompt: Write OKRs for a newly formed platform engineering team responsible for reducing developer toil across a twenty-team engineering organization. Define one objective that captures the team's mission in inspirational language. Write four key results that are specific, measurable, and directly under the team's influence, covering internal developer satisfaction, deployment frequency improvement, incident rate reduction, and platform adoption. Verify that each key result can be measured with data the team controls or can access within the current quarter.

title: Review the OKR framework for the current planning period
prompt: Review the OKR framework for the current planning period. Identify all objectives and key results at the company and team level. Assess whether each objective describes a meaningful outcome rather than a project or activity. Check whether key results are quantified with a current baseline, a target, and a measurement method. Find any key result that relies on data that is not currently collected — making it impossible to score objectively. Assess whether the OKR hierarchy is coherent — whether achieving all team KRs would plausibly achieve the company objectives they map to. Summarize the five OKR quality improvements that would most strengthen the planning framework.

title: Evaluate how effectively OKRs guide team behavior
prompt: Evaluate how effectively OKRs guide team behavior. Assess whether team members reference OKRs when making daily prioritization decisions or treat them as a compliance exercise. Find any significant project in progress that does not map to a current OKR — representing either strategic misalignment or an OKR that is too narrow. Review the scoring process — whether teams score their OKRs objectively or with excessive optimism. Assess whether OKR retrospectives produce learning that improves the next planning cycle. Identify the OKR process changes that would most increase the connection between planned objectives and actual work delivered.

title: You are a chief of staff facilitating the quarterly OKR review for a two
prompt: You are a chief of staff facilitating the quarterly OKR review for a two-hundred-person technology company. The company has been running OKRs for three years, but the program has drifted from its original intent: OKRs have become synonymous with the full project backlog, key results are mostly binary completion markers rather than outcome metrics, and the grading exercise at quarter-end is a formality that rarely influences the next quarter's planning. Your mandate is to reset the OKR program in a way that reconnects it to strategic decision-making and makes it genuinely useful to team leads rather than a compliance exercise. Begin by conducting a diagnostic survey of team leads to identify the specific pain points with the current OKR process, distinguishing frustrations with the tool implementation from frustrations with the method itself. Facilitate a leadership alignment session to reestablish the core principle that OKRs are for organizational learning and resource prioritization, not performance management, and to get explicit buy-in on what that means for how grades are used. Redesign the OKR writing process to include a structured pre-flight check that validates each key result against four criteria: it measures an outcome rather than an output, it has a numeric target grounded in a baseline, it is ambitious but not impossible, and it is owned by a single team. Design the mid-quarter OKR health check meeting format, including how to surface at-risk key results and make explicit resource reallocation decisions. Create the quarter-end retrospective format that extracts learnings from both high-scoring and low-scoring OKRs and feeds those learnings directly into the next planning cycle. Develop the facilitator guide and the updated OKR writing template. Deliver a program reset plan with a twelve-week implementation timeline and a success metric for the first quarter under the new approach.

title: You are a product manager writing OKRs for a product growth team in a co
prompt: You are a product manager writing OKRs for a product growth team in a company that is transitioning from founder-led sales to a product-led growth motion. The company has historically measured success by the number of enterprise deals closed, but the new motion requires measuring activation, expansion, and self-serve conversion at scale. Your task is to write a complete OKR set for the next quarter that reflects the new motion and creates accountability for the metrics that will determine whether PLG is working. Begin by defining the company-level objective that this team's OKRs roll up to, framing it around the business impact of a successful PLG transition rather than the activities involved. Write the team-level objective in language that is ambitious and describes the change the team is driving rather than the tasks it will complete. Write four to five key results that cover the leading indicators of PLG success including time to first value for new signups, activation rate at day seven, expansion revenue from self-serve accounts, and the ratio of product-qualified leads to sales-qualified leads. For each key result, document the current baseline, the target for the quarter, the data source, and the team member responsible for tracking it. Identify the single key result that is the strongest leading indicator of long-term retention and label it as the primary key result. Write a brief rationale explaining why this set of OKRs represents an appropriate level of ambition given the company's current PLG maturity. Anticipate the top two objections from leadership and write a concise response to each. Deliver the complete OKR set in a format ready for the planning document.

title: You are an OKR program lead designing an OKR framework for a fast-growin
prompt: You are an OKR program lead designing an OKR framework for a fast-growing startup that is setting company-wide OKRs for the first time. The company has thirty employees, is growing at fifteen percent month over month, and has never had a formal goal-setting process. The founders want OKRs to help the team stay aligned as headcount doubles in the next twelve months without requiring a large program management overhead. Your task is to design a lightweight OKR framework that is rigorous enough to drive alignment but simple enough that a thirty-person team can run it without a dedicated operations function. Begin by selecting the right OKR cadence for a company at this growth stage, choosing between annual, quarterly, and six-week cycles based on how quickly the business context is evolving. Define the number of OKRs appropriate for the company as a whole and for each team, establishing a maximum that prevents the objective list from becoming a catch-all. Design the company OKR writing workshop agenda that the founders will use to draft the first set of company objectives with the full leadership team, covering market context review, priority stack-ranking, objective drafting, and key result definition. Specify the check-in ritual, including frequency, format, and the specific questions the team will answer at each check-in to keep OKRs from becoming shelf documents. Define the grading approach that distinguishes a healthy stretch miss from a planning failure. Design the OKR retrospective format that the team will use at the end of the first cycle to improve the process for the next cycle. Create the OKR calendar for the first year, including kickoff, mid-cycle check-ins, grade review, and retrospective events. Deliver the framework as a single reference document the founders can share at the company all-hands.

title: Identify the biggest dependency risks in the roadmap
prompt: Review the current roadmap for hard dependencies — items that cannot start until another is complete, external team deliverables, third-party release schedules. Identify the critical path and the single dependency whose slip would delay the most.
prompt_short: Find the critical path and highest-risk dependency in the roadmap.
prompt_filtered: Dependency risk only. Critical path and slip impact analysis.

title: Find technical work missing from the product roadmap
prompt: Review the product roadmap and identify technical investments that are not represented — infrastructure upgrades, security hardening, test coverage, performance improvements, technical debt paydown. Assess whether the roadmap is sustainable without these.
prompt_short: Find technical investments absent from the roadmap that are needed.
prompt_filtered: Missing tech work only. Infrastructure, security, and debt not in roadmap.

title: Assess roadmap feasibility given current team capacity
prompt: Compare the roadmap commitments to the team's estimated capacity. Identify the quarters that are over-committed, the items most at risk of slipping, and the items that should be de-scoped or deferred to make the roadmap realistic.
prompt_short: Assess roadmap feasibility against team capacity and find what to cut.
prompt_filtered: Capacity vs roadmap only. Over-commitment identification.

title: Create a NOW/NEXT/LATER framework for the backlog
prompt: Organize the current backlog into NOW (this quarter), NEXT (next 1-2 quarters), and LATER (beyond 6 months). For each item in NOW, verify it has a clear owner, scope, and success criteria. Flag items in NOW without these.
prompt_short: Organize the backlog into NOW/NEXT/LATER with clear ownership per item.
prompt_filtered: Prioritization framework only. NOW/NEXT/LATER classification.

title: Review roadmap communication for stakeholder clarity
prompt: Assess how the roadmap is communicated to stakeholders. Identify information that is present for engineering but not for business stakeholders (why each item matters, business impact, risk), and information missing for engineers (acceptance criteria, technical constraints).
prompt_short: Review roadmap communication for gaps for both engineers and stakeholders.
prompt_filtered: Communication gaps only. Different audience information needs.

title: Create an opportunity solution tree for the top user problem
prompt: Build an opportunity solution tree for the top-priority user problem. Map the desired outcome at the root, identify 3-5 opportunity spaces, and under each opportunity list 2-3 candidate solutions. Identify which opportunity-solution pairs have the most evidence supporting them and which are assumptions.
prompt_short: Build an opportunity solution tree from user problem to candidate solutions.
prompt_filtered: OST only. Desired outcome, opportunities, and solution candidates.

title: Prioritize roadmap items using RICE scoring
prompt: Score the top 20 roadmap items using the RICE framework: Reach (users affected per quarter), Impact (0.25-3 scale), Confidence (percentage based on evidence), Effort (person-months). Rank by RICE score. Identify items ranked high by intuition but low by RICE — these gaps reveal hidden prioritization biases.
prompt_short: Score the top 20 roadmap items with RICE and find prioritization biases.
prompt_filtered: RICE scoring only. Top 20 backlog items ranked with bias analysis.

title: Find theme-based vs feature-based roadmap communication gaps
prompt: Assess whether the roadmap communicates at the theme level for external stakeholders versus the feature level for engineering. Identify stakeholder audiences receiving engineering-level detail that makes commitments before design is complete.
prompt_short: Find roadmap communications making premature feature-level commitments to stakeholders.
prompt_filtered: Roadmap communication only. Theme vs feature level per audience.

title: Identify which roadmap items have no clear success metric
prompt: Review all roadmap items in the NOW and NEXT columns. Identify items with no defined success metric — no measurable outcome that would confirm the item achieved its intended impact after launch. For each, add a primary success metric and a threshold that would trigger a follow-up improvement iteration.
prompt_short: Find roadmap items with no success metric defined before build starts.
prompt_filtered: Success metrics only. Items with no measurable outcome defined.

title: Evaluate the confidence basis for each roadmap item
prompt: For each roadmap item, document the evidence supporting it: user research, behavioral data, competitive analysis, stakeholder mandate, or intuition. Classify each item by confidence level. Find items in the NOW column backed only by intuition or stakeholder mandate — these have the highest risk of being wrong and expensive.
prompt_short: Document the evidence basis for each roadmap item and find low-confidence NOW items.
prompt_filtered: Evidence basis only. Confidence classification per roadmap item.

title: Design a stakeholder roadmap review process
prompt: Design a quarterly roadmap review process for stakeholders. Include: what level of detail is shared, how to handle requests to add items that would displace committed work, the communication format for roadmap changes mid-quarter, and the boundary between stakeholder input and PM decision authority.
prompt_short: Design a quarterly stakeholder roadmap review with decision authority boundaries.
prompt_filtered: Review process only. Stakeholder input, change communication, and PM authority.

title: Find technical prerequisites missing from the feature roadmap
prompt: Review the next quarter's feature roadmap against the current technical architecture. Identify features that require technical prerequisites — data migrations, infrastructure upgrades, new service integrations — that are not in the roadmap. Missing prerequisites are hidden blockers that will surface mid-sprint.
prompt_short: Find technical prerequisites for next quarter's features not yet on the roadmap.
prompt_filtered: Technical prerequisites only. Hidden blockers for committed feature work.

title: Assess the roadmap for scope appropriate to team velocity
prompt: Calculate the estimated total scope of the now/next roadmap in story points or person-weeks. Compare against the team's trailing 3-month average velocity. Identify the quarters where committed scope exceeds achievable velocity by more than 20%. Recommend which items to defer to restore realism.
prompt_short: Compare roadmap scope to team velocity and find over-committed quarters.
prompt_filtered: Velocity vs scope only. Over-commitment identification and deferral recommendations.

title: Create a customer-facing public roadmap with the right detail level
prompt: Design a public-facing roadmap for customers that communicates direction without making premature shipping commitments. Specify which items to share (themes and problem spaces), which to withhold (competitive-sensitive work, uncertain timelines), the review cadence, and how to handle customer questions about specific timelines.
prompt_short: Design a public customer roadmap that communicates direction without commitments.
prompt_filtered: Public roadmap only. What to share, withhold, and how to handle timeline questions.

title: Identify roadmap items that should be experiments first
prompt: Review the roadmap for items with high uncertainty about user value. These are candidates to run as experiments — a lightweight version tested with a subset of users — before committing to full build. Identify the experiment-first candidates and estimate the engineering saved if the experiment invalidates the hypothesis.
prompt_short: Find roadmap items uncertain enough to run as experiments before full build.
prompt_filtered: Experiment-first candidates only. High uncertainty items in the NOW column.

title: Write a roadmap brief for each major initiative
prompt: For each major roadmap initiative, write a one-page brief covering: the problem being solved, the user segments affected, the proposed solution approach, the success metrics, the key assumptions, and the major risks. This brief replaces lengthy PRDs for alignment without over-specifying implementation before discovery is complete.
prompt_short: Write a one-page brief for each major roadmap initiative before engineering starts.
prompt_filtered: Initiative brief only. Problem, approach, metrics, assumptions, and risks.

title: Assess roadmap balance between new features and reliability work
prompt: Calculate the proportion of roadmap capacity allocated to new feature work versus reliability, performance, and technical debt. Benchmark against the team's current reliability scores and technical debt backlog volume. Recommend the rebalancing that would most improve long-term development velocity without sacrificing near-term growth.
prompt_short: Calculate the new feature vs reliability investment ratio in the roadmap.
prompt_filtered: Roadmap balance only. Feature vs reliability capacity split analysis.
