prompt: Audit Roadmap Commitments

title: Check Dependency Map
prompt: Check Dependency Map

title: Surface Hidden Dependencies
prompt: Surface Hidden Dependencies

title: Rebalance Tech Debt vs Feature Work
prompt: Rebalance Tech Debt vs Feature Work

title: Validate Effort Estimates
prompt: Validate Effort Estimates

title: Identify Roadmap Gaps
prompt: Identify Roadmap Gaps

title: Challenge Low-Priority Items
prompt: Challenge Low-Priority Items

title: Review all items currently on the product roadmap
prompt: Review all items currently on the product roadmap. For each, identify what customer or business problem it solves and what evidence supports the prioritization. Find roadmap items with no documented problem statement or user evidence. Recommend a discovery process for the top three unsupported items before investment continues.

title: Identify roadmap items with cross-team dependencies that are not reflect
prompt: Identify roadmap items with cross-team dependencies that are not reflected in the delivery plan. For each unresolved dependency, assess the risk to the committed timeline. Recommend the dependency resolution conversation or planning adjustment needed to make the roadmap realistic.

title: Assess the roadmap time horizon balance
prompt: Assess the roadmap time horizon balance. Find how much work is allocated to near-term feature delivery versus platform investments, technical debt, and discovery. Identify whether the current balance would sustain velocity at 6 months, 12 months, and 24 months. Recommend rebalancing if the ratio is unsustainable.

title: Audit the current product roadmap for dependency completeness
prompt: Audit the current product roadmap for dependency completeness. List every initiative planned for the next two quarters. For each initiative, identify its technical, design, and cross-team dependencies. Flag any dependency that has not been confirmed with the owning team. Identify the critical path through the dependency graph and highlight the single dependency whose delay would push the most initiatives. Output a dependency risk register.

title: Evaluate the balance of the roadmap across four investment categories: n
prompt: Evaluate the balance of the roadmap across four investment categories: new feature development, platform and infrastructure, technical debt remediation, and compliance and security. Calculate the percentage of engineering capacity allocated to each category for the next quarter. Benchmark this allocation against the recommendation for a company at your growth stage. Identify whether any category is significantly under-invested and recommend a rebalancing that addresses the gap without disrupting committed deliverables.

title: Conduct a roadmap confidence assessment
prompt: Conduct a roadmap confidence assessment. For every initiative planned in the next quarter, rate confidence in the scope definition, the effort estimate, and the priority ranking on a scale of high, medium, or low. Flag initiatives with low confidence in any dimension. For low-confidence initiatives, define the single most important question that must be answered before the initiative can be reliably estimated. Prioritize discovery work for the top three most uncertain initiatives.

title: Assess stakeholder alignment on the roadmap for the next two quarters
prompt: Assess stakeholder alignment on the roadmap for the next two quarters. List the five most significant roadmap items and rate the level of agreement among engineering, product, design, sales, and customer success on the priority of each item. Identify any item where there is significant disagreement across stakeholder groups. For each contested item, articulate the strongest argument for and against its current priority. Recommend a facilitation approach for resolving each disagreement.

title: Design the roadmap communication plan for the next planning cycle
prompt: Design the roadmap communication plan for the next planning cycle. Define the audience segments including engineering teams, executive leadership, sales, customer success, and customers. For each audience, specify the level of roadmap detail appropriate, the format, and the update frequency. Create a roadmap FAQ that answers the ten most common questions stakeholders ask about roadmap priorities and timelines. Define the process for incorporating stakeholder feedback into roadmap revisions.

title: Evaluate the product roadmap for strategic alignment and delivery realis
prompt: Evaluate the product roadmap for strategic alignment and delivery realism. Identify every roadmap item and assess whether it maps to a stated company objective. Find items that are on the roadmap based on stakeholder requests rather than user evidence or strategic fit. Assess whether the estimated effort for each item is based on a structured estimate or a rough guess. Identify sequencing dependencies between items that are not reflected in the ordering. Look for any item that has been on the roadmap for more than two planning cycles without progress — indicating either persistent deprioritization or an underestimated scope. Summarize the five roadmap quality improvements that would most increase delivery predictability.

title: Review the roadmap planning process
prompt: Review the roadmap planning process. Identify how items get added to the roadmap — whether through a structured intake process with defined criteria or through ad-hoc requests. Assess whether roadmap items are validated with users before significant engineering investment. Find the average age of items on the roadmap — how long from ideation to delivery. Assess whether the roadmap is communicated clearly to all stakeholders, including engineering teams who need to make architectural decisions based on future direction. Review whether the roadmap is treated as a commitment or a living plan — and whether stakeholders understand the difference. Recommend the planning process improvements that would most increase roadmap confidence and stakeholder trust.

title: You are a director of product management facilitating the annual roadmap
prompt: You are a director of product management facilitating the annual roadmap planning process for a platform company with four product teams and an engineering organization of eighty engineers. The company has historically produced an annual roadmap in January that is abandoned by March as market conditions shift, leaving teams without clear direction for the remaining three quarters. Your mandate is to design a planning process that produces a roadmap flexible enough to adapt to change while providing enough stability for engineering to make sound architectural decisions. Begin by establishing the planning horizon philosophy: a committed three-month horizon with high confidence, a directional six-month horizon with medium confidence, and a thematic twelve-month horizon that captures strategic bets without committing to specific features. Design the bottom-up discovery process where each product team contributes a prioritized opportunity backlog informed by customer research, support data, and sales feedback before any top-down priorities are communicated. Facilitate the leadership alignment session where the executive team stacks-ranks the top twenty opportunities against a weighted scoring rubric covering strategic alignment, revenue impact, customer satisfaction impact, and implementation feasibility. Design the dependency mapping workshop that surfaces cross-team dependencies before the roadmap is finalized and creates a shared understanding of the critical path. Specify the roadmap governance model including the change control process for mid-quarter additions, the stakeholder review cadence, and the criteria that make a change urgent enough to displace a committed item. Design the roadmap communication artifacts for each audience segment, from the one-page customer-facing roadmap that avoids commitment dates to the detailed internal Gantt that engineering uses for sprint planning. Deliver the planning process guide, the opportunity scoring rubric, and the roadmap template as a ready-to-use package for the next planning cycle.

title: You are a senior product manager building the first structured roadmap f
prompt: You are a senior product manager building the first structured roadmap for a startup that has been operating in pure discovery mode for eighteen months and is now preparing for a Series A fundraise. The founding team has shipped forty-three features based on customer requests with no strategic filter, and the product now has a reputation for breadth but lacks the depth in any one area that would make it a clear category leader. Your task is to design a roadmap that shifts the company from reactive to strategic and demonstrates to investors that the team understands where to focus to win the market. Begin by conducting a portfolio audit of all forty-three shipped features, classifying each as core differentiator, table stakes, or nice-to-have based on customer usage data and competitive analysis. For the core differentiator features, assess the depth of investment made and identify where the company is superficially competitive versus where it has a genuine advantage. Design a focus framework that selects the two to three use cases where the product can be the undisputed best solution and explicitly deprioritizes the remaining use cases for the next twelve months. Apply a Jobs-to-Be-Done analysis to the selected use cases to identify the specific outcomes customers are trying to achieve and the gaps in the current product that prevent those outcomes. Build the twelve-month roadmap structured around three themes, each tied to a specific customer outcome, with each theme having a north star metric that defines success. Specify the capacity allocation across the three themes and the percentage reserved for technical debt that has accumulated during the reactive phase. Design the investor narrative that frames the roadmap as a deliberate choice to win specific segments rather than a reduction of ambition. Deliver the roadmap, the focus framework, and the investor presentation outline.

title: You are a chief of staff designing the quarterly planning process for a
prompt: You are a chief of staff designing the quarterly planning process for a company that runs three-month planning cycles across twelve product teams. The current planning process takes six weeks, requires over two hundred person-hours of meetings, and produces a roadmap that individual team leads describe as disconnected from the daily decisions they actually face. Engineering leaders report that the planning process creates alignment at the top but fails to cascade into actionable team-level plans. Your task is to redesign the planning process to produce the same strategic alignment in half the time while creating stronger team-level ownership. Begin by auditing the current process to identify the steps that generate the most alignment value versus the steps that are administrative overhead with low alignment payoff. Design a pre-planning phase where each team completes a structured self-assessment covering capacity, tech debt load, in-flight commitments, and top three customer problems before the first planning meeting, reducing the time spent on status updates during planning sessions. Design the strategy translation workshop that converts company-level strategic priorities into team-level focus areas using a structured cascade exercise that each team lead runs with their team before submitting a draft plan. Create the planning document template that forces teams to articulate the customer problem their plan addresses, the metric that will move if the plan succeeds, and the dependencies they need resolved before work can begin. Design the final roadmap review meeting as a thirty-minute per team rapid review rather than a four-hour all-hands, with asynchronous pre-reads and real-time decisions only on escalated conflicts. Specify the post-planning communication package including the executive summary, the team-level one-pagers, and the dependency registry. Deliver the redesigned process as a step-by-step planning guide with time allocations, templates, and a facilitator checklist.

title: Audit ticket quality for clarity and completeness
prompt: Review the 20 most recent tickets assigned to engineering. Identify tickets missing: acceptance criteria, context for why the work matters, technical constraints, definition of done, or design references. Score ticket quality and identify the most common gaps.
prompt_short: Audit recent tickets for missing acceptance criteria and context.
prompt_filtered: Ticket quality only. Missing fields and clarity issues.

title: Find features built without validating user need
prompt: Identify features shipped in the last 6 months that were built based on internal assumptions rather than validated user research or data. For each, assess whether usage data confirms the assumption or suggests it was wrong.
prompt_short: Find features shipped without user validation and assess their usage.
prompt_filtered: Validation gaps only. Assumption-built features and their outcomes.

title: Identify scope creep patterns in recent sprints
prompt: Review the last 5 sprints for scope changes mid-sprint — added tickets, expanded ticket scope, changed acceptance criteria. Identify the sources of scope creep and the patterns (specific stakeholders, feature types, or estimation errors).
prompt_short: Find recurring scope creep patterns across recent sprints.
prompt_filtered: Scope creep only. Mid-sprint additions and scope changes.

title: Define minimum viable definition of done for this team
prompt: Create a definition of done for this team's context — what must be true before a ticket is closed: tests written, docs updated, feature flagged, analytics event added, performance benchmarked, security reviewed. Tailor to the team's tech stack.
prompt_short: Create a definition of done checklist for this team's specific context.
prompt_filtered: Definition of done only. Required conditions before ticket close.

title: Find the biggest discovery process gaps before building
prompt: Assess the product discovery process. Identify steps that are skipped before building — user research, competitor analysis, technical spike, design prototype, stakeholder alignment. Find the gaps most likely to result in building the wrong thing.
prompt_short: Find discovery process steps being skipped before engineering starts.
prompt_filtered: Discovery gaps only. Pre-build validation steps not happening.

title: Audit product-market fit signals across the user base
prompt: Evaluate product-market fit using the Sean Ellis test, retention curve shape, and qualitative signals. Identify the user segment most likely to report very disappointed if the product disappeared, and the segment showing the weakest retention. Recommend the PMF focus — whether to deepen fit with the strongest segment or broaden fit.
prompt_short: Assess PMF signals and identify which user segment has the strongest fit.
prompt_filtered: PMF signals only. Ellis test, retention curve, and segment-level fit.

title: Assess the discovery-to-delivery ratio in the team's process
prompt: Calculate the ratio of time spent in product discovery (research, prototyping, validation) versus delivery (building, testing, releasing). Identify whether the team is in a perpetual build mode with inadequate discovery, or in analysis paralysis with insufficient delivery. Recommend a cadence that balances both.
prompt_short: Find whether the team spends too much time building vs discovering.
prompt_filtered: Discovery/delivery ratio only. Time allocation and recommended rebalancing.

title: Find features with no usage data after 90 days in production
prompt: Identify features shipped more than 90 days ago with no usage instrumentation or with instrumentation showing near-zero usage. Assess whether the low usage is due to poor discoverability, wrong targeting, or a failed hypothesis. Recommend whether to improve, deprecate, or leave each feature.
prompt_short: Find features shipped 90+ days ago with no usage data or zero usage.
prompt_filtered: Post-launch usage only. Features with no measurement or near-zero adoption.

title: Evaluate the activation rate and aha moment timing
prompt: Analyze the activation funnel to find what percentage of new users reach the product's aha moment — the first action that predicts long-term retention. Measure the time to aha from signup. Identify onboarding steps that delay or prevent reaching the aha moment and recommend the friction removals most likely to improve activation rate.
prompt_short: Measure activation rate and time to aha moment, then find what delays it.
prompt_filtered: Activation only. Aha moment identification and time-to-aha reduction.

title: Identify the highest-leverage feature for expansion revenue
prompt: Analyze the correlation between feature adoption and expansion revenue — upgrades, seat additions, plan tier increases. Identify the feature whose adoption most strongly predicts upsell. Recommend whether to make it more prominent in the core experience, gate it behind the higher plan, or use it as a conversion trigger.
prompt_short: Find the feature whose adoption most predicts expansion revenue.
prompt_filtered: Expansion triggers only. Feature adoption vs upsell correlation.

title: Audit the product specification format for engineer usability
prompt: Evaluate the current product specification format from an engineer's perspective. Identify specifications that lack clear acceptance criteria, are missing edge case handling, omit error state designs, or do not specify what happens when dependencies are unavailable. Recommend a spec template that eliminates engineering questions before sprint planning.
prompt_short: Audit product specs for missing edge cases, error states, and acceptance criteria.
prompt_filtered: Spec format only. Engineering usability and missing required spec sections.

title: Find the biggest misalignment between sales and product roadmap
prompt: Compare the top 10 feature requests from the sales team against the current roadmap. Identify features being promised to prospects that are not on the roadmap, features on the roadmap that sales has never mentioned to prospects, and the misalignment cost — deals delayed or lost due to missing features.
prompt_short: Compare sales feature promises to roadmap reality and find the gaps.
prompt_filtered: Sales-product alignment only. Promised vs planned features.

title: Assess the retention curve shape for product health
prompt: Analyze the product's user retention curve — the percentage of users still active at days 1, 7, 30, and 90 after signup. Identify whether the curve flattens, continues declining, or has a step drop at a specific point. Recommend the intervention for the most impactful curve improvement.
prompt_short: Analyze retention curve shape and find the intervention that most improves it.
prompt_filtered: Retention curve only. Day 1/7/30/90 shape analysis and step-drop identification.

title: Review feature flags usage and cleanup backlog
prompt: Audit all active feature flags in production. Identify flags that were used for a gradual rollout that is now 100% — dead code adding complexity. Find flags in partial rollout for more than 3 months without a decision to complete or revert. Recommend a cleanup plan and a flag retirement process.
prompt_short: Audit feature flags for stale 100% rollouts and stalled partial rollouts.
prompt_filtered: Feature flags only. Dead code flags and stalled partial rollout decisions.

title: Map competitive feature parity gaps to churn reasons
prompt: Compare the product's feature set against the top two competitors for features mentioned in churn surveys or exit interviews. Identify features cited as churn reasons that competitors have and the product lacks. Prioritize by churn frequency and the segment size affected.
prompt_short: Map competitor feature advantages to user churn reasons and prioritize gaps.
prompt_filtered: Competitive parity only. Features causing churn that competitors have.

title: Evaluate whether the beta program generates usable pre-launch signal
prompt: Assess the beta program structure. Identify whether beta users represent the target production user, whether feedback is collected systematically, whether beta exit criteria are defined, and whether past beta feedback has meaningfully changed launch decisions.
prompt_short: Assess whether the beta program generates signal that changes launch decisions.
prompt_filtered: Beta program only. Participant representativeness and feedback utilization.

title: Write a product teardown of the top competitor for the team
prompt: Write a structured product teardown of the top competitor: onboarding flow, core feature set, pricing model, design language, technical constraints visible from the UX, and areas where they appear to be investing based on recent changes. Identify their likely strategic bets and where your product has a genuine advantage.
prompt_short: Write a structured product teardown of the top competitor for team alignment.
prompt_filtered: Competitive teardown only. UX, features, pricing, and strategic bets.

title: Fill in Missing Data Gaps
prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

title: Gain full understanding before completing
prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

title: Find What's Broken
prompt: Find What's Broken

title: Recommend Next Steps
prompt: Recommend Next Steps

title: Score for Production Readiness
prompt: Score for Production Readiness

title: Audit PM Process
prompt: Audit PM Process

title: Check Discovery Rigor
prompt: Check Discovery Rigor

title: Define Problem Statement Precisely
prompt: Define Problem Statement Precisely

title: Separate Solution from Problem
prompt: Separate Solution from Problem

title: Validate Before Building
prompt: Validate Before Building

title: Write Acceptance Criteria
prompt: Write Acceptance Criteria

title: Identify Hidden Stakeholders
prompt: Identify Hidden Stakeholders

title: Audit the product discovery process
prompt: Audit the product discovery process. Identify the last five features shipped and trace each to its originating insight — user research, customer feedback, competitive analysis, or internal idea. Find features with no documented discovery phase. Recommend the minimum discovery step that would reduce the risk of building the wrong thing.

title: Assess the definition of done for product requirements
prompt: Assess the definition of done for product requirements. Find the most recent feature where the engineering team had to make significant assumptions because the requirements were incomplete. Identify the specific gaps — edge cases, error states, success metrics — that were missing. Recommend the requirement template that would prevent the same gaps in future specifications.

title: Review product-engineering collaboration health
prompt: Review product-engineering collaboration health. Identify the most recent sprint where engineering discovered a requirement ambiguity that caused rework. Trace where in the process the ambiguity should have been caught. Recommend the collaboration touchpoint or artifact that would catch similar ambiguities before they reach development.

title: Write a complete product requirements document for the top-priority feat
prompt: Write a complete product requirements document for the top-priority feature in the current roadmap. The PRD must include the problem statement with quantified user impact, the target user segment, the success metrics with baseline and target values, the in-scope and out-of-scope definition, three to five user stories with acceptance criteria, the key design constraints, and the open questions that must be resolved before development begins. Review the PRD with the engineering lead before finalizing.

title: Conduct a product discovery sprint for an unvalidated opportunity on the
prompt: Conduct a product discovery sprint for an unvalidated opportunity on the roadmap. Define the specific assumption about user behavior that must be validated before committing engineering resources. Design the lightest-weight experiment that can validate or falsify the assumption within two weeks. Specify the sample size required, the success criterion, and the decision rule that will determine whether to proceed, pivot, or stop. Run the experiment and document findings.

title: Evaluate the feature requests in the backlog that have been open for ove
prompt: Evaluate the feature requests in the backlog that have been open for over six months. For each request, check whether the underlying customer problem has been validated through research, whether the request has a champion on the customer success or sales team, and whether a workaround currently exists. Classify each request as urgent customer pain, nice-to-have, or already solved by another feature. Archive the already-solved category and prioritize the urgent pain items.

title: Design the product launch plan for a feature shipping in the next sprint
prompt: Design the product launch plan for a feature shipping in the next sprint. Define the internal readiness checklist covering documentation, support training, and analytics instrumentation. Write the launch communication plan for existing customers including the in-app notification, the email announcement, and the help center article. Define the rollout strategy including the percentage of users in the initial release and the criteria for a full rollout. Specify the rollback plan if a critical bug is found post-launch.

title: Assess the quality of the product feedback loop
prompt: Assess the quality of the product feedback loop. Map every channel through which customer feedback reaches the product team including support tickets, sales calls, NPS surveys, in-app feedback widgets, and user interviews. For each channel, estimate the volume, the recency, and the representation bias. Identify the customer segment most underrepresented in the current feedback mix. Design one new feedback mechanism specifically targeting that segment.

title: Evaluate the product management process for effectiveness and predictabi
prompt: Evaluate the product management process for effectiveness and predictability. Assess whether requirements are written with enough detail that engineers can implement them without constant PM involvement. Find recurring patterns where engineering returns requirements for clarification — edge cases, error states, or acceptance criteria gaps. Review the prioritization process — whether decisions are made transparently with documented rationale or through opaque stakeholder negotiation. Assess whether product managers maintain awareness of technical constraints that affect feasibility and effort estimates. Look for any product area where the PM and engineering team have divergent understanding of the current state. Summarize the five PM process improvements that would most reduce friction and rework.

title: Assess product metrics ownership
prompt: Assess product metrics ownership. Identify which metrics each product manager tracks and whether those metrics have baselines and targets that reflect strategic goals. Find product areas with no PM-owned success metric — where features are shipped without a clear definition of what good looks like. Review whether PMs have the data access and tooling needed to answer their own questions without depending on a data analyst for every query. Assess whether product metrics are reviewed at a cadence that enables course correction before the end of a quarter. Recommend the metrics ownership and tooling improvements that would most improve PM decision speed and quality.

title: You are a VP of product management leading a product organization of twe
prompt: You are a VP of product management leading a product organization of twelve PMs across four teams at a B2B SaaS company. The product team has been generating a high volume of output, shipping features every two weeks, but customer retention has stagnated at sixty-eight percent and the NPS has declined by fifteen points over the past year. The engineering team is frustrated that many features shipped in the past year are now rarely used, and the sales team is struggling to articulate the product's differentiation to prospects. Your diagnosis is that the team has confused shipping with success and is optimizing for velocity rather than impact. Your mandate is to redesign the product management practice to focus the team on measurable outcomes rather than feature delivery. Begin by conducting a retrospective analysis of the last twelve months of shipped features, measuring post-launch adoption at thirty days and ninety days for each feature and correlating adoption with whether a success metric was defined before development began. Identify the features with the highest engineering investment and the lowest adoption and use them as case studies for the new approach. Design the outcome-based planning framework where every initiative must define the user behavior change it is driving, the metric that measures that change, and the minimum measurable signal that will confirm the feature is having its intended effect before a full rollout. Redesign the PRD template to front-load the problem definition and success metrics before allowing any discussion of solution specifics. Design the PM coaching curriculum that will upskill the team on discovery methods, outcome definition, and experiment design within one quarter. Specify the management ritual changes including a monthly impact review replacing the monthly feature ship review. Deliver the practice transformation plan with a six-month roadmap and a quarterly milestone that demonstrates progress.

title: You are a principal product manager facilitating the discovery phase for
prompt: You are a principal product manager facilitating the discovery phase for a new AI-powered feature that the company is considering adding to its core product. The feature would automatically summarize long-form content for users, but the product team is divided: some believe it solves a real user problem, others believe it is a solution looking for a problem, and engineering estimates the implementation cost at three months of a senior engineer's time. Your task is to design and run a structured discovery process that produces a clear go or no-go recommendation within four weeks. Begin by articulating the core hypothesis: users who need to process large amounts of long-form content are currently spending significant time summarizing manually and would complete their task more effectively with automated summarization. Design a behavioral research study that tests whether target users actually read full content or already skim, and whether their reading behavior matches the problem hypothesis. Conduct a competitive analysis of how three direct competitors and two adjacent products have implemented summarization features, and collect whatever public data exists on feature adoption and customer response. Run a concept test with twelve target users using a low-fidelity prototype to measure whether the proposed summarization format actually helps them complete the task faster with equal comprehension. Define the go decision criteria including a minimum adoption intent threshold from the concept test, a minimum size of the affected user segment, and a maximum acceptable complexity score from engineering. Define the no-go criteria that would terminate the investigation regardless of user enthusiasm. Deliver a discovery findings report with a clear recommendation, the supporting evidence, and the confidence level in each piece of evidence.

title: You are a product operations lead building the product management operat
prompt: You are a product operations lead building the product management operating system for a company that has grown from five to twenty product managers in eighteen months. The team lacks shared processes, uses different templates across teams, and has no consistent way to communicate roadmap changes to stakeholders or measure the health of the product development process. Engineering leads are frustrated by inconsistent requirements quality, and design complains about being pulled in too late in the discovery process. Your task is to design the product management operating system that standardizes the key processes while preserving the autonomy each team needs to work effectively in their domain. Begin by auditing the current state of four core processes: how opportunities are identified and sized, how features are scoped and specified, how launches are planned and executed, and how outcomes are measured post-launch. Identify the highest-friction touchpoints by interviewing five PMs, three engineering leads, and three designers. Design the minimum set of standard artifacts that every team will use, including the opportunity brief, the feature spec template, the launch checklist, and the outcomes review report, keeping each template lean enough to complete in under two hours for a standard feature. Design the design-first process that ensures design is involved in problem framing before any solution is proposed, with a structured handoff protocol between product discovery and design exploration. Specify the requirements quality standard including the minimum definition of done for a feature spec before it enters the engineering backlog. Design the product operations rituals including a weekly PM council for cross-team dependency management, a monthly impact review, and a quarterly process retrospective. Deliver the operating system as a wiki section that every PM can onboard from independently.

title: Audit UI consistency across all screens and components
prompt: Review the application for UI consistency — inconsistent spacing, color usage outside the design system, mixed typography, buttons with different behaviors for the same action, and icon usage without a coherent language.
prompt_short: Audit all screens for UI inconsistency and design system violations.
prompt_filtered: Visual consistency only. Spacing, color, type, and component inconsistencies.

title: Find the highest-friction steps in core user flows
prompt: Walk through each core user flow and identify the steps with the most friction — unexpected behavior, unclear labels, too many required fields, missing feedback on actions, no progress indication, or confusing error states.
prompt_short: Find the highest-friction steps in the most important user flows.
prompt_filtered: Friction points only. Step-by-step flow analysis for core tasks.

title: Audit empty states and error states for all key screens
prompt: Review every key screen for empty state and error state design. Identify screens that show blank content with no guidance, raw error codes, or confusing messages. Recommend specific empty state copy and error recovery guidance.
prompt_short: Find missing or inadequate empty states and error states on key screens.
prompt_filtered: Empty and error states only. Missing guidance and confusing messages.

title: Review mobile responsiveness across all breakpoints
prompt: Audit the application at mobile (< 768px), tablet (768-900px), and desktop (> 900px) breakpoints. Identify layouts that break, overflow, clip, or become unusable at smaller screens. Prioritize by page traffic.
prompt_short: Audit all pages for responsiveness issues at mobile and tablet breakpoints.
prompt_filtered: Responsive layout only. Breakpoint-specific layout failures.

title: Assess accessibility compliance and keyboard navigation
prompt: Audit the application for accessibility issues — missing ARIA labels, insufficient color contrast, keyboard trap elements, missing focus indicators, images without alt text, and interactive elements unreachable by keyboard. Prioritize by WCAG 2.1 AA compliance.
prompt_short: Audit accessibility for ARIA, contrast, keyboard nav, and focus management.
prompt_filtered: Accessibility only. WCAG 2.1 AA compliance gaps.

title: Audit design token usage for consistency and drift
prompt: Review the design token system — color, spacing, typography, and elevation tokens. Identify components using hardcoded values instead of tokens, tokens defined in code that do not exist in the design tool, and tokens modified without a design system update. Recommend the sync process that keeps code and design in agreement.
prompt_short: Find design token drift between code and design tool.
prompt_filtered: Design tokens only. Hardcoded values and code-design sync gaps.

title: Review all form designs for usability and error recovery
prompt: Audit every form in the product for: inline validation timing (on blur vs on submit), error message specificity (what went wrong and how to fix it), field label clarity, required vs optional field indication, and form-level error summary for multi-field failures. Find the forms with the highest abandonment risk.
prompt_short: Audit all forms for validation timing, error messages, and abandonment risk.
prompt_filtered: Forms only. Validation, error recovery, and label clarity.

title: Assess loading state design for perceived performance
prompt: Review every asynchronous interaction for loading state design — skeleton screens, spinners, progress bars, optimistic updates. Identify interactions where the UI blocks with a spinner with no indication of progress duration. Recommend the loading pattern per context that most reduces perceived wait time.
prompt_short: Audit loading states for perceived performance and identify blocking spinners.
prompt_filtered: Loading states only. Skeleton screens, optimistic updates, and progress patterns.

title: Find navigation patterns causing disorientation in the product
prompt: Audit the navigation structure for patterns that disorient users — inconsistent back-button behavior, modal flows with no escape, deep navigation paths with no breadcrumb, tabs that reset state when navigated away. Identify the navigation anti-patterns most frequently correlating with user session abandonment.
prompt_short: Find navigation anti-patterns that disorient users and cause session abandonment.
prompt_filtered: Navigation patterns only. Disorientation sources and state reset issues.

title: Design an icon audit for consistency and recognizability
prompt: Audit all icons in the product for: visual consistency within the same style family, semantic consistency (same icon for the same action across screens), and recognizability without a label. Identify icons used for different meanings in different contexts.
prompt_short: Audit icons for visual consistency, semantic consistency, and recognizability.
prompt_filtered: Icon audit only. Style consistency and semantic meaning conflicts.

title: Review mobile touch target sizes for usability
prompt: Audit all interactive elements on mobile for touch target size compliance — a minimum of 44x44 points per Apple HIG and WCAG. Identify interactive elements below the minimum size, grouped targets that are too close together, and floating action button placements that overlap critical content.
prompt_short: Audit mobile touch targets for minimum size compliance and overlap issues.
prompt_filtered: Touch targets only. Size compliance and grouping on mobile.

title: Assess copy quality across all critical user decisions
prompt: Review the microcopy at the highest-stakes user decisions — delete confirmations, upgrade prompts, permission requests, destructive actions. Identify copy that is ambiguous about consequences, uses jargon, buries important context, or creates false urgency. Rewrite each with specificity and honesty.
prompt_short: Audit microcopy at high-stakes user decisions for clarity and honesty.
prompt_filtered: Microcopy only. High-stakes decision copy for destructive and upgrade actions.

title: Design the product's notification and alert hierarchy
prompt: Define a notification and alert hierarchy for the product — toast notifications for transient feedback, inline validation, modal alerts for blocking actions requiring confirmation, and system banners for degraded modes. Identify current uses of modals for information that should be a toast. Recommend the hierarchy and when each type is appropriate.
prompt_short: Design the alert/notification hierarchy and fix modals used for transient info.
prompt_filtered: Alert hierarchy only. Toast vs modal vs banner vs inline classification.

title: Assess onboarding design for progressive disclosure
prompt: Review the onboarding flow for progressive disclosure — presenting complexity only as the user is ready for it. Identify screens that present too many options simultaneously, force account setup before demonstrating value, or require configuration before letting the user experience the core product. Recommend the progressive disclosure restructuring.
prompt_short: Audit onboarding for premature complexity and missing progressive disclosure.
prompt_filtered: Progressive disclosure only. Onboarding complexity timing and sequencing.

title: Evaluate dark mode implementation quality
prompt: Audit the dark mode implementation. Identify components that invert incorrectly — images with white backgrounds, shadows that are invisible, text that loses contrast, colored UI elements that were never updated for dark context. Find system-inherited dark mode vs intentionally designed dark mode discrepancies.
prompt_short: Audit dark mode for incorrect inversions, contrast failures, and missing updates.
prompt_filtered: Dark mode only. Contrast, inversion errors, and component-level dark mode gaps.

title: Review confirmation dialog patterns for friction calibration
prompt: Audit all confirmation dialogs. Identify dialogs for low-risk reversible actions that add friction without safety benefit. Find high-risk irreversible actions with no confirmation or with confirmations easy to dismiss accidentally. Recommend which confirmations to remove (use undo instead), which to add, and which to make harder to dismiss.
prompt_short: Audit confirmation dialogs to find friction without safety and missing protections.
prompt_filtered: Confirmation dialogs only. Reversible vs irreversible action protection calibration.

title: Design a design critique process for the product team
prompt: Design a regular design critique process. Include: the critique format (presenter shares goal, audience gives structured feedback), the attendee mix (designer, PM, engineer, research), the feedback format (observation, impact, suggestion), and the cadence. Distinguish design critique from design review — critique is for improvement, review is for sign-off.
prompt_short: Design a structured design critique process separate from design review.
prompt_filtered: Critique process only. Format, attendees, feedback structure, and cadence.

title: Design Empty State for Engagement
prompt: Design Empty State for Engagement

title: Improve Error Message Clarity
prompt: Improve Error Message Clarity

prompt: Check Touch Target Sizes

title: Audit the design system for coverage gaps
prompt: Audit the design system for coverage gaps. Identify the five most commonly built UI patterns that have no corresponding design system component. For each, assess the consistency cost — how many different implementations exist across the product — and recommend a canonical component specification.

title: Find the three user flows with the highest drop-off rates
prompt: Find the three user flows with the highest drop-off rates. For each, map the UX friction points — unnecessary steps, confusing copy, slow load times, or non-obvious affordances. Recommend the specific design change most likely to reduce drop-off at each friction point.

title: Assess accessibility compliance across the primary user flows
prompt: Assess accessibility compliance across the primary user flows. Identify flows that fail WCAG 2.1 AA criteria — missing alt text, insufficient color contrast, keyboard navigation gaps, or missing ARIA labels. Order findings by the user population affected and recommend the fixes that would most improve accessibility.

title: Evaluate the onboarding flow for a new user completing the product for t
prompt: Evaluate the onboarding flow for a new user completing the product for the first time. Map every screen in the onboarding sequence and count the number of decisions the user must make before reaching their first moment of value. Identify the screen with the highest drop-off rate in analytics and describe the friction causing it. Recommend three specific changes that would reduce time to first value by at least thirty percent. Prioritize changes by impact and implementation effort.

title: Conduct a heuristic evaluation of the main dashboard using Nielsen's ten
prompt: Conduct a heuristic evaluation of the main dashboard using Nielsen's ten usability heuristics. For each heuristic, rate the current design on a scale of zero to four and provide one specific example of a violation if the score is below three. Identify the three heuristics with the lowest scores and for each, describe one design change that would move the score up by at least one point. Output a heuristic evaluation report with a prioritized remediation list.

title: Audit the design system for consistency gaps
prompt: Audit the design system for consistency gaps. Review the button component library and identify any cases where the same visual affordance carries different meanings in different contexts. Check whether all interactive elements have hover, active, focus, and disabled states defined. Identify components used in production that do not exist in the design system. Recommend a component consolidation plan that reduces the total number of button variants by twenty percent without reducing design expressiveness.

title: Evaluate the mobile experience for the three most-used product flows on
prompt: Evaluate the mobile experience for the three most-used product flows on a small screen device. For each flow, measure the number of taps required to complete the task, identify any tap targets smaller than forty-four pixels, and note any content that requires horizontal scrolling. Score each flow on a mobile usability rubric from one to five. For flows scoring below three, write specific design recommendations with estimated implementation effort.

title: Design an accessibility audit plan for the core product
prompt: Design an accessibility audit plan for the core product. Define the WCAG 2.1 AA compliance criteria that apply to a web application of this type. List the automated testing tools you will use and the manual testing procedures required for the checks that automated tools cannot cover. Specify the screen reader testing protocol and the assistive technology combinations to be tested. Create an accessibility issue severity classification system and a remediation prioritization framework.

title: Evaluate the UX and UI design quality across the primary user journeys
prompt: Evaluate the UX and UI design quality across the primary user journeys. Identify the five most critical user flows — onboarding, core task completion, error recovery — and assess whether each is intuitive for a first-time user. Find inconsistencies in interaction patterns across the product — where similar actions work differently in different areas, creating cognitive overhead. Assess whether the design system is applied consistently or whether components are reimplemented inconsistently in different parts of the product. Look for error states, empty states, and loading states that are missing or poorly designed. Summarize the five design quality improvements that would most improve user task completion rates.

title: Review the UX design process
prompt: Review the UX design process. Assess whether designers validate concepts with users before handoff to engineering — through prototypes, usability tests, or concept tests. Find features where design validation was skipped and assess whether the shipped result met user needs. Evaluate whether engineers have sufficient design guidance — component specifications, interaction details, and edge case handling — to implement designs correctly without constant designer involvement. Look for any product area where the current UI has known usability issues that have not been prioritized for redesign. Recommend the design process improvements that would most reduce redesign cycles and increase first-implementation quality.

title: You are a design director conducting a comprehensive UX audit of a SaaS
prompt: You are a design director conducting a comprehensive UX audit of a SaaS product that has grown organically over five years and now has significant interaction design debt. The product has forty-two distinct screens, uses three different design systems simultaneously, and has accumulated over two hundred component variants in Figma that are out of sync with the production codebase. The customer success team reports that onboarding new users takes an average of eight hours of guided assistance, and the NPS for ease of use is twenty-three, well below the industry median of forty-one. Your mandate is to produce a UX audit report that prioritizes the improvements with the greatest impact on user success and business outcomes. Begin with a heuristic evaluation of the five most critical user journeys: onboarding, core task completion, error recovery, settings management, and help and support access. Score each journey on Nielsen's ten heuristics and produce a severity-ranked issue list. Conduct a task analysis of the most complex user workflow, documenting every decision point, information input, and system response required to complete the task, and compare the actual step count to the theoretical minimum. Analyze the in-product analytics to identify the three screens with the highest exit rates and three screens with the longest average completion times, and investigate whether the cause is a cognitive load problem, an information architecture problem, or an interaction pattern problem. Review the information architecture against a card sorting study if one exists, or design and run a quick five-person tree test to validate the top-level navigation labels. Audit the visual hierarchy on the three most information-dense screens and identify where competing elements of equal visual weight are forcing users to read everything before they know where to focus. Deliver a prioritized audit report with severity ratings, before/after design sketches for the top ten recommendations, and an estimated engineering effort for each recommendation.

title: You are a senior interaction designer redesigning the settings and prefe
prompt: You are a senior interaction designer redesigning the settings and preferences area of a productivity application used by knowledge workers. The current settings section has grown to over eighty individual options spread across twelve sub-sections with inconsistent labeling, and user research shows that sixty percent of users cannot find the setting they are looking for without using search, and thirty percent give up entirely. Your task is to redesign the settings architecture from first principles while ensuring that power users who have memorized the current layout can adapt quickly. Begin by conducting a card sorting study with twenty representative users to reveal how they naturally group the eighty settings options. Compare the resulting grouping model to the current information architecture and identify the five most significant structural mismatches. Apply progressive disclosure principles to design a three-tier architecture where the most commonly used settings are surfaced on a quick-access overview page, the next tier of settings appears in categorized sections, and the advanced settings are housed in a clearly labeled expert mode that requires a deliberate click to access. Design the settings search experience to cover fuzzy matching and semantic matching so that a user searching for dark mode finds the theme settings even if the setting is labeled appearance. Define the labeling standard for settings options, requiring each option to be named using plain language that describes the user outcome rather than the technical mechanism. Design the settings confirmation and undo system for changes with significant consequences such as deleting data or changing plan tiers. Create the prototype and test it with ten users measuring find-time for seven standard settings tasks, comparing against a baseline from the current design. Deliver the redesigned information architecture, the interaction specification, and the usability test findings with before-and-after task completion times.

title: You are a design systems lead establishing a design system from scratch
prompt: You are a design systems lead establishing a design system from scratch for a company with a design team of six and an engineering team of forty. The company currently has no shared component library, design and engineering work from different references, and shipping a consistent UI requires extensive review and correction cycles that slow the team by an estimated thirty percent. Your mandate is to build a design system that reduces design-to-engineering handoff friction, accelerates feature development, and enables the company to maintain visual consistency as the team scales to fifteen designers and eighty engineers. Begin by auditing the existing product UI to identify the component patterns that appear most frequently, noting variations and inconsistencies in similar components. Prioritize the component library build by starting with the fifteen components that cover eighty percent of the UI surface area, following the Pareto principle to create maximum impact with minimum initial investment. Design the token architecture covering color, typography, spacing, border radius, and shadow, using a semantic token layer that maps design decisions to purpose rather than value so that a theme change only requires updating the token values. Specify the component documentation standard requiring each component to include usage guidelines, a prop reference, accessibility requirements, and an anti-patterns section showing what not to do. Design the contribution model for engineers to propose new components or flag discrepancies between design and implementation, with a clear review process and a named design system owner responsible for approvals. Define the versioning and deprecation policy so that breaking changes are communicated clearly and teams have a migration path. Specify the Figma library structure including how components are organized, how variants are named, and how the library is kept in sync with the production component library. Deliver the design system foundation document including the token definitions, the initial fifteen component specifications, the contribution guidelines, and the twelve-month roadmap for expanding coverage.

title: Audit sprint velocity trends for capacity signals
prompt: Review sprint velocity over the past 8 sprints. Identify trends — declining velocity, high variance, consistent over-commitment. Assess whether velocity changes correlate with team composition changes, technical debt accumulation, or process issues.
prompt_short: Analyze sprint velocity trends for capacity and process issues.
prompt_filtered: Velocity trends only. Trend direction and correlation analysis.

title: Find recurring blockers that stall work mid-sprint
prompt: Review sprint retrospectives and ticket history for recurring blockers — waiting on design, blocked by another team, environment issues, unclear requirements, production incidents derailing work. Identify the top 3 systemic blockers and recommend solutions.
prompt_short: Find the most recurring mid-sprint blockers and recommend fixes.
prompt_filtered: Blockers only. Recurring impediments and systemic causes.

title: Assess story point estimation accuracy and calibration
prompt: Compare estimated story points to actual time spent across recent sprints. Identify the ticket types consistently underestimated (technical debt work? new integrations? design-heavy features?). Recommend calibration adjustments per ticket type.
prompt_short: Find estimation patterns that consistently miss and recommend calibration.
prompt_filtered: Estimation accuracy only. Underestimated categories and calibration.

title: Design a healthier sprint ceremony structure
prompt: Assess the current sprint ceremonies — planning, standup, review, retrospective. Identify ceremonies that run too long, produce low value, or are skipped. Recommend a leaner ceremony structure with specific time boxes and required outputs for each.
prompt_short: Redesign sprint ceremonies to reduce time waste and improve value.
prompt_filtered: Ceremony design only. Duration, output, and attendance requirements.

title: Find incomplete work patterns that carry over sprints
prompt: Identify tickets that are regularly carried over from one sprint to the next without completion. Find the patterns — tasks too large to fit a sprint, tasks with unclear done criteria, tasks that surface hidden complexity. Recommend specific solutions.
prompt_short: Find tickets that repeatedly carry over and why they don't complete.
prompt_filtered: Carryover patterns only. Root cause of incomplete sprint work.

title: Design a sprint health scorecard for the team
prompt: Create a sprint health scorecard tracking: velocity trend (improving/flat/declining), commitment accuracy (committed vs completed), carryover rate (percentage of tickets rolling to next sprint), ceremony adherence, and team happiness indicator. Run it for 3 sprints to establish a baseline before drawing conclusions.
prompt_short: Design a sprint health scorecard with 5 key indicators to track trend.
prompt_filtered: Sprint scorecard only. Velocity, commitment, carryover, ceremonies, happiness.

title: Find the largest recurring retro action item never implemented
prompt: Review the last 6 retrospective outputs. Identify action items that have appeared two or more times without being implemented. For each recurring item, assess whether the blocker is ownership ambiguity, organizational friction, or low genuine priority. Recommend a resolution or an explicit decision to stop proposing it.
prompt_short: Find retro action items that keep recurring without being implemented.
prompt_filtered: Retro follow-through only. Recurring unimplemented retrospective actions.

title: Assess the sprint review for stakeholder engagement quality
prompt: Evaluate the sprint review meeting. Assess whether stakeholders attend, whether what is demoed represents completed shippable work or work-in-progress, whether stakeholder feedback is captured and fed into the backlog, and whether the review generates alignment or merely informs. Recommend format changes to increase feedback quality.
prompt_short: Assess whether the sprint review generates real stakeholder feedback.
prompt_filtered: Sprint review only. Stakeholder engagement and feedback capture quality.

title: Reduce ceremony fatigue with a ceremony audit
prompt: Calculate the total time the team spends in sprint ceremonies per week. Identify ceremonies that consistently run over time, produce low-quality outputs, or have low team energy. For each, recommend a format change, time box reduction, async alternative, or elimination. Calculate the engineering hours per sprint recovered by the optimized ceremony structure.
prompt_short: Calculate ceremony time per week and recommend eliminations or reductions.
prompt_filtered: Ceremony audit only. Total time, output quality, and reduction recommendations.

title: Design a sprint backlog grooming process that scales
prompt: Design a backlog grooming process that keeps the top 2 sprints always refined without requiring the full team in every session. Include: who participates in grooming, the minimum viable ticket format before it enters a sprint, a lightweight estimation protocol, and the trigger for a grooming session versus async review.
prompt_short: Design a backlog grooming process keeping 2 sprints pre-refined at all times.
prompt_filtered: Grooming process only. Who, format, estimation, and grooming trigger.

title: Find the estimation anti-patterns causing sprint plan failures
prompt: Analyze the last 10 sprint plans for estimation patterns that cause failure — large tickets that explode, design-dependency tickets starting before design is ready, integration tickets starting before APIs are confirmed, and research spikes estimated as delivery tasks. Recommend ticket type-specific estimation rules that eliminate these patterns.
prompt_short: Find estimation anti-patterns causing sprint plan failures across ticket types.
prompt_filtered: Estimation anti-patterns only. Ticket type rules to prevent sprint plan failures.

title: Assess the standup format for information density
prompt: Evaluate the daily standup for information density. Identify whether the format generates actionable coordination or serves as a status report to management. Find standups running longer than 15 minutes. Recommend a format that surfaces only blockers and coordination needs, not individual status updates.
prompt_short: Audit standup format for status-report vs coordination value.
prompt_filtered: Standup format only. Blocker and coordination signal vs status report.

title: Create a sprint zero checklist for new team member onboarding
prompt: Design a sprint zero process for new engineers joining the team. Include: environment setup, codebase orientation tasks, shadow tickets (work alongside an existing engineer), first solo ticket criteria (isolated, well-defined scope), and the check-in cadence with a buddy for the first 30 days. Make it completable in one sprint.
prompt_short: Design a sprint zero process for new engineers to be productive by day 14.
prompt_filtered: Sprint zero only. Setup, orientation, shadow, and first ticket criteria.

title: Find technical debt tickets that never make it into sprint plans
prompt: Audit the backlog for technical debt tickets that are consistently pushed out of sprint planning. Identify the total estimated debt backlog and calculate what percentage of sprint capacity has been allocated to technical debt over the past quarter. Recommend a minimum debt capacity reservation per sprint and a prioritization framework for debt items.
prompt_short: Find technical debt tickets perpetually skipped and recommend capacity reservation.
prompt_filtered: Debt scheduling only. Backlog debt volume and sprint capacity allocation.

title: Design a sprint definition of ready for backlog tickets
prompt: Create a definition of ready — the minimum conditions a ticket must meet before it can enter a sprint. Include: a user story format with acceptance criteria, design reference or explicit no-design-needed declaration, edge cases documented, dependencies identified and resolved or acknowledged, and estimation within the team's calibrated range.
prompt_short: Create a definition of ready checklist that tickets must pass before sprint entry.
prompt_filtered: Definition of ready only. Minimum ticket quality for sprint entry.

title: Assess the team's capacity planning for unplanned work
prompt: Review how capacity planning accounts for unplanned work — production incidents, urgent bug fixes, support escalations, and ad-hoc requests. Identify the historical ratio of planned vs unplanned work per sprint. Recommend a buffer reservation — typically 15-20% of capacity — for unplanned work to prevent sprint plan failures.
prompt_short: Find how much capacity unplanned work consumes and recommend a buffer.
prompt_filtered: Unplanned capacity only. Historical unplanned work ratio and buffer recommendation.

title: Create a sprint goal format that unifies team direction
prompt: Design a sprint goal template that gives the team a single north star for the sprint — distinct from the sum of individual tickets. The goal should describe the user outcome the sprint achieves, be testable at the sprint review, and be achievable even if 20% of tickets slip. Rewrite the last 3 sprint goals using this format.
prompt_short: Design a sprint goal template focused on user outcome not ticket completion.
prompt_filtered: Sprint goal only. Outcome-based format that survives 20% ticket slip.

prompt: Fill in Missing Data Gaps

prompt: Gain full understanding before completing

prompt: Find What's Broken

prompt: Recommend Next Steps

prompt: Score for Production Readiness

prompt: Audit Sprint Velocity

prompt: Find Scope Creep Patterns

prompt: Identify Sprint Scope Creep

prompt: Unblock Stalled Stories
