prompt: Estimate Capacity Accurately

title: Write Clear Sprint Goal
prompt: Write Clear Sprint Goal

title: Reduce Context Switching Cost
prompt: Reduce Context Switching Cost

title: Analyze sprint velocity trends over the past six sprints
prompt: Analyze sprint velocity trends over the past six sprints. Identify whether velocity is stable, improving, or declining. Find the root causes of the three sprints with the lowest velocity — unplanned work, scope creep, dependency blockers, or team capacity reduction. Recommend the process change that would most stabilize velocity.

title: Audit the sprint planning process for estimation accuracy
prompt: Audit the sprint planning process for estimation accuracy. Find the story point or effort estimate deviation between planned and actual for the past three sprints. Identify the item types — new features, bug fixes, integrations — with the highest estimation variance. Recommend the estimation calibration process that would most reduce variance.

title: Identify the top three recurring impediments that appear in sprint retro
prompt: Identify the top three recurring impediments that appear in sprint retrospectives without being resolved. For each, trace why the impediment persists — lack of ownership, insufficient priority, or a systemic issue outside the team's control. Recommend the escalation or resolution path for each.

title: Review the current sprint for scope creep
prompt: Review the current sprint for scope creep. List every ticket added to the sprint after the sprint planning meeting. For each mid-sprint addition, identify whether it displaced a planned ticket, who requested the addition, and whether it aligned with the sprint goal. Calculate the percentage of sprint capacity consumed by unplanned work. Recommend a scope change control process that reduces unplanned additions by fifty percent in the next sprint.

title: Assess team velocity over the past six sprints
prompt: Assess team velocity over the past six sprints. Calculate the average velocity, the standard deviation, and the range between highest and lowest velocity. Identify the top three causes of velocity variance by reviewing sprint retrospective notes. Determine whether variance is driven by story size inconsistency, unplanned interruptions, external dependencies, or team capacity changes. Recommend one process change targeting the highest-impact variance driver.

title: Design the sprint planning meeting agenda for a team of eight engineers
prompt: Design the sprint planning meeting agenda for a team of eight engineers and two designers. Allocate time for sprint goal alignment, capacity calculation, backlog refinement confirmation, story breakdown, and task assignment. Specify the inputs required before the meeting begins including refined stories with acceptance criteria, updated team capacity based on planned time off, and dependency confirmations from other teams. Define the definition of ready that stories must meet before they can be pulled into a sprint.

title: Write the sprint retrospective facilitation guide for a remote-first tea
prompt: Write the sprint retrospective facilitation guide for a remote-first team. Design a structure that covers what went well, what did not go well, what to try differently, and how to track improvement from the previous retrospective's action items. Include specific icebreaker and engagement techniques for distributed teams. Define the format for documenting retrospective output and assigning action item owners. Specify the time allocation for each section in a ninety-minute retrospective.

title: Evaluate the team's definition of done for completeness
prompt: Evaluate the team's definition of done for completeness. Review the current definition of done and assess whether it covers all the conditions required for a story to be considered shippable, including unit test coverage, integration test passing, code review approval, documentation updated, accessibility checked, and analytics instrumentation verified. Identify any missing criteria. Rewrite the definition of done as a checklist and get sign-off from engineering, design, and QA.

title: Evaluate sprint management effectiveness
prompt: Evaluate sprint management effectiveness. Review the past six sprints and assess the ratio of planned to delivered work. Find recurring patterns in what causes work to carry over — late requirement changes, technical blockers, underestimation, or unplanned interruptions. Assess whether sprint ceremonies — planning, review, retrospective — are producing the outcomes they are designed for or becoming process overhead without behavioral improvement. Look for any systemic issue — a dependency team, a testing bottleneck, or a deployment process — that consistently appears as a blocker across sprints. Summarize the five sprint management improvements that would most increase predictability.

title: Assess the team's retrospective effectiveness
prompt: Assess the team's retrospective effectiveness. Identify whether retrospective action items are tracked, assigned, and reviewed in subsequent sprints. Find retrospectives from the past quarter where the same problem appeared multiple times without a resolution. Assess whether the retrospective format enables honest feedback or whether team dynamics suppress surfacing of real issues. Look for teams that have stopped holding retrospectives or treat them as perfunctory. Review whether the retrospective outputs influence sprint planning — whether process improvements get allocated time in future sprints. Recommend the retrospective process changes that would most improve team-level continuous improvement.

title: You are an engineering manager coaching a scrum team of seven engineers
prompt: You are an engineering manager coaching a scrum team of seven engineers that has struggled with consistent sprint delivery for three quarters. The team regularly commits to more work than it can complete, carries over stories from sprint to sprint, and has a sprint goal that is rarely achieved intact. Retrospectives surface the same complaints each cycle: stories are too large when they enter the sprint, dependencies are discovered mid-sprint rather than during planning, and urgent requests from stakeholders disrupt the sprint without a clear intake process. Your task is to redesign the team's sprint practices to achieve a sprint completion rate of eighty-five percent or higher within two months. Begin by analyzing the past six sprints to identify the primary drivers of incomplete work, distinguishing between stories that were larger than estimated, stories blocked by external dependencies, stories deprioritized due to scope changes, and stories that were technically unclear and required mid-sprint clarification. Design the backlog refinement practice that resolves all four root causes before stories enter sprint planning, including a three-session weekly refinement cadence, a definition-of-ready checklist, and a story size limit of eight story points with mandatory decomposition for larger items. Redesign the sprint planning meeting to calculate capacity from actual individual availability rather than a team average, to set a single clear sprint goal that acts as a decision filter for scope additions, and to leave a ten percent buffer for unplanned work. Create the scope change intake process that routes urgent requests through the product manager rather than directly to engineers, evaluating each request against the sprint goal and making an explicit trade-off visible to all stakeholders. Design the daily standup format that surfaces blockers within the first twenty-four hours of a story becoming stuck rather than waiting for the story to be overdue. Deliver the updated sprint practices playbook with a six-week adoption timeline and a weekly health metric the team will track to measure improvement.

title: You are a program manager coordinating a cross-team initiative spanning
prompt: You are a program manager coordinating a cross-team initiative spanning four agile teams with different sprint cadences, working on a shared feature that requires synchronized delivery in ninety days. Three teams run two-week sprints and one team runs one-week sprints. Two of the three two-week sprint teams are offset by one week from each other, creating a situation where there is never a single moment where all teams are at the start of a sprint simultaneously. The initiative has eight inter-team dependencies, three of which are on the critical path. Your task is to design the coordination model that ensures dependencies are managed proactively without forcing teams to abandon their individual sprint rhythms. Begin by mapping all eight dependencies as a directed graph, identifying which teams are the producers and which are the consumers for each dependency, and classifying each dependency as hard blocking or soft coordination. Design the program-level increment review that runs every two weeks regardless of individual sprint cadences, where team leads share the status of every dependency and escalate blockers before they hit the critical path. Create the dependency tracking artifact that is visible to all teams and updated at least once per sprint, showing the status of each dependency, the expected delivery date, and the risk level. Design the integration planning meeting that runs four weeks before the final delivery date and ensures all teams have a shared understanding of the integration sequence, the testing environment requirements, and the rollback plan. Specify the escalation protocol for when a dependency slips, including who is notified, what the default response is, and how the program manager decides whether to absorb the slip into buffer or escalate to leadership. Develop the cross-team retrospective format that runs at the midpoint of the initiative to surface coordination friction before the final delivery crunch. Deliver the coordination model as a one-page visual with supporting process documentation.

title: You are an agile coach working with a newly remote-first engineering tea
prompt: You are an agile coach working with a newly remote-first engineering team that is struggling to maintain the collaboration quality it had when the team worked in the same office. The team runs two-week sprints, and since going remote, sprint velocity has dropped by thirty percent, daily standups have become status updates with no collaborative problem-solving, pair programming has stopped entirely, and the team reports in surveys that they feel disconnected from the work of their teammates. Your task is to redesign the team's agile practices for a remote-first context in a way that restores collaboration and psychological safety without requiring synchronous meetings that do not fit across the team's four time-zone spread. Begin by identifying the five collaboration activities that were most valuable when the team was co-located and determining which of those can be replicated asynchronously, which require synchronous but short sessions, and which cannot be replicated remotely and need to be replaced with a different mechanism that achieves the same outcome. Redesign the daily standup as a fifteen-minute focused blocker resolution meeting rather than a status update round-robin, using an async check-in bot for status and reserving the synchronous time for the one or two stories that have been stuck for more than twenty-four hours. Design the remote pair programming practice using a structured rotation schedule that pairs every engineer with a different teammate each week, a shared protocol for when pairing is initiated for a complex story, and tooling recommendations for low-latency screen sharing and collaborative editing. Redesign the sprint planning and retrospective as working sessions with high interaction density rather than presentation-heavy formats, using digital collaboration tools for real-time voting, silent writing, and group clustering of insights. Design a team connection ritual that is not work-focused and runs for fifteen minutes at the start of one meeting per sprint to rebuild the informal relationship layer. Deliver the remote agile playbook with a tool stack recommendation, a four-sprint adoption timeline, and a team health survey you will run monthly to track the recovery of collaboration quality.

title: Identify the highest-converting acquisition channels
prompt: Analyze user acquisition data across all channels. Identify which channels deliver users with the highest conversion rate to retained users, not just highest volume. Find underinvested channels with strong conversion and over-invested channels with poor retention.
prompt_short: Find acquisition channels with best conversion and retention, not just volume.
prompt_filtered: Acquisition efficiency only. Conversion and retention by channel.

title: Find the biggest drop-offs in the activation funnel
prompt: Map the activation funnel from sign-up to first meaningful action. Identify the steps with the highest drop-off rates. For each, hypothesize the root cause and recommend the smallest experiment to test an improvement.
prompt_short: Find the biggest activation funnel drop-offs and propose experiments.
prompt_filtered: Activation funnel only. Step-by-step drop-off analysis.

title: Assess viral or referral mechanics in the product
prompt: Evaluate whether the product has mechanisms for organic growth — referral programs, shareable content, network effects, embed codes. Identify the moments in the user journey where organic sharing is most likely and whether the product facilitates it.
prompt_short: Assess viral mechanics and sharing opportunities built into the product.
prompt_filtered: Viral mechanics only. Sharing, referral, and network effect opportunities.

title: Identify retention risks in the first 30 days
prompt: Analyze user behavior in the first 30 days post sign-up. Identify the actions most correlated with long-term retention. Find users who churned in month 1 and what they didn't do that retained users did. Recommend the "aha moment" to optimize for.
prompt_short: Find the actions most correlated with 30-day retention and optimize for them.
prompt_filtered: Early retention only. 30-day churn predictors and aha moment.

title: Find product-led growth opportunities in the current UX
prompt: Identify places in the product where natural usage moments could drive growth — invite flows triggered by collaboration, social sharing of achievements, upgrade prompts at the moment of value, SEO-indexable user-generated content.
prompt_short: Find UX moments in the product that could drive organic growth.
prompt_filtered: PLG opportunities only. Natural growth triggers in existing UX.

title: Calculate and optimize the CAC payback period by channel
prompt: Calculate CAC payback period — how many months of average revenue recover the customer acquisition cost — broken down by acquisition channel. Identify channels with payback periods exceeding 12 months. Recommend the channels to scale (short payback, high LTV) and channels to reduce investment (long payback, low retention).
prompt_short: Calculate CAC payback period by channel and find which to scale vs cut.
prompt_filtered: CAC payback only. Per-channel payback calculation and investment recommendation.

title: Identify the viral coefficient and growth loop mechanics
prompt: Calculate the viral coefficient: how many new users each existing user generates. Identify the growth loop — the user action that triggers invitation or sharing, the conversion rate of invitees, and the cycle time. If K is less than 1, the product has no viral growth. Find the loop change that would most improve K.
prompt_short: Calculate viral coefficient and find the loop change that most improves it.
prompt_filtered: Viral growth only. K factor calculation and loop optimization.

title: Audit SEO content gaps for organic acquisition
prompt: Analyze organic search traffic against competitor keyword rankings. Identify high-volume keywords where competitors rank in the top 3 and the product has no indexed content. Find the content types — comparison pages, use case pages, tutorial content — that drive the most qualified organic traffic in this category.
prompt_short: Find high-volume keywords where competitors rank but the product has no content.
prompt_filtered: SEO gaps only. Competitor-ranked keywords with no product content.

title: Design a referral program with the right incentive structure
prompt: Design a referral program. Define: the referral incentive for referrer and referee (symmetric vs asymmetric, discount vs feature unlock vs cash), the referral trigger moment, the friction minimization (one-click share), and the anti-fraud guardrails. Estimate the cost per acquired user against CAC to assess economic viability.
prompt_short: Design a referral program with incentive structure and anti-fraud guardrails.
prompt_filtered: Referral program only. Incentive design, trigger moment, and fraud prevention.

title: Find the channels with the highest LTV to CAC ratio
prompt: Calculate LTV to CAC ratio by acquisition channel. Identify channels with a ratio greater than 3:1 (the minimum healthy threshold). Find channels with high volume but a ratio below 1:1 — where the business loses money on every acquired user. Recommend the channel mix reallocation that maximizes blended LTV to CAC.
prompt_short: Calculate LTV to CAC by channel and recommend channel mix reallocation.
prompt_filtered: LTV to CAC only. Per-channel ratio and mix rebalancing toward profitable channels.

title: Identify the product's network effect and how to accelerate it
prompt: Assess whether the product has a network effect — direct (value increases as more same-side users join), indirect (more users on one side attract more on the other side), or data (more usage generates better data for all users). If a network effect exists, identify the critical mass threshold and the tactics to cross it faster.
prompt_short: Identify the network effect type and the tactics to reach critical mass faster.
prompt_filtered: Network effects only. Effect type, critical mass threshold, and acceleration tactics.

title: Map the growth model across paid, viral, and content loops
prompt: Map all three growth loops active in the product — paid (paid acquisition to activation to revenue to reinvest), viral (user to invite to new user to invite), and content (create content to indexed by search to acquire user to create more content). Identify the loop with the best unit economics and the highest potential for compounding growth.
prompt_short: Map all three growth loops and find which has the best compounding potential.
prompt_filtered: Growth loops only. Paid, viral, and content loop mapping and unit economics.

title: Find the trigger for the first meaningful value moment
prompt: Identify the specific in-product event that represents the first moment of genuine value delivery — the action that most strongly correlates with 90-day retention. Find the percentage of new users who reach this event within 7 days. Redesign onboarding to route every user to this event as the primary first session goal.
prompt_short: Find the first meaningful value event and redesign onboarding to reach it faster.
prompt_filtered: Value moment only. First meaningful event correlation with 90-day retention.

title: Assess the impact of pricing changes on acquisition velocity
prompt: Analyze how pricing changes over the past 12 months have affected: trial start rate, trial-to-paid conversion, plan mix (upward vs downward), and gross revenue per new customer. Identify pricing thresholds where conversion drop-off was significant versus changes that had minimal acquisition impact.
prompt_short: Analyze how pricing changes affected trial starts, conversion, and plan mix.
prompt_filtered: Pricing impact only. Acquisition and conversion effects of past pricing changes.

title: Design a re-engagement campaign for churned users
prompt: Design a re-engagement campaign targeting users who churned within the past 6 months. Define: the target cohort (recently churned, was active before leaving), the re-engagement trigger (new feature announcement, personalized progress summary, relevant use case), the offer, and the success metric (reactivation rate).
prompt_short: Design a re-engagement campaign for recently churned users.
prompt_filtered: Re-engagement only. Cohort targeting, trigger, offer, and success metric.

title: Find the conversion-blocking friction in the free-to-paid path
prompt: Map the path from free user to first payment. Identify the steps with highest drop-off — upgrade page views, checkout page abandonment, payment form failure. Audit the upgrade page for: clarity of plan differences, the prominence of the primary call to action, social proof, and risk reduction. Recommend the friction removal with the highest expected conversion lift.
prompt_short: Find the highest drop-off step in the free-to-paid conversion path.
prompt_filtered: Conversion path only. Drop-off identification in the free-to-paid funnel.

title: Identify untapped geographic or segment expansion opportunities
prompt: Analyze the current user base distribution by geography, company size, industry, and use case. Identify segments with high natural adoption that have not been targeted by marketing — where users found the product organically and retained well. Recommend the expansion segment with the best risk-adjusted growth opportunity.
prompt_short: Find user segments with high natural adoption not yet targeted by marketing.
prompt_filtered: Segment expansion only. High organic adoption segments without marketing investment.
