title: Find Feedback Without Action Records
prompt: Find Feedback Without Action Records

title: Audit Survey Response Rates
prompt: Audit Survey Response Rates

title: Map Feedback to Roadmap Items
prompt: Map Feedback to Roadmap Items

title: Find Unmonitored Feedback Channels
prompt: Find Unmonitored Feedback Channels

title: Assess Detractor Follow-Up Rate
prompt: Assess Detractor Follow-Up Rate

title: Map the user journey end to end and identify moments where feedback shou
prompt: Map the user journey end to end and identify moments where feedback should be collected but isn't — after task completion, after a failed interaction, at cancellation, at the first meaningful value moment. For each gap, recommend the appropriate prompt format, frequency cap, and routing destination. Sequence collection additions by data quality impact and user friction tradeoff.

title: Trace user feedback from collection to action across the organization
prompt: Trace user feedback from collection to action across the organization. Identify pipeline gaps — feedback collected but never reviewed, feedback reviewed but never prioritized, feedback prioritized but never actioned, feedback actioned but never communicated back to the user. Find the share of feedback that completes the full loop versus dropping out at each stage. Recommend the pipeline interventions that would most increase actioned-feedback rate.

title: Analyze the verbatim feedback from NPS detractors and low-CSAT responden
prompt: Analyze the verbatim feedback from NPS detractors and low-CSAT respondents over the past quarter. Identify the most common themes, the specific features or experiences referenced, and how detractor language differs from promoter language. Cross-reference detractor patterns with churn data to identify themes correlated with departure. Recommend the top three product or experience improvements most likely to convert detractors.

title: Evaluate the NPS program for quality of insight and actionability
prompt: Evaluate the NPS program for quality of insight and actionability. Identify whether NPS is collected at a representative sample of the user base or only from the most engaged users who are less likely to churn. Find NPS detractors who were never followed up with, passives who were never presented with a conversion path, and promoters who were never asked for a referral or case study. Assess whether NPS trends are reported and acted on at the product leadership level or treated as a marketing metric. Recommend the NPS program changes that would most increase the insight quality and business impact of each survey cycle.

title: Map all feedback collection points in the user journey and identify coll
prompt: Map all feedback collection points in the user journey and identify collection gaps. Identify moments where users form strong product opinions — onboarding completion, first encounter with a confusing flow, first successful core action — and verify feedback is collected at or near each moment. Find feedback collection points that fire too late — exit surveys after cancellation when the user has already decided — versus those timed to catch opinions while they are still actionable. Assess whether in-product feedback collection creates survey fatigue by prompting users multiple times in a session. Recommend the feedback timing and collection point changes that would most improve volume and quality of actionable responses.

title: Evaluate the user feedback system as a product input source
prompt: Evaluate the user feedback system as a product input source. Assess whether feedback collection is intentional and journey-aware — capturing user input at high-signal moments rather than randomly interrupting flows. Identify the collection mechanisms in use — in-app prompts, email surveys, NPS, support ticket sentiment, sales call notes, churned customer interviews, and community forums — and whether the data flows into a single repository or fragments across team-specific tools. Find collection gaps where high-signal moments — first-value achievement, repeated feature failure, cancellation — go uninstrumented. Examine whether feedback volume is sustainable for the team to actually review or whether it accumulates as unread backlog. Look for response and frequency design — whether prompts respect prior dismissal, whether respondents are over-surveyed across teams running parallel programs, and whether response rates are tracked to detect declining engagement. Summarize the five feedback collection improvements that would most increase actionable signal volume.

title: Audit the feedback-to-action pipeline end to end
prompt: Audit the feedback-to-action pipeline end to end. Identify whether feedback is reviewed on a defined cadence by a person with authority to act, whether themes are identified across feedback rather than treated as individual data points, and whether prioritized feedback enters the same backlog as engineering-driven work or sits in a parallel system. Find feedback that has been received multiple times across years without action — the strongest signal that the pipeline is broken. Examine the closing-the-loop process — whether users who reported issues hear back when fixes ship, whether feature request submitters are notified at release, and whether the platform builds reputation as a place where feedback matters. Look for the connection between feedback themes and roadmap planning — whether quarterly planning explicitly considers feedback trends, or whether roadmap is set by other inputs and feedback is interpreted as confirmation. Recommend the pipeline architecture changes that would most increase the share of feedback that influences product direction.

title: Evaluate the organizational infrastructure that converts user feedback i
prompt: Evaluate the organizational infrastructure that converts user feedback into product decisions. Assess whether there is a defined owner responsible for reviewing, categorizing, and routing feedback from all channels — NPS, in-product, support tickets, community, sales calls — or whether each channel is siloed within a separate team. Find feedback themes that appear consistently across multiple channels but have never been synthesized into a product problem statement. Examine whether the product team has a regular cadence for reviewing aggregated feedback — a weekly or monthly ritual that surfaces emerging pain points before they become churn drivers. Look for feedback-to-action traceability — whether a user who reported a problem can be notified when it is fixed, and whether the roadmap links features to the user feedback that motivated them. Assess whether feedback from enterprise customers receives disproportionate weight relative to their share of the user base, potentially distorting product priorities for a broader audience. Summarize the five feedback loop improvements that would most increase the product team responsiveness to user needs.

title: Audit bug backlog for severity and age distribution
prompt: Review the entire bug backlog. Analyze distribution by severity, age, and component. Identify bugs older than 90 days that are still open, P0/P1 bugs not in the current sprint, and bugs with no owner assigned. Recommend a triage process to keep the backlog actionable.
prompt_short: Audit bug backlog for old unresolved issues and missing ownership.
prompt_filtered: Bug backlog analysis only. Age, severity, and ownership distribution.

title: Find bugs that should be production incidents
prompt: Review the bug backlog for issues that affect users in production today but are tracked as low-priority tickets rather than active incidents. Identify any bug affecting more than 1% of users, causing data loss, or impacting revenue that is not in an active sprint.
prompt_short: Find bugs in the backlog that should be escalated to production incidents.
prompt_filtered: Severity reclassification only. Low-priority bugs with production impact.

title: Identify components generating the most bugs consistently
prompt: Analyze bug history by component over the past 6 months. Identify the components with the highest bug rate. Assess whether high bug density is from complexity, missing tests, unclear ownership, or accumulated technical debt. Recommend targeted investment for the top 3 bug-generating areas.
prompt_short: Find the components generating the most bugs and why.
prompt_filtered: Bug density by component only. Hot spots and root cause analysis.

title: Create a bug reproduction and reporting template
prompt: Design a bug report template for this project that ensures enough information is captured to reproduce the issue without back-and-forth. Include: steps to reproduce, expected vs actual behavior, environment details, logs, screenshots, and frequency. Make it short enough that reporters will actually fill it in.
prompt_short: Design a bug report template that ensures reproducibility without follow-up.
prompt_filtered: Template design only. Required fields for reproduction without back-and-forth.

title: Find bugs with no regression test after the fix
prompt: Identify bugs that were fixed but had no regression test added to prevent recurrence. These are the most likely to be re-introduced. For each, assess the risk of reintroduction and recommend the specific test case that would have caught the original bug.
prompt_short: Find fixed bugs with no regression test preventing reintroduction.
prompt_filtered: Regression coverage only. Fixed bugs with no corresponding test.

title: Audit bug severity triage and P0/P1 tracking
prompt: Evaluate bug triage: are severity levels defined and consistently applied? What criteria makes a bug P0 vs P1? Are critical bugs tracked separately from backlog bugs? Is there a service level agreement (SLA) for bug fixes by severity? Are P0 bugs assigned to someone immediately?
prompt_short: Audit bug severity triage and priority tracking.
prompt_filtered: Triage only. Severity levels and SLA definitions.

title: Find bugs reported multiple times (duplicate detection)
prompt: Identify bugs that were reported multiple times as separate issues, indicating poor duplicate detection. Assess the cost: multiple investigation cycles, fragmented workarounds shared by users, and delayed fixes from dispersed developer attention. Improve bug search/tagging.
prompt_short: Find duplicate bug reports and improve duplicate detection.
prompt_filtered: Duplicates only. Redundant reports and search/tagging gaps.

title: Assess bug report quality and reproducibility information
prompt: Review recent bug reports: are they reproducible from the description? Do they include browser/OS/version info? Are reproduction steps clear? Are error messages and logs included? What % of reports require follow-up questions? How much time do developers spend clarifying vs fixing?
prompt_short: Audit bug report quality and reproducibility information completeness.
prompt_filtered: Report quality only. Reproducibility and context completeness.

title: Review bug lifecycle and time-to-fix for user impact
prompt: Evaluate bug lifecycle: how long from report to fix? From fix to deployment? Are bugs fixed in batches or deployed immediately (hotfixes)? What's the median time-to-fix by severity? Are there blockers preventing quick fixes? Is fix validation (QA) adding delay?
prompt_short: Audit bug lifecycle from report to production deployment.
prompt_filtered: Lifecycle metrics only. Report-to-fix and deploy-to-production time.

title: Find bugs with vague or incomplete root cause analysis
prompt: Review closed bugs: how many have detailed root cause analysis? Are there bugs marked "fixed" with no explanation of why it happened? Are there patterns across bugs (same module, same developer)? Are lessons documented? Are systemic fixes applied to prevent similar bugs?
prompt_short: Audit root cause analysis completeness and systemic fixes.
prompt_filtered: Root cause only. Analysis depth and pattern detection.

title: Assess bug monitoring and notification for silent failures
prompt: Evaluate detection: are errors logged and monitored? Would a silent failure (no error message to user, but functionality broken) be detected? Are there synthetic tests checking core functionality? Do alerts fire for increased error rates? Is monitoring proactive or reactive (bug reports)?
prompt_short: Audit bug detection and error monitoring proactiveness.
prompt_filtered: Monitoring only. Silent failure detection and alerting.

title: Review bug communication to affected users and transparency
prompt: Assess user communication: if a critical bug affects users, are they notified? Is there status communication during the fix? Are workarounds provided? Is transparency about impact (how many users affected) shared? Are affected users proactively notified once fixed?
prompt_short: Audit bug communication to affected users and status updates.
prompt_filtered: User communication only. Notifications and workaround guidance.

title: Audit bug triage cadence and severity assignment consistency
prompt: Review the bug triage process. Verify there is a defined cadence (daily or weekly triage meeting), a severity rubric applied consistently across reporters, and a clear distinction between P1 (data loss, security, revenue impact) and P2 (functional degradation without workaround). Find bugs assigned severity based on who reported them rather than actual user impact.
prompt_short: Audit bug triage cadence and severity assignment consistency.
prompt_filtered: Triage process only. Cadence, severity rubric, and reporter bias in classification.

title: Design a bug bash process for targeted defect density reduction
prompt: Design a bug bash program for high-risk areas before major releases. Define the scope (surface areas with most recent changes), the participant mix (engineers, QA, product, design, CS), the duration (half-day sprint), the bug logging format, and the severity threshold for blocking release. Find releases where bug bash was skipped and defect density spiked post-launch.
prompt_short: Design a bug bash program structure and link to post-launch defect reduction.
prompt_filtered: Bug bash design only. Scope, participants, and release gate criteria.

title: Evaluate defect leakage rate from QA to production
prompt: Measure defect leakage — the percentage of bugs found by users in production that should have been caught in QA or staging. Identify the test coverage gaps, environment parity issues, or test data limitations that allowed each leaked defect. Assess whether high-leakage areas have dedicated regression coverage. Recommend the test investment that would most reduce production defect rates.
prompt_short: Measure defect leakage rate from QA to production and find coverage gaps.
prompt_filtered: Leakage analysis only. QA escape rate by feature area and coverage gaps.

title: Review bug recurrence and root cause closure discipline
prompt: Identify bugs that have been reported, fixed, and reopened more than once. For each recurrence, assess whether the fix addressed the root cause or only the symptom, whether the fix was accompanied by a regression test, and whether the area has accumulated technical debt enabling recurrence. Recommend root cause discipline improvements for high-recurrence bug categories.
prompt_short: Audit bug recurrence patterns and root cause closure completeness.
prompt_filtered: Recurrence only. Reopened bugs and root cause vs symptom fix patterns.

title: Assess bug SLA compliance and escalation path for missed deadlines
prompt: Review SLA compliance by severity. For each severity level, measure the percentage of bugs resolved within the target window. Identify severity categories consistently missing SLA, the common causes (resource contention, unclear ownership, underestimated complexity), and the escalation path when a bug approaches or breaches its SLA deadline.
prompt_short: Audit bug SLA compliance rates by severity and find escalation gaps.
prompt_filtered: SLA compliance only. Resolution time vs target by severity and escalation paths.

title: Evaluate automated regression testing coverage for known bug areas
prompt: Cross-reference the bug history with the automated test suite. Identify previously fixed bugs with no corresponding regression test, areas with high historical bug density but low test coverage, and tests that would have caught recent production incidents if they had existed. Recommend the regression test additions that would most protect against defect recurrence.
prompt_short: Find historical bugs with no regression test and prioritize coverage gaps.
prompt_filtered: Regression coverage only. Known bug areas without automated tests.

title: Design bug bounty or responsible disclosure process for security bugs
prompt: Assess the process for receiving and responding to externally reported security vulnerabilities. Verify there is a public disclosure policy (security.txt, responsible disclosure page), a defined response SLA (acknowledge within 24 hours, fix within 90 days for critical), a process for rewarding reporters, and a mechanism for coordinated disclosure. Find security bugs reported via support tickets with no formal security response path.
prompt_short: Design responsible disclosure process and bug bounty framework for security bugs.
prompt_filtered: Security disclosure only. Policy, SLA, coordination, and reporter reward.

title: Audit environment parity gaps causing production-only bug reproduction failures
prompt: Identify gaps between staging and production environments that cause bugs to appear only in production. Common gaps: anonymized or synthetic data missing edge cases present in real data, infrastructure differences (memory, cache size, network latency), feature flags enabled in production but not staging, and third-party integration stubs that don't reproduce vendor failure modes.
prompt_short: Find staging vs production environment parity gaps causing unreproducible bugs.
prompt_filtered: Environment parity only. Staging vs production differences causing reproduction failures.

title: Map High-Defect Components
prompt: Map High-Defect Components

title: Audit the entire bug backlog for severity, age, and ownership distributi
prompt: Audit the entire bug backlog for severity, age, and ownership distribution. Identify P0 and P1 bugs not in the current sprint, bugs older than 90 days still open, and bugs with no assigned owner. Cross-reference with affected user count and business impact to find under-prioritized issues. Recommend the triage process changes and the immediate action list to bring the backlog into a healthy state.

title: Review bug history by component over the past six months
prompt: Review bug history by component over the past six months. Identify the components generating the most bugs and assess root cause — code complexity, missing test coverage, unclear ownership, accumulated technical debt, or churn from frequent changes. For each top hotspot, recommend the targeted investment — test coverage, refactor, ownership clarification, or freeze on non-critical changes — that would most reduce ongoing bug rate.

title: Find bugs in the backlog that should be escalated to active production i
prompt: Find bugs in the backlog that should be escalated to active production incidents. Identify any bug affecting more than one percent of users, causing data loss, blocking revenue, or creating compliance exposure that is currently tracked as a low-priority ticket. Cross-reference bug descriptions with support ticket volume to find issues whose backlog severity understates their actual user impact. Recommend the escalation list and the process change preventing similar misclassification.

title: Evaluate the bug regression detection and prevention pipeline
prompt: Evaluate the bug regression detection and prevention pipeline. Identify bug categories that recur across releases — the same component, the same interaction pattern, or the same edge case manifesting repeatedly. Find bugs that were fixed, closed, and then re-introduced in a later release without a regression test preventing it. Assess whether bug-fix pull requests include a regression test as a requirement before merge, and whether test coverage reports surface coverage gaps in high-defect components. Recommend the regression prevention investments that would most reduce the rate of recurring bugs.

title: Audit the bug-to-feature boundary and technical debt management
prompt: Audit the bug-to-feature boundary and technical debt management. Identify bugs that are effectively feature requests in disguise — misrouted to the wrong queue — and features designed to work around unresolved bugs. Find bugs that have been deferred so many times they have become an accepted limitation documented in the knowledge base rather than a tracked commitment. Assess whether the backlog has a defined purge or review process — bugs marked invalid or no-longer-reproducible being closed, duplicates being consolidated, and old low-priority bugs with no recent activity being archived. Recommend the backlog hygiene changes that would most improve the signal quality of the bug tracking system.

title: Evaluate the bug tracking and resolution system as an engineering qualit
prompt: Evaluate the bug tracking and resolution system as an engineering quality signal. Assess the bug backlog as a whole — total open count, age distribution, severity distribution, and the trend over time. Identify whether the backlog is growing, stable, or shrinking and the relationship between intake rate and resolution rate. Find bugs that have been open for over 90 days without action — these represent either issues that should be closed as wontfix, issues that should be elevated to active work, or issues that no one has the authority to resolve. Examine bug ownership — whether every open bug has a named owner, whether unowned bugs accumulate without triage, and whether ownership transfer is tracked when components change hands. Look for severity classification consistency — whether the same impact level receives the same severity across teams, and whether high-severity bugs receive proportional attention. Examine the regression rate — bugs reintroduced after being fixed — as a quality signal. Summarize the five bug tracking improvements that would most increase backlog actionability.

title: Audit the bug intake, triage, and resolution workflow
prompt: Audit the bug intake, triage, and resolution workflow. Identify whether new bugs receive timely triage — severity assignment, owner assignment, and reproduction confirmation — or sit in untriaged backlogs. Find the bug report quality patterns — whether reporters provide reproduction steps, expected versus actual behavior, and environment context, or whether triage requires significant back-and-forth. Examine resolution patterns — whether fixes include regression tests, whether root cause analysis is performed for severe bugs, and whether postmortems generate process or architectural changes. Look for bug clusters — patterns of similar bugs across components that indicate a systemic issue rather than isolated problems. Assess the relationship between bug volume and team capacity — whether teams have allocated time to bug work or whether bugs compete with feature work in a way that systematically deprioritizes them. Recommend the workflow changes that would most improve bug resolution quality and reduce regression rate.

title: Audit the relationship between bug volume and engineering process health
prompt: Audit the relationship between bug volume and engineering process health. Assess whether bug intake rate is trending up or down across quarters, and whether the trend correlates with changes in release cadence, test coverage, code review rigor, or team growth. Find releases that shipped disproportionately high bug counts and identify the process conditions — compressed timelines, skipped test phases, insufficient review — that contributed. Examine whether the team tracks escaped defects — bugs found by users in production that should have been caught in testing — and whether escaped defect rates are used to calibrate the release process. Look for relationships between code areas with high churn rate and high defect density, indicating insufficient refactoring investment or inadequate test coverage in evolving code. Assess whether the time between bug report and verified fix is measured, and whether bottlenecks in that pipeline — slow triage, no reproduction steps, blocked verification — are addressed systematically. Summarize the five process improvements that would most reduce the rate of user-impacting bugs reaching production.

title: Find the highest-cost resources by service and usage
prompt: Analyze infrastructure costs by service, resource type, and environment. Identify the top 10 highest-cost resources. For each, assess whether the cost is proportionate to the value delivered and identify obvious optimization opportunities — right-sizing, reserved capacity, or elimination.
prompt_short: Find the top 10 highest-cost resources and obvious optimization opportunities.
prompt_filtered: Cost breakdown only. Top resources by spend and right-sizing opportunities.

title: Identify unused or orphaned resources wasting money
prompt: Find resources that are provisioned but not in use — stopped instances, unattached storage volumes, idle load balancers, unused IP addresses, empty databases. Estimate monthly cost of each category and recommend safe deletion candidates.
prompt_short: Find all unused provisioned resources wasting infrastructure budget.
prompt_filtered: Unused resources only. Orphaned and idle resource identification.

title: Assess reserved capacity vs on-demand usage ratio
prompt: Compare reserved instance coverage against actual on-demand usage. Identify workloads with steady, predictable usage that should be on reserved capacity but are still on more expensive on-demand pricing. Estimate the savings from converting to reserved or savings plans.
prompt_short: Find predictable workloads on expensive on-demand that should be reserved.
prompt_filtered: Reserved capacity analysis only. On-demand vs reserved cost comparison.

title: Review data transfer costs between services and regions
prompt: Analyze data transfer costs — between availability zones, between regions, to the internet, and from CDN. Identify the highest data transfer cost flows and assess whether architectural changes could reduce cross-zone or cross-region traffic.
prompt_short: Find the highest data transfer cost flows and reduction opportunities.
prompt_filtered: Data transfer costs only. Cross-zone, cross-region, and egress analysis.

title: Create cost attribution by feature or team for chargeback
prompt: Design a cost attribution model that maps infrastructure spend to specific features, teams, or cost centers. Define tagging requirements, allocation methodology for shared resources, and a reporting cadence. Identify the biggest unattributed spend currently.
prompt_short: Design a cost attribution model to enable chargeback by team or feature.
prompt_filtered: Attribution model only. Tagging strategy and shared resource allocation.

title: Identify cost anomalies and unusual spending patterns
prompt: Analyze historical cloud spend for trends and anomalies. Identify: sudden spending spikes (potential misconfiguration or attack), seasonal patterns, spend growth outpacing traffic growth, and unused resources. Set up alerts for unusual spend patterns.
prompt_short: Identify cost anomalies and unexpected spending increases.
prompt_filtered: Anomaly detection only. Spending spikes and trend analysis.

title: Audit reserved instances and savings plans for optimization
prompt: Review commitment-based discounts: are reserved instances or savings plans used? Is the coverage ratio appropriate (too low = lost discounts, too high = waste)? Are commitments sized correctly for actual usage? Are there unused reservations? Could hybrid reservations+spot be more cost-effective?
prompt_short: Audit reserved instances and savings plan utilization and sizing.
prompt_filtered: Reservations only. Coverage and sizing optimization.

title: Find cost optimization opportunities in compute and storage
prompt: Identify optimization quick wins: over-provisioned compute (low utilization), storage that's never accessed, database connections pooled inefficiently, or excessive data replication. Estimate savings per optimization. Prioritize by effort and ROI.
prompt_short: Identify quick-win cost optimizations in compute and storage.
prompt_filtered: Optimization opportunities only. Utilization and sizing gaps.

title: Review vendor and contract negotiation opportunities
prompt: Assess vendor spend: what are the top 5 vendors by spend? Are there multiyear discounts available? Can volume be consolidated across teams to negotiate better rates? Are there open-source or cheaper alternatives? When do current contracts renew?
prompt_short: Audit vendor spend and contract negotiation opportunities.
prompt_filtered: Vendor negotiations only. Top vendors and renewal timing.

title: Assess cloud data egress costs and data residency impact
prompt: Evaluate data transfer costs: where is data going (regions, out of cloud, customer downloads)? Is data being transferred inefficiently? Could data residency in a different region or commitment reduce egress? Are there overly large API responses? Are there unused data replicas?
prompt_short: Audit cloud data egress and data residency cost drivers.
prompt_filtered: Data transfer only. Egress by direction and optimization.

title: Review cost allocation and visibility across teams
prompt: Assess ownership: can each team see their own costs? Are costs allocated to products, teams, or cost centers? Is there chargeback or cost awareness programs? Do engineers feel empowered to optimize costs? Are there cost reduction incentives or penalties?
prompt_short: Audit cost visibility and team accountability for cloud spend.
prompt_filtered: Accountability only. Cost allocation and visibility per team.

title: Evaluate FinOps maturity and continuous cost optimization culture
prompt: Assess organizational maturity: is cost optimization a team responsibility or finance-only? Are there regular cost reviews? Is there a procurement process or is IT spending unchecked? Do teams have self-service cost tools? Are there cost targets and KPIs? Is cost optimization in engineering hiring and promotion criteria?
prompt_short: Audit FinOps maturity and continuous cost optimization culture.
prompt_filtered: Organizational maturity only. Processes and accountability.

title: Analyze infrastructure costs by service, resource type, and environment
prompt: Analyze infrastructure costs by service, resource type, and environment over the past 90 days. Identify the top ten highest-cost line items and assess whether each cost is proportionate to the value delivered. For each, identify obvious optimization opportunities — right-sizing, reserved capacity conversion, or elimination. Sequence the optimizations by cost reduction potential and operational risk.

title: Find unused or orphaned resources draining budget — stopped instances, u
prompt: Find unused or orphaned resources draining budget — stopped instances, unattached storage volumes, idle load balancers, unused IP addresses, empty databases, expired snapshots, and abandoned development environments. Estimate monthly cost per category and recommend the safe deletion sequence with the right confirmation gates for shared infrastructure.

title: Compare reserved capacity coverage against actual on-demand usage patter
prompt: Compare reserved capacity coverage against actual on-demand usage patterns over the past quarter. Identify workloads with steady, predictable utilization currently running on more expensive on-demand pricing. Estimate savings from converting to reserved instances or savings plans at appropriate commitment levels. Recommend the reservation strategy that balances savings against flexibility for changing workload patterns.

title: Audit cost tagging and attribution completeness across all infrastructur
prompt: Audit cost tagging and attribution completeness across all infrastructure. Identify resources with no cost center tag, no owning team tag, and no environment label — making it impossible to attribute spend to a business unit or determine whether a resource is in production or development. Find resources where inconsistent tagging prevents accurate cost rollup across environments or services. Assess whether the tagging policy is enforced at resource creation — via policy-as-code or cloud policy engine — or applied retroactively and incompletely. Recommend the tagging governance changes that would most improve cost attribution accuracy.

title: Evaluate unit economics for the top three cost drivers
prompt: Evaluate unit economics for the top three cost drivers. For each, calculate the cost per active user, cost per request, or cost per processed unit and compare against the revenue each unit generates. Identify cost drivers where the unit economics are negative or trending negative as scale increases, indicating a structural cost problem rather than a volume problem. Find optimizations that would reduce per-unit cost without degrading the user experience — architectural changes, pricing tier migrations, algorithm improvements, or third-party vendor replacements. Recommend the cost reduction investments ranked by ROI and implementation complexity.

title: Evaluate the FinOps practice across the engineering organization
prompt: Evaluate the FinOps practice across the engineering organization. Assess whether infrastructure cost is treated as an engineering quality metric alongside reliability and performance, or as a finance concern surfaced only at budget reviews. Identify whether cost data is available to engineering teams in real time at the granularity they need to make decisions — per service, per environment, per feature — or aggregated to the point of being unactionable. Find cost anomalies in the past quarter — unexpected spend spikes, runaway costs from misconfigured resources, and costs from services no one remembers provisioning. Examine whether cost optimization is a recurring engineering investment or a periodic crisis-driven activity. Look for systemic waste patterns — over-provisioned compute, expensive storage tiers used for cold data, idle development environments, and data transfer costs from cross-region or cross-zone architecture decisions made without cost awareness. Summarize the five FinOps practice improvements that would most reduce ongoing cloud spend without compromising reliability.

title: Audit the cost attribution and accountability architecture
prompt: Audit the cost attribution and accountability architecture. Identify whether infrastructure spend is mapped to teams, features, or cost centers through resource tagging — and whether the tagging discipline is consistent enough to produce reliable attribution. Find unattributed spend categories that obscure ownership — shared resources with no clear consumer breakdown, cross-team services with no chargeback, and vendor invoices without internal allocation. Examine whether cost shows up in team dashboards alongside other engineering metrics or remains invisible until budget escalation. Look for opportunities to install cost guardrails — budgets and alerts at the team or service level, automated detection of expensive misconfiguration patterns, and pre-deployment cost estimates for significant new infrastructure. Assess whether teams are incentivized to optimize their cost — through chargeback, public dashboards, or shared savings — or whether reduction effort is uncompensated. Recommend the attribution and accountability changes that would most align engineering decisions with cost reality.

title: Audit the FinOps maturity across the engineering organization
prompt: Audit the FinOps maturity across the engineering organization. Assess whether cloud costs are reviewed at least monthly by a dedicated owner — not only when a billing alert fires. Find services where the engineering team has no visibility into the cost of the infrastructure they operate, creating decisions that optimize for engineering convenience at the expense of cost efficiency. Examine whether cost efficiency is considered at architecture review time — whether high-cost patterns like synchronous fan-out, large data transfers across regions, and full-table scans are flagged before implementation. Look for cost governance gaps in the development environment — whether dev and staging environments are sized proportionally to their needs, whether long-lived development resources are automatically shut down outside working hours, and whether engineers have an easy path to request ephemeral environments rather than maintaining persistent ones. Assess whether cost anomaly detection is in place — automated alerts when daily spend deviates beyond a threshold from the rolling average. Summarize the five FinOps improvements that would most improve cost efficiency without slowing engineering delivery.

title: Identify the highest-impact hire for the current roadmap
prompt: Assess the current team's capability gaps against the next 6 months of roadmap. Identify the single hire that would have the greatest unblocking impact — what skill is most constraining delivery, and what background should that person have.
prompt_short: Find the single most impactful hire given the current roadmap gaps.
prompt_filtered: Hiring priority only. Capability gap vs roadmap impact.

title: Design a technical interview for this role and stack
prompt: Create a technical interview process for the role that tests real job-relevant skills without relying on whiteboard algorithms irrelevant to daily work. Define: take-home project vs live coding, evaluation rubric, focus areas, and how to assess for collaboration and communication.
prompt_short: Design a job-relevant technical interview for this specific role and stack.
prompt_filtered: Interview design only. Real work signals vs whiteboard problems.

title: Audit developer onboarding for time to first contribution
prompt: Review the new developer onboarding process. Measure or estimate time to first commit, first feature contribution, and first independent production change. Identify the steps adding the most friction — environment setup, context transfer, code review cycle time.
prompt_short: Find what makes new developer time-to-first-contribution long.
prompt_filtered: Dev onboarding only. Time-to-contribution friction analysis.

title: Find knowledge transfer gaps when engineers leave
prompt: Identify the knowledge that would be lost if a given engineer left today — systems they own, decisions they remember, processes only they perform. For each, recommend a specific handoff artifact: documentation, pair programming session, recorded walkthrough, or runbook.
prompt_short: Identify what knowledge would be lost if this engineer left today.
prompt_filtered: Offboarding knowledge only. Tacit knowledge and handoff artifacts needed.

title: Assess team communication and async collaboration quality
prompt: Evaluate how the team communicates across async channels — documentation quality, decision-making transparency, meeting culture, and whether remote or cross-timezone members have equal access to information and influence. Identify the biggest communication anti-patterns.
prompt_short: Assess async collaboration quality and find communication anti-patterns.
prompt_filtered: Communication quality only. Async patterns and information access equity.

title: Evaluate hiring process and candidate experience
prompt: Assess recruiting: how long from application to offer? Are candidates given feedback on rejection? What's the candidate satisfaction score? Are interviews consistent or varies widely? Are there bias traps (brand preference, non-diverse panel)? Is the hiring process documented and standardized?
prompt_short: Audit hiring process quality and candidate experience.
prompt_filtered: Hiring process only. Time-to-offer and candidate satisfaction.

title: Assess diversity, equity, and inclusion in hiring
prompt: Review diversity metrics: what's the demographic breakdown of hires vs applicants? Are there gaps in representation? Are there unconscious bias issues in job descriptions or interviews? Are there diversity hiring goals or accountability? Is there inclusive recruitment (underrepresented groups)?
prompt_short: Audit diversity and inclusion in hiring and representation.
prompt_filtered: Diversity only. Applicant demographics and hiring gaps.

title: Review employee engagement and satisfaction surveys
prompt: Evaluate engagement: is there a regular pulse survey? What's the overall engagement score? Are there low-scoring dimensions (compensation, growth, leadership)? Do results lead to action or are they ignored? How is feedback from surveys communicated back to employees?
prompt_short: Audit employee engagement survey results and action plans.
prompt_filtered: Engagement surveys only. Pulse results and follow-up actions.

title: Assess professional development and learning opportunities
prompt: Evaluate growth: is there a career development framework? Are learning budgets sufficient? Are there mentorship programs? Can employees attend conferences or take courses? Are there internal training or knowledge-sharing sessions? Is growth tracked and encouraged?
prompt_short: Audit professional development and learning budget allocation.
prompt_filtered: Development only. Budget, mentorship, and growth paths.

title: Review manager quality and support for people development
prompt: Assess management: do managers get training on feedback and people development? Are there regular 1-on-1s and performance conversations? Do managers feel supported? Is there a process for managing underperformers? Are promotions merit-based and transparent?
prompt_short: Audit manager training and quality of people conversations.
prompt_filtered: Management quality only. Training and feedback processes.

title: Evaluate compensation fairness and pay equity
prompt: Assess compensation: is pay equitable (same for same role, experience, performance)? Are there unexplained pay gaps by gender, race, or tenure? How does compensation compare to market? Are there transparent pay ranges? Are raises merit-based or cost-of-living?
prompt_short: Audit pay equity and compensation fairness.
prompt_filtered: Compensation equity only. Pay gaps and market alignment.

title: Evaluate total rewards beyond salary and benefits quality
prompt: Assess total value: what's the total comp breakdown (salary, bonus, equity, benefits)? Are benefits competitive (health, retirement, time off)? Do employees understand and value the benefits? Is there flexibility (remote work, flexible hours)? Are there perks that add value?
prompt_short: Audit total rewards package and benefits quality.
prompt_filtered: Total rewards only. Benefits, equity, and flexibility.

title: Identify Attrition Risk Signals
prompt: Identify Attrition Risk Signals

title: Assess the current team's capability gaps against the next six months of
prompt: Assess the current team's capability gaps against the next six months of roadmap commitments. Identify the single hire with the greatest unblocking potential — what skill is most constraining delivery, what level of seniority is required, and what background should that person have. Cross-reference with current team composition to ensure the hire fills a real gap rather than duplicating existing strength. Recommend the role definition and sourcing approach.

title: Design a technical interview process for the next role to fill
prompt: Design a technical interview process for the next role to fill. Define the take-home or live-coding format, the evaluation rubric, the focus areas that test real job-relevant skills, and how to assess collaboration and communication quality. Eliminate gatekeeping signals unrelated to actual work — whiteboard algorithms, trivia questions, unpaid extensive take-homes — that filter for performative skill rather than productive contribution. Recommend the interview structure and scoring discipline.

title: Audit the developer onboarding journey from offer acceptance to first in
prompt: Audit the developer onboarding journey from offer acceptance to first independent production change. Measure or estimate time to first commit, first feature contribution, and first independent deploy. Identify the steps adding the most friction — environment setup, codebase orientation, decision context transfer, code review cycle time. Recommend the onboarding redesign sequence prioritized by time-to-contribution impact.

title: Evaluate the engineering performance management system for clarity, fair
prompt: Evaluate the engineering performance management system for clarity, fairness, and development value. Identify whether performance expectations are documented in a leveling framework engineers can use to self-assess, whether feedback is delivered on a regular cadence rather than only at formal review cycles, and whether compensation decisions are transparently tied to the framework. Find calibration gaps — engineers at the same level with significantly different performance expectations across teams, or ratings that are consistently skewed by manager leniency or harshness. Assess whether the performance system supports growth or primarily functions as a rating mechanism with no development plan. Recommend the performance management changes that would most improve clarity and development impact.

title: Assess attrition risk across the engineering team
prompt: Assess attrition risk across the engineering team. Identify engineers who have not received a meaningful role change, promotion, or scope expansion in over 18 months and assess whether stagnation is creating departure risk. Find engineers who are the sole owner of critical systems — at elevated departure risk from a bus factor perspective. Examine whether exit interview data reveals patterns — consistent themes around management quality, career growth, compensation, or work environment — and whether these themes are addressed. Assess whether compensation is competitive against current market rates for each role and level. Recommend the people investment changes that would most reduce departure risk for the engineers most critical to the engineering organization.

title: Evaluate the engineering hiring and people practice as a long-term capab
prompt: Evaluate the engineering hiring and people practice as a long-term capability investment. Assess whether hiring decisions are driven by current capacity gaps versus a deliberate capability roadmap — whether the team has the skills it needs in 18 months, not just for next quarter's commitments. Identify the highest-impact open roles given the roadmap, the capability dependencies between hires, and the realistic time to fill given the candidate market. Find the recurring hiring failure patterns — roles that take too long to fill, roles where new hires consistently underperform expectations, and roles that turn over within the first year. Examine whether the interview process predicts performance — whether the strongest interview signals correlate with the strongest on-the-job contributors. Look for diversity, equity, and inclusion gaps in the hiring funnel — sourcing channels, candidate experience, evaluation calibration, and offer terms — that systematically underrepresent qualified candidates from underrepresented groups. Summarize the five hiring practice improvements that would most increase team capability and retention.

title: Audit the team's communication and collaboration architecture
prompt: Audit the team's communication and collaboration architecture. Identify whether the team operates async-first with documentation as the source of truth, or whether critical knowledge lives in synchronous meetings and chat rooms inaccessible to those not present. Find the meeting culture patterns — whether meetings have clear purpose, defined outcomes, and right-sized attendance, or whether they accumulate without periodic pruning. Examine whether decisions are documented in a discoverable format — architecture decision records, design docs, written principal-level updates — or whether new team members must reconstruct context through conversation. Look for information equity gaps — whether remote, cross-timezone, or part-time members have equal access to information and influence as colocated, full-time members. Assess whether the team has psychological safety mechanisms — anonymous feedback channels, upward feedback culture, postmortem blamelessness — that surface concerns before they become attrition risk. Recommend the collaboration practice changes that would most increase information equity and team effectiveness.

title: Audit the engineering culture and team health for sustainability and inc
prompt: Audit the engineering culture and team health for sustainability and inclusion. Assess whether the team operates with psychological safety — whether engineers raise concerns about quality, timelines, or decisions without fear of blame, and whether post-incident reviews focus on system improvement rather than individual fault. Find burnout risk signals — engineers consistently working beyond sustainable hours, on-call rotations with insufficient recovery time, and sprint velocity driven by crunch rather than capacity. Examine whether the team communication norms exclude or disadvantage certain communication styles — whether decision-making happens primarily in synchronous formats that favor extroverts or in timezones that exclude distributed team members. Look for inclusion gaps in hiring pipelines, promotion rates, and representation in technical leadership compared to the general team composition. Assess whether the team has shared norms for code review feedback, design critique, and technical disagreement that make collaboration feel safe and constructive. Summarize the five culture and team health improvements that would most improve engineering sustainability and retention.

title: Audit content structure for consistency and reuse
prompt: Review the content model. Identify duplicate content stored in multiple places with no single source of truth, content fields without consistent naming, and content types that overlap in purpose. Recommend a normalized content structure.
prompt_short: Audit content model for duplication, inconsistency, and overlap.
prompt_filtered: Content structure only. Model design and normalization gaps.

title: Find content lacking localization support infrastructure
prompt: Identify content types and fields in the CMS that have no localization support — no locale-specific variants, no translation workflow, no locale fallback logic. Assess the effort to add localization to each content type.
prompt_short: Find content types with no localization support in the CMS.
prompt_filtered: CMS localization only. Content types without locale support.

title: Review content publishing workflow for approval gaps
prompt: Audit the content publishing workflow. Identify paths that allow content to be published without review, content types with no approval step, and the role permissions that allow bypassing the workflow. Assess the risk of publishing incorrect or harmful content by accident.
prompt_short: Find content publishing paths that bypass the approval workflow.
prompt_filtered: Publishing workflow only. Approval gaps and bypass paths.

title: Assess content delivery performance and caching
prompt: Evaluate how content is delivered to users. Identify content served without CDN caching, content requests creating database queries on every page load, and content delivery with no stale-while-revalidate strategy for frequently accessed but rarely changing content.
prompt_short: Find content delivery paths with no CDN caching or unnecessary DB queries.
prompt_filtered: Content delivery only. Caching and DB query reduction opportunities.

title: Find orphaned content with no active references
prompt: Identify content entries that are no longer referenced by any live page, template, or API response. Assess whether orphaned content should be archived, deleted, or is actually needed but the reference was accidentally removed.
prompt_short: Find content entries with no active references on live pages.
prompt_filtered: Orphaned content only. Unreferenced content identification.

title: Audit CMS performance and content delivery speed
prompt: Evaluate performance: how long to load content pages? Are there database N+1 queries? Is content cached at edge or origin only? Are images optimized? Is content delivery geographically distributed? Do slow pages correlate with bounce rates?
prompt_short: Audit CMS query performance and content delivery speed.
prompt_filtered: Performance only. Load times and query optimization.

title: Review content versioning and rollback capabilities
prompt: Assess versioning: when content is updated, are previous versions retained? Can you rollback to an earlier version if a change is problematic? Is there audit logging of who changed what when? Are there approval workflows before publishing?
prompt_short: Audit content versioning and rollback capabilities.
prompt_filtered: Versioning only. History and approval workflows.

title: Assess content search and discoverability for users
prompt: Evaluate search: can users find content with full-text search? Are search results relevant? Is search fast? Are there filters? Is navigation intuitive for users unfamiliar with taxonomy? Is search analytics collected to improve results?
prompt_short: Audit content search functionality and discoverability.
prompt_filtered: Search only. Relevance and performance.

title: Review content access controls and editing permissions
prompt: Evaluate access: are permissions granular (edit vs publish vs delete)? Can different teams edit different content areas? Are there role-based controls? Can a single editor delete all content? Are permissions audited? Can access be revoked instantly?
prompt_short: Audit content access controls and editing permissions.
prompt_filtered: Access control only. Role-based permissions and audit.

title: Assess content freshness and staleness detection
prompt: Evaluate staleness: how old is the oldest published content? Are there time-dependent content updates (dates, offers)? Is there an automated check for stale content? Are outdated pages flagged or archived? What's the process for deprecating old content?
prompt_short: Audit content freshness and staleness detection.
prompt_filtered: Freshness only. Age and deprecation processes.

title: Review content workflow and editorial calendar
prompt: Evaluate workflow: is there an editorial calendar? Can editors schedule content for future publish? Are there drafts and review stages? Is publishing manual or scheduled? Is there a handoff between writers and editors? Are delays in publishing common?
prompt_short: Audit content workflow and editorial publishing process.
prompt_filtered: Workflow only. Drafts, scheduling, and review stages.

title: Evaluate content metadata and structured data for SEO
prompt: Assess metadata: are content pages tagged with rich metadata (title, description, keywords, OpenGraph)? Is structured data (JSON-LD, schema.org) used? Are metadata fields validated? Do all pages have unique titles and descriptions? Is metadata consistency checked?
prompt_short: Audit content metadata completeness for SEO and sharing.
prompt_filtered: Metadata only. Completeness and validation.

prompt: Identify Content Delivery Cache Gaps

title: Audit the content model for duplication, inconsistency, and overlap
prompt: Audit the content model for duplication, inconsistency, and overlap. Identify content stored in multiple places with no single source of truth, content fields with inconsistent naming across content types, and content types whose purposes overlap. Cross-reference with editor workflow to find content types that exist but are rarely used. Recommend the content model normalization sequence prioritized by editor confusion impact.

title: Review the content publishing workflow for approval and governance gaps
prompt: Review the content publishing workflow for approval and governance gaps. Identify paths that allow content to publish without review, content types missing approval steps, and role permissions that allow workflow bypass. Cross-reference with recent publishing incidents — incorrect copy, broken links, premature releases — to find workflow weaknesses. Recommend the workflow tightening that preserves editor velocity while reducing publication risk.

title: Find orphaned content with no active references — content entries not li
prompt: Find orphaned content with no active references — content entries not linked from any live page, template, or API response. Assess whether each orphan should be archived, deleted, or is actually needed with a reference accidentally removed. Identify content types most prone to orphaning and the workflow changes that would prevent future orphans. Recommend the cleanup approach with appropriate confirmation gates for content that may be referenced externally.

title: Audit the content search and discoverability architecture
prompt: Audit the content search and discoverability architecture. Identify content that is published but unreachable through search — missing metadata fields, untitled entries, empty descriptions, or no tags. Find search queries with zero results where content exists but is not indexed correctly. Assess whether the search ranking model surfaces the most relevant content for common queries or returns alphabetically ordered results. Examine whether content navigation relies entirely on hierarchical browsing without a search escape valve for users who know what they want. Recommend the search and discoverability improvements that would most increase organic content engagement.

title: Evaluate the content versioning and rollback capability
prompt: Evaluate the content versioning and rollback capability. Identify whether content edits create a version history that editors can review and restore from, whether version diffs are visible to reviewers during approval, and whether a published change can be rolled back in under five minutes if it introduces an error. Find content management systems where publishing is irreversible without a full re-edit — creating risk for time-sensitive corrections. Assess whether scheduled content publishes are auditable — who scheduled, what changed, and when. Recommend the versioning and rollback improvements that would most reduce the risk of incorrect content reaching the audience without a safe recovery path.

title: Evaluate the content management architecture as both an editor experienc
prompt: Evaluate the content management architecture as both an editor experience and a delivery system. Assess whether the content model reflects the actual structure of content needed by the product — flexible enough to handle real content variations without forcing editors into workarounds, structured enough to enforce consistency where it matters. Identify content types that have grown organically without periodic refactoring, fields whose purpose has drifted from their original intent, and rich-text editors that allow editors to bypass structured fields with HTML escapes. Find editor workflow friction — steps that take too long, fields that require obscure formatting knowledge, and validation that prevents reasonable content from being published. Examine whether translations, localization, and locale-specific variants are handled at the content model level or bolted on through workarounds. Look for content lifecycle gaps — drafts that accumulate, content scheduled to publish that never publishes, and content with no expiration policy that ages out of relevance. Summarize the five content management improvements that would most reduce editor friction and content quality issues.

title: Audit the content delivery and performance architecture
prompt: Audit the content delivery and performance architecture. Identify whether content is served from edge caches with appropriate TTLs and stale-while-revalidate behavior, or whether each request hits the origin and triggers database queries. Find content delivery paths that bypass CDN caching, content APIs that return larger payloads than the consumer needs, and rendering pipelines that re-fetch the same content multiple times during a single page render. Examine cache invalidation — whether content changes propagate to edges quickly enough for the use case, whether bulk invalidations during large content migrations cause origin overload, and whether stale content is served when it shouldn't be. Look for content delivery failure modes — what happens when the CMS is unreachable, whether the application degrades gracefully or fails entirely, and whether critical content has appropriate fallbacks. Assess whether content delivery performance is measured — TTFB, payload size, cache hit rate — and whether regressions trigger alerts. Recommend the delivery architecture changes that would most improve performance and reliability.

title: Audit the content governance model for accountability and freshness
prompt: Audit the content governance model for accountability and freshness. Assess whether every published content piece has a named owner responsible for accuracy, a defined review cadence, and an expiry or review date after which the content is flagged for update or retirement. Find content published more than 12 months ago with no review record — documentation that references deprecated features, pricing that no longer applies, or screenshots of a UI that has since changed. Examine whether the content audit process is manual or automated — whether an automated job flags content meeting staleness criteria or whether the review cadence depends on editor memory. Look for content governance gaps in high-risk categories — legal terms, safety instructions, compliance documentation — where stale content creates regulatory or liability exposure. Assess whether non-technical content contributors have a clear escalation path when they identify content that should be updated but cannot edit themselves. Summarize the five content governance improvements that would most reduce stale and inaccurate content in production.

title: Audit media upload pipeline for reliability and validation
prompt: Review the media upload pipeline. Verify that uploads are validated server-side (magic bytes, not just MIME type), have a maximum file size enforced, support resumable uploads for large files, and have error states that communicate clearly what went wrong.
prompt_short: Audit media upload pipeline for server-side validation and error handling.
prompt_filtered: Upload pipeline only. Validation, size limits, and error states.

title: Find media processing jobs with no failure recovery
prompt: Identify media processing pipelines — transcoding, image resizing, thumbnail generation — that have no retry on failure, no dead-letter queue, and no way for users to know the processing failed. Assess the user experience impact.
prompt_short: Find media processing pipelines that fail silently with no user notification.
prompt_filtered: Processing failure handling only. Retry, DLQ, and user feedback.

title: Assess media storage costs and delivery optimization
prompt: Review media storage and delivery costs. Identify media stored in more expensive tiers than necessary, media not compressed to appropriate quality levels, and CDN delivery gaps where media is served from origin instead of edge.
prompt_short: Find media storage and delivery cost reduction opportunities.
prompt_filtered: Media cost only. Storage tier, compression, and CDN coverage.

title: Review media security and access control implementation
prompt: Assess how access to private media is controlled. Verify pre-signed URLs expire appropriately, public media doesn't include private files, and there is no way to enumerate media objects belonging to other users by guessing keys or IDs.
prompt_short: Audit media access control for private content exposure risks.
prompt_filtered: Media access control only. Pre-signed URL expiry and enumeration risks.

title: Find media format gaps blocking user segments
prompt: Identify media format limitations that block users — audio formats not supported on certain browsers, video codecs incompatible with older devices, image formats not supported in email clients. Recommend format conversion or fallback strategies.
prompt_short: Find media format limitations blocking users on specific platforms.
prompt_filtered: Format compatibility only. Browser and device support gaps.

title: Audit media storage and retrieval efficiency
prompt: Evaluate storage: are media files deduplicated (same file not stored twice)? Is storage organized by type and access pattern? Are frequently accessed files cached at edge? Are rarely accessed files archived to cheaper storage tiers? Is storage cost per media type tracked?
prompt_short: Audit media storage efficiency and retrieval performance.
prompt_filtered: Storage only. Deduplication and tiering strategy.

title: Review media metadata and searchability
prompt: Assess metadata: are media files tagged (keywords, alt text, descriptions)? Is metadata complete and accurate? Can users search for media by keywords? Are OCR results (for images) searchable? Is metadata validation enforced?
prompt_short: Audit media metadata completeness and searchability.
prompt_filtered: Metadata only. Tagging and search capability.

title: Evaluate media processing pipeline and quality gates
prompt: Review processing: are uploaded media scanned for viruses? Are images resized/optimized automatically? Are videos transcoded to multiple formats? Are there quality checks (resolution, size, duration)? Are failed processing jobs retried? Is there error visibility to users?
prompt_short: Audit media processing pipeline and quality validation.
prompt_filtered: Processing pipeline only. Validation and error handling.

title: Assess media delivery speed and CDN optimization
prompt: Evaluate delivery: is media served via CDN or origin? Are appropriate caching headers set? Is image optimization (WebP, lazy loading) used? Are videos streaming (HLS/DASH) or progressive download? What's the median delivery time? Are there geographic variations?
prompt_short: Audit media delivery speed and CDN optimization.
prompt_filtered: Delivery performance only. CDN usage and caching headers.

title: Review media access controls and permissions
prompt: Evaluate access: can all authenticated users access all media or are there permission controls? Can private media be accessed by direct URL (security issue)? Is download access tracked? Can media be downloaded, shared, or only viewed? Are there rate limits on downloads?
prompt_short: Audit media access controls and download permissions.
prompt_filtered: Access control only. Private media and download limits.

title: Assess media expiration and lifecycle management
prompt: Evaluate lifecycle: when media is deleted, is it actually removed or archived? Are temporary files (previews, thumbnails) cleaned up? Are there retention policies? Can users permanently delete their media? Is there a grace period before permanent deletion?
prompt_short: Audit media deletion and expiration policies.
prompt_filtered: Lifecycle management only. Deletion and archive policies.

title: Evaluate media accessibility and alt text quality
prompt: Assess accessibility: do images have alt text? Is alt text descriptive (not just filename)? Are there captions for videos? Is audio described for videos? Are media players keyboard accessible? Is there a way for users to add alt text if missing?
prompt_short: Audit media accessibility and alt text completeness.
prompt_filtered: Accessibility only. Alt text and captions.

title: Audit the media upload pipeline end to end
prompt: Audit the media upload pipeline end to end. Verify server-side validation uses magic byte detection rather than trusting MIME type alone, that maximum file size is enforced before bytes reach storage, that resumable upload is supported for large files, and that error states communicate clearly which constraint failed. Identify upload failure patterns and the user experience gaps each creates. Recommend the upload pipeline hardening sequence.

title: Review the media processing pipeline — transcoding, image resizing, thum
prompt: Review the media processing pipeline — transcoding, image resizing, thumbnail generation, waveform extraction. Identify pipelines with no retry on failure, no dead-letter queue, and no user-visible status when processing fails. Find processing jobs that fail silently and leave the upload in an unusable state. Recommend the processing reliability improvements that would most reduce silent failures and orphaned upload artifacts.

title: Examine media storage and delivery for cost and performance optimization
prompt: Examine media storage and delivery for cost and performance optimization opportunities. Identify media stored in expensive hot tiers that could move to cooler tiers based on access patterns, media compressed at suboptimal quality levels for the use case, and CDN coverage gaps where media is served from origin instead of edge. Recommend the storage and delivery optimization sequence that balances cost reduction with quality preservation.

title: Audit media processing pipeline observability
prompt: Audit media processing pipeline observability. Identify whether processing job success rate, queue depth, processing latency, and failure rate are monitored in real time. Find job types with no alerting — where a sustained failure would not be detected until a user reports missing media. Examine whether failed processing jobs are automatically retried with appropriate backoff, written to a dead-letter queue for investigation, and surfaced in a dashboard for manual replay. Assess whether per-format and per-quality-tier success rates are tracked separately — coarse aggregate metrics can mask format-specific failures. Recommend the observability investments that would most reduce time to detection for media pipeline failures.

title: Evaluate media storage costs, access patterns, and lifecycle optimizatio
prompt: Evaluate media storage costs, access patterns, and lifecycle optimization. Identify media objects that have not been accessed in over 90 days but remain in hot storage, media versions — original, transcoded, thumbnail — with no lifecycle rule transitioning them to lower-cost storage tiers, and storage buckets with no deletion policy for soft-deleted or expired content. Assess whether media delivery is optimized per device and connection — whether the correct quality tier is selected based on client capabilities rather than always serving the highest quality. Find duplicate media uploads — identical files stored under different keys — that could be deduplicated with content-addressed storage. Recommend the storage lifecycle and deduplication changes that would most reduce media storage cost.

title: Evaluate the media processing pipeline as a reliability and quality syst
prompt: Evaluate the media processing pipeline as a reliability and quality system. Assess the upload path — whether server-side validation catches malicious files, whether file size limits protect storage costs, whether resumable upload handles large files on unreliable connections, and whether the user receives accurate progress feedback during long uploads. Identify the post-upload processing steps — transcoding to multiple bitrates for HLS, generating thumbnails, extracting metadata, computing perceptual hashes — and whether each step has retry logic, dead-letter handling, and observability. Find processing pipelines that fail silently, leaving uploads in an unusable state without alerting either the user or operations. Examine format coverage across the user base — whether the formats produced match the playback capabilities of target devices and browsers, including older platforms that lack modern codec support. Look for media quality regression patterns — settings changes that reduced quality without notice, processing pipelines that produce different output than the original specification, and quality monitoring that catches regressions before users report them. Summarize the five media pipeline improvements that would most increase reliability and quality.

title: Audit media security, access control, and lifecycle management
prompt: Audit media security, access control, and lifecycle management. Identify how access to private media is enforced — whether pre-signed URLs are used with appropriate expiration, whether public buckets contain only intentionally public content, and whether object naming patterns prevent enumeration of media belonging to other users. Find access control gaps where private content is reachable through guessable URLs, where pre-signed URLs are issued with longer-than-necessary expiration, and where access logs are insufficient to trace data exposure incidents. Examine the media lifecycle — whether deleted user content is actually removed from all storage tiers including backups within an appropriate window, whether GDPR data subject access requests can produce a complete media inventory for a user, and whether retention policies are enforced rather than aspirational. Look for storage cost leaks — orphaned media uploaded but never associated with a parent record, processing artifacts retained after the source was deleted, and old media versions retained without lifecycle policy. Recommend the security and lifecycle improvements that would most reduce data exposure risk and storage waste.

title: Audit the media upload security and content moderation pipeline
prompt: Audit the media upload security and content moderation pipeline. Assess whether uploaded media is validated server-side against magic bytes rather than relying on client-provided MIME types, whether size and dimension limits are enforced at upload time rather than after processing, and whether executable content disguised as media files is detected and rejected. Examine whether content moderation — automated detection of prohibited content categories — is applied before media is made publicly accessible or only after a user report. Find gaps in the moderation pipeline — file types that bypass moderation, processing paths that skip safety checks, and media that becomes publicly accessible during the window between upload completion and moderation completion. Look for access control gaps — media from private or deleted accounts accessible via direct URL, pre-signed URLs with excessive expiration windows, and media CDN caching that retains content after the source has been deleted. Summarize the five security improvements that would most reduce the risk of prohibited content reaching the audience and sensitive media being exposed without authorization.

title: Regular Project Update Post
prompt:
Write a regular project update post — not a launch announcement, just a natural update sharing what I'm building.

Tone: conversational, genuine, low-pressure. Like talking to followers who already know the project.

Include only what I provide:
- What the project is (brief context for new readers)
- Current goals or direction
- Any recent changes or decisions (only if I mention them)
- What comes next (only if I mention it)

Do NOT:
- Make it sound like a big reveal or launch
- Add hype or launch language
- Invent details I did not provide
- Add hashtags unless I ask

Adapt the length and platform to what I specify. Default to a short LinkedIn/Instagram-style paragraph post.
